i'm usually hesitant to try new things, but when i noticed the revamp of one of my favorite childhood games was on sale, i just knew i had to give it a shot - and i'm really glad i did

there may be a slight nostalgia-induced bias in this review, considering i've been playing this game on-and-off for 15 years (i have very fond memories of playing couch co-op with my grandma, as well as the gamehouse games collection in general), but i'll try to be as objective as possible in my review :)

right off the bat, i was greeted by a charming sense of familiarity - beautiful artwork in the iconic claymation style, an updated version of a banger soundtrack, and even the layouts of the levels themselves have been faithfully recreated

the gameplay is largely identical to the original platypus (which itself is already a blast), not just in terms of mechanics and the the "overall vibe", but also a bunch of quality-of-life improvements over the 2002 game, such as (but not necessarily limited to):
- easier-to-see attacks (a distinct glow around the red dots/pellets, and other attacks)
- audio & visual warning indicators for falling rocks and other "surprises"
- bonus fruit gravitating towards you when you get near it, making it easier to collect
- power up pickups granting a short grace period of immunity

aside from quality-of-life improvements, the gameplay has also been enhanced with new levels, enemies and powerups, all of which are a perfect fit for the game, even including the sillier additions. while the base game itself is pretty short (iirc, around 90 minutes of playtime), there is still plenty of fun to be had, like:
- mastering the gameplay: at higher difficulties, getting perfect clears, etc...
- platypus classic, a built-in mod which closely resembles the original game
- two free DLC (at the time of writing this review, winter and halloween themed), very short but still enjoyable
- local couch co-op, and when combined with steam remote play together, anyone online can join you without even having to own the game
- workshop support for modders and players alike to enhance the vanilla gameplay or add entire new campaigns altogether (would highly recommend trying out "Desert Mission" and "Casinopolis", but there's many more custom levels and campaigns to be made)

speaking of gameplay, two interesting design choices that differ from the original are the loss of powerups upon death, as well as powerups disappearing if not picked up on time (which means not being able to save them for later). both of these changes can make the gameplay slightly more tedious, especially during boss fights, but the increased risk in keeping them makes it more rewarding - i'm not sure whether i like or dislike this change honestly, it's simply "different" with its own pros and cons. fortunately, if you don't like this behavior for whatever reason, the built-in classic mod reverts this behavior akin to the original game

while we're on the topic of the classic mod, i won't bother nitpicking small details as they're either deliberate design choices or aren't detrimental to the gameplay itself, but i will note that my main gripe is that, even on the normal difficulty, enemies simply shoot too much and too often. while this sort of more consistent, less RNG dependent gameplay fits reclayed really well, it makes the classic mod much harder than it should be. for comparison, normal difficulty is significantly harder than the original game's normal difficulty, where enemies shoot far less projectiles and far less often. nevertheless, the attention to detail is spot-on, and is still a great recreation of the original platypus

compared to the classic mod and the original game, the respective music for each level is mixed around a bit in reclayed, but i personally quite enjoy the change, and i was quite pleasantly surprised to even hear a song from platypus 2. one great feature for us tinkerers is that we're given the ability to choose our own songs for each level, and seeing which songs are copyrighted is a nice (and, quite honestly, unique) touch for anyone interested in streaming or recording gameplay content

there is a slight issue at the time of writing this review where the game tends to enter windowed mode (exiting fullscreen mode in the process) every time the game starts or a setting is changed, nothing too serious though. i will admit, i kind of miss the "over 100%" bug from the original game, but it's pretty obvious why that wasn't included :p (maybe someone will mod the behavior in though, who knows...)
https://steamcommunity.com/sharedfiles/filedetails/?id=3627430434

overall, this is a fantastic revamp of the legendary platypus, and i can't recommend this game enough, regardless whether you've played the original or are new to the series

Platypus Reclayed is a lovingly reconstructed horizontal shoot-’em-up that blends classic arcade sensibilities with a modern indie production ethos, standing as both a tribute to and a reinvention of the original Platypus. Developed and published by Claymatic Games, the title does not merely upscale an old design but rebuilds it from the ground up, preserving the fast, reflex-driven gameplay while expanding its audiovisual identity and mechanical polish. From the opening moments, it is clear that Platypus Reclayed is designed to appeal equally to nostalgia-driven players and newcomers who appreciate tightly focused action games with strong personality.

The most striking element of the experience is its claymation-inspired presentation. Every ship, enemy, projectile, and background element appears handcrafted, giving the game a tactile quality that is rare in the shoot-’em-up genre. The environments feel like miniature sets rather than flat scrolling backdrops, with layered depth and subtle motion that bring each stage to life. This visual approach does more than provide novelty; it reinforces readability during chaotic encounters, making enemy silhouettes and projectiles easy to track even when the screen fills with explosions. The handcrafted aesthetic also lends the game a whimsical tone that contrasts nicely with the intensity of its action.

Gameplay remains firmly rooted in classic horizontal shooter design, emphasizing movement, positioning, and pattern recognition. Players pilot the iconic Platypus fighter across a series of scrolling stages packed with enemies, hazards, and screen-filling boss encounters. Controls are responsive and immediately intuitive, allowing players to focus on dodging incoming fire and choosing when to engage aggressively. The shooting mechanics feel satisfying and deliberate, striking a balance between accessibility and challenge that encourages repeated attempts and gradual mastery.

A defining feature of Platypus Reclayed is its power-up system, which introduces variety without overwhelming the player. Instead of permanent upgrades, weapons are temporary and situational, forcing players to adapt their playstyle on the fly. The arsenal ranges from traditional spread shots to delightfully absurd options that reflect the game’s playful tone. Because these power-ups expire after a short time, players must decide when to grab them and how best to use them, adding a layer of tactical decision-making to the otherwise straightforward action.

Enemy and level design help sustain momentum throughout the campaign. Each stage introduces new threats and environmental twists, preventing the experience from becoming monotonous despite its relatively short length. Boss battles are a particular highlight, featuring multi-phase encounters that test pattern recognition and reflexes rather than raw firepower alone. Difficulty ramps up quickly, especially for players unfamiliar with arcade shooters, but the challenge feels fair and consistent, rewarding learning and persistence rather than punishing experimentation.

Sound design plays a crucial supporting role, with energetic music and punchy sound effects reinforcing the pace of combat. The soundtrack channels an arcade spirit without leaning too heavily on retro clichés, providing rhythmic backing that heightens tension during intense sequences. Audio cues for enemy attacks and power-ups are clear and impactful, contributing to the game’s strong sense of feedback and flow. Together with the visuals, the soundscape helps maintain immersion even during the most frantic moments.

While Platypus Reclayed can be completed in a relatively short time by skilled players, its design encourages replayability rather than longevity through sheer content volume. Adjustable difficulty settings, score chasing, and cooperative play offer reasons to revisit stages and refine performance. The inclusion of local co-op adds a social dimension that complements the arcade roots of the game, turning it into a shared experience that amplifies both challenge and enjoyment.

Ultimately, Platypus Reclayed succeeds because it understands its scope and executes within it with confidence and care. It does not attempt to reinvent the shoot-’em-up genre, but instead focuses on delivering a polished, charming, and mechanically solid experience that respects its origins while benefiting from modern production values. For fans of classic arcade shooters, it offers a nostalgic yet refreshed experience, and for newcomers, it serves as an inviting entry point into a genre defined by precision, intensity, and timeless design.

It's one of those games I can pick up and play a couple levels whenever I want (without a big time dedication), but I can also do a full run all the way through each level in a night. This is a difficult to master game. There are lots of frustrating moments but it makes it even more rewarding when you complete an achievement. I have only completed the game on normal so far but I plan to train up to complete nasty mode for that sweet, sweet achievement. 
That said, the graphics are amazing. The team at Claymatic painstakingly modeled these sprites from real clay and the dedication to that shines through. There's something to be said about these clay games. They give such a fun and unique vibe that is missing from today's gaming landscape. 
I would certainly recommend this game to fans of the original and new fans alike. 
tldr; Easy to play, difficult to master. Excellent remaster with real clay.

Hey there, so happy when my childhood game was announced to have a remake. Unique and optimal graphics, banger soundtracks, careful attention to detail, and peak fanservice as always.

However, the difficulty for the classic mod is too hard compared to the original game. I played Regular mode (easiest diff in Reclayed Classic), yet it feels like Hard mode in the OG. I don't remember the big green eggs constantly shooting full spreads and the orange ones always shooting lasers. Even the mines explode into a full circle of smaller mines, which only happens in OG's Hard mode (the smaller mines only cover half the circle, sometimes quarter in easier modes). Regular mode bosses also fire much more bullets and missiles than OG's Easy and Normal mode.

Much appreciated if the classic mod is rebalanced.

Excellent remake. Beautiful claymation, same perfect soundtrack and additional levels and enemies!
Also includes classic mode, which is basically the good old Platypus I game!
The new gaming of Platypus. However, the problem is, there's no online coop to play—which only the flop. Otherwise, it is great because it has achievements and workshop!

The nostalgia is strong for this one indeed. Platypus Reclayed is everything anyone could ever want in a side-scroller fly'n'shoot game, end of story. Twenty years ago, my family crowded around our computer taking turns at beating the high score. Twenty years later, we're here revelling in the artfully executed remaster of the game, with content additions that spark that same sense of excitement from back in the day.

Been following this game ever since I saw its announcements and it was absolutely worth the wait. This game's a beautiful reimagining of original game with much welcomed improvements and changes and pays homage to its history
i think i miss getting jumpscared in platypus II and crying in tears because of John Slithax Greenhead from platypus II though

A claymation shootemup?! Yes indeed it is! This animation style well known in such 90s and early 00s works like Pingu, Chicken Run, Wallace and Gromit etc. There is no need for health bars when enemies visibly take damage in the form of holes and broken parts. The game uses a unique claymation font that animates between each number smoothly too.

Gameplay is classic 90s shootemup fare, with powerups like spread gun, autocannon, homing missiles, etc, but also some ones I haven't seen anywhere else, like shooting out cheese donuts and fish. 

The game is well programmed and I haven't encountered any bugs.

An absolute banger of a classic shoot em up. And on top of all the visual and gameplay improvements made compared to the original, this one actually runs on modern software. This one is worth your time and money.

I stumbled upon this game since I was a kid. I religiously played it until I was a teenager. So glad that they made an improved version and it's the fastest game purchase I ever made! I eternally love this game!

This is an awesome revitalisation this game needed. ill be real it was soo hard to get games remastered lke this. 
Honestly I have watched my mum played the orignial back in 2002 on windows XP. Now there is a 64bit version, so it can live on.
its peak. they made it better and more beginner friendly. i can finally show my friends this game
Hori STG! Modern remake! Ornithorhynchus anatinus! Destroy your enemies with junk food! Also: FISH! And ridiculous amounts of clay..  (8/10)

Fantastic shmup. I never have much to write about GOOD shmups because they are straightforward games that really either are good or just boring and bad for whatever reason.
But this is a very solid game and the reasons I love it are
1) 4K high FPS support
2) Cool music
3) Multiple ship with different strengths and weaknesses
4) The main reason, crazy weapon power ups which I am a sucker for.

You also get the original game included but working on modern systems without any compatibility work arounds.

I recommend supporting this game if you like 2D shoot em ups, as it's got the goods. Levels aren't too long either and there are multiple difficulty settings to suit your skill level.

A really fun game.

This game definitely has a major nostalgia factor but it also has new contents that makes it stand out from 2002 original as well. With its upgraded visuals and new (sometimes silly) contents, I really love this remake of a good ol' classic shooter game.

My favourite new addition in particular is the weapons, both regulars and sillies.
[list]
    [*]Fireball makes a great melee and piercing damage with its charge-up.
    [*]Lightning (I'm not sure if it returned from Platypus II or it was always there in Platypus 2002) grants the power of Zeus in your ship, especially when paired with weapon pods.
    [*]Homing missiles and Bombs gave me a feeling of multitasking, Bombs lean more into niche usage since it recommends flying on higher altitude, while Homing missiles are Rocket's jack-of-all-trades brethren.
    [*]Tail gun is also another niche weapon meant to counter rear enemies while attacking the front.
    [*]Silly weapons are good giggle factor playing this game since they're just as powerful as regular weapons, some of them even are counterparts of regular weapons too. Just be sure you don't get too hungry when you use them.
[/list]

What I'm interested in the most is mod add-on support, I'm interested to see what the community can cook in their Mungolan kitchens.

I think I'll leave this game 9/10. Solid remake. Giving the classic game a new life while retaining the classic gameplay elements. Overwhelming at times but with enough skill, you can pull it through. A lot of attraction comes from nostalgia and the desire to finish what my past self couldn't. Thank you for the game, Claymatic.

I don't know how to write a proper review, so I am just gonna point out what I really like about this remake.
Spoilers
[spoiler] In the original, I always thought it's weird that there's only 4 levels despite each having 5 area. Guess what, THEY ADDED ONE MORE!.
The story is also got elaborated, though I don't mind the original's presentation. Now they give you more exposition on the story at the end. And I really appreciate the simple yet important message of the story.
My favorite level is the 4th level 3rd area (the area with the Lightning power up). It is SO FUN, SO SATISFYING. And they didn't shy away from adding more sections like that throughout the game, but not too much that it takes away the fun.[/spoiler]

Also, I love background scenery in this game, probably what sparks my interest in those kinda stuff in the first place. That being said, it would be nice if they added more.

Anyways, really great remake, if you like the original you will most likely like this one.

One of the highlights in my childhood games. I was waiting until Winter Sale to bought it (because no money) & have 0 regrets. What I didn't expect is the dev log in the gallery, which makes it even better.

A friend gifted me Platypus Reclayed for Christmas, and I am so happy to finally play my childhood game that got remade. Everything's so good and high quality, this remake or... you could say reclayed is overall fantastic! (I still suck playing Platypus, though lol)

One of the coolest indie scrolling shooters of all time just got even more beautiful. As soon as I heard about this, I was immediately sold. It's immaculate... The soundtrack is absolutely incredible, and the game is very unique among shoot-em-ups in that it is controlled entirely with the mouse. This allows you move at exactly the speed you want at any moment instead of just, say, holding the shift key and alternating between 2 speeds with binary arrow keys. This also gives movement more of a feeling that you are actually piloting a ship and flying free as a bird with a good sense of speed, control, and risk.

The backdrops are so grand and epic in scale, it creates a real sense of adventure and wonder, along with the levels themselves being very long and gradually changing scenery over time. Totally enrapturing and hypnotizingly immersive, and seamless since there are no load screen transitions. Once you're playing, it just keeps going and going and totally captures your attention. There are a ton of different powerups thrown at you along the way, including some silly ones. So this game maintains a strong sense of fun and whimsy and going with the flow, while also offering a fair respectable challenge.

There are no bombs, so it's purely about the shooting and dodging. There are multiple difficulties. Playing on regular, I find that you certainly do have to try and play smart or you could slip up real fast and quick, but not to a rage-inducing degree. There is a high degree of freedom and flexibility, it's fun to always be switching up your strategy with the different powerups that come your way and try to go for the fruit that drop from enemies. And when you shoot the fruit, they break apart into separate bits! But, you can still collect them... And you see the enemies parachute away safely, so you can have a clean conscience from you brutally assailing them with cheese. It feels like hardcore old school gaming, but also not too serious.

There are 3 ships and you can pick a color for any of them, and they all pop so vibrantly. It has a fascinating behind-the-scenes gallery to flip through showing the real world clay work. It has jump-in multiplayer. It just got a free winter DLC on top of Steam Workshop support, so for a humble price there is a ton of quality fun to be had here. It's just a good freaking wonderful time every time you boot it up, a treat for the senses, and you can tell how much passion went into this and that it was made so lovingly. Please make a Platypus 2 Reclayed, or a Platypus 3!
A lovely shoot-em-up that scratched a nostalgia itch I didn't even realize I had.  Claymation models were a wonderful touch.

A story of development poetic justice as Anthony Flack returns from the pits of HECK to deliver...whatever this is! It's good, I swear! It has [b] fish! [/b] And donuts! And boxing gloves!

In all seriousness, this game is great. The sneaky buggers at Claymatic advertise this game as "Platypus with a new coat of paint!" They never tell you about the new enemy types, weaponry, ships, and all sorts of wacky stuff they threw in and hoped you won't notice,

They even have a FIFTH LEVEL. That's right, every person who cried about Level X not being real can finally feel vindicated because now there's another level beyond Colossatropolis for them to sink their teeth into!

Pick up the game. Whether you've played the original PC release, the one on the PSP, or that one weird mobile version, this game is the DEFINITIVE way to play the original.

[i] (And if you want to play the original, Reclayed has that too. Just sayin'.) [/i]

game_name: Just a To the Moon Series Beach Episode
review_text: 11/10. Such a phenomenal ending to the series that I'm shocked it's *only* the penultimate release. I have no complaints, and it may have broken my personal record for the hardest (and loudest) I've openly wept over a game. Nobody can pierce my soul to its core and throw all my deepest feelings on life, longing, and loss back in my face like Kan Gao and his team.

game_name: Just a To the Moon Series Beach Episode
review_text: I knew going in what was basically happening in this game. Most people surely did. But unlike the cathartic release/resolution of the previous titles. All I felt was dread. It seeped through the game continually. By the end I was not too particularly sad, but when I was forced to pull the trigger.. I couldn't. What was at that point only a very mellow sense of dread magnified itself as I moved to the key on screen. I literally COULD NOT do it. And as seconds passed into minutes I found myself feeling a surge of emotion each time I did attempt to end it. It took me nearly 20 minutes to end the game and by then dread had spiraled into near panic.

game_name: Just a To the Moon Series Beach Episode
review_text: It takes a special kind of game to have an obvious twist be so heartwrenching, but Freebird Games still manages to somehow pull it off. For fans of the To the Moon series, Just a To the Moon Series: Beach Episode is a vital story.

game_name: Just a To the Moon Series Beach Episode
review_text:
A beautiful game filled with a gentle denial and a strong emotional relief, and a lot of metaphors to find throughout the game. The beach itself has a profound meaning, but this episode has managed to densify a meaningful story in a short amount of time. 

You experience a day with a group of friends and share emotional intimacy and more importantly, [i]memories[/i]. 

Following the same principle as the first iteration of the game, you will encounter some fun minigames and a sense of hedonistic touch. It is nicely done and not excessive by any means. 

[spoiler]The game acts implicitly as a mental pause in comparison to To The Moon. The beach symbolizes the frontier between the past and the future, between reminiscences and the present, and between what hurts and what we accept. And more importantly: it is supposed to be a "filler". That's a direct metaphor to life itself: everything doesn't need a strong [i]narrative[/i] to have value. There's also an implicit touch of a specific philosophy found multiple times in the game, which is the amor fati.[/spoiler] 

If you’re open to a quiet, emotional pause, this episode shines.

game_name: Just a To the Moon Series Beach Episode
review_text: I don't know if I should be surprised or not but what starts out as a lighthearted silly Freebird fanservice game, takes a heavy turn into exactly what you should expect by now. An excellent final epilogue chapter to the story of the games so far but you definitly need to have played at least a few of their previous games.

game_name: Just a To the Moon Series Beach Episode
review_text:
没有任何一款游戏的结尾能在故事、设计、情感、和代入感上带给我如此震撼的感觉。哪怕这只是一个很小的平面像素剧情游戏，其各种独到的设计没有任何别的游戏可以比拟。更不用说这一系列一直优秀的配乐、音效等等其他元素。

推荐给所有人。

[spoiler] 结束游戏后，当我重新进入游戏并选择最后一个自动存档点来确认结尾的退出是不是制作组有意为之时，我能注意到最后的自动检查点之后的最后一段剧情随着我的重新进入而改变，恰恰就对应了剧情中Eva对于这段记忆的反复播放、回忆。能设计出这样的剧情、互动的制作组值得一切。这是真正的代入感，真正的艺术。[/spoiler]

也许我也会反复播放这段回忆吧

game_name: Just a To the Moon Series Beach Episode
review_text: I’m honestly not sure what I expected going into this game. I promise I am ALL TOO FAMILIAR with the previous games in the series and how heart shattering they are, but for some reason I thought this one would go easy on me. Now I’m sitting here weeping loudly, violently, and devastatingly at what has to be the most gut wrenching, heart breaking ending yet in this series. Throughout this game, you notice something is a little… off, but off is an extreme understatement of the reality. Nothing could have prepared me for the laughs and cries I experienced while playing this game. Honestly, a phenomenal game. Hands down 11/10.

game_name: Just a To the Moon Series Beach Episode
review_text:
Wow… what an ending.

At first, I was genuinely confused about why characters from the previous games were suddenly here, and why it didn’t directly continue from Imposter Syndrome. But once the credits rolled, everything clicked—and I cried like a baby all over again.

The emotional payoff was incredibly bittersweet, and the leap in writing and storytelling compared to Imposter Syndrome is huge. Even if I now better understand what that game was setting up, this entry felt truly special in its own right. My heart absolutely breaks for Eva.

Knowing I’m finally fully caught up with the franchise makes me even more excited for what comes next. I can’t wait to see where it all leads, and this series will always hold a special place in my heart.

game_name: Just a To the Moon Series Beach Episode
review_text: "Just a To the Moon Series Beach Episode" was a nice, short story about grief. I've played all prior games up to this point which I heavily recommend doing before playing this one. It's a very unique atmosphere and the short length of the game, for me at least, was actually a good thing - I often get stuck on the 100+ hours games while these ones are easier to just launch and complete. There's one thing I didn't like though which was the mini games. Freebird Games uses RPG Maker and are probably pushing the limits of the engine at times but the mini games were just a little too janky for me. Otherwise, great execution and great storytelling!

game_name: Just a To the Moon Series Beach Episode
review_text: Such a beautiful ending to wrap up a long journey. I'm glad to have been one of kickstarter supporters for this game. Thanks for all the experience, from my first playthrough almost 9 years ago.  💞💞

game_name: Just a To the Moon Series Beach Episode
review_text: 11/10. Such a phenomenal ending to the series that I'm shocked it's *only* the penultimate release. I have no complaints, and it may have broken my personal record for the hardest (and loudest) I've openly wept over a game. Nobody can pierce my soul to its core and throw all my deepest feelings on life, longing, and loss back in my face like Kan Gao and his team.

game_name: Just a To the Moon Series Beach Episode
review_text: I knew going in what was basically happening in this game. Most people surely did. But unlike the cathartic release/resolution of the previous titles. All I felt was dread. It seeped through the game continually. By the end I was not too particularly sad, but when I was forced to pull the trigger.. I couldn't. What was at that point only a very mellow sense of dread magnified itself as I moved to the key on screen. I literally COULD NOT do it. And as seconds passed into minutes I found myself feeling a surge of emotion each time I did attempt to end it. It took me nearly 20 minutes to end the game and by then dread had spiraled into near panic.

game_name: Just a To the Moon Series Beach Episode
review_text: It takes a special kind of game to have an obvious twist be so heartwrenching, but Freebird Games still manages to somehow pull it off. For fans of the To the Moon series, Just a To the Moon Series: Beach Episode is a vital story.

game_name: Just a To the Moon Series Beach Episode
review_text:
A beautiful game filled with a gentle denial and a strong emotional relief, and a lot of metaphors to find throughout the game. The beach itself has a profound meaning, but this episode has managed to densify a meaningful story in a short amount of time. 

You experience a day with a group of friends and share emotional intimacy and more importantly, [i]memories[/i]. 

Following the same principle as the first iteration of the game, you will encounter some fun minigames and a sense of hedonistic touch. It is nicely done and not excessive by any means. 

[spoiler]The game acts implicitly as a mental pause in comparison to To The Moon. The beach symbolizes the frontier between the past and the future, between reminiscences and the present, and between what hurts and what we accept. And more importantly: it is supposed to be a "filler". That's a direct metaphor to life itself: everything doesn't need a strong [i]narrative[/i] to have value. There's also an implicit touch of a specific philosophy found multiple times in the game, which is the amor fati.[/spoiler] 

If you’re open to a quiet, emotional pause, this episode shines.

game_name: Just a To the Moon Series Beach Episode
review_text: I don't know if I should be surprised or not but what starts out as a lighthearted silly Freebird fanservice game, takes a heavy turn into exactly what you should expect by now. An excellent final epilogue chapter to the story of the games so far but you definitly need to have played at least a few of their previous games.

game_name: Just a To the Moon Series Beach Episode
review_text:
没有任何一款游戏的结尾能在故事、设计、情感、和代入感上带给我如此震撼的感觉。哪怕这只是一个很小的平面像素剧情游戏，其各种独到的设计没有任何别的游戏可以比拟。更不用说这一系列一直优秀的配乐、音效等等其他元素。

推荐给所有人。

[spoiler] 结束游戏后，当我重新进入游戏并选择最后一个自动存档点来确认结尾的退出是不是制作组有意为之时，我能注意到最后的自动检查点之后的最后一段剧情随着我的重新进入而改变，恰恰就对应了剧情中Eva对于这段记忆的反复播放、回忆。能设计出这样的剧情、互动的制作组值得一切。这是真正的代入感，真正的艺术。[/spoiler]

也许我也会反复播放这段回忆吧

game_name: Just a To the Moon Series Beach Episode
review_text: I’m honestly not sure what I expected going into this game. I promise I am ALL TOO FAMILIAR with the previous games in the series and how heart shattering they are, but for some reason I thought this one would go easy on me. Now I’m sitting here weeping loudly, violently, and devastatingly at what has to be the most gut wrenching, heart breaking ending yet in this series. Throughout this game, you notice something is a little… off, but off is an extreme understatement of the reality. Nothing could have prepared me for the laughs and cries I experienced while playing this game. Honestly, a phenomenal game. Hands down 11/10.

game_name: Just a To the Moon Series Beach Episode
review_text:
Wow… what an ending.

At first, I was genuinely confused about why characters from the previous games were suddenly here, and why it didn’t directly continue from Imposter Syndrome. But once the credits rolled, everything clicked—and I cried like a baby all over again.

The emotional payoff was incredibly bittersweet, and the leap in writing and storytelling compared to Imposter Syndrome is huge. Even if I now better understand what that game was setting up, this entry felt truly special in its own right. My heart absolutely breaks for Eva.

Knowing I’m finally fully caught up with the franchise makes me even more excited for what comes next. I can’t wait to see where it all leads, and this series will always hold a special place in my heart.

game_name: Just a To the Moon Series Beach Episode
review_text: An incredibly impactful masterpiece. For fans of the previous games this is gonna hit hard.

game_name: Just a To the Moon Series Beach Episode
review_text: "Just a To the Moon Series Beach Episode" was a nice, short story about grief. I've played all prior games up to this point which I heavily recommend doing before playing this one. It's a very unique atmosphere and the short length of the game, for me at least, was actually a good thing - I often get stuck on the 100+ hours games while these ones are easier to just launch and complete. There's one thing I didn't like though which was the mini games. Freebird Games uses RPG Maker and are probably pushing the limits of the engine at times but the mini games were just a little too janky for me. Otherwise, great execution and great storytelling!

game_name: Just a To the Moon Series Beach Episode
review_text: I didn’t watch the trailer, so I expected just a simple episode. Turns out it was a complete prank on me, and now here I am, crying my eyes out over that bittersweet finale. Kan Gao and his team are absolute geniuses.

Finally, I can write my thoughts about this game after unlocking all the achievements. Papa’s games have always been fun to play. Personally, I’ve been playing Flipline Studio games since the old Flash game era, and they’ve always been a joy.

Although I have a lot of criticisms and suggestions I’d like to share, I’m honestly too lazy to type them all out (and I’m not even sure anyone would read them anyway, lmao). So yeah… I’ll just wait for the next Deluxe series. Hopefully, there will be more unique and interesting features if Flipline decides to make another Papa’s game. Thank you for filling my childhood days with so many fun memories.

For those of you reading this who are still unsure whether to play or buy the game… just go for it, it’s really fun.
As a small tip for fellow budget-conscious players, it’s better to buy it during a sale (sorry, Flipline lol, maybe someday I’ll buy it at full price).

That’s all.
Adieu.

Papa’s Pizzeria began life as a humble spin-off of Flipline Studios’ mainline platformer series Papa Louie, but it quickly eclipsed the popularity of its predecessor and became a ubiquitous cultural touchstone for the Adobe Flash generation. Pizzeria laid the groundwork for over a dozen restaurant-simulation sequels with its catalogue of unique characters and its emphasis on hands-on gameplay. Even though it was revolutionary within the genre, the original 2007 version had many one-off quirks that would get smoothed out by later entries, so it’s the recent Deluxe re-release on Steam that I’ll be reviewing here.

The pizzeria’s setting of Tastyville doesn’t have as distinct of a visual aesthetic as some other Flipverse locales, but the lack of a cohesive theme is a surprising upside of this title. It precludes any ingredient from feeling too out-of-place, no matter how exotic, and facilitates one of the most diverse Special Recipe Cookbooks in the series. Moment-to-moment gameplay is also relatively strong, allowing for a fair bit of player interpretation when determining how best to place toppings.

With the praise out of the way, now is as good a time as any to discuss a problem that has been plaguing almost all of the latest Gamerias: their difficulty curve doesn’t just flatline throughout a run, it actively plummets.

Papa’s Pizzeria Deluxe is at its tightest in the first few weeks, when the player must paranoically switch between all four stations, leaving no pie overcooked and no customer underattended. After only a few paydays, though, Delivery Boy Roy can afford powerful upgrades that completely shatter the time management aspect of the game. No longer must the player check the lobby or the oven while busy with another task. There are alarms that do it automatically. Even the Cut Station, which is one of the most enjoyable parts of the base experience, is dumbed down significantly by guides that make it easier to cut consistent slices.

These brain-numbing upgrades wouldn’t be nearly as egregious if they required some sort of tradeoff or if gameplay complexity increased in a substantial way with the changing seasons, but instead they’re just further exacerbated by the perpetual absence of basic difficulty options.

At the end of the day, Papa’s Pizzeria is a casual game. Its timeless appeal is drawn from many different factors, most of which have nothing to do with design principles. That being said, I still hope that someday in the future Flipline Studios will make another attempt at recreating the frantic, fast-paced food service environment that the series initially tried to capture.

At best, Pizzeria and Freezeria Deluxe is a great remaster duology for it's relatively cheap price. It has hella customization, all the additional content from the most recent Papa's restaurant games, and even a freeplay mode. So there's a lot more incentive for a relatively classic time management browser game.

I must re-iterate though; these are good games for their price. At worst, there are details missing in a bunch of places. For a few examples: There's no funny descriptions in the shop items, there's no freshness mechanic, there's no third row for furniture, there's no floor mats in the lobby for lining up... And I kinda wished the mechanics were just a little more polished and consistent. But much more importantly, not even the playable chef's misery survived all that well!

The story would always drive your character all "how do I get into these situations?!" and "Wow, I hate this job already!" but they downplay this drama in the intro to day one, making them excited and always happy to see customers. I don't know why the original browser Pizzeria, the SERIES DEBUT as far as I know, is the only game in the whole franchise to do this right; where there was practically no escape from Roy's anxious face language. It doesn't need to be quite on that level but still -- The story is there for a reason.

I'm also wondering why in the helium Pizzeria Deluxe is using the title screen music from Pasteria and not from the game they're trying to remake.

Overall, this duology is a tad bit overrated in the sense that it looks and sounds peak, but far from it. Time management is a nice genre for when you don't know what to do. (Watch your screentime tho.) I'm fairly excited for Flipline to slowly but surely remaster the entire series on steam. I would love to see how the other games and their unique mechanics will work in this modern formula with added content. Plus that way I-- I mean... YOU can play them on your Steam Deck. Given the details, I'm not sure I like the direction of this deluxe series. If it's just six dollars per game, I'll be all for it. They are just nitpicks, but they radiate enough uncertainty to feel at least a little underwhelmed by the final product, even if Flipline appears to be an independent and empathetic studio.

As a fan of the Papa Louie games for most of my life, the original Papa's Pizzeria would always be my last choice from all the games available. There are much more unlockable ingredients, and all the features introduced in later Papa Louie games are introduced here! Path of Achra is an esoteric lore-based game. You have your spells from a god that you pick, you have the option to move, you have the option to attack. It is from the items and skills that modify these to which you obtain a shocking amount of depth.

It's a roguelike. You'll die for reasons you don't know or couldn't have prevented, you'll have builds you want to work but never really quite do on the higher 'ascension levels,' but you can learn enemies.

Which ones to avoid on a given build. Ones with high accuracy that one-shot you if you're low health high dodge. I had a series of what I would genuinely say is 20 runs that didn't make it past the third level back to back to back to back.

For $5, what I got it on sale at, it is very much worth it. For 10 I'd still give it a hearty recommend. I don't think I'll ever come back to this game - I got to a point where the skill I had plateued versus the difficulty of the fights overall, but for 80 hours of an experience I very much enjoyed, especially from a developer as small as this one, it'd be cruel for me not to sing its praises.

[h1]English[/h1]
[i]Path of Achra[/i] celebrates the heroic journey of life - the individual's voyage into the mystical twilight, where chaos and mastery, blood and magic converge.

The game is a roguelike autobattler. The focus is on the respective build, on complex synergies that weave together into something greater. You choose culture, class and religion, the three fundamental pillars of the hero. You learn skills from varying elements that unleash deadly energies, generate healing or spawn loyal companions. You find equipment to carry or ignore. Death ends the journey and forces a new beginning.

Our hero moves through temples and catacombs, across wild lowlands and starless plains, striving for the obelisk of the distant sphere, then for the astrolith in the cosmic over-realm. Hideous creatures of the shadow veil, sorcerers and zealots, merciless beings in a bestial frenzy guard the withered rooms. The journey is framed by dark, archaic poems, litanies and sayings that carry the reverent, mystical atmosphere. The steps are accompanied by music that is dreamlike and solemn.

As joyless as the path may seem in the face of countless adversaries, it is not a journey of heavy heart. We walk it in a strangely serene, almost triumphant calm. For even doom, the fall into the shadows, is not a pure failure, but a chapter in the great myth. Even as he perishes, the wanderer knows: I will return - in another form. Cycle and repeat.

Even if the beasts destroy you, even if you are shattered in the obelisk or torn apart in the astrolith, the fire never dies. "O pilgrim, walk the path" is the promise of [i]Path of Achra[/i]. The heart responds inevitably - and steps in.

[h1]Deutsch[/h1]
[i]Path of Achra[/i] zelebriert die heldische Lebensreise - den Gang des Einzelnen hinein in den  mystischen Dämmerschein, wo Chaos und Erkenntnis, Blut und Magie aufeinandertreffen. 

Das Programm ist ein Roguelike-Autobattler. Der Fokus liegt auf dem jeweiligen Build, auf komplexen Synergien, die sich zu etwas Größerem verweben. Du wählst Kultur, Klasse und Religion, den dreifachen Grundbau des Helden. Du lernst Fähigkeiten aus vielfältigen Elementen, die tödliche Energien entsenden, Heilung hervorrufen oder treue Gefährten gebären. Du findest Ausrüstung, um sie anzulegen oder nicht. Der Tod beendet die Reise und zwingt zum Neubeginn.

Unser Held bewegt sich durch Tempel und Katakomben, über wilde Niederungen und sternlose Ebenen, strebt zum Obelisken der fernen Sphäre, dann zum Astrolithen im kosmischen Überraum. Gräßliche Kreaturen des Schattenreichs, Zauberer und Eiferer, grausame Geschöpfe in bestialischem Rausch bewachen die verdorrten Räume. Umrahmt ist die Reise von düster-archaischen Gedichten, Litaneien und Sprüchen, welche die ehrfürchtig-mystische Atmosphäre tragen. Die Schritte werden von Klängen begleitet, die traumselig wie weihevoll sind.

So freudlos der Pfad erscheinen mag im Angesicht zahllos gefächerter Widersacher, so wenig ist er eine Reise des schweren Gemüts. Wir schreiten ihn in einer seltsam besonnenen, fast triumphalen Ruhe. Denn selbst das Niedergehen, der Fall in die Schatten, ist kein reiner Fehlschlag, sondern ein Kapitel im großen Mythos. Auch wenn er zugrunde geht, weiß der Wandernde: Ich kehre zurück - in anderer Art. Zyklus und Wiederkunft.

Selbst wenn die Bestien dich zerstören, selbst wenn du im Obelisken zerschmettert oder im Astrolithen zerrissen wirst, stirbt das Feuer nie. "O pilgrim, walk the path", lautet die Verheißung von [i]Path of Achra[/i]. Das Herz antwortet unweigerlich - und schreitet hinein.

Arguably the single greatest single player character builder. The game is entirely centered around player creativity and provides plenty of challenge to spur you to greater and greater heights as you uncover more and more synergies. 

Unlike most other roguelikes you will reliably encounter all build options each run, the challenges and order of loot are what gets randomized. The game can be incredibly challenging while you are learning it, and there is an additional post-game dungeon where you can test your successful builds against an endless mode with exponentially scaling difficulty to see how far you can go.

I am saddened that development seems to have stopped, but the existing game is complete. I look forward to the developers future games.

Deep customization and many reasons to try another combo. RNG is limited to what items show in a run, but you'll likely run into the majority of the ones you want for your build. You will fail a lot. It takes a long time to even learn from your failures. That's when the game starts to really take off.
This is very much a thinking game; if you do not figure out the synergies you are going to get curb stomped. Yet it feels amazing to successfully figure out which builds are completely busted.

A very fun rougelike centered around finding synergies between a wide range of races, classes, gods and items. Most of the difficulty comes in planning out the build, the combat can be played by just holding tab, unless you're playing a character that has to stay at range. Deep and addictive.

This is exactly what it sets out to be - a "broken build sandbox". And very good at that. There is an huge amount of build possibilities and the esoteric/mythical barbarian world is a wonderful, immersive background.

A "broken-build-simulator" done right.

Platonic ending options? In my somewhat niche favorite crass-humored meme-spewing competitive monster dating sim party game that started out as a spiritual successor to The Yawhg...'s fourth entry?

Monster Prom 4: Monster Con is a return to the classic Monster Prom formula and likely the mechanically strongest game in the series at the current moment, carrying over the QoL changes that Monster Roadtrip made, as well as their stat exchange system in which there are no wrong decisions, simply ones that favor a different stat. A largely new main cast of characters with two returning favorites and complemented by the guest system resembling the hitchhikers from Roadtrip. Additionally, the game serves as homage to so much geek culture with beautiful art and references everywhere you go.

What little I can criticize about the game is its depth, not in terms of content, but rather in terms of character. In story, it makes sense, the game takes place over a few *days*, so I wouldn’t expect characters to open up to the degree that they did back in Prom, Camp or, notably, Roadtrip. You see growth, but you don’t get as much of it to dig into. Alongside that, the secret endings feel simpler and more impersonal. “Zoe’s Big Adventure” does not feel like a Zoe event.

In terms of fun with friends, though? This game is a blast, each completed run leaving me wanting for more. I can say the same for the gacha event in the middle of a run, wishing there were two per run like Juan’s drinks in Camp, but that’s more of a cherry-pick on my end.

I highly recommend this game if you enjoy playing it with friends and if you are a fan of anime, board games, comics, and the like. However, if you buy the game expecting something as engaging as what we got from Roadtrip: End of the Road, I’d just warn that this game is much more lighthearted (though salacious) and goofy.

Very fun game with an interesting story and appealing art to boot. The story is nice and simple, with twists that caught me off-guard, but were appropriately foreshadowed so it doesn't feel cheap or too forced. As a lover of puns, the names were a delight to decipher the dual meanings XD. The gameplay is similar to poptropica where you move around the map looking for clues.

The devs have added replayability additions like speeding through cutscenes (is also available from the start through settings) which help when grinding for the missed achievements. There's also other replay stuff that basically allows you to cheat, but it's hidden behind a series of steps ([spoiler] which results in an achievement: [/spoiler] [spoiler] "His Last Bow" [/spoiler]) ... and I unlocked it after finishing everything else so it wasn't too much use to me...
There are also post-completion dev notes on each character and dev art that is a charming way to connect with the creators and understand the creation of the world better which I particularly enjoyed.

Overall a fun game that for me has tons of nostalgia. Just note: expect [b] a LOT of reading [/b] and there is no saving during dialogue, so make sure you have at least 45min-1hr once you feel like you're nearing the final showdown because you get hit with a ton of text to wrap the story up, not that it's a bad thing (there's no wall of text because it's all broken up into seperate dialogues).

Absolutely stellar writing (some spelling mistakes but considering the game is translated they are few and far between)! Great characters and art! Love the game.
The game is interesting as it allows you choices to do things the character (based on their personalities) would not do, but you get less rewards at the end. So the game incentivizes you to play as the character. And since there are 3 characters you are balancing 3 different personalities. And this seems like an inventive way to avoid all characters you play feeling samey.
Great game! Highly recommend!

This game starts out just fine, but it gets progressively better in all departments. The writing is excellently done, and the investigation-like gameplay compliments it really well. I can't remember when I last encountered a twist that left me so positively bewildered before playing this game. This is truly a diamond in the rough and I can heartily recommend the game to anyone reading this.

Very fun game with an interesting story and appealing art to boot. The story is nice and simple, with twists that caught me off-guard, but were appropriately foreshadowed so it doesn't feel cheap or too forced. The devs have added replayability additions like speeding through cutscenes (is also available from the start through settings) which help when grinding for the missed achievements. There's also other replay stuff that basically allows you to cheat, but it's hidden behind a series of steps ([spoiler] which results in an achievement: [/spoiler] [spoiler] "His Last Bow" [/spoiler]) ... and I unlocked it after finishing everything else so it wasn't too much use to me...
There are also post-completion dev notes on each character and dev art that is a charming way to connect with the creators and understand the creation of the world better which I particularly enjoyed.

Overall a fun game that for me has tons of nostalgia. Just note: expect [b] a LOT of reading [/b] and there is no saving during dialogue, so make sure you have at least 45min-1hr once you feel like you're nearing the final showdown because you get hit with a ton of text to wrap the story up, not that it's a bad thing (there's no wall of text because it's all broken up into seperate dialogues).

Great game! Highly recommend!

The Buttered Cat paradox is a rather famous one. Studies have shown that cats always land on their feet. Whenever you drop a buttered piece of toast, the buttered side is always the one hitting the countertop. However, what happens if you put a buttered piece of toast on a cat? Which hits the floor? 
I immediately knew I wanted to play this game when I saw it for the first time. I’m a sucker for puzzle-platformers, and I also love games with goofy concepts. I knew this game was for me.

Story
Our two pals, the buttered toast and the cat, want to fix a machine that gives the cat milk and the toast butter. They set out on an epic adventure to find parts to fix it.
Were you really expecting a super-complex story with this game? This story fits it perfectly. Somehow, the buttered piece of toast is the one doing the talking, not the cat. 

Gameplay
As mentioned before, Cato: Buttered Cat is a 2D puzzle-platformer. You play as both the cat and the buttered piece of toast. The controls are very simple; on his (or her) own, the cat can only move around. He can climb on some surfaces, but that’s about it. The buttered piece of toast on its own can only jump and change its direction. Paired together, the cat and toast can jump infinitely and move around. When separate, you control both characters simultaneously. No swapping needed. This leads to lots of puzzle potential. Lots of the time, the two are separate and you need to unite them, but the other way around is also true. You can disband them whenever you want. The play control is very good. Both of them are quite fast and you’ll get the hang of it quite easily. For some reason, the game says that it’s better with a controller, but I don’t really see a problem with the keyboard controls. A lot of platformers say this, but it’s good with a keyboard or controller.
The game gets interesting when it adds more mechanics. There are a host of things to interact with, such as power switches, spaces that will only exist when the two are apart (or together), bouncy spaces, and a bunch more. These give the levels tons of depth. 
Most of the levels are a similar length. They’ll usually take anywhere from 3-10 minutes to beat, so this game is very good on the go. Some of the levels are divided into a couple of screens, each being their own puzzle. There’s a reset button if you make a mistake which you can’t come back from, and it just resets the screen. If you get stuck in a level, there is a hint button, but I found these pretty unhelpful. You can also just skip the level if it's giving you too much trouble.The levels are organized via worlds, and at the end of some worlds, there’ll be an auto-running section where you need to collect these stars. For the other ones, there are bossfights, which I’m a little iffy on. I’ve never been a fan of boss fights in a puzzle game, but I suppose they’re fine here. 
I love this gameplay so much. This has to be one of the most fun and engaging puzzle-platformers I’ve played. It has a pick-up-and-play style, so if you have a Steam Deck this would be perfect. Even if you don’t, it’s still tons of fun.


Presentation
The game very clearly goes for a cute aesthetic. It’s all very colorful, and there are tons of smaller touches. If you hold R, the level resets, but if you just tap it, Cato lets out a meow. If you spin around in the air too much then he’ll get dizzy. I love details like these, it makes the game feel very cozy. 
As for how the game actually looks, most of the time it has a pixelated but VERY smooth aesthetic. It kinda reminds me of Game Boy Advance games with how vibrant it is and how smooth the animations are. Then, sometimes it switches to 3D models but with the pixelated look, and that looks nice as well. The environment variety is pretty solid, with all of them having similar elements but different colors. 
If I were to describe the music, it would be plucky. There aren’t tons of tracks in the game, but what there is is pretty great. The sound effects are very good, and the voice acting of Cato is very good.

Content
According to Howlongtobeat.com, the game is about 6 hours long. There’s 5 worlds, and over 200 levels, at least according to the description. As mentioned before, there are the skating segments, which you can perfect, but there are also other minigames. This is a cool touch, these minigames were completely unnecessary but it really was just the devs going above and beyond. There are unlockable skins for both the cat and the toast, too. There are over 75 of them, according to the description. You get these from finding secrets in the levels, and perfecting minigames like the sliding segment. You can tell which levels have secrets in them because they have a plug image at the bottom. 
The campaign isn’t super long, but there’s lots of side content in Cato. This really is an example of an indie dev doing more than what’s necessary.

Positives:
-Great play control
-Cozy and warm presentation
-Tons of extra surprises

Negatives:
-Hints are sorta unhelpful
-Bosses

Cato: Buttered Cat was a wonderfully pleasant surprise. It’s the type of game you play cozied up in a cabin in the Rocky Mountains. I would never guess this is this studio’s first game from how good it is. There’s so much heart put into this game, and I hope they keep it up with future endeavors.
As for pricing, do I even gotta say it? It’s a bargain at full price. If this looks like the game for you, that’s because it probably is. This is the type of game where if you see the trailers and screenshots and it looks like fun, then you’ll most likely enjoy it.

I plan on 100%ing this game, and while I've played a handful of puzzle-minded games recently, I think this is  a very solid puzzle platformer mix game.

The puzzles feel very cool and nuanced, from momentum to additional mechanics and toying around with more momentum and rewarding well-thought routing and clever level tuition. Schrodingleberg or whatever his name is must feel stupid for not having thought of this paradox. Made for a deceptively cute game.

Keyword is deceptively, because holy kazooks, this is easily far more difficult than you'd anticipate. Not all the puzzles mind you, more on the optional stuff being pretty hard, but it's all possible without guides. Feels rewarding when you pull off a level and complete it, no matter how many tries and retries it takes. Except the bosses, I admit they're not all that, feels like a difficulty spike and not the fun kind. Least they don't last too long.

If you like puzzle games with platforming elements, I'd moderately recommend this. It's not perfect, it has some flaws, but it's fun and challenging.

CATO: Buttered Cat runs with the classic cat + buttered toast paradox and fully commits to the bit. Cat always lands on its feet, toast always lands butter-side down… so obviously, now it can fly. That’s the logic, and it sets the game’s tone. You’re a cat on a quest for more milk to make more butter, with your trusty toast buddy along for the ride.

The game’s split into five worlds, each adding new rules on top of what you already learned. What starts super simple slowly turns into really clever puzzles that make you stop and rethink the whole room. Since the cat and the toast can move separately, things get surprisingly layered without ever feeling overwhelming and can be broken into logical steps.

Sometimes it leans more into platforming than straight puzzle-solving, especially in the hidden levels where timing can get pretty tight. It can be tough, but it never feels unfair.

The best part is how the game teaches you. There’s basically no tutorial, but the level design is so clean that you naturally figure things out. If something doesn’t click right away, a little trial and error usually leads to a nice “ohhh gotcha!”.

I had a great time with it. It’s cute, smart, and just challenging enough to stay interesting. There’s also a skin system that doesn’t change the gameplay at all, but playing as a gray cat with a croissant on its back was top-tier. Easy recommendation if you like puzzle games.

A very unique puzzle game. Somehow these puzzles feels different than other games. I somehow need to think different in this one, hard to explain. I also love how the jumping mattresses behave in the game, very cool idea. I really enjoy playing this one.
A CAT and a TOAST together have the power to rival God, and indeed, the mighty eagle.

Clever and fresh puzzle game that forces you to think with two seperate character's unique movement sets. Thankfully not too difficult with a gentle curve, very pleasing visuals and soundeffects as well. My only complaint is that there is not enough variety in the music. The music that exists is nice, but that doesn't mean I want to hear it in an endless loop for an hour.

This is the game I didn’t know I needed. Tight, responsive controls mixed with mechanics that have a fair amount of depth. Some puzzles have multiple solutions and the game never felt overly difficult even when the solution required more precise movement or timing. Plus, if you do get stuck, you could always try to hunt for secret rooms/costumes or play the minigames. (My favorite is COLL, reminiscent of Terry Cavanagh’s Super Hexagon.)

At the time of writing I have 100% the game with all secrets and unlock-ables so there is a lot of value for your money, especially when it’s on discount. CATO also works well on Deck so it was a great way to unwind before bed or when I had some downtime. My only wish at this point is more levels in the form of a secret world, or Workshop support so the community can really break the mechanics open and add even more playability to this hidden gem.

Lastly, more on a personal note, the titular CATO reminds me of my soul cat that sadly passed earlier this year and it’s been really hard without him. When I unlocked the 1st anniversary skin I cried a little because it reminded me of how my little guy chonked up so fast too. I’d like to think he’s enjoying all the butter and milk he can handle until we meet again.

I really enjoy this game. Cute puzzle game that keeps introducing mechanics to keep it fresh for you. The controls flow nicely and my actual cat was enthralled by little pixel kitty running around on my monitor.
The difficulty spike later on is very steep and I struggled, sometimes not even the hints helped. For idiots like me, I'd welcome the possibility of a second hint, maybe animated one, so I don't get stuck.

CATO: Buttered Cat runs with the classic cat + buttered toast paradox and fully commits to the bit. Cat always lands on its feet, toast always lands butter-side down… so obviously, now it can fly. That’s the logic, and it sets the game’s tone. You’re a cat on a quest for more milk to make more butter, with your trusty toast buddy along for the ride.

The game’s split into five worlds, each adding new rules on top of what you already learned. What starts super simple slowly turns into really clever puzzles that make you stop and rethink the whole room. Since the cat and the toast can move separately, things get surprisingly layered without ever feeling overwhelming and can be broken into logical steps.

Sometimes it leans more into platforming than straight puzzle-solving, especially in the hidden levels where timing can get pretty tight. It can be tough, but it never feels unfair.

The best part is how the game teaches you. There’s basically no tutorial, but the level design is so clean that you naturally figure things out. If something doesn’t click right away, a little trial and error usually leads to a nice “ohhh gotcha!”.

I had a great time with it. It’s cute, smart, and just challenging enough to stay interesting. There’s also a skin system that doesn’t change the gameplay at all, but playing as a gray cat with a croissant on its back was top-tier. Easy recommendation if you like puzzle games.

[h1]An Unparalleled Surprise, One of My Favorite Puzzle Games I’ve Ever Played![/h1]
-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
[h1]Introduction[/h1]
CATO: Buttered Cat is the debut title of Chinese independent develepers “Team Woll”, which got published by CGORES PUBLISHING in September 2024. But to simply call CATO a “debut title” so matter-of-factly tragically circumvents one simple fact: this is not just any ole indie team’s debut title, this is one of the best puzzle games I have ever played. It took me an embarrassingly long time to actually beat CATO, as I started earlier this year and only recently came back to play it through to the end, which is where it really sunk its cat claws into me!

[h1]Premise[/h1]
You know the rules, right? Cats always land on their four paws. You also may know of the rule that toast always tends to (quite upsettingly, I might add) always fall with the buttered side to the floor, if you happen to drop it. Well, what if you strapped a toast, with the buttered side exposed, to an adorable tabby cat? The cat and toast would, of course, be stuck in a paradox, and would be thus able to fly indefinitely in the air. Is this science-approved? Probably not, but it is me-approved!

[h1]Review[/h1]
This rule is the basic rule of movement throughout the game, but the real fun begins when the game starts introducing switches that only activate when the cat stands on them, or when the toast lands on them. The end goal of every platforming puzzle room in CATO is simple: find and or activate the exit out of the room/out of the level – most levels are single room affairs, but not all – and reunite your cat and buttered toast to be able to actually climb into the portal/exit the room. The end state, invariably, is always that cat and toast have to leave a room and level together. This, of course, gets tricky with the early introduction of the disassemble mechanic and the aforementioned cat or toast-specific mechanics. The game takes its base concept and runs the gamut with it. The puzzles range from thinking-based switch sequences to momentum-based catapulting of the toast with the help of moving floors and walls. There are also a plethora of secret levels that use a third character “Swappy” who does what his name suggests and can swap the position of your cat and toast in the blink of an eye, which is also used in puzzles to, for instance, swap the cat and toast mid-air for the toast to use the momentum gained from one side of the level, to propel itself past resistant pink jelly, which can only be pierced with the prerequisite speed, or launch into a toaster-based wall switch. Every secret and well (but not frustratingly) hidden challenge provides you with character skins for your cat and/or toast.

I especially love that last part, as I really miss, even with the modern era of indie games, which borrow from and modernize old, charming but archaic graphical styles, mechanics and design conventions, the feeling of accomplishment that you’d get from many old games, where you were rewarded for challenges by changes to your character’s look or entirely new and different ways of play and engagement with the content. There is no puzzle or challenge in CATO that is not rewarded. The mainline challenges are mostly rewarded with being able to experience ever more exciting mechanics, and a feeling of self-actualization through intelligent thought, as is typical for puzzle games of this nature, but the occasional hidden wall will also reward you with either these aforementioned skins, straight away or a whole chain of secret brain teaser mini worlds (about 2-5 levels each) which end with your prize instead. This disparity between big and small secrets also helps to make every secret exciting, as you’re never sure what’s right around the corner. Whether a secret is in a level or not is smartly telegraphed by a little polaroid-esque icon under the level number on the level select screen, that fills when a secret has been acquired. This is just good quality of life design.

The only things I can criticize about CATO is that some of the dialogue seems to feel just slightly stilted in the English translation (though one can always, rather clearly parse what it is going for), the fact that the story is hardly there and very simple and low stakes (though it’s hardly the focus), and that some levels can end in softlocking yourself and having to restart the level entirely. This is no problem at all in the single room levels, but it can be a little more annoying when a room has two or three rooms and you end up stuck somewhere later down the line. Thankfully, while puzzles are genuinely difficult at times, once you have the light bulb moment with them once, you can easily replicate any solution again within seconds. This is a big plus; the game’s puzzles are tiny, but complex, making the thinking and figuring out parts long, but the execution part short, which is a really hard balance to strike for any puzzle design.

[h1]Conclusion[/h1]
It is very hard to design a puzzle game, even more so for a debut team. With how hard [i]CATO: Buttered Cat[/i] knocks it out of the park, becoming genuinely one of my favorite puzzle games of all time alongside things like Valve’s [i]Portal[/i], I wouldn’t be surprised to learn that the dev team is comprised of either some serious Chinese video game veterans (that I am simply not knowledgeable about, due to the cultural boundary) or some actual, honest-to-god geniuses. Whatever the case, I will be following this team’s future work closely.

[h3]Many Thanks To Team Woll and CGORES PUBLISHING For Gracing Me With Their Astounding Art![/h3]

The Buttered Cat paradox is a rather famous one. Studies have shown that cats always land on their feet. Whenever you drop a buttered piece of toast, the buttered side is always the one hitting the countertop. However, what happens if you put a buttered piece of toast on a cat? Which hits the floor? 
I immediately knew I wanted to play this game when I saw it for the first time. I’m a sucker for puzzle-platformers, and I also love games with goofy concepts. I knew this game was for me.

Story
Our two pals, the buttered toast and the cat, want to fix a machine that gives the cat milk and the toast butter. They set out on an epic adventure to find parts to fix it.
Were you really expecting a super-complex story with this game? This story fits it perfectly. Somehow, the buttered piece of toast is the one doing the talking, not the cat. 

Gameplay
As mentioned before, Cato: Buttered Cat is a 2D puzzle-platformer. You play as both the cat and the buttered piece of toast. The controls are very simple; on his (or her) own, the cat can only move around. He can climb on some surfaces, but that’s about it. The buttered piece of toast on its own can only jump and change its direction. Paired together, the cat and toast can jump infinitely and move around. When separate, you control both characters simultaneously. No swapping needed. This leads to lots of puzzle potential. Lots of the time, the two are separate and you need to unite them, but the other way around is also true. You can disband them whenever you want. The play control is very good. Both of them are quite fast and you’ll get the hang of it quite easily. For some reason, the game says that it’s better with a controller, but I don’t really see a problem with the keyboard controls. A lot of platformers say this, but it’s good with a keyboard or controller.
The game gets interesting when it adds more mechanics. There are a host of things to interact with, such as power switches, spaces that will only exist when the two are apart (or together), bouncy spaces, and a bunch more. These give the levels tons of depth. 
Most of the levels are a similar length. They’ll usually take anywhere from 3-10 minutes to beat, so this game is very good on the go. Some of the levels are divided into a couple of screens, each being their own puzzle. There’s a reset button if you make a mistake which you can’t come back from, and it just resets the screen. If you get stuck in a level, there is a hint button, but I found these pretty unhelpful. You can also just skip the level if it's giving you too much trouble.The levels are organized via worlds, and at the end of some worlds, there’ll be an auto-running section where you need to collect these stars. For the other ones, there are bossfights, which I’m a little iffy on. I’ve never been a fan of boss fights in a puzzle game, but I suppose they’re fine here. 
I love this gameplay so much. This has to be one of the most fun and engaging puzzle-platformers I’ve played. It has a pick-up-and-play style, so if you have a Steam Deck this would be perfect. Even if you don’t, it’s still tons of fun.


Presentation
The game very clearly goes for a cute aesthetic. It’s all very colorful, and there are tons of smaller touches. If you hold R, the level resets, but if you just tap it, Cato lets out a meow. If you spin around in the air too much then he’ll get dizzy. I love details like these, it makes the game feel very cozy. 
As for how the game actually looks, most of the time it has a pixelated but VERY smooth aesthetic. It kinda reminds me of Game Boy Advance games with how vibrant it is and how smooth the animations are. Then, sometimes it switches to 3D models but with the pixelated look, and that looks nice as well. The environment variety is pretty solid, with all of them having similar elements but different colors. 
If I were to describe the music, it would be plucky. There aren’t tons of tracks in the game, but what there is is pretty great. The sound effects are very good, and the voice acting of Cato is very good.

Content
According to Howlongtobeat.com, the game is about 6 hours long. There’s 5 worlds, and over 200 levels, at least according to the description. As mentioned before, there are the skating segments, which you can perfect, but there are also other minigames. This is a cool touch, these minigames were completely unnecessary but it really was just the devs going above and beyond. There are unlockable skins for both the cat and the toast, too. There are over 75 of them, according to the description. You get these from finding secrets in the levels, and perfecting minigames like the sliding segment. You can tell which levels have secrets in them because they have a plug image at the bottom. 
The campaign isn’t super long, but there’s lots of side content in Cato. This really is an example of an indie dev doing more than what’s necessary.

Positives:
-Great play control
-Cozy and warm presentation
-Tons of extra surprises

Negatives:
-Hints are sorta unhelpful

I was actually confused. Mirage Feathers is unique.
What differentiates Mirage Feathers from Touhou or Danmaku Unlimited is its dimensional aspect. Mirage Feathers is 2.5D, moving forward into depth, while Touhou is purely 2D, moving upward. This made Mirage Feathers slightly difficult at first, but it became unique and enjoyable after I got used to its 3D illusion.

Small spoiler (not story-related, more about technical level design):
At first, it appears “2.5D”, using 2D sprites for enemies, the player, environment, levels, and everything else. It maintains the illusion of depth within a 2D space. However, after reaching a later story level, the player enters a section that appears more “3D”, flying through a laser tunnel until reaching the enemy base.

The interesting part is during the base destruction level. The game provides a smooth transition from a “forward view” into a “top-down view”. There, you destroy tanks, aircraft taking off, turrets, and more. All of them are still 2D sprites, but with layer stacking that creates a 2.5D to 3D illusion. It makes the game feel like it is truly 3D, even though it technically is not.

From my observation, as a player who pays attention to small details, this game still runs on a “2D engine”. Perhaps with very precise calculations by Oyasumi Workshop *cooking in the kitchen*, they managed to make the illusion incredibly smooth. That part honestly blew my mind.

A hidden gem. For $5, this is an incredibly complete package—fast and frenetic shooting, a nice story, beautiful pixel art, and a catchy as hell soundtrack. 

As in any good shooter, you’ll be doing 2 things: blasting enemies and dodging attacks. You primarily attack by sweeping your reticle over enemy groups to lock on, then dispatching them with the weapon of your choice. Your offensive kit has some depth to it, with a handful of homing main weapons to choose from, as well as a non-locking chaingun. Most of your main weapons drain gauge as you use them, so you have some choices to make here: Wipe out basic mobs with low powered weapons and put yourself at greater risk of attack to save your gauge for the boss fight, or spend the gauge to melt them with your laser and save health for the boss? 

As much as I love that damn laser, I love the dodging even more. Bullet patterns spray across the screen at hyper speed and demand quick reactions; at first it will look unintelligible, but you’ll soon find the holes in these dense patterns. The bullets look very inspired by CAVE, which almost makes it feel like you’re playing something like Dodonpachi Resurrection or Mushihimesama in 3D, and that is cool as ♥♥♥♥ to me. 

The story is short, sweet, and simple, with themes of friendship, betrayal, the allure of illusions, and never letting go of what matters most. It’s not the most complex narrative, but it provides motivation for the action and makes you care about what’s happening. 

Completing the story unlocks the Endless mode, which randomizes scenes and enemies. In theory, this adds infinite replayability for you to chase new high scores. In practice, I have some quibbles. First is the length; yes, it’s supposed to be endless, but my first real run in this mode went for over an hour, which is a significant time sink to put a score on the board, particularly when you consider the average shmup can be completed in 20-30 minutes. (The alternative is playing the story mode again without the cutscenes, but this is still probably a good 45min to an hour.) There’s also a maximum achievable score, which takes out a lot of the incentive to keep going. Finally, there’s a meta-progression upgrade system, which in my opinion undercuts the sense of accomplishment based on skill alone (and basically invalidates high scores set with an inferior build). I’d prefer a Crimzon Clover-type system, where you have a balanced set of choices with different strengths and weaknesses to start your run. 

Maybe I’m taking this a little too seriously, but I think that is a compliment to the dev—I see the potential for their games to be up there among the great shooters. Highly recommended.
