Mirage Feathers is a fast, anime-styled rail shooter that’s all about dodging dense bullet patterns while staying aggressive. It pushes you forward along fixed routes, so the challenge comes from reading the screen quickly and making smart micro-movements rather than exploring or aiming with slow precision. The game’s tone is a mix of cute character aesthetics and surprisingly intense combat.

The shooting feels built around momentum. You’re encouraged to keep your crosshair sweeping through targets and maintain pressure, because enemies come in waves and the screen can get crowded in seconds. When the flow clicks, it has that satisfying “thread the needle” rhythm where you’re constantly balancing offense and survival.

A nice part of the progression is experimenting with different weapon behaviors. Instead of everything feeling like the same gun with bigger numbers, loadouts can change how you approach crowds, elites, and tricky angles. That variety helps keep retries from feeling identical, especially when you’re learning tougher sections.

In terms of structure, it offers a story-driven experience with dialogue and character moments, and it also caters to players who just want pure score/skill grinding. If you like having a narrative wrapper, it’s there, but the main attraction is still the action and the repeated push to clear a stage cleaner and faster than last time.

Difficulty is the main selling point and also the main filter. The game doesn’t hesitate to overwhelm you, and it expects you to improve through pattern recognition, better positioning, and calmer decision-making under pressure. Some runs will end abruptly, but the “one more try” pull is strong if you enjoy challenging shooters.

Presentation is stylish and energetic. Character art and UI reinforce the anime vibe, and the soundtrack does a good job keeping the pace high. Even when you’re struggling, the visuals and audio make it feel like you’re in the middle of a dramatic aerial battle rather than just solving a mechanical puzzle.

Overall, Mirage Feathers is best for players who like shmups/rail shooters with heavy dodging, quick reactions, and repeated attempts to master stages. If you want a relaxed shooter or lots of exploration, it may feel too relentless. If you’re into high-intensity arcade-style action with a polished anime presentation, it’s an easy one to recommend.

A hidden gem. For $5, this is an incredibly complete package—fast and frenetic shooting, a nice story, beautiful pixel art, and a catchy as hell soundtrack. 

As in any good shooter, you’ll be doing 2 things: blasting enemies and dodging attacks. You primarily attack by sweeping your reticle over enemy groups to lock on, then dispatching them with the weapon of your choice. Your offensive kit has some depth to it, with a handful of homing main weapons to choose from, as well as a non-locking chaingun. Most of your main weapons drain gauge as you use them, so you have some choices to make here: Wipe out basic mobs with low powered weapons and put yourself at greater risk of attack to save your gauge for the boss fight, or spend the gauge to melt them with your laser and save health for the boss? 

As much as I love that damn laser, I love the dodging even more. Bullet patterns spray across the screen at hyper speed and demand quick reactions; at first it will look unintelligible, but you’ll soon find the holes in these dense patterns. The bullets look very inspired by CAVE, which almost makes it feel like you’re playing something like Dodonpachi Resurrection or Mushihimesama in 3D, and that is cool as ♥♥♥♥ to me. 

The story is short, sweet, and simple, with themes of friendship, betrayal, the allure of illusions, and never letting go of what matters most. It’s not the most complex narrative, but it provides motivation for the action and makes you care about what’s happening. 

Completing the story unlocks the Endless mode, which randomizes scenes and enemies. In theory, this adds infinite replayability for you to chase new high scores. In practice, I have some quibbles. First is the length; yes, it’s supposed to be endless, but my first real run in this mode went for over an hour, which is a significant time sink to put a score on the board, particularly when you consider the average shmup can be completed in 20-30 minutes. (The alternative is playing the story mode again without the cutscenes, but this is still probably a good 45min to an hour.) There’s also a maximum achievable score, which takes out a lot of the incentive to keep going. Finally, there’s a meta-progression upgrade system, which in my opinion undercuts the sense of accomplishment based on skill alone (and basically invalidates high scores set with an inferior build). I’d prefer a Crimzon Clover-type system, where you have a balanced set of choices with different strengths and weaknesses to start your run. 

Maybe I’m taking this a little too seriously, but I think that is a compliment to the dev—I see the potential for their games to be up there among the great shooters. Highly recommended.

Everything makes this game work well. There's a rustic, gnarly sound and visual to everything, but also beautiful scenes, imagery and music. The controls are frustrating, but pushing up against the tense feeling of charged upon by a demon in a tight, dirty corridor. Characters are written so well I really appreciate and care for some of them, but am also disgusted and hateful towards others. I feel smart for solving the puzzles, despite there essentially being one mechanic outside of walk, use, and shoot.

This game does a lot with what little it presents, which really speaks to the amazing work they put into it.

This is maybe the most beautiful game I've ever played in my life. The character designs are so brilliant I won't shut up about them to my friends, the story is unique and interesting, and the music is awesome. My one real gripe is just not being able to change aiming sensitivity, since it makes it somewhat difficult to get turned the right way around if I'm facing slightly off when aiming.

It does the survival horror part really well while also adding a few unique twists to it, especially how it switches to first person whenever you aim. The characters are super fun, the atmosphere is great, the plot is interesting... it's overall just a REALLY good experience.

I'm pretty sure some people will be put off by their first impression of the game on the store page but trust me, it's AMAZING. I really hope for more like this.

What this game really nails is the vibe. 
Sorry We’re Closed has sharp, entertaining writing and a confident sense of style.
The mechanics can feel a bit clunky at times - switching perspectives takes a moment, and not being able to walk in first person can get frustrating, but nothing the optional infinite healing water can’t fix.
It’s less a “git‑gud” kind of game and more like wandering through someone’s very stylish, very gay, lofi-PS1-silenthillish fever dream.
If that sounds appealing to you, it’s worth your time.

TLDR: Amazing game. Great for newer players, as the combat stakes are raised gradually and gameplay is pretty intuitive. The game is pretty short, with about 8 hours on my first playthrough, but it has a lot of replay value. Story beats are captivating, the art direction is amazing, and I highly recommend Sorry, We're Closed. 

AAAHHHH I LOVE!!! I just finished my first playthrough on Normal Mode. As someone who has a history of being bad at video games, this game does a very good job of easing the player into combat and giving you a chance to get good at it before the stakes rise. I've died a few times, for sure, but never felt like I was losing my mind trying to beat bosses. I would highly recommend this for players wanting to get into survival horror (especially early console-style horror), as the scope and difficulty of the game are great for inexperienced players. If you're a more advanced player that likes a little brow-beating, I'd recommend playing on Hard Mode from the get-go. 

The setting and art and atmosphere creates amazing tension, especially in the later levels. I liked that decisions that would affect the ending were sort of intuitive. I had a goal, and was able to make decisions that led  me where I wanted to end up. The story beats were well paced, and I really got into all of the different relationships I could play into and affect. It's also refreshing to see queer horror represented with both tragic and happy endings, especially with a well developed cast that represents many queer experiences without making tropes out of them. Will definitely be taking advantage of my NG+ save to try and get more endings. 

Overall, 11/10 I can't wait to see what this studio does next!

I put off playing this game for so long and I'm so glad I finally bought it. While the price tag is a bit too high for my liking I still clocked in about 12 hours including a few completion achievements. The art in this game is amazing. From the character designs to the actual places you find yourself in it's always a joy to meet new characters. The game is categorized as "horror" but it's not super spooky so it shouldn't be a problem for those who aren't fans of the genre.

The story is where this game really shines for me. I made sure to read every piece of dialogue i could find in this game and it was worth it. Each character has a distinctive personnality and you can choose to help them just like you can choose to f*ck them over. While your choices don't have a massive impact on the game they can lock you out of certain endings and achievements. The actual plot is pretty decent though I can see how some people may be underwhelmed by it.

Gameplay wise it's probably the least interesting aspect, though I still enjoyed the levels quite a bit. The gun play is alright and the exploration and very light puzzles are pretty fun. There's a nice balance of shooting bad guys and walking around so the game doesn't let you get bored.

TL;DR: Great characters and dialogue, very gay, decently fun gun play and combat but story and worldbuilding is what you play this game for.

I delayed providing a review for this game until I had the time to thoroughly explore every detail the game had to offer, and I am glad that I did.

The late-90s to early-2000s grungy, punk-alternative aesthetics and PS1/PS2-like format with fixed cameras tugged a few nostalgic chords in my heart.  Reminding me of my fondness for these older game mechanics, as well as its resemblance to those of [i]Resident Evil 2 (1998)[/i], [i]Silent Hill 2 (2001)[/i], and [i]Fatal Frame (2002)[/i].  Even if it was a rough start in getting the gist of the controls... especially with aim.  And, on the note of aesthetics, the in-game artwork overall is stunning.  With the clash of vibrant advertisement postings and artwork against the grunginess of 90s-00s London environments hauled out this inexplicable feeling of sentimentality for me, one that I struggle to put into words.  'Dazzled' or 'nostalgic' or 'fond' don't seem to cut it well enough.
And, of course, not to disregard the opposing design elements; the grittiness and brutality of the "dungeons" and the Hotel.  Once again, major nostalgia of prior PS1/PS2 titles.

Moving forward... I acknowledge that making good replay value, let alone creating a video game, takes enough effort as is.  And it is infrequent that I find games that [i]really[/i] have a grip on my attention just enough to [i]want[/i] to dedicate time and energy into replaying a game to its fullest, over and over and over again.  And this game hit the mark.  The unique dialogues and outcomes, dry or witty humour, let alone the storytelling sold it for me.  Each character had significance to the story, regardless of main and/or side-quest route that was executed, and presented something intriguing (or cheeky) to further add to the narrative.  And [b]some[/b] of which had surprised me.  [strike]Looking at you, Lucy.[/strike]

On that topic of the narrative, I especially took a liking to the themes covered.  The complexity of human (or... even non-human) emotions, morals, rationale, love, and the processing of such an abstract, subjective, tangled mess of thoughts.  And how that complexity of thoughts and emotions can shape the outcomes of relationships (whether with others or yourself), all determined on how you [b]process[/b] and [b]perceive[/b] the world around you and reflect it back.  As well as the imperative of deep inner work, emotionally and mentally.  I, myself, often struggle to encapsulate these feelings into verbal delineations.  Thus, I took great appreciation in how this was portrayed throughout the narrative.

Additionally, around 2SLGBTQ+ themes; how the subject matter among the characters is perceived as entirely mundane, like an everyday "Oh yeah, that's just Robyn; they're cool" mentality and aspect in the lives of people within society, rather than a grand spectacle to gawk and gasp at?  Just people living regularly as people without batting an eye?  Gold star.  I wish for more portrayals and representation as such in future media, without the recurring propensity to [i]willingly[/i] shine a spotlight on it and paint it as a "big deal" or "taboo" or "other".

Overall, I loved this game.  Definitely an 8.5 or 9/10 for me.

There's more talking than shooting but if y'er fine with that, give it a go. The survival horror aspects of it were also mostly good, although I wish we got a lot more of it. The combat ie. shooting mechanics were enjoyable, but the melee weapon swung too slow it was hard to combo with it or to not get hit by an enemy yourself. Although, if it was faster then it might've been too OP. This however is not as issue, as I never ran out of bullets and actually found the game way too easy on normal mode, which is a shame. You can always change the difficulty settings mid-game but I didn't bother. 

The bosses were cool as heck, especially with their tailored OSTs to go with them, and I enjoyed fighting them. They mostly felt unique, even when there aren't too too many game mechanics to use. The bosses were obviously harder than the normal enemies and sometimes felt a bit unfair until you got the hang of it (and realized you don't have to actually be in range for the otherworld -vision heart thingies to use your Special Attack boss killing weapon).

Some of the enemies don't stay dead (for one chapter), which could've added some much needed difficulty in regards to avoiding enemies, slowly winding down your resources, and being overall more tactical with your approach (and more spookier!). But we get a weapon that permanently kills these enemies after the first section of the game...

The game was lacking in your typical obscure survival horror puzzles (this is either a good or a bad thing depending on who you ask).  Personally the puzzles we got were too easy and a breeze. Mostly it was just get item, know immediately where the item goes, repeat.

The scoring system did however cater to a pro gamer audience, heavily lowering your score for even just a heal or two. However, from my knowledge the scoring system does nothing and is just there for the achievement hunters. This was something I tried to play into and chose not to use the abundant healing items around me, even when I was low on health.

I saw some people claim the game was too hard, and for clarity I played on mouse and keyboard for easier aiming. When you enter first person mode you ALWAYS look at where your character is currently looking, so turn around before entering first person. There is also a dedicated "turn around" -button. Also, when you enter other-world mode, you stun enemies for a brief moment (which you can also increase), so it shouldn't be that big of an issue. (Get good).

The story, setting, music, characters and a unique combat system would still make me heavily recommend the game to anyone regardless of skill or experience with survival horror titles.

It's alright. Gameplay is killer 7 mixed with resident evil although it definitely lacks on the survival horror side.

The game uses a mission/level structure and the levels start pretty basic and linear and actually become more linear by the end. You won't be finding anything like the spencer mansion here. The only additional twist here is the third eye gimmick which can reveal a different world in a small circle around your character. This gets some use in puzzles but I think doesn't see enough use in navigation until the 3rd area.

Combat is usually just shoot a weak point 2-3 times and move on with your life. It was serviceable but I feel there were very few enemy types that felt meaningfully different from each other. It was at its strongest during forced group encounters where I actually had to move and make decisions but these were few and far in between throughout the game. I think running past enemies is actually a bit too easy as well usually.  

Story seems like where most of the work went. There are more than a few choices along the way and a variety of different endings to get. I liked it but not enough to do another playthrough of the game.  On another note the artstyle was neat but I think the ingame assets didn't really match with it. 

Overall though I would recommend on sale. It's around 6 hours long and definitely worth at least one playthrough.

This beautifully combines puzzles and violence, the storyline is as delightfully unpredicatble as a crazy ex-girlfriend going from talking to a beautifully drawn character to fighting for your life in seconds flat. It's the perfect mixture of gameplay and dialogue (plus a lot of freedom to skip storyline elements if you don't want as much of it). All the girls, gays and theys should play this game. If you are new to gaming and easily frustrated they have tons of acessibility options to make the game less repetitive and if you aren't I assume people may find some of the fighting elements a bit easy but, what they might lack in super diverse enemies to face they make up for with interesting puzzles, resource scarcity and menacing atmospheres where you have no idea what too expect around any corner. This has made me fall in love with dungeon crawlers 11/10 <3

This game is good fun, engaging, and doesn't overstay its welcome.

- The combat system isn't very difficult, but is varied and presents the player with the opportunity to challenge themselves if they want to.
- The story, level design, and puzzles are all pretty simple, but they give you just enough pretext and momentum to thread you along through the game.
- The game is short but satifying, and doesn't drag on past the point where it has nothing more to offer.

TLDR : This is great little game, made with a passion and heart that carries you through whatever minor blemishes it has. It's the 2D pixel-art equivalent of the movie School of Rock.

9/10

I call this game a true triumph; given the journey the developers, Rob and Ritual Studios went through to make this game possible. I loved every moment of it.

I bought to play this game becuz of Rob, had it in my wishlist as a support after the vid where he reveals his mental struggles, but I finish the game with a conclusion of it's a wonderful indie game.
 
I like how there was a balance of the fantasized world lore and music elements, and ofc the unique and creative mechanisms off instruments, u can tell how passionate the team was to create the game
As well as the music, especially the acoustic guitar Crescendos theme is something that could make your tears drop!

*However was a few issues:
- (Accidentally) Sold items aren't repurchasable, which requires extra grinds to earn back
- Some description are quite confusing even as someone who plays instruments like me were confused, especially the Synthesizer was an ache to understand how to utilize it.
- Journal weren't sync at the end before going into Riffson's office, the journal was just showing the guide  before going to 2nd round of battle of bands, that leads us to backdoor entrance of stage. (I'm not sure does this issue appears anywhere else of the game progress)
- There was a situation where boss's Crescendos (ulti) animation, partial of frame stuck on the screen causing me unable to see the character and the riffs, I was able to parry but have to guess it. (It happens on the last battle with Riffson)
- Wasn't a big issue but kinda annoying when you can only proceed the dialogues with left click instead of E or Space or any other keys, since sometimes when you're just walking around to chat with NPCs you might feel to rest ur right hand.

This is another game that I found through a Next Fest some time ago. Normally, I am a staunchly unapologetic HATER of Paper-Mario-likes that cuck your performance in turn-based combat with pointless QTEs, but the fact that this game implements music into its gameplay makes a TOTAL difference. I do enjoy some rhythm games and having the QTEs activate on the beats of music makes them much more intuitive and rewarding to hit. In terms of playstyle, the game offers four weapons that all function very differently and are all very creative in forcing you to adapt to different playstyles. However, I only found myself using the Acoustic Guitar for fighting mobs and the Bass Guitar for fighting bosses since I just couldn't figure out how to use the Synth and 8-String properly. Part of the problem, though, is that the game does actually feature some amount of farming, which is always a drag. If you want to buy all of the attacks for any one of the weapons, you'll need to grind money. If you want to then upgrade all those riffs, you'll need to farm resources, which - as far as I can tell - do not have a dedicated farming method. Because of this, I just picked the two weapons I liked best (also the first two you get) and only upgraded the attacks I needed; good luck to anyone out there who decides to go after the achievement for fully upgrading a weapon.

Overall, this is a super solid title. Even if you're like me and despise (genuinely [i]HATE[/i]) Paper-Mario-likes, I would still recommend this so long as you at least like music and rhythm games, because your skill in this game will depend on your ability to follow a beat.

A short and charming game about a musician aiming to compete in the battle of the bands, it does a good job at primarily being a deck builder, and integrating elements of rhythm gameplay.

You collect four "decks" in the form of instruments, each with their own unique cards and strategies: Acoustic guitar, bass, synth, and electric guitar. Some decks fare better against certain enemies/bosses, but the game is built in such a way that you can either spread your resources and be a multi-instrumentalist, or just be an 8-string djent [strike]devil worshipper[/strike] god.

If you are a fan of music, or play any instruments, this game is sure to put a smile on your face :)

I'll preface by saying that I just barely recommend this game at 6.5/10. Around 3/4ths of the way in I was trying to end it as quickly as possible.
It starts very strong. I had so much fun with the demo just re-fighting enemies. You're never too far away from a music-based pun or a youtuber appearance (I think, I only know Davie504.) Unfortunately, it really frontloads its ideas and then runs out of steam around halfway through.

You acquire the bass, synth, and especially the eight string in rapid succession, which overwhelmed me with different ways to explore the engaging rhythm combat system. I discovered, however, that there was plenty of game ahead of the halfway point to get too familiar with the systems and trivialize the difficulty of standard enemies and even bosses. Lava Riffson was my favorite boss that I've fought in a while, presenting a challenge that forced me to switch up my strategies and eventually my instrument, but the three bosses after that, including the finale, didn't live up to that one. Riffson himself annoyed me. I had to repeatedly clear the easy stages in order to lose to his final one. Turns out that switching from the eight-string to the bass made it a cakewalk. I also miss the option from the demo to increase or decrease the difficulty of the QTE timing.

-The accoustic was facile to use. It didn't have a interesting gimmick like the others, and it's a suitable beginner weapon but I never used it after getting the bass. It's unfortunate, because its battle theme is the best out of the weapons.
-The bass was also pretty easy, but had a slight learning curve with its combo system. I was the most disappointed by this battle theme. I think the QTE attacks necessitate a simpler background track to make the attack timing stand out more, but the timed button presses on the super move could have been more difficult. That critique goes for all of the weapons; if I'm going to be doing rhythm inputs for a super move across my entire playthrough, I want them to be more engaging.
-The synth was the most fun at the start as I was getting the hang of its unique, status-based playstyle. Eventually I realized it was a lot of work for less reward than the bass or eight-string. It has the most different battle theme out of the instruments, and someone out there probably loves its complexity.
-The eight string became my weapon of choice. I really enjoyed reducing my own HP to deal tons of damage at first, but it became unviable for bosses because they'd deal too much damage in addition to my own. Much like the other weapons, once I figured out a good strategy, I didn't have to mix it up much and it became less fun.

The story hints at greater moments like Strum's involvement in creating the monsters you fight and the concept of sonic energy as a whole, but it doesn't do much with any of them besides beating you over the head with Riffson's capitalist schemes. "Get it? Record companies are shady!!" You'd be better off skipping it as it's a waste of time. Strum isn't a major enough character to have an arc with guilt and redemption, and he never even meets Riffson in the story. I think the lack of an epilogue is a waste of potential. 

SPOILERS!

You defeat Riffson and all the bad guys become unbrainwashed, and the world goes back to normal. Even if I just got new dialogue from Strum or Riffson & goons, or got to take a walk through the world and see all the happy, non-transformed creatures that would have been enough for me. I actually cried when the credits rolled, not because of the story, but just having a reminder that relationships end got me choked up. I wish there had been an ending to this game.

A really fun, catchy, somewhat relaxing card battle adventure. It's sad to see how close these devs came to giving up, overcoming the odds, but then see relatively low sale numbers. Highly recommend as an easy starter deck builder but I think any fans of those and music should play this.

My only complaints are:
- Final boss was still in an invulnerable state even after killing his "goons" and didn't go vulnerable until the next round. I wasted my "super" on that and lost on my first try because of that. Really annoying.
- Some of the QTE block mechanisms could be smoothed out. Which beat you're supposed to hit on isn't intuitive - at least for me.

I play a lot of games, I have for nearly 30 years.  My entire life, has been music, and gaming.  Sometimes, you come across special little gems in gaming, just as you would with a song that finds you at the right time in your life.  As a fan of JRPGs, Emo, Punk, Metal, and MUSIC in general... This game is a masterpiece.  I can tell that so much love and passion went into making it something quirky and fun, but with heart, and care.  Sure, a bug here and there.  Some options I'd like to see added.  But overall... man, what can I say.  It's a non-traditional turn based RPG with Guitar Hero/Rock Band segments.  References to all sorts of music and bands I love (I see you Mr. Kem Mikkel), and just SO many creative nods to music, musicians, writers, and little things that musicians know and love, like riffs, pedals, and such.  I love it.  I am sitting here writing this, just before what I know has to be the final boss.  And I can't wait to play the game again.  Even more so, I really hope this game gets a sequel.  I can only imagine what could be done from here.  Maybe we command a full band?  Maybe we can compose our own riffs using some sort of MIDI system, and use those in battle?  It feels like the sky's the limit.

Devs, if you read this... you did a great job.  Everyone involved, I do mean everyone.  I'm giving this my #1 game of 2025 spot.  I gifted it to like 3 or 4 people last night.  You deserve the support.

*UPDATE* And I beat it, and enjoyed going back through what I missed to get the rest of the Achievements... my first 100% on Steam, by the way!

A fantastic story about a one man's djent project which will make him reach the stars and beyond. But for real, the gameplay is fun, the music is superb, the graphics one of a kind, what's more to ask for? The only downside is the stealth mission which tbh was dreadful and I wish it was changed/removed, everything else is top notch and I hope that this isn't the end of Rob's venture into videogames. Greg approves!

Lots of fun if you are a musician! I would have loved it to be longer because I really enjoyed it. It would have been great to have more locations and especially more instruments (DLC? 2-player DLC? Follow-on game?).

In this RPG, as you unlock them, your weapons of choice will be classical guitar, electric bass, synth, and electric guitar (8 strings, mind you lol). Each instrument has a different passive power and a different ultimate (which plays like a QTE). They all play with a very different strategy; it is not just aesthetics. You fight monsters by using your own selection of instrument-specific riffs that you unlock (and upgrade) and your own choice of pick ups, strings, etc, and up to three pedals.

In addition, your powers are more powerful if you press a key in rhythm at the right moment and, similarly, you get less damage if you do that when attacked. The soundtrack also adapts to the instrument that you choose, and to the attack you make (and receive). The bosses are particularly fun – with metal of course.

Great sountrack. Cult of Luna made an original song for this game, called "Lord of the Riff". How cool is that :)! https://dovetail.bandcamp.com/album/fretless-the-wrath-of-riffson-original-game-soundtrack

Wildly interesting build variety and turn based gameplay balances an interesting world with a servicable story.  Could easily see replaying this one later with a totally different strategy.  Would reccommend for anyone looking for a low time commit, replayable game with depth but a defined ending.

I remember when Rob initially announced this as an idea FOREVER ago, and I've just had it sitting in my Wishlist till it finally came out. When it finally did, a friend gifted it to me before I could grab it for Christmas, and man, this has been AWESOME. You can tell a lot of love went into this. And to have quite a few other musicians from YouTube as characters in this has been super cool lol. The music choices and small elements of melody hidden throughout each area is awesome, and fits a great common melodical theme. The battles are fun, the mods you can do to each instrument are cool, all around, I just love this game. 10/10
Very fun game, I loved the different instrument builds you could do.  I loved the story, the art, the music.

Very sweet! It leans into a classic movie trope in a lighthearted way. The gameplay is fun, and even though the campaign is a bit short, it's still a game worth checking out. Plus for fans of Rob and other music creators, there's a fun cameo!

Short but sweet game, 100%ed it around 7 and a half hours. If you're a fan of Rob and music Youtube, it's got a good chunk of nice references to Rob's music and videos, and a few cameos from fellow Youtubers in the space. Great soundtrack, love every single track. Only thing I found a bit annoying was upgrading riffs for that one achievement.
Really fun and tight game. Short but enjoyable all the way through.

This game is good fun, engaging, and doesn't overstay its welcome.

- The combat system isn't very difficult, but is varied and presents the player with the opportunity to challenge themselves if they want to.
- The story, level design, and puzzles are all pretty simple, but they give you just enough pretext and momentum to thread you along through the game.
- The game is short but satifying, and doesn't drag on past the point where it has nothing more to offer.

TLDR : This is great little game, made with a passion and heart that carries you through whatever minor blemishes it has. It's the 2D pixel-art equivalent of the movie School of Rock.

9/10

Great history. The pixel art is amazing. The musical world in which it takes place is something I had been looking for since I first played Brutal Legend. It's really beautiful. The different instrument and riffs upgrades makes combat interesting. I found enemies were no so varied, but they're interesting as they are.

I'd buy it again for sure. I hope they continue making games like this.

If you're a fan of Rob Scallon you should just pick this up to support the dev team, they worked really hard for a long time to put this out.

I'm someone quite familiar with deckbuilder games, so this game was fairly easy for me. The music and QTE system kept me engaged once I'd snowballed to a ridiculous power level.

The different instruments (and the riffs you can get for them) provide variable play methods even if you stick with the same weapon the whole time. I'll probably do a second playthrough at some point and limit myself to using the instruments I didn't touch that much. I mainly used the acoustic guitar and dipped into the synth for one part of the game that had enemies I couldn't easily deal with otherwise.

A couple things I feel that the game is missing are optional super bosses/post game content. It'd be nice to see an endless fight dungeon or something.

It's a nice short game. I recommend it if you like music.

Even if there are some slight bugs, I am willing to put them aside because I sense so much passion that went into this passion project of musician Rob Scallon. As a fan of his channel for years, I had goosebumps whenever I found an easter egg or reference to his channel/other musicians channel (e.g. Mary Spender, Andrew Huang, etc.). The music is something out of a Brian Eno ambient album mixed in with Rob's immediately recognizable melodies.

I call this game a true triumph; given the journey the developers, Rob and Ritual Studios went through to make this game possible. I loved every moment of it.

Was very fun. I can recommend especially on sale. Player building has a lot of options so you are sure to find a playstyle you like. I didn't 100% the game but explored every area pretty well and got about 20 hours out of it. 

My only complaints are that it's a bit too easy (played on normal). A few boss fights made me adjust for them but most of the time you can clear just playing however you want. I would have liked some traversal abilities or puzzles to make exploration a little more interesting.

I do recommend, but barely.
The game is ok, but a bit slow, pacing could be better. Story is interesting but not great, translation and localization could also use a bit more work, you can get the idea of what it's saying sure, but it doesn't read or flow the best so you don't get sucked into it like you might if it was rewritten a bit better for english.
The gameplay itself is interesting, a mix of metroidvana and little sprite turn based combat (limbus company/library of Ruina vibes for me), making it an interesting experieince. But you really do have to just fall back to spamming shields for so many fights that your actual combat for many enemies never really can vary that much. Finding ways beyond shielding to survive would have been good for the game, but it is what it is for now.

Overall, I do think it was an alright purchase, though did take awhile to play, and was nothing super enjoyable, just ok. So a slight recomend, worth getting on sale for those interested, but don't put off anything you really are excited about to play this one, it's a lower priority time killer I'd say.

It's impressive that this game is made by a solo developer, but for it to also have qualities almost on par with metroidvanias made by a dedicated team? It's no small feat and truly deserves the Overwhelmingly Positive rating on Steam.

What I find even more impressive is this game almost got the Ender Lilies/Ender Magnolia charm - a world slowly being consumed by evil, combined with music that inspires hope and strength to persevere. It's not as good as both of those to be fair, but it's close enough that I can see where the inspiration of this game comes from.

Then there's the combat aspect of the game. Rather than real-time combat like other metroidvanias usually employ, this game took a turn-based approach that is done really well. There are plenty of builds you can do in this game, and this game encourages you to experiment with different builds since stat allocations can be reset at any time. Because of how complex the game can get, the game is mouse-heavy since there are lots of effects that require you to mouse over them to understand what they do, know the source of the buff/debuffs since the same buff/debuff can stack as long as they come from different source, or just the ease of operation of pointing your mouse and click at the skills you want rather than having to use a dpad to navigate through various skills. Fret not, the game has controller support, but the dev made it clear when you start the game that this game is primarily designed for keyboard + mouse.

My only complaint about the game is that this game likes to give you tips on what you should do before a boss. Usually a player might be able to deduce the gimmick of a boss given the environment of the area (if I'm in an area that constantly applies poison to me, surely the boss of the area will have something related to poison), or in most other metroidvanias, you simply jump into the boss to find out their gimmick, die because your build completely gets countered by them, then change your build to counter the gimmick. This game outright tells you before a boss what their gimmick is so you prepare beforehand (for example, purge a debuff that makes you heal less because boss' damage scales with the debuff). In my time playing on Hard difficulty(the second hardest difficulty of the game), I've only died 3 times - once to an elite enemy I underestimated their strength, and twice to a same boss because I was underlevelled at the time. I had near-deaths during some boss fights, but I was only able to do so because I was made aware of what the boss do before I jumped into them.

Complaints aside, I would still recommend this game, because it's a well-made one. The game being cheap is a steal; I'd happily pay the usual $15 - $20 price tag of metroidvanias for this game.
Very solid Metroidvania RPG that has a fun combat system with good customizability

This is an indie game made by a single developer, Chinese made.  Draws upon the aesthetics and themes of the ENDER games, but is a turn based RPG instead of a Souls-inspired Metroidvania.  You play a small ethereal girl trying to save the world from corruption by summoning spirits to fight for you.

The exploration is as a side view platformer, with fairly large and detailed maps, lots of twists and opening shortcuts.  While it seems like you'll eventually get Metroidvania style unlocks, you never get any, instead it's finding ways to deal with the many forms of corruption you're dealing with.  Until you complete a few quests, it can be difficult to manage these corruption, poison, and burning environmental effects.

Combat's great, you have to manage several resource pools to summon spirits, change stances (each of which gives you passives and a set of skills), use spells, and limited use per save items.  There are a huge number of skills, passives, stances, and summonable spirits to assist you, and builds can get very complex.  The combat felt great, flowed well, and there's a few bosses that require specific tricks to manage (there's usually ghosts giving a hint nearby if you get devastated).  There's also a pretty extensive post-game, harder re-fights, a combat wave arena, and a boss rush, along with what looks like a really good NG+ system.  The difficult is not that bad on normal, only a few mid and a single late game boss (plus the final boss re-fight but not the initial fight) caused me to wipe.  There are difficulty options for more experienced and less experienced turn based RPG strategists.  For the $8 I spent, I got 24 hours of enjoyment out of it.

Visually it looks great, it makes excellent use of color and lighting.  Music's quite good, mostly mellow piano music, with a few intense tracks for important boss fights.  Sound effects are decent but a few come in very loud (such as the sound when attacking block, or using certain late game items).

That said, it's a Chinese game and the translation (while better than Afterimage) is still not great, and it's a little tough to follow at times.  I also ended up running into the final boss and was really surprised when I got the quest update to visit the town and talk to the people you saved.  It seemed rather abrupt and I thought there would have been a boss that connected all the disparate groups and sources of corruption together.  I also found a jump/movement glitch that could let me do a larger than normal jump in certain places.  This happens when you jump against a sloped wall, there were only a few places I saw where you could get somewhere you probably shouldn't.

I play on controller, as keyboard and mouse is painful to me, and while it did play decently well, there were some weird issues.  Some tips and details screens were hard to access on controller, switching targets on your summons is a bit awkward to do (push in the right stick then move the right stick left or right, push in the right stick to switch back).  Also I'd sometimes get persistent tooltips explaining the buffs or debuffs I had on stuck, I couldn't find a way to fix this without clicking the mouse and moving it off the edge of the screen so it didn't interfere.

Overall still great and I liked it quite a bit!

Game was pretty enjoyable. Was fun enough to get the last few achievements after getting the final boss. 
I will say it was generally pretty easy and there were some really dominant strategies that were able to be used that kinda made the game a cakewalk I probally shoulda bumped up the difficulty but didnt process when the start didn't allow it.
The translation isn't very good I must say too. Skills were a bit hard to parse and the story was even harder to parse.
All said if you like customization, cool abilities, and some interesting design space for how an rpg works I would rec.

Was very fun. I can recommend especially on sale. Player building has a lot of options so you are sure to find a playstyle you like. I didn't 100% the game but explored every area pretty well and got about 20 hours out of it. 

My only complaints are that it's a bit too easy (played on normal). A few boss fights made me adjust for them but most of the time you can clear just playing however you want. I would have liked some traversal abilities or puzzles to make exploration a little more interesting.

The music, art, and aesthetic reminded me greatly of Ender Lilies, the highly customizable skills and builds make the game play very fun, although it was too easy even on second highest difficulty, with the highest difficulty being locked from first playthrough.

While overall good, again it's a bit slow paced and lacks variety in actual combat. Yes there are tons of different builds, stances, and lots of gear and options to customize stuff... But you really do have to just fall back to spamming shields for so many fights that your actual combat for many enemies never really can vary that much. Finding ways beyond shielding to survive would have been good for the game, but it is what it is for now.

Overall, I do think it was an alright purchase, though did take awhile to play, and was nothing super enjoyable, just ok. So a slight recomend, worth getting on sale for those interested, but don't put off anything you really are excited about to play this one, it's a lower priority time killer I'd say.

Then there's the combat aspect of the game. Rather than real-time combat like other metroidvanias usually employ, this game took a turn-based approach that is done really well. There are plenty of builds you can do in this game, and this game encourages you to experiment with different builds since stat allocations can be reset at any time. Because of how complex the game can get, the game is mouse-heavy since there are lots of effects that require you to mouse over them to understand what they do, know the source of the buff/debuffs since the same buff/debuff can stack as long as they come from different source, or just the ease of operation of pointing your mouse and click at the skills you want rather than having to use a dpad to navigate through various skills. Fret not, the game has controller support, but the dev made it clear when you start the game that this game is primarily designed for keyboard + mouse.

I had near-deaths during some boss fights, but I was only able to do so because I was made aware of what the boss do before I jumped into them.

Complaints aside, I would still recommend this game, because it's a well-made one. The game being cheap is a steal; I'd happily pay the usual $15 - $20 price tag of metroidvanias for this game.

In my time playing on Hard difficulty(the second hardest difficulty of the game), I've only died 3 times - once to an elite enemy I underestimated their strength, and twice to a same boss because I was underlevelled at the time. I had near-deaths during some boss fights, but I was only able to do so because I was made aware of what the boss do before I jumped into them.

Complaints aside, I would still recommend this game, because it's a well-made one. The game being cheap is a steal; I'd happily pay the usual $15 - $20 price tag of metroidvanias for this game.
Very solid Metroidvania RPG that has a fun combat system with good customizability

This is an indie game made by a single developer, Chinese made.  Draws upon the aesthetics and themes of the ENDER games, but is a turn based RPG instead of a Souls-inspired Metroidvania.  You play a small ethereal girl trying to save the world from corruption by summoning spirits to fight for you.

The exploration is as a side view platformer, with fairly large and detailed maps, lots of twists and opening shortcuts.  While it seems like you'll eventually get Metroidvania style unlocks, you never get any, instead it's finding ways to deal with the many forms of corruption you're dealing with.  Until you complete a few quests, it can be difficult to manage these corruption, poison, and burning environmental effects.

Combat's great, you have to manage several resource pools to summon spirits, change stances (each of which gives you passives and a set of skills), use spells, and limited use per save items.  There are a huge number of skills, passives, stances, and summonable spirits to assist you, and builds can get very complex.  The combat felt great, flowed well, and there's a few bosses that require specific tricks to manage (there's usually ghosts giving a hint nearby if you get devastated).  There's also a pretty extensive post-game, harder re-fights, a combat wave arena, and a boss rush, along with what looks like a really good NG+ system.  The difficult is not that bad on normal, only a few mid and a single late game boss (plus the final boss re-fight but not the initial fight) caused me to wipe.  There are difficulty options for more experienced and less experienced turn based RPG strategists.  For the $8 I spent, I got 24 hours of enjoyment out of it.

Visually it looks great, it makes excellent use of color and lighting.  Music's quite good, mostly mellow piano music, with a few intense tracks for important boss fights.  Sound effects are decent but a few come in very loud (such as the sound when attacking block, or using certain late game items).

That said, it's a Chinese game and the translation (while better than Afterimage) is still not great, and it's a little tough to follow at times.  I also ended up running into the final boss and was really surprised when I got the quest update to visit the town and talk to the people you saved.  It seemed rather abrupt and I thought there would have been a boss that connected all the disparate groups and sources of corruption together.  I also found a jump/movement glitch that could let me do a larger than normal jump in certain places.  This happens when you jump against a sloped wall, there were only a few places I saw where you could get somewhere you probably shouldn't.

I play on controller, as keyboard and mouse is painful to me, and while it did play decently well, there were some weird issues.  Some tips and details screens were hard to access on controller, switching targets on your summons is a bit awkward to do (push in the right stick then move the right stick left or right, push in the right stick to switch back).  Also I'd sometimes get persistent tooltips explaining the buffs or debuffs I had on stuck, I couldn't find a way to fix this without clicking the mouse and moving it off the edge of the screen so it didn't interfere.

Overall still great and I liked it quite a bit!

All said if you like customization, cool abilities, and some interesting design space for how an rpg works I would rec.

It's impressive that this game is made by a solo developer, but for it to also have qualities almost on par with metroidvanias made by a dedicated team? It's no small feat and truly deserves the Overwhelmingly Positive rating on Steam.

What I find even more impressive is this game almost got the Ender Lilies/Ender Magnolia charm - a world slowly being consumed by evil, combined with music that inspires hope and strength to persevere. It's not as good as both of those to be fair, but it's close enough that I can see where the inspiration of this game comes from.

Then there's the combat aspect of the game. Rather than real-time combat like other metroidvanias usually employ, this game took a turn-based approach that is done really well. There are plenty of builds you can do in this game, and this game encourages you to experiment with different builds since stat allocations can be reset at any time. Because of how complex the game can get, the game is mouse-heavy since there are lots of effects that require you to mouse over them to understand what they do, know the source of the buff/debuffs since the same buff/debuff can stack as long as they come from different source, or just the ease of operation of pointing your mouse and click at the skills you want rather than having to use a dpad to navigate through various skills. Fret not, the game has controller support, but the dev made it clear when you start the game that this game is primarily designed for keyboard + mouse.

My only complaint about the game is that this game likes to give you tips on what you should do before a boss. Usually a player might be able to deduce the gimmick of a boss given the environment of the area (if I'm in an area that constantly applies poison to me, surely the boss of the area will have something related to poison), or in most other metroidvanias, you simply jump into the boss to find out their gimmick, die because your build completely gets countered by them, then change your build to counter the gimmick. This game outright tells you before a boss what their gimmick is so you prepare beforehand (for example, purge a debuff that makes you heal less because boss' damage scales with the debuff). In my time playing on Hard difficulty(the second hardest difficulty of the game), I've only died 3 times - once to an elite enemy I underestimated their strength, and twice to a same boss because I was underlevelled at the time. I had near-deaths during some boss fights, but I was only able to do so because I was made aware of what the boss do before I jumped into them.

Complaints aside, I would still recommend this game, because it's a well-made one. The game being cheap is a steal; I'd happily pay the usual $15 - $20 price tag of metroidvanias for this game.

Ambidextro is the definitive two-handed, two-character platforming challenge, and is a masterpiece by developer Majorariatto.

What we have here is an onslaught of meticulous, perfected level design that is extremely demanding at every turn, yet completely doable with practice.  I like difficult "rage games" but often find myself uninstalling them like a total puss far before they end.  Despite how much I suffered, cursed, and sweat from my extremities, I could find no unconquerable or unfair difficulty spike.  New mechanics came, went, and returned in a concise manner.  By the end of the game, I had actually gained significant skill at controlling two characters at once using both hands.

How artistic Ambidextro is, too.  An epic dynamic soundtrack continually heightens the suspense as you advance.  I love the theme and brief story that packages it up, and the pixel art is satisfying and clear with a nice CRT effect that's optional.  The sound effects are great, too.  It's all a home run with just as much care and attention as the core gameplay received.  I'm going to remember this game.

Pro tip, don't cross your legs while playing this.  Also, completing it will make you much better at pleasuring your partner with both hands.
there's that dopamine

Extremely rewarding and fun game, albeit somewhat stressful at parts, especially if played when not in the correct state of mind. Causes a weird feeling in some parts of your cortex and cerebellum. Completion feels like a true achievement.

Probably will not do another playthrough nor try to get the rest of the achievements, but strongly recommend to anyone who likes uniquely challenging and original games.

Good job, Majorariatto.

Oh my. Oh my goodness. This is a masterpiece. I don't say that lightly. This is a masterpiece. I thought Pureya was good, but somehow, Majorariatto outdoes themselves and delivers one of the best (the best?) single screen arcade platformers ever.

Ambidextro is a game whose core idea -shouldn't- work. It's a precision platformer where you have to control two characters simultaneously. On a timer. Let me repeat: on a timer. That's right: it's a speedrun game. In a game where you control two characters at once. Good lord. How did they pull this off.

Now, you'd assume the game would pull its punches with the level design to make it more manageable. Nope! Spikes galore, fireballs, vanishing platforms, the works. And you know what's crazy ... it DOES work. The game absolutely works. It takes a hell of a learning curve, and it is very, very challenging, but because respawning takes no time at all, you are able to practice over and over until you start surprising yourself. I can't imagine how obscenely difficult it is to design levels around this core concept, but holy smokes they actually did it. And not only do they do it, it's the game's biggest strength: the level design is -incredible-. Absolutely incredible. Some of the best I've ever seen. Brilliant stuff. New mechanics get introduced every couple levels, and everything is barely, just barely doable. This is finely crafted level design by absolute masters of their craft. Just inspired design.

That's not to say you won't be straining every ounce of gamer skill you have in your body to conform to the uniqueness of this game and what it asks of you. The skills that are tested here are not tested in any other game. Think about how awesome that is: in the over 60+ years the video game industry has been around, no one has made a game that's quite like this. You will develop strategies you never had to develop before, and build hand eye coordination you didn't know you were capable of. And as hard as the ambidexterity of the game is, you'll quickly learn that where your eyes are looking at is just as important. You have to have your other character in your peripheral vision at all times, or, do what I had more success with, I autopiloted with the one I'm looking at, and built a mental model in my head of the one I couldn't see and played my inputs out in my head. I was basically dual monitoring but with my mind's eye as my other "monitor". Insane. And it works. I beat the game that way. WHAT OTHER GAME DO YOU DO THAT FOR.

Another cool skill the game tests (that is again wholly unique to it) is building a route which optimizes consistency for your two characters. Some sections are simply too tough to be able to consistently control two characters, so you'll want to slow down, switch to that one hand for a bit, and then swap back to moving both at once. Other sections are tough, but you want to time going through it with another easy part with your other hand (let's say you have a hard jumping section with your left hand but an easy straightaway with your right; you can focus on the left hand and have your eyes on the left character while your other hand simply holds right). You will build routes and strategies to not have two tough sections for each hand overlap with one another; the levels are designed such that you never have to do anything too obscene with both hands at once if you are smart at avoiding it. So your eyes will be darting back and forth between both characters as each requires more or less attention than the other, while finding a consistent path that you can reliably perform. It feels wonderful, and is only possible because the designers clearly knew that these were the skills they were trying to build in the player, and designed everything around it.

The difficulty of this game is finely balanced too. Yes, this is an incredibly challenging game, which most people will not beat (and is therefore why it will likely not get the worldwide acclaim for the masterpiece it is; it takes a true sicko to beat this one). However, it does a great job of easing you into the controls, and you actually start mastering them with time (and 1000s of deaths, lol). It took over EIGHT-THOUSAND deaths for me to beat the game, but ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ I did it. And it felt so good. Oh my god that was satisfying. I don't know if other players are quite as bad at the game as I was, and I think having some musical / piano skills would have helped me a ton here (legacy skill from other games actually kind of hurts; my muscle memory was off in all the wrong places because I've played so many other platformers), but boy did I struggle. But even me, who had a hell of a time wrapping my head around the controls, was able to persevere. The game does a great job at teaching you things slowly, and having it all come together.

As with all good arcade games, the game is short and sweet on story, with just enough to have as a nice backdrop for the gameplay. What's there is well done, and ties very well into the gameplay premise. [spoiler]The final sequence especially is so freaking hype. ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ that was well done. I could have a whole game like that final level and be happy. What a cool way to end a banger of a game. A true final test.[/spoiler] The music and visuals are great too, and perfect for a game like this.

This is one of those games you really have to experience for yourself. And you know what, if it's too hard for you, there's no shame in playing the game co-op. It won't be the full experience, but I bet the game is still hella fun if played that way. For the brave few who dare to conquer a game like this though, you'll absolutely fall in love. Gem of a game. I've already beat every Majorariatto game, but this and Pureya (another banger) guarantees that I'll be checking out whatever they make, for the rest of time. There's nothing like it (literally; no other game I know has this mechanic. I can think of a few minigames like in Ni No Kuni but not an entire game), it's wholly unique, and it executes on its core concept brilliantly.

Bravo.

Very fascinating little game. Really makes you feel like your doing something completely new like you felt the first time you picked up a controller and also is such a great example of how human being just cannot do two things at the same time. Also this game single handedly proves the balmer peak is real lmao.

The whole game is very challenging, I said to myself "no ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ way this game expects this of me" and then I challenged myself and succeeded multiple times over. It is very well balanced and respects the player and I don't think introduces anything unfair but constantly challenges the player to improve 

There is a difficulty slider to give you more time but I really do recommend attempting to beat the game without messing with it, I will admit I relented on level 100 because it was just that hard and I wanted to move on (but I did come back to it and beat it fair and square under the time limit)

Really if you like games that are tough as nails that constantly challenges you and you want the serotonin hit for when you finally achieve greatness I can't recommend it enough.
game reminds me of when i used to try soloing coop levels in little big planet. very fun

Here's a neat game. It's kind of a puzzle platformer, but in order to do what it does, it's very serious on execution - there is a mastery expected of each level you go through. Expect to be challenged! What you get, though, is a game that teaches a very special kind of skill - the skill of how to go on autopilot with one hand/wizard while focusing on the other, and quickly switching your focus between the two. It really is the ambidexterity that the game's marketing is all about, and it's impressive to see this idea carried out so well.

If you're looking for something you've never seen before, this is certainly worth the eight dollar price. I haven't beaten the game, but the game does have an accessibility option which allows you to make the time limits in each level longer. This makes the execution required in each stage more forgiving, and I'll consider it if I'm in the mood to play again.

I've only played this game a short while, and it might stay that way.  I really only have capacity to do a few minutes at a time.  It is fairly challenging, and doesn't inspire a compulsion to complete level after level in the same sitting.

Within moments of first starting the game, I thought I would have to return it.  The game's default setting is basically impossible for me.  It's not a matter of practice, I actually cannot operate each character simultaneously and separately.  My vision cannot even take in sufficient information, let alone coordinate my hands.  I am playing on a handheld, I'm not sure if it's easier (or harder) to take in the entirety of the frame on a monitor.

In any case, I then used the accessibility menu to add 100% more time to each stage.  Now it was an engaging challenge, though as I said not a compulsive one.  I still finish most stages just under the buzzer.  I don't know who is actually completing them in the default time, or if the developer applied the Mario Maker test and completed them personally before publishing them.  If so, there is some genetic difference between me and those people who can do it.

With the added time, I am actually controlling the characters sequentially for the most part, rather than simultaneously.  But the time limit still forces my brain to process differently than it would for a normal platformer, as I need to assess each puzzle for a moment at a time before switching my attention to the other.  I think this is why it is easy to be satisfied with even a few minutes of play at a time.

Probably will not do another playthrough nor try to get the rest of the achievements, but strongly recommend to anyone who likes uniquely challenging and original games.

Good job, Majorariatto.

Oh my. Oh my goodness. This is a masterpiece. I don't say that lightly. This is a masterpiece. I thought Pureya was good, but somehow, Majorariatto outdoes themselves and delivers one of the best (the best?) single screen arcade platformers ever.

Ambidextro is a game whose core idea -shouldn't- work. It's a precision platformer where you have to control two characters simultaneously. On a timer. Let me repeat: on a timer. That's right: it's a speedrun game. In a game where you control two characters at once. Good lord. How did they pull this off.

Now, you'd assume the game would pull its punches with the level design to make it more manageable. Nope! Spikes galore, fireballs, vanishing platforms, the works. And you know what's crazy ... it DOES work. The game absolutely works. It takes a hell of a learning curve, and it is very, very challenging, but because respawning takes no time at all, you are able to practice over and over until you start surprising yourself. I can't imagine how obscenely difficult it is to design levels around this core concept, but holy smokes they actually did it. And not only do they do it, it's the game's biggest strength: the level design is -incredible-. Absolutely incredible. Some of the best I've ever seen. Brilliant stuff. New mechanics get introduced every couple levels, and everything is barely, just barely doable. This is finely crafted level design by absolute masters of their craft. Just inspired design.

That's not to say you won't be straining every ounce of gamer skill you have in your body to conform to the uniqueness of this game and what it asks of you. The skills that are tested here are not tested in any other game. Think about how awesome that is: in the over 60+ years the video game industry has been around, no one has made a game that's quite like this. You will develop strategies you never had to develop before, and build hand eye coordination you didn't know you were capable of. And as hard as the ambidexterity of the game is, you'll quickly learn that where your eyes are looking at is just as important. You have to have your other character in your peripheral vision at all times, or, do what I had more success with, I autopiloted with the one I'm looking at, and built a mental model in my head of the one I couldn't see and played my inputs out in my head. I was basically dual monitoring but with my mind's eye as my other "monitor". Insane. And it works. I beat the game that way. WHAT OTHER GAME DO YOU DO THAT FOR.

Another cool skill the game tests (that is again wholly unique to it) is building a route which optimizes consistency for your two characters. Some sections are simply too tough to be able to consistently control two characters, so you'll want to slow down, switch to that one hand for a bit, and then swap back to moving both at once. Other sections are tough, but you want to time going through it with another easy part with your other hand (let's say you have a hard jumping section with your left hand but an easy straightaway with your right; you can focus on the left hand and have your eyes on the left character while your other hand simply holds right). You will build routes and strategies to not have two tough sections for each hand overlap with one another; the levels are designed such that you never have to do anything too obscene with both hands at once if you are smart at avoiding it. So your eyes will be darting back and forth between both characters as each requires more or less attention than the other, while finding a consistent path that you can reliably perform. It feels wonderful, and is only possible because the designers clearly knew that these were the skills they were trying to build in the player, and designed everything around it.

The difficulty of this game is finely balanced too. Yes, this is an incredibly challenging game, which most people will not beat (and is therefore why it will likely not get the worldwide acclaim for the masterpiece it is; it takes a true sicko to beat this one). However, it does a great job of easing you into the controls, and you actually start mastering them with time (and 1000s of deaths, lol). It took over EIGHT-THOUSAND deaths for me to beat the game, but ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ I did it. And it felt so good. Oh my god that was satisfying. I don't know if other players are quite as bad at the game as I was, and I think having some musical / piano skills would have helped me a ton here (legacy skill from other games actually kind of hurts; my muscle memory was off in all the wrong places because I've played so many other platformers), but boy did I struggle. But even me, who had a hell of a time wrapping my head around the controls, was able to persevere. The game does a great job at teaching you things slowly, and having it all come together.

As with all good arcade games, the game is short and sweet on story, with just enough to have as a nice backdrop for the gameplay. What's there is well done, and ties very well into the gameplay premise. [spoiler]The final sequence especially is so freaking hype. ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ that was well done. I could have a whole game like that final level and be happy. What a cool way to end a banger of a game. A true final test.[/spoiler] The music and visuals are great too, and perfect for a game like this.

This is one of those games you really have to experience for yourself. And you know what, if it's too hard for you, there's no shame in playing the game co-op. It won't be the full experience, but I bet the game is still hella fun if played that way. For the brave few who dare to conquer a game like this though, you'll absolutely fall in love. Gem of a game. I've already beat every Majorariatto game, but this and Pureya (another banger) guarantees that I'll be checking out whatever they make, for the rest of time. There's nothing like it (literally; no other game I know has this mechanic. I can think of a few minigames like in Ni No Kuni but not an entire game), it's wholly unique, and it executes on its core concept brilliantly.

Bravo.

Very fascinating little game. Really makes you feel like your doing something completely new like you felt the first time you picked up a controller and also is such a great example of how human being just cannot do two things at the same time. Also this game single handedly proves the balmer peak is real lmao.

The whole game is very challenging, I said to myself "no ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ way this game expects this of me" and then I challenged myself and succeeded multiple times over. It is very well balanced and respects the player and I don't think introduces anything unfair but constantly challenges the player to improve 

There is a difficulty slider to give you more time but I really do recommend attempting to beat the game without messing with it, I will admit I relented on level 100 because it was just that hard and I wanted to move on (but I did come back to it and beat it fair and square under the time limit)

Really if you like games that are tough as nails that constantly challenges you and you want the serotonin hit for when you finally achieve greatness I can't recommend it enough.
game reminds me of when i used to try soloing coop levels in little big planet. very fun

Here's a neat game. It's kind of a puzzle platformer, but in order to do what it does, it's very serious on execution - there is a mastery expected of each level you go through. Expect to be challenged! What you get, though, is a game that teaches a very special kind of skill - the skill of how to go on autopilot with one hand/wizard while focusing on the other, and quickly switching your focus between the two. If you're looking for something you've never seen before, this is certainly worth the eight dollar price. This makes the execution required in each stage more forgiving, and I'll consider it if I'm in the mood to play again.

I've only played this game a short while, and it might stay that way.  I really only have capacity to do a few minutes at a time.  It is fairly challenging, and doesn't inspire a compulsion to complete level after level in the same sitting.

I am playing on a handheld, I'm not sure if it's easier (or harder) to take in the entirety of the frame on a monitor.

I still finish most stages just under the buzzer. I think this is why it is easy to be satisfied with even a few minutes of play at a time.

Alright. [i]Far Fishing[/i] is done and dusted. This is actually a pretty simple game overall, and yet it does feature a reasonably robust RPG system in the form of a deep skill tree along with some other features that spruce up the experience. Ultimately, your goal is to get to the end of the world and take a look over the edge. It's what every Flerfer wants, lol, and so if you are a Flerfer, this game comes about as close to indulging your fantasies as you might ever see. Take heed. :)

https://steamcommunity.com/sharedfiles/filedetails/?id=3628337904

Anyway, how this game functions is pretty simple as mentioned. You are a fishing boat captain, and you are trying to get to the edge of the Earth. Sadly, this can and will take many many years in-game, and so (presumably) when you pass, one of your descendants takes on the mantle and the quest. First off, this is node-based exploration. As you catch fish and earn cash from said fish, one thing you can buy is the ability to unlock the next node along the path to the Edge.

So your first goal is to catch as many fish as possible. Each new area features more valuable fish, but also potentially new hazards that can destroy your ship. Initially, you are just a weak fishing boat that only hold up to five fish at any moment. When your hold is full, that's the end of a turn, and one or more years pass. The first area costs a year to fish in. Each new area has a greater cost attached. You begin with only ten years to do whatever you can do.

This is where the RPG mechanics come into play. Some fish you can catch leave a trail of sparkles behind them. In addition to having a greater sale value, they also give you prestige points, which are this game's version of experience points. Prestige points can be spent on an expansive and expanding skill tree. As you buy an unlock, it typically unlocks new options on the skill tree. Some of these options include the ability to expand the number of years on your journey. As well as offering other buffs such as increased sales value for fish, faster fishing, greater fishing radius, etc...

https://steamcommunity.com/sharedfiles/filedetails/?id=3628338004

Additionally, as you progress further, you unlock captain's points, which give you the opportunity to select from three random pre-run buffs on offer. Which can also include similar things to what you will earn in your skill tree. You can unlock up to three Captain's points slots, and so you can potentially start each run with up to three buffs. These are only good for your current run, however, after which the options reset.

https://steamcommunity.com/sharedfiles/filedetails/?id=3628337924

Finally, during your exploration, as you sell fish, you earn cash. That cash can be spent on boat upgrades that last for your current run. They expand your fishing radius, your ship's holding capacity, your fishing speed, your hull's resistance to damage, offer extra time to fish in each round, and movement speed (which will come into play with the faster fishies).

With that out of the way, you select the unlocked node you wish to fish in, and then you get to fishing. You are on a timer, and each round ends when you either reach the end of the timer of your hold has been filled to capacity with fish. You will rinse and repeat until you either run out of years available OR you actually finish the game.

Initially, it can seem like you're caught in a bit of a pointless loop, but as you upgrade your skill tree (which is permanent from run to run, btw), your options will increase as will your ability to progress further along the linear node map. Eventually, you WILL reach the end.

Personally, I thought it was an enjoyable gameplay loop with a humorous ending that I appreciated. I enjoyed my time with this game. I spent $1.94 during this current Steam Winter Sale (at the time of this writing), but the $2.99 base price is more than fair for the experience you'll have with this game. It is probably a one and done game for most people, so just take note of that before buying. Anyway, good times were had, and I think it's worth a look.

Full review:
I ended up just zoning out playing this while having a bad pain day and 100%'d it in around 3hours. Game play is some of the simpleist you could ask for, no silly gimmicks to try and stand out or somthing which i appreciated. 
Only problem i had which is one a lot of games like this have is the snowball effect gets out of control so fast. You spend half you play timestuck in the early game (first 1 to 3 levels) actively playing then you hit that point that helps you make big progress and then before you're even at the final level you have maxed out everything and feel like you're having to slog through the last 20mins to finish. That said i still had a good bit of fun playing, each of the levels having their own gimmick and differnt visual style was appreciated and nice, never felt like i was just playing the same level again with bigger fish or faster fish. Hated that fog level tho as i couldnt see crap i got through that level by blind luck lol all in all 4/5 hope to see them make other games like this
