An exceptionally solid and well polished short incremental experience. It hits just about every mark with flying, or I guess I should say sailing colors. It doesn't overstay its welcome and is well worth the price, I wouldn't say its [b] perfect [/b] but it is basically the picture of what a short incremental game should look like.

To start off, there is pretty much no idling here, its basically 95% active, with gameplay that is quite satisfying and rewards skill to a reasonable degree. It might be slightly frustrating in a few spots towards the start, but as long as you don't play overly greedy you will be fine. The basic premise is very simple, keep fish within a certain radius of your boat to catch them. You won't find any additional weapons or powerups to spice things up, but you won't really need them. The game throws in enough novelty and different gimmicks in each level to keep things perfectly fresh within its runtime, and this particular style of gameplay is very well suited to making the basic stat upgrades satisfying and engaging.

Perhaps the biggest strength of this game (and it is full of strengths) is its very friendly prestige system and skill-tree. Each run gives you a certain number of years before you are forced to prestige, helping mitigate some of the often nebulous decision making around when to prestige that can easily get people trapped in a sunk cost fallacy. Additionally, the skill-tree, despite being wide open with many attractive options is very kind in that every path leads to upgrades to your prestige point economy. These are crucial upgrades that can sometimes be missed in other, less forgiving skill-trees. These features make the game very beginner friendly to newcomers getting into the short incremental genre. However, this isn't to say that the game [b] completely [/b] holds your hand every step of the way, prior experience and intuition with the genre will still be rewarded and there are many micro and macro decisions to be made.

Then the other stuff is about as what you would expect for a game like this, all the fanfare such as simple yet still charming pixel graphics, alongside pleasant and appropriate music. This is probably the weakest part of the game, but that isn't saying too much considering its overall sterling quality.

All in all, this one is just great! If you are a long (or short)-time fan of the genre I'd say that you are almost guaranteed to enjoy it. There are few things better than a simple job executed very well, and this one is pretty damn close to flawless. It's beginner friendly nature makes it great for easing people into the genre too. In an ever more competitive genre, this game has more than earned its place at the top!

A really fun and interesting game about higher dimensions. Aside from being impressive work on a technical level, it actually manages to feel not just like an interesting tech demo but like a complete and genuinely fun game - wrapping your head around 4D is of course a challenge, but the game  gives you a lot of tools to help you navigate and orient yourself. Some of the levels are hard, but the game feels at no point intentionally confusing, and instead like it is actually trying to help you understand the challenges that the levels represent. With the abundance of tools it gives you the first few levels can even feel 3-dimensional almost, but the game introduces elements that force you to actually interact with higher dimensions in nontrivial ways over time, to the point that by the time you have beaten the last world you can likely quite intuitively navigate 4D levels.

The music is also great, and the game is respectful of your time - 100% completion takes only around 20 hours, so I can recommend this even to those without a lot of time to spare. My only disappointment with the game is that it lacks an online multiplayer mode, but it has a local one at least.

I went into Cats in China thinking, what’s the big deal? It’s just about finding cats. How hard could that be?

The beginning only reinforced that confidence. Spotting the first 90 percent of the cats felt effortless, almost relaxing. I genuinely thought I had the game figured out. That was my mistake.

Those last few cats completely changed the experience. Suddenly I was squinting at the screen, putting on my specs, scanning every corner with a level of focus I did not expect from such a calm looking game. And it was only when I was down to the very last cat that I realized my folly. I had badly underestimated this game.

I will shamelessly admit it. I had to search online to find that final cat.

With the Winter Sale going on, I picked this game up for very cheap, and honestly, it felt like stealing. What looks simple at first slowly turns into a clever test of patience, observation, and attention to detail.

If you enjoy cozy games that quietly challenge you and then humble you, I highly recommend buying this one.

It Does Exactly What It Says On The Tin

The "Cats Hidden in..." series is perhaps the most literal set of games on Steam. You pick a location, you get a sprawling, hand-drawn illustration, and you find the cats. There are no grand plot twists or complex mechanics here—just you, a magnifying glass, and a lot of hidden felines.

The Experience:

Short but Sweet: These games are incredibly brief. You can usually 100% a single location in under an hour (or even 30 minutes if you’re quick). It’s the definition of a "micro-game."

Interactive Touches: A nice highlight is that the world isn't entirely static. There are various interactive items scattered around the map that react when clicked, which gives the scenes a bit more life and personality than a standard hidden object photo.

Steam Extras: If you’re into the "metagame" of Steam, the trading cards for this series are actually quite nice. They’re a great bonus if you enjoy customizing your profile or collecting badges.

The Verdict:
It's hard to give this a definitive "must-buy" or a "hard pass." It really depends on what you're looking for. If you want a deep, long-lasting puzzle game, this isn't it. But if you want a very chill, low-stakes way to kill 20 minutes while looking at cute art, it hits the mark perfectly.

It’s an enjoyable, "one-and-done" casual experience. I wouldn't go out of my way to recommend it as a masterpiece, but I certainly didn't regret the time spent playing it.

Recommended for: Casual players, cat lovers, and profile collectors.

AWESOME. Berserk system kicks ass. Boost system kicks ass, Scoring kicks ass. Everything about this game kicks ass. There's a lot of nice extras too like the stage select, boss practice, retro mode, galleries, etc.

This is a remake of an old PS1 Shmup, I believe it was done by the same team who created the original. 

I prefer this style of STG over the Bullet Hell stuff personally. The vibes, art, music is all excellent. Plays great on the Steam Deck, as you’d expect. Would have never heard of this if not for Mark MSX’s channel The Electric Underground. His channel really converted me from just liking arcade Beatemups to enjoying Shmups as well. 

The game has an unlock menu (you can even unlock the original PS1 version of the game) and this actually encourages/incentivizes  newcomers to keep trying and learning the game. Shmups have to be replayed over and over and I don’t think some casual people understand that at first. Which is probably why they look at a 30m-1hr experience as not being worth it for $20 but they have to understand that you are supposed to keep playing until you get your 1 credit clear.

Pros:
- accessible to most people (I cleared the game on easy difficulty on the 2nd try)
- 20+ mins each successful run on easy difficulty, not sure about higher difficulty. Level selection is available for people who want even shorter runs.
- there are unlockables to go for (increasing continues to use in runs, pictures, music etc.)
- berserk system to get higher scores
- good graphics
- decent pricing, I got it at a -30% sale

Cons:
- no auto fire in settings so I have to keep holding on the fire button
- unskippable small scenes between levels. It gets old very fast after playing for a few runs
- no co-op, single player only
- compared to other shoot em ups, there is only 2 modes of shooting, wide and focused. No homing, laser etc.

Conclusion:
Not a bad game, but not a game that I can keep playing consecutively for long hours in one sitting. Content wise can feel a bit lacking.

Devil Blade Reboot is an awesome remake of the classic 90s freeware shmup Devil Blade, and a great game for all skill levels. This game has some of the greatest 2D graphics I have ever seen, vibrant saturated colors, a great mix of detailed sprites with a chunky look, pseudo-3D effects for enemies and projectiles entering the screen perpendicularly to your ship, lots of layered effects to simulate lighting, warp speed and smoke effects (especially on the first, third and final stage), it's just a treat to behold and the presentation never overstays its welcome or bores the player in between gameplay. The music is a faithful remake of the original's, but with studio polish and nice drums and a little slap bass guitar. All of the bosses have synthesized voice lines which I enjoy, especially for Lloyd Gunner and the Zork Master. 
The gameplay is quite fun, you're rewarded with a Berserk multiplier for getting close to the enemies when you're shooting them down, creating a fun risk and reward element. You can up the stakes further by using your Bombs as Boosts, which can ratchet your multiplier far higher than normal, but at the cost of the bullet speed and densities increasing, along with a slight increase in enemy HP. You cash in your multiplier at the end of the stage if you survive, and it resets for the boss, where you can choose again to bomb or boost. 
Easy is properly easy, you autobomb when hit until you only have your shield and hull left. The Normal difficulty is quite fair and allows you to reset your shields when bombing. The nastiest attacks from enemies in this game are telegraphed, so it never really feels unfair. Lasers will show as dim lines before they're harmful, when they'll flash and glow. Bigger fast projectiles will have a straight line drawn showing the trajectory the projectile will soon take. Hard difficulty will keep my attention for a while, it mostly plays like Normal if you were boosting the entire time, and gets even harder with boost. There seems to be extra enemies here and there as well, but the layout is mostly the same. You can only restore shields with the shield pickups. Inferno is totally nuts, you have to score well or you die. 
You can play through the stages as a Boss Rush, or individually in the Stage Select mode which also has a boss only setting. You get in-game coins in between stages and at the end of successful runs, and these can be used in the unlock shop. There's a lot of extra menu artwork, concept art, sound test items in the shop, and you can buy continues here if you wish. 
Included as well is the original Devil Blade in all it's glory, and it's still quite fun and a testament to how much they were able to expand this remake while keeping it faithful to the original. This game is tight, the price is right and even if you have no experience with shmups, I think you'll really enjoy it.

[h1]This Soundtrack Gets Laid More Than You Do[/h1]
Insanely fun SHMUP whose origins are cute and humble. What was originally a fan made shooter game on the PS1 engine back in the 90s has been remixed multiple times, now with this new release which is a blockbuster shooting game. 

It has a scoring system that is more on the simpler side, mostly getting multipliers from being close to targets when you kill them. Kill enough enemies that are close to you fast enough, and you build up a Berserk meter that supercharges your score. This may bore pro players, but for the majority of us, this is a good scoring system and an insanely fun shooter. Awesome soundtrack, wicked explosions, screen filling bosses, perfect difficulty, and a decent unlock and progression system that gives you some fun extra content to purchase and unlock (and of course you can buy extra credits if you're like me and suck at these games).

If you're looking for a beginner shmup, this is a great place to start!

Devil Blade Reboot is an awesome remake of the classic 90s freeware shmup Devil Blade, and a great game for all skill levels. This game has some of the greatest 2D graphics I have ever seen, vibrant saturated colors, a great mix of detailed sprites with a chunky look, pseudo-3D effects for enemies and projectiles entering the screen perpendicularly to your ship, lots of layered effects to simulate lighting, warp speed and smoke effects (especially on the first, third and final stage), it's just a treat to behold and the presentation never overstays its welcome or bores the player in between gameplay. The music is a faithful remake of the original's, but with studio polish and nice drums and a little slap bass guitar. All of the bosses have synthesized voice lines which I enjoy, especially for Lloyd Gunner and the Zork Master. 
The gameplay is quite fun, you're rewarded with a Berserk multiplier for getting close to the enemies when you're shooting them down, creating a fun risk and reward element. You can up the stakes further by using your Bombs as Boosts, which can ratchet your multiplier far higher than normal, but at the cost of the bullet speed and densities increasing, along with a slight increase in enemy HP. You cash in your multiplier at the end of the stage if you survive, and it resets for the boss, where you can choose again to bomb or boost. 
Easy is properly easy, you autobomb when hit until you only have your shield and hull left. The Normal difficulty is quite fair and allows you to reset your shields when bombing. The nastiest attacks from enemies in this game are telegraphed, so it never really feels unfair. Lasers will show as dim lines before they're harmful, when they'll flash and glow. Bigger fast projectiles will have a straight line drawn showing the trajectory the projectile will soon take. Hard difficulty will keep my attention for a while, it mostly plays like Normal if you were boosting the entire time, and gets even harder with boost. There seems to be extra enemies here and there as well, but the layout is mostly the same. You can only restore shields with the shield pickups. Inferno is totally nuts, you have to score well or you die. 
You can play through the stages as a Boss Rush, or individually in the Stage Select mode which also has a boss only setting. You get in-game coins in between stages and at the end of successful runs, and these can be used in the unlock shop. There's a lot of extra menu artwork, concept art, sound test items in the shop, and you can buy continues here if you wish. 
Included as well is the original Devil Blade in all it's glory, and it's still quite fun and a testament to how much they were able to expand this remake while keeping it faithful to the original. This game is tight, the price is right and even if you have no experience with shmups, I think you'll really enjoy it.

[h1]This Soundtrack Gets Laid More Than You Do[/h1]
Insanely fun SHMUP whose origins are cute and humble. What was originally a fan made shooter game on the PS1 engine back in the 90s has been remixed multiple times, now with this new release which is a blockbuster shooting game. 

It has a scoring system that is more on the simpler side, mostly getting multipliers from being close to targets when you kill them. AWESOME. Berserk system kicks ass. Boost system kicks ass, Scoring kicks ass. Everything about this game kicks ass. There's a lot of nice extras too like the stage select, boss practice, retro mode, galleries, etc.

This is a remake of an old PS1 Shmup, I believe it was done by the same team who created the original. 

I prefer this style of STG over the Bullet Hell stuff personally. The vibes, art, music is all excellent. Plays great on the Steam Deck, as you’d expect. Would have never heard of this if not for Mark MSX’s channel The Electric Underground. His channel really converted me from just liking arcade Beatemups to enjoying Shmups as well. 

The game has an unlock menu (you can even unlock the original PS1 version of the game) and this actually encourages/incentivizes  newcomers to keep trying and learning the game. Shmups have to be replayed over and over and I don’t think some casual people understand that at first. Which is probably why they look at a 30m-1hr experience as not being worth it for $20 but they have to understand that you are supposed to keep playing until you get your 1 credit clear.

Pros:
- accessible to most people (I cleared the game on easy difficulty on the 2nd try)
- 20+ mins each successful run on easy difficulty, not sure about higher difficulty. Level selection is available for people who want even shorter runs.
- there are unlockables to go for (increasing continues to use in runs, pictures, music etc.)
- berserk system to get higher scores
- good graphics
- decent pricing, I got it at a -30% sale

Cons:
- no auto fire in settings so I have to keep holding on the fire button
- unskippable small scenes between levels. It gets old very fast after playing for a few runs
- no co-op, single player only
- compared to other shoot em ups, there is only 2 modes of shooting, wide and focused. No homing, laser etc.

Conclusion:
Not a bad game, but not a game that I can keep playing consecutively for long hours in one sitting. Content wise can feel a bit lacking.
Genuinely a fun sh'mup with an iconic soundtrack, I wholly recommend it whether you're a scoring fanatic or you just want something to play for a while.
Feels awesome, and has incredible spritework. I was blown away by the visuals tbh. The TTS voices are pretty bad, but that's the main negative that I can recall - everything else is great.

It Does Exactly What It Says On The Tin

The "Cats Hidden in..." series is perhaps the most literal set of games on Steam. You pick a location, you get a sprawling, hand-drawn illustration, and you find the cats. There are no grand plot twists or complex mechanics here—just you, a magnifying glass, and a lot of hidden felines.

The Experience:

Short but Sweet: These games are incredibly brief. You can usually 100% a single location in under an hour (or even 30 minutes if you’re quick). It’s the definition of a "micro-game."

Interactive Touches: A nice highlight is that the world isn't entirely static. There are various interactive items scattered around the map that react when clicked, which gives the scenes a bit more life and personality than a standard hidden object photo.

Steam Extras: If you’re into the "metagame" of Steam, the trading cards for this series are actually quite nice. They’re a great bonus if you enjoy customizing your profile or collecting badges.

The Verdict:
It's hard to give this a definitive "must-buy" or a "hard pass." It really depends on what you're looking for. If you want a deep, long-lasting puzzle game, this isn't it. But if you want a very chill, low-stakes way to kill 20 minutes while looking at cute art, it hits the mark perfectly.

It’s an enjoyable, "one-and-done" casual experience. I wouldn't go out of my way to recommend it as a masterpiece, but I certainly didn't regret the time spent playing it.

Recommended for: Casual players, cat lovers, and profile collectors.

Fairly challenging as far as these games go.

There are technically 3 scenes to complete.
- 1 very large scene with 100 cats to find, and 29 "interactable" items. 1 achievement per cat, for 100 achievements.
- Same scene in colour with same locations for the cats & interactables.
- Smaller bonus scene, accessed by finding all 29 "interactables" items. Progress of interactables continues through both the plain & coloured scene.

- The cats mostly look pretty much like cats.
- The art style for the cats is the same as the background art style, so they blend in well.
- The bonus level has an entirely different art style, but the cats still manage to blend in. Wonderfully done.
- Zoom is decent, and very much needed.
- Scene is "visually busy" and kind of made me a bit dizzy to be honest. The coloured version was much easier on my eyes, although I found it more difficult to find the cats.

Aliya: Timelink is not for everyone. I think its narrative targets a very particular demographic. Neither is it a perfect experience. There is a lot of waiting due to the real-time sync mechanic, the translations have their issues, and it is very simplistic in nature.

For some people, those qualities will make this a frustrating or boring experience that they cannot engage with. Luckily, I was not one of these people, so the story hit me like a truck. I very nearly cried while playing through the first ending of this game and I can't remember the last time an interactive story made me feel that way.

At risk of spoiling the game for anyone who wants to play it, I won't go into heavy detail about why it left such an impact. I can only say that I did not expect to care. Aliya: Timelink was a game I only discovered because of the Steam Points Shop, and I only bought it because it was cheap and looked vaguely interesting. My expectations were surpassed in every way.
Very unique beautiful visual novel - 100% recommend

One of the most innovative and immersive experiences to date, despite this being such an incredibly simple game. The real-time sync with Earth and wherever Aliya finds herself is incredibly charming, and I hope to see more like it in future games - by the same developer or others. I would give this game a Mixed if I could. I'll give this a thumb up since it's certainly worth 3 dollars, but not much more than that.

I completed Ending 1 (the first part of the game, honestly) before writing up this review. I probably won't be advancing to the next stage for awhile due to the main gimmick of the game. It's kind of a headache.

The big thing about this game is that it's real time. It takes time for the main character (Aliya) to do things. On the other hand, the game continues even when it's shut off. This wouldn't be too bad on it's own ("Why did you leave me for a month?"), but there's one big problem that a bunch of you with limited time will hate: The oxygen mechanic.

Due to some defect in the spaceship Aliya is in, you and only you can operate the oxygen generator in the ship. So, if you drop the game for awhile, Aliya will suffocate and die. The first playthrough seems to be lenient in this regard; I went for sleep for half a day and the oxygen went down by about a quarter, but I hear the time crunch is much more punishing when the second playthrough begins.

Oh, and that's not all! Since Aliya can take 10-15 minutes to do certain tasks, you could be sitting there for a very considerable amount of time doing nothing at all. And, there's no way to know how long you'll be doing nothing, either. So, the only way to really play this game is to run it on the side while you play something else in the meantime.

But there is still more! Sometimes certain events pop up where Aliya tries to talk to you, but since you're busy doing something else, you're not around to chat. So then she goes "Ah whatever" and the event is cancelled. That's kind of annoying.

And this might be a low blow, but my suspension of disbelief is a bit disrupted about the whole "1000 years in the future" thing. Since I am a real person, and Aliya is an anime person, there's a certain amount of unspoken disconnect. Maybe future people are nocturnal like owls.

All that being said, the story is okayish. This game tries hard to pull on the player's heartstrings. I don't really feel one way or another about games that try to do that, so most of the story fell flat for me. If you're someone who likes emotional stories, you might enjoy it more than me. The space stuff and art are cool though, which is why I'm just barely giving this a positive rating.

I wish the player had access to a map to see the layout of the ship and where everyone is. The only thing you can really do is play with the heart monitor, so why can't I have a pretty map?

So yeah. The game is kinda whatever, but decent for 3 dollars.

Can't wait for the next one to make me feel again, I enjoyed this game experience even if it was short.

Personally, I love this game, it is simple, it is emotional and although it has small details (Clarification: did not affect the gaming experience), it is certainly a game worth trying. 

Although it is not an extensive game, its main story develops in such a way that the player really connects with the character. Also, although the dialogues are pre-defined, as the time in the game passes in real time, it gives the feeling of having a real conversation which is a detail that makes this game unique. 

Regarding the story, just as there are dialogues and options that do not alter the course of the story, there are other key points that determine the end to be obtained. When getting the different endings and dialogs there is also a "Skip time" option so it does not become tedious for those looking to find all the existing details.

The music within the game is simply beautiful, very emotional. (I liked that much I even bought the art bundle)

game_name: Star Fetchers : Escape from Pork Belly
review_text: Hopecore straight into my veins

I have played every game mode and gotten every collectable. I have dedicated 63 hours of my life as of writing to this game, so I will be writing a thorough review. But TL;DR, overall, this is a fun game to fiddle with and activate some neurons, with some genuinely challenging and interesting puzzles and game modes, a solid 7/10. If you enjoy logic puzzles, definitely give the demo a try.

The game starts off pretty easy and ramps up steadily in challenge. If you're new to logic puzzles, you'll be able to learn strategies in the early dungeons that will be very helpful in later dungeons. There is a timer, but it's pretty much a nonfactor as you upgrade your character, pick up consumables that refill the guts timer, and reset the timer by buying a meal in town every 10 levels. I disagree with those who say some game modes are unfair or don't make sense. It does take some time to adjust your approach for some of them, but they are all logical and solvable once you understand it. I honestly enjoyed the more difficult game modes, Doubt is actually my favorite. There are some helpful features that allow you to label chests as you solve the puzzle and open all the chests you think are correct. It's a very convenient feature, though I do wish the triangle for "uncertain" was in every game mode and not just a few.

The logic puzzles are overall pretty good, but there are some cons due to the procedurally generated aspect. Some puzzles are way too easy and some puzzles are way too ambiguous. Many times I've lost a run due to ambiguity in the puzzle, where there is technically more than one "valid" answer and you don't really know which one is the actual puzzle solution. Sure, you should be able to use your Logic Sense to detect when a puzzle is ambiguous, but it's still very frustrating to have runs end on a solution that was technically correct, but not THE solution. There are items that can help you get around this issue and the game straight up tells you that sometimes this happens, but when you're trying to solve puzzles as fast as possible, you don't usually have time to check every possibility and it's easy to miss potential ambiguities. I also don't really appreciate the jumpscare death screen when you open a mimic chest, but maybe that's just a personal preference. Another rather small nitpick, the game says that with higher difficulties, the variety of statements to deduce increases, and this is true. However, when chests talk about the contents of other chests, they only refer to gold and don't include gear or items. This isn't a big deal and maybe introducing those factors would make it way too hard, but it was something that came to mind. The points system is also somewhat of a mystery. I think it's unfair that time spent in menus or in town still count against our score even if it doesn't deplete the guts timer, and it also is frustrating that it you stop in the middle of a dungeon and then pick it up later, you get a penalty to your score for time being "unable to be determined". I don't like being penalized for taking breaks and attending to other things outside the game, though maybe there might be a programming reason for this, I don't know.

The "rpg/combat" aspect isn't that exactly, I'd honestly say it's closer to resource management. Enemy encounters are essentially gates that you can only pass if you're strong enough (opened enough chests for resources). There's a little juggling to do due to item durability, and I've had runs end where I've passed all the puzzles but wasn't strong enough to get past a battle, which can be frustrating. But I wouldn't really say it's as frustrating as losing to an ambiguous puzzle and it doesn't happen too often. Despite this not really being an "rpg", there actually is a wide variety of items to collect. Each weapon and armor looks unique, different weapon types have different attack animations and sound effects, and I really appreciate that level of detail. You can equip several different kinds of Gun in this game. I do wish there was more variety in the enemy character designs, though it doesn't bother me too much as combat is not really the true focus of the game.

There are reoccurring NPCs that also all have unique sprites. They offer all sorts of services, there's of course a shopkeeper and an innkeeper, but also a bunch of others as well, including a masseuse, a special shopkeeper, and others. Unfortunately, I feel like it's not really worth interacting with two common ones, the lottery ticket guy and the doctor guy, where you gamble money or health for the chance to get more. The random chance to lose a big chunk of health or money is usually not worth the risk if you do have enough money or health to gamble on their services, and I wish there'd be more incentive to engage with them. Though, I do like the personalities of these NPCs for the most part, hearing the doctor say "making medicine's my hobby" after nearly killing you is kind of funny, and rock collector guy is my favorite. The game in general has a sense of humor that I appreciate, the trophy descriptions are charming. There is a little story in this game, it's not super elaborate but it was still nice to have. The sprites in general are cute, and the more detailed portraits are very nice, if rarely seen. The music is serviceable and the themes for home and the shop are maybe the nicest tracks, though the more quiet and solemn tracks are not my favorite as they're a bit too "empty" sounding. The final boss theme is great but I find that I don't really get to listen to it properly as the boss battle is usually over pretty quickly, whether you win or lose. There is an unlockable "sound test" that lets you play the music tracks of the game, which I like.

The game is a little rough around the edges, but I still really enjoyed it, and I found the concept of a mimic logic dungeon really fun and creative. Despite being frustrated at times, I still kept coming back for one more run. I have been trapped in the logic dimension for 63 hours. Each run is 10-30 min long. I recommend this game.
Cool mix of puzzle and RPG gameplay elements!
Good puzzles.

This is a fun puzzle solver, with more complicated puzzles that twisted my mind a bit.  And a little combat thrown in for fun.  Don't waste your time reading more reviews, play the demo, and if you like it, get the real thing.

Given how much fun I have with this game I really should've reviewed it a long time ago lol. Great, mind-bending, super fun to just play a few rounds every so often. i only wish the tools were slightly better, what's there is useful but i have pulled out pen-and-paper many times lol

There are items that can help you get around this issue and the game straight up tells you that sometimes this happens, but when you're trying to solve puzzles as fast as possible, you don't usually have time to check every possibility and it's easy to miss potential ambiguities. I also don't really appreciate the jumpscare death screen when you open a mimic chest, but maybe that's just a personal preference. Another rather small nitpick, the game says that with higher difficulties, the variety of statements to deduce increases, and this is true. However, when chests talk about the contents of other chests, they only refer to gold and don't include gear or items. This isn't a big deal and maybe introducing those factors would make it way too hard, but it was something that came to mind. The points system is also somewhat of a mystery. I think it's unfair that time spent in menus or in town still count against our score even if it doesn't deplete the guts timer, and it also is frustrating that it you stop in the middle of a dungeon and then pick it up later, you get a penalty to your score for time being "unable to be determined". I don't like being penalized for taking breaks and attending to other things outside the game, though maybe there might be a programming reason for this, I don't know.

The "rpg/combat" aspect isn't that exactly, I'd honestly say it's closer to resource management. Enemy encounters are essentially gates that you can only pass if you're strong enough (opened enough chests for resources). There's a little juggling to do due to item durability, and I've had runs end where I've passed all the puzzles but wasn't strong enough to get past a battle, which can be frustrating. But I wouldn't really say it's as frustrating as losing to an ambiguous puzzle and it doesn't happen too often. Despite this not really being an "rpg", there actually is a wide variety of items to collect. Each weapon and armor looks unique, different weapon types have different attack animations and sound effects, and I really appreciate that level of detail. You can equip several different kinds of Gun in this game. I do wish there was more variety in the enemy character designs, though it doesn't bother me too much as combat is not really the true focus of the game.

There are reoccurring NPCs that also all have unique sprites. They offer all sorts of services, there's of course a shopkeeper and an innkeeper, but also a bunch of others as well, including a masseuse, a special shopkeeper, and others. Unfortunately, I feel like it's not really worth interacting with two common ones, the lottery ticket guy and the doctor guy, where you gamble money or health for the chance to get more. The random chance to lose a big chunk of health or money is usually not worth the risk if you do have enough money or health to gamble on their services, and I wish there'd be more incentive to engage with them. Though, I do like the personalities of these NPCs for the most part, hearing the doctor say "making medicine's my hobby" after nearly killing you is kind of funny, and rock collector guy is my favorite. The game in general has a sense of humor that I appreciate, the trophy descriptions are charming. There is a little story in this game, it's not super elaborate but it was still nice to have. The sprites in general are cute, and the more detailed portraits are very nice, if rarely seen. The music is serviceable and the themes for home and the shop are maybe the nicest tracks, though the more quiet and solemn tracks are not my favorite as they're a bit too "empty" sounding. The final boss theme is great but I find that I don't really get to listen to it properly as the boss battle is usually over pretty quickly, whether you win or lose. There is an unlockable "sound test" that lets you play the music tracks of the game, which I like.

The game is a little rough around the edges, but I still really enjoyed it, and I found the concept of a mimic logic dungeon really fun and creative. Despite being frustrated at times, I still kept coming back for one more run. I have been trapped in the logic dimension for 63 hours. Each run is 10-30 min long. I recommend this game.

I also don't really appreciate the jumpscare death screen when you open a mimic chest, but maybe that's just a personal preference. Another rather small nitpick, the game says that with higher difficulties, the variety of statements to deduce increases, and this is true. However, when chests talk about the contents of other chests, they only refer to gold and don't include gear or items. This isn't a big deal and maybe introducing those factors would make it way too hard, but it was something that came to mind. The points system is also somewhat of a mystery. I think it's unfair that time spent in menus or in town still count against our score even if it doesn't deplete the guts timer, and it also is frustrating that it you stop in the middle of a dungeon and then pick it up later, you get a penalty to your score for time being "unable to be determined". I don't like being penalized for taking breaks and attending to other things outside the game, though maybe there might be a programming reason for this, I don't know.

The "rpg/combat" aspect isn't that exactly, I'd honestly say it's closer to resource management. Enemy encounters are essentially gates that you can only pass if you're strong enough (opened enough chests for resources). There's a little juggling to do due to item durability, and I've had runs end where I've passed all the puzzles but wasn't strong enough to get past a battle, which can be frustrating. But I wouldn't really say it's as frustrating as losing to an ambiguous puzzle and it doesn't happen too often. Despite this not really being an "rpg", there actually is a wide variety of items to collect. Each weapon and armor looks unique, different weapon types have different attack animations and sound effects, and I really appreciate that level of detail. You can equip several different kinds of Gun in this game. I do wish there was more variety in the enemy character designs, though it doesn't bother me too much as combat is not really the true focus of the game.

There are reoccurring NPCs that also all have unique sprites. They offer all sorts of services, there's of course a shopkeeper and an innkeeper, but also a bunch of others as well, including a masseuse, a special shopkeeper, and others. Unfortunately, I feel like it's not really worth interacting with two common ones, the lottery ticket guy and the doctor guy, where you gamble money or health for the chance to get more. The random chance to lose a big chunk of health or money is usually not worth the risk if you do have enough money or health to gamble on their services, and I wish there'd be more incentive to engage with them. Though, I do like the personalities of these NPCs for the most part, hearing the doctor say "making medicine's my hobby" after nearly killing you is kind of funny, and rock collector guy is my favorite. The game in general has a sense of humor that I appreciate, the trophy descriptions are charming. There is a little story in this game, it's not super elaborate but it was still nice to have. The sprites in general are cute, and the more detailed portraits are very nice, if rarely seen. The music is serviceable and the themes for home and the shop are maybe the nicest tracks, though the more quiet and solemn tracks are not my favorite as they're a bit too "empty" sounding. The final boss theme is great but I find that I don't really get to listen to it properly as the boss battle is usually over pretty quickly, whether you win or lose. There is an unlockable "sound test" that lets you play the music tracks of the game, which I like.

The game is a little rough around the edges, but I still really enjoyed it, and I found the concept of a mimic logic dungeon really fun and creative. Despite being frustrated at times, I still kept coming back for one more run. I have been trapped in the logic dimension for 63 hours. Each run is 10-30 min long. I recommend this game.

[h2]Role Model[/h2]

[b][i]Mullet Madjack's world paints a bleak, consumerist future—but the one the game itself paints for retro shooters is that much brighter.[/i][/b]

https://steamcommunity.com/sharedfiles/filedetails/?id=3456369068
[hr][/hr]
Picture this, if you will: somewhere, on the Internet, New Blood CEO Dave Oshry makes a funny joke, YouTuber Civvie11 finally uploads his latest video, and voice actor Gianni Matragrano has found more work. That’s right—a new boomer shooter has been released.

There was definitely a chill down my spine as I was getting into Mullet Madjack, the debut title from developer Hammer95, as its marketing, at least on a surface level, made the game seem like “just another” boomshoot described above, minus a soundtrack from Andrew Hulshult. The retro-cyberpunk paint job that’s been splattered across its every surface, the word “Mullet” not just making up half of the title but it being in all caps, the sweet, sexy eurobeat that plays as soon as you hit the main menu—I was convinced I was walking into yet another “Doom clone” with its own overreliance on some kind of aesthetic gimmick.

Then I hit play and watched the intro cutscene. By the end of my first session, I realised I couldn’t have been more wrong. Six hours later, I cleared a few secondary modes and finished my second playthrough of the story. I sat there and lamented that there wasn’t more I could sink my teeth into.

[h3]The Cure to Male Loneliness[/h3]
If you’re like me and late to the party, you’re in luck, because Mullet Madjack’s soda-fueled premise is easy to relate to: the year’s 2095, Robillionaires have taken over the world, and humans have become addicted to dopamine. They fuel this addiction by a) mass consumerism, b) killing said Robillionaires, and c) watching people kill said Robillionaires. Haven’t you ever?  

You get to experience this messed-up future as “moderator” Jack Banhammer, the kind of guy who gets his fix through option b), downing one can of soda after another in the process, especially now that a group of ‘em has kidnapped the unfathomably popular Influencer Princess. Jack himself isn’t a stranger to content creation, as how you’ll be getting a lil’ lick of that succulent dopamine is through a camera in his sunglasses, presented as a livestream by PEACECORP, the benevolent conglomerate running the show.

[quote]For some expanded thoughts on [i]Mullet Madjack[/i] and how it compares to your average boomshoot, feel free to check out the uncut version of this article on the [b][url=https://summitreviews.biz/]Summit Reviews Website[/url][/b].[/quote]

See, like a boomer shooter Mullet Madjack does look, but like one it does not play. Of course, a certain “feel” can’t be escaped, but you won’t find yourself blasting around arenas or steadily revealing a maze-like map trying to find the next coloured key. No, the dominant way you’ll be experiencing these levels—and the word ‘level’ is used quite literally—is by moving forward, if you want to live, that is.

When the game tells you humans are addicted to dopamine, it means it. On the left side of your screen is a handy-dandy device that’ll count down from ten to zero, adding precious seconds every time Jack kills an enemy. This timer also represents the health bar, and letting it empty out, either through receiving damage or going long enough without kills, means that the mullet is no more.

The levels in question are, well, levels of a high-rise that the Influencer Princess has been taken to by one especially smugly-unhinged prick, Mr. Bullet (hello, Gianni!). Reaching the elevator that takes you further up is an exhilarating bloodbath that lasts about a minute as you race forward, popping groin shots, sliding on walls, and kicking enemies out of your way and into the various hazards dotted about. In these circumstances, there’s no room for elaborate level design or time for prolonged fights. A “prolonged fight” in Mullet Madjack is clearing a room of baddies and scrounging up anything useful you might find in about five seconds tops. There’s also a bit of a roguelite in there, so in-between floors, the handler of your stream—a commanding anime gal—will offer you a few upgrades to choose from, all the while a colourful chat scrolls by in the background. And should you die, down you go ten levels, or wherever you ended up after your last boss fight.

https://steamcommunity.com/sharedfiles/filedetails/?id=3669940526
Having all of that laid out, the cynic in me still wants to scoff at the notion of a first-person roguelite with an ironic retro aesthetic being anything to write home about. And if we stopped right here, he’d have a point. As solid as Mullet Madjack is, it’s hardly the first game to mix and match these things. For one, the mechanical foundation itself—blazingly-fast yet flashy movement, a glass cannon avatar that heals from killing enemies, short levels that encourage perfection rather than a mere clear, all wrapped up in a retro package—is building off of Ultrakill, and stumbling onto 2020’s Post Void reveals similarities that make me wonder if I can call Mullet Madjack a remake and get away with it.

So, why? Why am I so impressed by it, regardless? Why did it not only stick to my mind so tightly that thinking of it still gives me a rush, but also made me dig into retro shooters like never before to see if anyone else is trying something equally fresh?

Think back to that ridiculous premise, its neon lights, and eurobeat soundtrack. It’d come across as vapid parody, were it not so well thought out. The premise itself is how Jack’s health bar comes about in the first place; without it, and the need to wrap things up as soon as possible, there’d be no gameplay. Without the world of 2095 being the way it is, there’d be no livestream to watch, nothing to participate in. That sort of cohesion ties all of Mullet Madjack together. It feels like its own genesis, and not a different game someone wanted to redo.

Gameplay is no exception—the boomer shooter that doesn’t play like a boomer shooter; so much so, that its highly requested boomer shooter mode (which gets rid of the time limit in favour of a traditional health bar) ends up as an undeniably inferior way of playing the game. It even tries to tell a story, tweak and throw curveballs to its core loop when that story requires it, ending in a bossfight so bombastic it somehow stands out in a game that’s pure adrenaline.

https://steamcommunity.com/sharedfiles/filedetails/?id=3669941273
The idiosyncrasies in Mullet Madjack, imperfect as you may find them, come together to deliver a game that’s excellent front to back, and bring the kind of originality I’d like to see from the genre more broadly applied, rather than settling for reiterating on the same old same old. I guess we haven’t fully brought back Duke Nukem yet, so we’ve still a ways to go!

As for the Mullet, it’s over the top just enough in its presentation to come across as sincere, rather than trying too hard. Its retro elements and FPS fundamentals are tasteful, supporting the rest of the game rather than the game itself serving as mere window dressing for another bout of interactive deja vu. At the very least, a hearty characterisation of its leading man (hello, [url=https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Darryl_Kurylo]Darryl[/url]—wait, what?!) that’s just…well, perfect would be the world I’d use, makes it quite hard not to find cute. When Mullet Madjack tells me to “stay in school”, I listen. When he muses on how you should “make a new friend every day”, I aspire to heed that advice. When he riffs “all shell, no ghost”, I put on a movie I haven’t seen before. When he proclaims that it’s “mullet time”, I believe him.

After everything is said and done, I guess there’s one final question to ask: [url=https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=x-_fR_Sv9JA]“How’d I do?”[/url].

Woah I have to say this game was WILD. 90s anime with boomer shooter and sprinkle in some rogue-like. What could go wrong here? Almost nothing to be precise! The premise of the game is fairly simple. Rescue the girl from a tower while only having ten seconds to live! Okay the latter does sound much more intimidating than it actually is: Your entire rampage is livestreamed to an audience and you have to keep their attention otherwise they will let you die and to keep them entertained you have to be stylish! Everything to get that sweet dopamine. Yeah shooting robots is easy but it's much better to go for the multikill, kick em into fans or go for the area where it REALLY hurts. You have to do anything to get a bit more seconds of attention to reach the elevator to the next floor. Once you got there you can choose an upgrade to make traversing the tower easier. If for whatever reason you bite the dust all your upgrades are gone and you are back not at the beginning but the last 10th floor reached. The normal campaign is a bit more forgiving in that regard.

Now you would think "How could I possibly manage all the inputs in such a short time?" and you would be right. That was a thought that bothered me a lot at first too and caused me many many deaths until I realized (and maybe really understood THAT tooltip) "I don't need to be fast... I need to be stylish!". After that switch happened blasting my way through the floors became much easier. I could choose the save route and shoot the robot to gain 1 second OR I could tank a hit (-1 second) and kick him into a vending machine to gain +3 seconds AND autopickup a soda to have full time again. Mullet Madjack incorporates the good old old saying: "Work smart not hard". And suddenly 80 floors and a lot of cheesy one liner later I left that tower in flames and drove into the sunset.

This is one of these games I stumbled upon by complete accident and I am very lucky that I did. Although it's a very short game campaign-wise it offers different modes and a few challenges to keep you entertained for a few hours longer which could ramp up the total playtime to maybe 10 hours total. If you like leaderboards then maybe you can sink in even more hours. 
I'd give this game a very solid 8.5/10. If you are interested in either 90s themed games and boomer shooter Mullet Madjack should belong in your library. No need to wait for a discount. Go. NOW! Times wasting!

Gameplay is no exception—the boomer shooter that doesn’t play like a boomer shooter; so much so, that its highly requested boomer shooter mode (which gets rid of the time limit in favour of a traditional health bar) ends up as an undeniably inferior way of playing the game. It even tries to tell a story, tweak and throw curveballs to its core loop when that story requires it, ending in a bossfight so bombastic it somehow stands out in a game that’s pure adrenaline.

https://steamcommunity.com/sharedfiles/filedetails/?id=3669941273
The idiosyncrasies in Mullet Madjack, imperfect as you may find them, come together to deliver a game that’s excellent front to back, and bring the kind of originality I’d like to see from the genre more broadly applied, rather than settling for reiterating on the same old same old. I guess we haven’t fully brought back Duke Nukem yet, so we’ve still a ways to go!

As for the Mullet, it’s over the top just enough in its presentation to come across as sincere, rather than trying too hard. Its retro elements and FPS fundamentals are tasteful, supporting the rest of the game rather than the game itself serving as mere window dressing for another bout of interactive deja vu. At the very least, a hearty characterisation of its leading man (hello, [url=https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Darryl_Kurylo]Darryl[/url]—wait, what?!) that’s just…well, perfect would be the world I’d use, makes it quite hard not to find cute. When Mullet Madjack tells me to “stay in school”, I listen. When he muses on how you should “make a new friend every day”, I aspire to heed that advice. When he riffs “all shell, no ghost”, I put on a movie I haven’t seen before. When he proclaims that it’s “mullet time”, I believe him.

After everything is said and done, I guess there’s one final question to ask: [url=https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=x-_fR_Sv9JA]“How’d I do?”[/url].
[hr][/hr][i]A copy of Mullet Madjack was provided to Summit Reviews for the purpose of this coverage.[/i]

Woah I have to say this game was WILD. 90s anime with boomer shooter and sprinkle in some rogue-like. What could go wrong here? Almost nothing to be precise! The premise of the game is fairly simple. Rescue the girl from a tower while only having ten seconds to live! Okay the latter does sound much more intimidating than it actually is: Your entire rampage is livestreamed to an audience and you have to keep their attention otherwise they will let you die and to keep them entertained you have to be stylish! Everything to get that sweet dopamine. Yeah shooting robots is easy but it's much better to go for the multikill, kick em into fans or go for the area where it REALLY hurts. You have to do anything to get a bit more seconds of attention to reach the elevator to the next floor. Once you got there you can choose an upgrade to make traversing the tower easier. If for whatever reason you bite the dust all your upgrades are gone and you are back not at the beginning but the last 10th floor reached. The normal campaign is a bit more forgiving in that regard.

Now you would think "How could I possibly manage all the inputs in such a short time?" and you would be right. That was a thought that bothered me a lot at first too and caused me many many deaths until I realized (and maybe really understood THAT tooltip) "I don't need to be fast... I need to be stylish!". After that switch happened blasting my way through the floors became much easier. I could choose the save route and shoot the robot to gain 1 second OR I could tank a hit (-1 second) and kick him into a vending machine to gain +3 seconds AND autopickup a soda to have full time again. Mullet Madjack incorporates the good old old saying: "Work smart not hard". And suddenly 80 floors and a lot of cheesy one liner later I left that tower in flames and drove into the sunset.

This is one of these games I stumbled upon by complete accident and I am very lucky that I did. Although it's a very short game campaign-wise it offers different modes and a few challenges to keep you entertained for a few hours longer which could ramp up the total playtime to maybe 10 hours total. If you like leaderboards then maybe you can sink in even more hours. 
I'd give this game a very solid 8.5/10. If you are interested in either 90s themed games and boomer shooter Mullet Madjack should belong in your library. No need to wait for a discount. Go. NOW! Times wasting!

A chill 2D Zelda-shooter. 

[h3]GOOD[/h3]

[+] Clean Visuals: Bright, crisp, and readable even during bullet hell.

[+] Balanced Difficulty:  It’s rarely mean, but it keeps you on your toes.

[h3]BAD[/h3]

[-] No Frills: It hits the notes but doesn't have the ambition or physics-based creativity of PixelJunk Shooter Ultimate.

[-] Linear Progression: Weapons and powerups are basic. Don’t expect deep customization; it’s mostly numerical upgrades (speed, damage, bullet count).

[-] Little Replayability: It’s a 10–12 hour "one-and-done" experience. There's a hard mode, but with no branching paths or variety, there aren't many reasons to keep playing.

[-] Navigation: It sucks, even by Zeldavania standards. Locations and enemies aren't named, so good luck finding a specific guide for that last 5%.

[h3]VERDICT[/h3] 

It’s a good weekend game, but it left me wanting a (2.5D) sequel with greater depth in weapon variety and world-building.

Recommended on deep discount.

Minishoot’ Adventures is a short but thoroughly enjoyable journey through a compact, fully realized world. Clocking in at around 8–10 hours, it never overstays its welcome and feels complete from start to finish. While it’s technically a bullet hell, it’s designed in a way that accommodates players of all skill levels, making it approachable without sacrificing challenge.

One of the game’s biggest strengths is how naturally everything comes together. No guides are needed for achievements or secrets, exploration and progression feel intuitive, as if the game gently nudges you in the right direction without ever holding your hand. It’s clear the developer put a lot of thought into every system and encounter. The gameplay is fluid and highly responsive, and when you take a hit, it never feels unfair or cheap, just an honest reminder that you misplayed. Minishoot’ Adventures is a confident, well-crafted experience that knows exactly what it wants to be, and executes on that vision exceptionally well.

A twin-stick shooter which is absolutely delightful to play.

+ Short and sweet: ~10h of content and never repetitive.
+ Metroidvania elements. The main objective is to beat 4 boss areas scattered in different corners of the map. Each of the first 3 boss areas unlocks a new ability ([spoiler] dashing, supershot, surfing [/spoiler]), which allows you to reach previously inaccessible areas.
+ Fun exploration in the overworld: biomes with different sceneries & enemies and various Zelda-like dungeons; collectibles which enhance your ship; hidden (but not too well-hidden so no pixel hunt or trial & error necessary) paths/areas galore.
+ Tight gameplay and feedback loop induced by the utterly satisfying upgrades and the well-designed system behind them.
    * Crystals for enhancing your weapon and unlocked abilities are rewarded for leveling-up from collecting experience by defeating any enemy. A particular enriching feature is that the game allows you to reallocate your crystals anytime in-game, even during boss fights. This enables you to gear towards every situation reactively.
    * Modules with bonus effects are found during exploration, or rewarded from collectibles, or bought with currencies gained from beating non-regenerable mini-bosses. This design incites you to explore further and more thoroughly.
+ Smooth and gentle difficulty curve.
+ Astonishingly complete in terms of (difficulty) accessibility and quality of life features:
    * Three difficulties (easy, normal, advanced), modifiable anytime you want when in-game.
    * Options for aiming assistance; invincibility; infinite energy, etc.
    * Modules for showing overworld completion percentage, indicators for completely cleared areas or missed collectibles, etc. Especially helpful for 100% the game; I managed to do so without any guide online, which is extremely rare for Metroidvania games. In addition, these modules are purchasable (not imposed) virtually from the very beginning, thus leaving the choice for players to adapt to their preferred exploration style.
    * Backtracking and map traversing, another major pain point in Metroidvania games, are rendered relatively painless by various shortcuts and energy refills (which allow you to dash longer).

Conclusion: 100% recommend. Minishoot's Adventures is the kind of games which have nothing innovative but get everything right and make you wish for more. Another well-merited [url=https://www.rockpapershotgun.com/minishoot-adventures-review] Bestest Best from Rock Paper Shotgun [/url]!

Wonderful game!

For those who enjoy spaceship shooters, Zelda, and metroidvania-style games, this one is a real treat.
It’s based on the classic/2D Zelda formula, with a very good level of exploration, well-designed dungeons, solid gameplay, great power-ups, and a map that’s easy and fun to explore.

On top of that, it isn’t very long — you can complete 100% of the map in about 9 hours.
I highly recommend it to everyone, especially since it’s very affordable.

I loved it.

Great game! Looks good, sounds good, handles good, simple and self explanatory, works well l very well on a Steam Deck, and even has accessibility options. 100% completed (game shows 10 hours), beaten all the bosses, waiting for the next game, recommended ❤️
Zelda + bullet hell with clean, attractive art and responsive, twin-stick combat. Easy recommendation.

This game is genuinely what being a wonderful indie game is all about. 

This game is clever, knows how to give a slight challenge to the player without inducing any form of anger, gives progressive unlocks/power-ups that make 100%ing this game not a difficult task at all. I played for 16 hours, beat the game twice, and 100% it without using an external guide. Minishoot Adventures plays buttery smooth and I had such a fantastic time with it, I would absolutely recommend it to everyone looking at this review right now!

A Metroidvania that combines A Link to the Past and Geometry Wars?  
I think this was made for me.
Smooth, satisfying movement, quick upgrades, and fun fights that are hard but fair.
Couldn't recommend it more.

Who even thinks of combining a Zelda-like and a bullet hell game? Crazy ideas make for the best games apparently.

Just overall perfection - really great world design, interesting puzzles, fun upgrades (first time I’ve seen a game where you can downgrade to reorganize your upgrades, amazing design choice), challenging bosses, cool story.

10/10 - a strange one but something truly unique, never before seen.

Одна из немногих игр которая, для меня, оправдала свой высокий рейтинг.
Четкое, отзывчивое управление, простая но приятная глазу графика, немного метройдвании а-ля зельда, не затянута, прикольный булетхелл и сложность самое-то для меня, престарелого. Из субъективных минусов только то, что не всегда очевидно куда дальше двигаться, из-за чего пару раз "тупил" и приходилось смотреть подсказку. 
Проходил на стимдеке, без нареканий.

Recovering from drug addiction? Replace them with this game! 
I've never been killed so many times in the same spot, and maintained a compulsory urge to try just one (many) more time, with zero frustration. Plays like a blend of 2D Zelda and top down, arcade-y bullet-hell shooter, and this lovechild of genres got all the dominant genes.

yes, i have fun with this game. 

this is solid metroidvania with bullet hell combat that is very pretty quick pace if you decide to play on the hardest difficulty. the overall exploration is pretty excellent since you unlock powers then you can access towards other areas but in any order you want. 

the progression feels nices and the ability to refund with no cost feels nice. 

the ost can be cozy or either pretty hard if you are in a boss. 

honestly surprised about the ending, its pretty controversial move to add [spoiler] delete your save during the end of the game as the real ending, although the story of the game itself make sense. so i cant complain too much[/spoiler]

despite being only 10 hours, its was overall 10 hours of enjoyment, and not a single frustration to be had. no guides, no handholding or walkthrough. it was pure exploration done by the player themselves, and that to me is the best example of how make an engaging game. 

And Minishoot adventure is pretty much solid

Alguns jogos são obras primas em sua simplicidade, em serem "curtos", na verdade, terem o tempo certo. Esse é um desses jogos. 
A combinação de SHMUP com Metroidvania é simplesmente muito bem implementada, sistema de combate  no ponto e estética+narrativa encantadoras, mesmo com a arte sendo bem "rústica" e simples, porém os devs conseguem passar expressão e informação com muito profissionalismo (o que muitos jogos de alto custo de produção as vezes falham pelo excesso).

9/10 no mínimo, um dos melhores de ambos os gêneros que o compõe.
Very fun twin stick shooter, tight combat and somehow I kinda find the robots very cute haha.
Incredible little game. So clever, so charming.

Lovely game, if you enjoy bullethell and world exporation, finding secrets with unlocking new areas by getting new abilities, you will love this. great game to 100%. 
[spoiler]  Im glad this game has achievements otherwise there would be nothing left. [/spoiler]

A unique and charming adventure game as a sentient drone. There's some challenging content here and there, but otherwise it's pretty easy, fun and feels good to learn and play throughout. 

I got this game close to when it launched and was so addicted and in the groove I completed 90% of the content available in just 6-hours. I wish I would have given it a positive review sooner, but it seems to have done very well without my review. 

In 2026 and on, it'd definitely be worth picking up in the $6-$10 range. 

The bundle with Galactic Glitch, another unique game I love is definitely worth it. (Man do I love being able to rip parts off enemies and throw around as electrified mini-nukes.)

A mix of a top down shooter, with an adventure game (legend of zelda-ish) that plays well.

On the positive, the gameplay, and environmental designs are great. Its a really fun game to play.

On the negative, the story is shallow (barely exists), and the production values are a bit lacking.

Overall, I would say it is a really fun game to play, but not an interesting game to think about.
Loved it, gets gradually harder but with lots of settings for shooting, aiming and so on you can tailor it to your likes. Played on SteamDeck only and had no issues.

I've just finished so I'm a bit speechless but I'll try to gather some of my thoughts here.

I went into this game expecting a short, silly Touhou fangame. Instead I got likely my favorite Touhou fangame ever. It's hard to talk about all the things it does well, because I think being surprised by them is better, so my thoughts will be somewhat vague.

The gameplay is a lot of fun. The loadout system allows you to make very unique builds, the bullet patterns were consistently varied and fun in their own ways, and the difficulty ramps up until some stages are fairly difficult. It also has a couple of optional, even tougher challenges for the masochistic, which I'll be trying in more depth soon.

The story is also fun, with the recurring location of the Bunny Train providing repeated opportunities to learn more about and get attached to the main cast of Reisen, Tewi, and the bunnies (I love them all), and the overarching narrative linking everyone else together too. The whole story went in a direction that I wasn't fully expecting at first, and I only got more and more invested. Really the entire second half had me extremely excited, and I have only played more and more every day since I started the game. I was not expecting it to go so hard, but it did, and I ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ love it for that.

The art is simply delightful, especially the sprites. I love them I love them I love them!!! I ADORE all the outfits that were unique to this game, and the couple of characters that got long hair... I don't know who decided that, but thank you, I'm devouring your headcanon and consuming it, it's mine now, I'll never recover and I'll be drawing those characters with long hair forever.

It's just a really solid game!! It's honestly one of my favorite games that I've played in months, and again I think it's my favorite Touhou fangame. I look forward to trying the DLCs, and I'll also be checking out the Prologue (which I know is more of a demo or early build, but it features Remilia?! I GOTTA try that). Thank you so much to all the developers!!
the beginning is pretty difficult, but it's fun once you figure it out

This is a chill puzzle game with a calm, relaxing soundtrack and a light pastel colour palette.

The gameplay consists of placing different characters into a seating plan that meets all of their needs. As the levels progress more variables have to be accounted for, plus you will need to store their luggage in compartments with limited amounts of space.

You have some characters that don't like noise, some that want to chat, some that slack off, some that need to focus on their work, some that wear cologne and some who don't like strong smells amongst a variety of other character traits they can possess that all have a knock on effect on each other. You want to place them in their ideal spot so their needs are met and they have no one that ticks them off sitting nearby. If you place everyone in their correct spot you will achieve a perfect score.

The game is relaxing and the levels provide a decent challenge as you get further into the game, it can get a little bit repetitive over longer play sessions but it is good fun and I would recommend it if you want a laid back puzzle game.

I'm giving a positive review, but take it with a grain of salt: it's not good enough to be "overwhelmingly positive". It's an inventive, fun, and at some points challenging puzzle game with a competent, cute art style. If you like the sound of that already, you're good to buy. That being said, there's a caveat. As short as the game is, the actual content is even shorter, and by the end levels and mechanics blatantly repeat - it gets boring, frankly, but it's not long enough to get really boring. I'm not saying that you can achieve a massive gameplay variation with dragging people around spots, but the sixth time I've seen the exact same room to solve, I actually groaned. Some of these levels should've been challenge mode, not actual levels. Innovation mostly stops on chapter 3, and it gets real samey. The story is also meh, but the game is clearly not about the story. It feels tacked on just to excuse the "travel" theme. Otherwise, good game.

Cons:
This is a shorter game, and like most puzzle games, aside from going back to get a perfect score there isn't much replay-ability, so expect a good 5-6 hours out of this one.

A solid 10/10 for me! This game is genuinely so much fun, and it's so cute. Even if you've watched someone else play it before, I think it's definitely still worth playing for yourself! For some of them you need to be careful about where you put characters with nachos/popcorn or other food, since they will dirty their spot and then you have to play around it! I also really enjoyed the story that took place over the course of the game, it was cute to see and was honestly a great fit for the gameplay. This is a game I think I'll be coming back to when I need to destress, or just miss it! It played wonderfully on my computer, the visuals were very charming, the music was great (and memorable), and I honestly can't think of any criticism to give (besides the fact I wish it was longer so I could play more lol). I can see how it might not be for everyone, but if you're somebody who enjoys games like this then I think it's worth the money. Once again a 10/10, highly recommend!

[i]In Stick it to the Stickman you play as an unhappy office worker fighting his way up the corporate ladder after he had enough of his bosses' disrespect. And by "fighting" I mean beating up your co-workers with katanas, nunchuks, chainsaws and thrown coffee cups.[/i]

​The gameplay is a [u]mixture of rogue-like and beat 'em up[/u], containing a total of [u]16 unlockable characters[/u] with different starting abilities. After each run you receive some money to upgrade different skill trees that make your next run more powerful by adding permanent upgrades. On the last floor of each run, you fight your previous CEO self from the last successful run to take his place as the new boss.

The [u]fights are incredibly entertaining[/u] and make a lot of fun. The higher you climb the office, the more temporary skills you unlock for your recent run. Every few stages you also encounter a random miniboss with special abilities. The music is absolutely one of the best parts -Stick it to the Stickman would definitely not be half as fun without the funky OST.

[b]​Overall, I [u]highly recommend[/u] the game if you're searching for some hilarious beat 'em up action in between. Totally [u]worth the 7€[/u].[/b]

This game is insanely fun but baaaaaaadly optimised.

Granted it's in Early Access, but this is the base game and I have to set the game down to 60fps (or 59.96hz in-game) for this game to run [i]mostly[/i] smoothly. This issue is worsened even more when I play with a second player. Launching the game also takes a couple of minutes which is frustrating. Menus are buggy with cursors often times going off screen or vanish entirely and tends to happen most when playing (while appreciative of its inclusion) with a controller. Frames drop, physics can be amusing but buggy (I literally got stuck under a conveyor belt and had to reload the level)

I hate to front load this with such negativity because ultimately, the game's a lot of fun. Like, a LOT of fun. And it's funny. The game satirises white-collar office culture as well as making not so subtle jabs at the notoriously and  gratuitously strict working environment of a blue collar worker in a [i]certain[/i] large fulfillment centre warehouse. Between the moves, the supers, the audio, and even the characters themed around stereotypical office personalities; everything has a touch of sarcasm and irony to it. And some very neat references to pop culture.

The game play is fun, with a decent amount to unlock although you have to be addicted to the gameplay loop to be motivated enough to earn all the available unlocks (because of the bad optimisation). But the characters, multiple difficulties, as well as a Day Shift/Night Shift variants in climbing the tower, there's a decent amount to do. There's a GTA-esque "overworld", a fulfilment centre that is akin to speedrun-platforming, and even a bloodsport mode. All of which, in some way or another tie into the main game. 

The new level editor with community levels is a fantastic addition and was surprised at how early they added this in the game's development but a wise one as community engagement will really give this game longevity. 

The combat is fun, if not ridiculous in all the right ways, even when it works out poorly for the player, and learning how to master and optimise a particular job is a journey itself. The only thing I really wish right now, is if the main game itself had longer variants. By the time I've reached the top of the tower, especially with some characters, I've barely felt like I started at all.

I'm glad to see this game is still getting love. It's a game that has almost cemented itself as one of my "just chuck it on and play a few rounds -- oh crap 3 hours passed by", contingent on optimsations. In fact its the main reason I haven't gotten more hours on it. I've actually struggled recommending this game to my friends because of how poorly optimised the game and it's UI is. Right now for the price, it's easier to justify, but if the price does end up increasing and these aren't resolved, I would have to change it to Not Recommended. I can play games like Forza Horizons in 4K Ultra settings at a smooth 60fps, or even Marvel Rivals in 100-165fps at 2K without issue. My system isn't the issue.

This game has truthfully become one of my favourites in my steam library (seriously, I stayed up until 4am playing when I had to get up at 8am at least a few times) and I would be over the moon if I decide to boot it up again and things are smoother. 

Continue the work, please continue to look into optimisations, this has fast become a beloved game for me and I want it to stay there.

Best parts of the game:
- Get paid to pet cats!! Incredibly well-implemented, cute sound effects, even the cursor changes when you're petting them.
- The art is cohesive and beautiful, with good customization options. 
- The gameplay is fairly straightforward to learn and not too frustrating. Later on there are QoL features for all the aspects that could start to get annoying. 
- The villager commentary is very funny
The game is cute and fun, and I think the purchase price is just right considering the short length of the main game. I've 100% the achievements, which adds perhaps a couple hours to the 5 hours or so to complete the missions. 

Suggestions for improvement:
- Obviously more missions would be cool, I think there's a lot of room for different challenges
- Another level to the service center that lets you re-order buildings on the street would be awesome
- The onsen and yokai tree both let you upgrade indefinitely, but at some point run out of visual cues. Letting there be more flowers/statues until it runs out would be cool.

Minami Lane is a short and enjoyable chill game about sprucing up a street with buildings.

The gameplay is quite simple, you build buildings and manage your street. There are 5 levels, and it mostly revolves around making sure your street buildings expand, keeping your street happy, tapping on racoons/trash/cats/cyclers, and making sure your shops have good selection. There is absolutely no pressure in the game, and you can really take it at your own pace. Even getting 100% achievements is pretty simple (challenges in levels are pretty easy to get). The art is extremely cute, especially when the street gets filled up more later on with people. The music also adds to the relaxing nature of the street and game. Honestly, the vibes as a whole are just so chill and wholesome.

Overall, I would highly recommend playing Minami Lane. It is inexpensive, short, cute, and a great relaxing game to play.

Minami Lane is a [b]cute and cozy management simulation[/b] that tasks players with fostering a profitable and vibrant community at their own pace. This is an excellent title to unwind to, as there are plenty of customization and accessibility options, such as pausing and time flow, that encourage one to creatively design streets at their leisure. Moreover, this experience contains an adjustable [b]sandbox mode[/b] that features content from all the missions. The sound design is also worth mentioning, as everyday noises integrate wonderfully with the soft, playful melodies and expressively wholesome hand-drawn art. This game is [b]short[/b], yet it is well worth the asking price.
