It took me almost 20 hours to beat a single run on the "Experience" difficulty, described as basically the "Easy" difficulty level for those who just want to experience the game.

I consider myself relatively decent at games in general but had never played a twin-stick shooter like this before.

Yet, I did keep coming back and trying run after run no matter how many times I died in the last world and how frustrated I sometimes got at my silly run-ending mistakes.

The music is an absolute banger and the visuals are fabulous, although they can get overwhelming at times.

There are some nice accessibility options to make things clearer in the visual noise. I especially appreciated the option to make the strike recharge indicator more visible.

If you already know that you like difficult twin stick shooters, I think Sektori is a must-buy.

If you like being challenged over and over and slowly building up your own skill to overcome the hardship, but you've never played a twin stick shooters before, I think you should give it a try.

I don't know if I'll ever beat the middle difficulty, which is described as "the intended way to play", but I think that's alright, I still had fun and even beating the "easy" difficulty was extremely satisfying.

These games I'm telling you truly are the games that I live for! If you have been gaming as long as I have and you have a very distilled understanding of what good game design is than this game will make you very happy. Sektori is a modern "Arcade Style" twin-stick shooter. Its heavily inspired by geometry wars but with a slightly more forgiving difficulty curve and more modes and difficulty settings.

If you find yourself enjoying this kind of game please take my recommendations below as this genre is very much so a passion of mine.

MUST PLAY NEXT:                       
1. Nex Machina - Literally the single BEST modern arcade style game! Every game made by HouseMarque pre Returnal are absolute BLESSINGS!

2. Resogun - Another game by Housemarque. This is a playstation exclusive but seriously go play it

3. Geometry Wars

4. Super Stardust HD

5. Any Shoot em' up made by the developer CAVE / Dodonpachi, ESP Galuda 1&2, ESP RADE, Ketsui. Guwange, Akai Katana.

Amazing. Sekori feels like a modern evolution of Geometry Wars — sharper, bolder, and way more ambitious. The visual style is insane in the best way: the typography that pops up when you grab upgrades is slick, the color palette is gorgeous, and the psychedelic, constantly shifting arenas give every run a fresh vibe and a ton of replay value.

What really surprised me is how much content there is. There are a ton of different game modes to mess with, and the full campaign is genuinely epic — packed with clever level ideas, perspective shifts, and boss fights that feel like events. Those camera flips before major encounters are wild.

And yes, this game is hard. But it’s the good kind of hard — the kind where you feel yourself improving, and finally beating a brutal wave or a boss hits you with that perfect “I earned this” rush. It’s easily one of the best bullet‑hell shoot‑’em‑ups we’ve gotten in years.

Totally worth the $14.99.

If you liked Geometry Wars. this is a no‑brainer.

This game is pretty great for what it wants to be. A simple dungeon crawler made by monkeys and for monkeys; neither of which are an insult. I think this handles what you'd want from a roguelite pretty perfectly; giving you a constant reason to want to dive further and further into the dungeons for unlocks and materials for each unlock you can find. Each run feels different and that was /before/ getting the outfits, which basically feel like a whole new character with each seeming to have their own gimmicks to 'em. 

Storywise, I haven't a clue what it's about yet since you need to get through the game a few times before you can start figuring stuff out; which is fine. I don't think it really needs it for what it is, but I'll be glad to see the story eventually and I'll be happy to work for it... Like a monkey on a spear shooting type-writer, I'll find success eventually.

Gameplay wise, I have no real complaints for the main set up of the game, except the difficulty can be a bit too heavily leaning towards dodging; something I suck at 'cause my brain is bad and the controls require you to press a lot of buttons. 

A lot, since you're using your Main Weapon (Mostly Melee), and then three 'Skills' which range from throwing spears, throwing down traps, throwing down a sentry gun, or shooting beams of pure energy in different ways. Dodging can be pretty hard during these times that you're spamming, since it can be hard to get the dodge off in the middle of a casting/swinging animation; it doesn't come up often, but it's there! Usually though, I'm just too busy pressing another button to dodge and that's on me.

That said, when I /do/ forget to dodge, I feel like the damage numbers are a bit too high on some things. Bosses and traps can do 300-500 damage with just one hit; that's nearly half or entirely half your health if you haven't upgraded a bit. But being a roguelite, you're obviously supposed to stat up, but... that takes awhile! 

As you progress, you also get Level Ups through drops that you can also use to upgrade your stats during that run, which is fun. From Lifesteal%, Critchance%, Health%, and even Defense and Dodge! The last three are imperative to survival, but ... can come across as pretty weak, if you don't focus on them entirely. Defense especially doesn't feel that great, as I was taking around 550 damage from a Area 2 Boss with 6% Defense or something like that. Dodge is a 100% Defense Upgrade, technically~

All that aside, the weapon system is fun and I appreciate how varied they all feel, from the melee weapons to the special attacks. If I had a complaint, it's that a lot of them are stuck behind buying them with Diamonds you find in runs and that can be pretty rough when you're also buying outfits with those. Hard to choose and it's a lot~

But hey, a complaint being "I have too much stuff to choose from!" isn't really a complaint when it comes to this kinda game.

For actual complaints, I only have two; one of which is merely a bug.

The PS4 Controller I tried to use doesn't seem to work properly with this game. Specifically the control sticks just lock up the character if I'm pressing to the right after jumping or something similar. This is not an issue with my controller; I've tested it on other things and it works fine. Additionally, other controllers, such as a Switch Pro Controller, did not have this issue. Trying other settings also messed with it a bit, but I wasn't able (or capable) of figuring out what the issue was. Could just be on my end anyway, despite my testing, but... I dunno. <(' '<)

Outside of that, my only actual gameplay issue was finding out out that there are a limit on the relics you can find throughout the game. Relics are basically passive upgrades you can get that increase your stats or give you special effects, such as summoning stuff when you dodge or making enemies you beat literally explode. You're limited to a number of them (28, specifically), which may sound like a lot but it felt like it really limited my capabilities.

Perhaps that can be increased with an upgrade thing later, but if it is something you can modify, I wasn't aware of it... Granted I haven't played the game /that/ much, so maybe I just missed it. Still! That kinda thing only really comes up in runs when you're nearing the end, so it's not a huge deal regardless; I just felt it limited the fun a bit.

Anyway, this review is too long. So enough monkeying around! If anything I said sounded interesting, then grab the game! It's well worth its price and if it still doesn't sound right, then snag that demo and see if you enjoy it.

Hope you have a great time in the Dunjungle; I did and I'm barely Dun.

[h1] Sal's Review of... [/h1]

[b] Dunjungle! [/b] (As of version 1.0 release)

[b] Summary: [/b] Dunjungle is an action-packed, Rogue-lite Metroidvania set in the tropical jungles of... somewhere, latin america probably. Lol. You play as the ookful Monkey Hero, who sets out on a quest to defeat the corruption destroying his home. On the way, you'll be assisted by the different apes (and a snake... and a human...) who are residents of the jungle temple, providing you with upgrades, primary and secondary weapons, and some nice relics along the way. Every time you die, you are sent back to the start with more knowledge and more progression - being able to use diamonds and banana crystals to unlock new content and upgrade everything from the powerups you get each run to the baby monkies you rescue along the way.

[b] Gameplay: [/b] Dunjungle is exactly what it says on the tin. If you've ever played Metroid, Castlevania, Dead Cells, etc., you'll be familiar with Dunjungle's style and mechanics. You have a primary weapon and 3 secondary weapons - your primary serving as your trusty melee weapon for any occasion, while the secondaries serve as additional damage boosts on a cooldown or utility items that enhance your melee weapon. When it comes to the variety of playstyles and weapons, Dunjungle has everything you might want in a metroidvania - from heavy hitters and rapid fire weapons to more unconventional tools fitting for gimmick playstyles. 

Secret Spoiler warning:
 [spoiler] Dunjungle also features a plethora of outfits that change your playstyle, unlocked by completing special challenges. These outfits push your build in a specific direction and require you to hunt specific relics and weapons to utilize them to their fullest. They were fun to play with and all offer a different way to play the game! I'm considering making a full tierlist not just of them but the weapons as well. The Wukong unlock for beating the game at the hardest difficulty is also a really nice touch, because that was a genuinely grueling task - 0 invincibility frames is tough. [/spoiler] 

When it comes to enemy variety and boss fights, there's also pleanty of diversity! The main enemies you'll be seeing throughout the game in most areas are all posessed tribesmen wearing masks, corrupted into attacking The Hero. They come in a bunch of different varieties, so their omnipresence doesn't really bore me. Of course, every area of the game has unique, thematically appropriate enemies and bosses that serve to improve the area's identity and the game's gameplay diversity. My personal favourite area has to be the Sunken Shrine. Mechanics-wise, Enemies all flash red with their outline when they prepare to attack you, and thus, the dodging mechanic is your primary way of avoiding damage. The bosses all have unique attack patterns and are fun and challenging to fight! My only gripe is that I wish there was some more variety to it all... The Jungle Temple has the most variety, with 3 unique bosses and 2 unique minibosses, while every other area, except the final areas, has 2 unique bosses each. An extra boss or two would be a great addition for more random factor and diversity!

[b] Aesthetics: [/b] Dunjungle's art style, at first, bored me a little. I found the artstyle a little too simplistic for my liking, but it very quickly grew on me when a friend compared it to Enter The Gungeon, especially with the Monkey Hero's dumb little beady eyes (endearing). The pixel art, although simple in style, is really charming, and all the different enemies and bosses have really well done spritework. I'm especially fond of the camp NPCs for their diversity in ape species (or well, species in general, considering the snake and human...) and the really cute spritework they have going on. The different areas of the game all have distinct identities and color palletes that are all really gorgeous to look at, though the one area i think the game could improve on is the soundtrack - no unique music for the alternative path levels? I expected at least the alternative final area to have a unique track, but no. All of the alt path areas have the same music as the main path. The soundtrack itself is very fun and thematically fitting, and I don't think there's any issues with it sonically, i just think it could use a bit of tinkering post-launch - a track or two at least.

[b] Progression and Endgame (SPOILER WARNING): [/b]

 [spoiler] Dunjungle's progression revolves around completing challenges, collecting diamonds and banana crystals to upgrade your roster of weapons and in-run upgrades, all for the purpose of eventually reaching the Corrupted Jungle or the Neon Jungle - though on your first run on the normal difficulty, this will be the Corrupted Jungle. Here you will fight a corrupted amalgamation of the human and the tribesmen, mutated into one giant being by the corrupting force ruining the jungle. Upon defeating the final boss, you get hit with a Ghouls 'n Ghosts style "beat the game on a higher difficulty, ♥♥♥♥♥" rugpull. You don't get a title card or credits, but rather, you're rewarded with some new unlocks and currency for your premanent progression upgrades. Congratulations! You've unlocked the game's real intended difficulty - Hard mode. By completing the game on normal, you also unlock the Neon Jungle, which is in my opinion where the true final boss lies. The game's difficulty massively spikes in hard mode, rendering healing less effective and all enemies much stronger, but, you also get more resources for your progression upgrades. You also unlock the endless mode - a wave clearing mode where you fight ever-increasing levels of enemies who progressively become more and more difficult, but you grow more and more powerful each room. In my personal experience, the Vampire Monkey and the Panda suit are the 2 absolute strongest suits for beating any of the game's content. One just gives you extremely strong lifesteal and plays very forgiving, while the other scales your stats infinitely with one of the game's most common sources of healing - food. Slap on a relatively strong weapon like the bamboo staff or any charge-based heavy hitter and you have an easy build to break the game with. The Very Hard difficulty is extremely unforgiving, but by the time you unlock it, you should be able to master dodging and reacting to anything enemies or bosses throw at you. Even at this difficulty i managed to trivialize the final boss with lifesteal and some well timed dodges. The Lifesteal nerfs, if you invest enough into damage and lifesteal, become entirely ignorable. I think some form of additional debuff at the highest difficulty would be in order, to at least make lifesteal not the most viable strategy at lower skill levels. I also think some of the relics could use a re-ballancing? some of them are just flad out overpowered with certain setups or useless in nearly all situations. [/spoiler] 

[b] Closing thoughts: [/b]

Dunjungle is a very fun roguelite for those of monkeyful inclinations and joy in their hearts. I love monkies. I love this game.

I think this handles what you'd want from a roguelite pretty perfectly; giving you a constant reason to want to dive further and further into the dungeons for unlocks and materials for each unlock you can find. Each run feels different and that was /before/ getting the outfits, which basically feel like a whole new character with each seeming to have their own gimmicks to 'em. 

Storywise, I haven't a clue what it's about yet since you need to get through the game a few times before you can start figuring stuff out; which is fine. I don't think it really needs it for what it is, but I'll be glad to see the story eventually and I'll be happy to work for it... Like a monkey on a spear shooting type-writer, I'll find success eventually.

Gameplay wise, I have no real complaints for the main set up of the game, except the difficulty can be a bit too heavily leaning towards dodging; something I suck at 'cause my brain is bad and the controls require you to press a lot of buttons. 

A lot, since you're using your Main Weapon (Mostly Melee), and then three 'Skills' which range from throwing spears, throwing down traps, throwing down a sentry gun, or shooting beams of pure energy in different ways. Dodging can be pretty hard during these times that you're spamming, since it can be hard to get the dodge off in the middle of a casting/swinging animation; it doesn't come up often, but it's there! Usually though, I'm just too busy pressing another button to dodge and that's on me.

That said, when I /do/ forget to dodge, I feel like the damage numbers are a bit too high on some things. Bosses and traps can do 300-500 damage with just one hit; that's nearly half or entirely half your health if you haven't upgraded a bit. But being a roguelite, you're obviously supposed to stat up, but... that takes awhile! 

As you progress, you also get Level Ups through drops that you can also use to upgrade your stats during that run, which is fun. From Lifesteal%, Critchance%, Health%, and even Defense and Dodge! The last three are imperative to survival, but ... can come across as pretty weak, if you don't focus on them entirely. Defense especially doesn't feel that great, as I was taking around 550 damage from a Area 2 Boss with 6% Defense or something like that. Dodge is a 100% Defense Upgrade, technically~

All that aside, the weapon system is fun and I appreciate how varied they all feel, from the melee weapons to the special attacks. If I had a complaint, it's that a lot of them are stuck behind buying them with Diamonds you find in runs and that can be pretty rough when you're also buying outfits with those. Hard to choose and it's a lot~

But hey, a complaint being "I have too much stuff to choose from!" isn't really a complaint when it comes to this kinda game.

For actual complaints, I only have two; one of which is merely a bug.

The PS4 Controller I tried to use doesn't seem to work properly with this game. Specifically the control sticks just lock up the character if I'm pressing to the right after jumping or something similar. This is not an issue with my controller; I've tested it on other things and it works fine. Additionally, other controllers, such as a Switch Pro Controller, did not have this issue. Trying other settings also messed with it a bit, but I wasn't able (or capable) of figuring out what the issue was. Could just be on my end anyway, despite my testing, but... I dunno. <(' '<)

Outside of that, my only actual gameplay issue was finding out out that there are a limit on the relics you can find throughout the game. Relics are basically passive upgrades you can get that increase your stats or give you special effects, such as summoning stuff when you dodge or making enemies you beat literally explode. You're limited to a number of them (28, specifically), which may sound like a lot but it felt like it really limited my capabilities.

Perhaps that can be increased with an upgrade thing later, but if it is something you can modify, I wasn't aware of it... Granted I haven't played the game /that/ much, so maybe I just missed it. Still! That kinda thing only really comes up in runs when you're nearing the end, so it's not a huge deal regardless; I just felt it limited the fun a bit.

Anyway, this review is too long. So enough monkeying around! If anything I said sounded interesting, then grab the game! It's well worth its price and if it still doesn't sound right, then snag that demo and see if you enjoy it.

Hope you have a great time in the Dunjungle; I did and I'm barely Dun.

[h1] Sal's Review of... [/h1]

[b] Dunjungle! [/b] (As of version 1.0 release)

[b] Summary: [/b] Dunjungle is an action-packed, Rogue-lite Metroidvania set in the tropical jungles of... somewhere, latin america probably. Lol. You play as the ookful Monkey Hero, who sets out on a quest to defeat the corruption destroying his home. On the way, you'll be assisted by the different apes (and a snake... and a human...) who are residents of the jungle temple, providing you with upgrades, primary and secondary weapons, and some nice relics along the way. Every time you die, you are sent back to the start with more knowledge and more progression - being able to use diamonds and banana crystals to unlock new content and upgrade everything from the powerups you get each run to the baby monkies you rescue along the way.

[b] Gameplay: [/b] Dunjungle is exactly what it says on the tin. If you've ever played Metroid, Castlevania, Dead Cells, etc., you'll be familiar with Dunjungle's style and mechanics. You have a primary weapon and 3 secondary weapons - your primary serving as your trusty melee weapon for any occasion, while the secondaries serve as additional damage boosts on a cooldown or utility items that enhance your melee weapon. When it comes to the variety of playstyles and weapons, Dunjungle has everything you might want in a metroidvania - from heavy hitters and rapid fire weapons to more unconventional tools fitting for gimmick playstyles. 

Secret Spoiler warning:
 [spoiler] Dunjungle also features a plethora of outfits that change your playstyle, unlocked by completing special challenges. These outfits push your build in a specific direction and require you to hunt specific relics and weapons to utilize them to their fullest. They were fun to play with and all offer a different way to play the game! I'm considering making a full tierlist not just of them but the weapons as well. The Wukong unlock for beating the game at the hardest difficulty is also a really nice touch, because that was a genuinely grueling task - 0 invincibility frames is tough. [/spoiler] 

When it comes to enemy variety and boss fights, there's also pleanty of diversity! The main enemies you'll be seeing throughout the game in most areas are all posessed tribesmen wearing masks, corrupted into attacking The Hero. They come in a bunch of different varieties, so their omnipresence doesn't really bore me. Of course, every area of the game has unique, thematically appropriate enemies and bosses that serve to improve the area's identity and the game's gameplay diversity. My personal favourite area has to be the Sunken Shrine. Mechanics-wise, Enemies all flash red with their outline when they prepare to attack you, and thus, the dodging mechanic is your primary way of avoiding damage. The bosses all have unique attack patterns and are fun and challenging to fight! My only gripe is that I wish there was some more variety to it all... The Jungle Temple has the most variety, with 3 unique bosses and 2 unique minibosses, while every other area, except the final areas, has 2 unique bosses each. An extra boss or two would be a great addition for more random factor and diversity!

[b] Aesthetics: [/b] Dunjungle's art style, at first, bored me a little. I found the artstyle a little too simplistic for my liking, but it very quickly grew on me when a friend compared it to Enter The Gungeon, especially with the Monkey Hero's dumb little beady eyes (endearing). The pixel art, although simple in style, is really charming, and all the different enemies and bosses have really well done spritework. I'm especially fond of the camp NPCs for their diversity in ape species (or well, species in general, considering the snake and human...) and the really cute spritework they have going on. The different areas of the game all have distinct identities and color palletes that are all really gorgeous to look at, though the one area i think the game could improve on is the soundtrack - no unique music for the alternative path levels? I expected at least the alternative final area to have a unique track, but no. All of the alt path areas have the same music as the main path. The soundtrack itself is very fun and thematically fitting, and I don't think there's any issues with it sonically, i just think it could use a bit of tinkering post-launch - a track or two at least.

[b] Progression and Endgame (SPOILER WARNING): [/b]

 [spoiler] Dunjungle's progression revolves around completing challenges, collecting diamonds and banana crystals to upgrade your roster of weapons and in-run upgrades, all for the purpose of eventually reaching the Corrupted Jungle or the Neon Jungle - though on your first run on the normal difficulty, this will be the Corrupted Jungle. Here you will fight a corrupted amalgamation of the human and the tribesmen, mutated into one giant being by the corrupting force ruining the jungle. Upon defeating the final boss, you get hit with a Ghouls 'n Ghosts style "beat the game on a higher difficulty, ♥♥♥♥♥" rugpull. You don't get a title card or credits, but rather, you're rewarded with some new unlocks and currency for your premanent progression upgrades. Congratulations! You've unlocked the game's real intended difficulty - Hard mode. By completing the game on normal, you also unlock the Neon Jungle, which is in my opinion where the true final boss lies. The game's difficulty massively spikes in hard mode, rendering healing less effective and all enemies much stronger, but, you also get more resources for your progression upgrades. You also unlock the endless mode - a wave clearing mode where you fight ever-increasing levels of enemies who progressively become more and more difficult, but you grow more and more powerful each room. In my personal experience, the Vampire Monkey and the Panda suit are the 2 absolute strongest suits for beating any of the game's content. One just gives you extremely strong lifesteal and plays very forgiving, while the other scales your stats infinitely with one of the game's most common sources of healing - food. Slap on a relatively strong weapon like the bamboo staff or any charge-based heavy hitter and you have an easy build to break the game with. The Very Hard difficulty is extremely unforgiving, but by the time you unlock it, you should be able to master dodging and reacting to anything enemies or bosses throw at you. Even at this difficulty i managed to trivialize the final boss with lifesteal and some well timed dodges. The Lifesteal nerfs, if you invest enough into damage and lifesteal, become entirely ignorable. I think some form of additional debuff at the highest difficulty would be in order, to at least make lifesteal not the most viable strategy at lower skill levels. I also think some of the relics could use a re-ballancing? some of them are just flad out overpowered with certain setups or useless in nearly all situations. [/spoiler] 

[b] Closing thoughts: [/b]

Dunjungle is a very fun roguelite for those of monkeyful inclinations and joy in their hearts. I love monkies. I love this game.

[h2] 9.5/10. [/h2]

Not fully done with this game but it's really really enriching to me it feels nice to play something so self-understood. At least so far it kind of hearkens back to the gamefeel of Anodyne 1 in a "Traditional adventure gameplay conventions used to exploring dynamic and multi-tiered storytelling" way but also surpasses the scope of anodyne 1 in every way possible.

Also! As someone who is in a lot of leftists and queer spaces i think its really important the way that tets and arkas and the world engages with religion in like. a super non-antagonistic way. I think theres a lot of strong emotion about religion in communities like this that will cause people to vilify religious ideas (namely christian) on sight and i think its important for queer media to actually engage with it all in earnest.

Please please please but this game its from some of the most important devs in the scene right now along with thecatamites and other visionaries. They're doing amazing things and as long as you are here to listne and engage then i think you'll have a fantastic time

I put in many of hours over Christmas break it cements itself within that time perfectly as significant storytelling. The unique combat paired with a wonderfully executed progression system whereas each level indicates intention behind progression: (story-progression, level-up, or side-quest/discovery) helps the player feel as though they are not limited to a linear path, yet the game is not overwhelming in that it is not a completely open-world since you still have to clear areas to get to new maps or paths.

The messages and how those messages are conveyed reminds me of jRPGs that many of us played in formative yrs, like there's throughlines, but those wacky nonsequitors that mess with established themes and are largely interpretive exist. All in all ts gives me all the thrill of being on a family vacation being allowed a trip to gamestop in a strange city only to find a discounted jrpg in the pre-owned section for the ds and having a life changing experience that sticks with you through the yrs (true story). I mean it's not outerwilds profound, but it's a reallyyyy ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ solid piece of art, and then some, as in I'm 35 hours in and it has a meta-game with 100% completion on one save (after the games complete), different gamemodes, and a new ability post-credit. 

I'm aware this dev is known for atmospheric and out of the box storytelling. I haven't played any of their other games, but this is a good first impression. I'd recommend this game to any one who has a fuzzy spot for a longforgotten jrpg, doesn't mind sitting with a game for a bit, and really likes that cronchy art style from the late-n64/ps1/early-ps2 era in 4:3 Aspect Ratio! Or at least that's how I got into it, I always wanted to get into Zelda and this seemed a similar strain without the baggage of decades worth of content, I'm glad I judged it at face-value for the moment I pressed purchase on steam lol, because it surpasses the comparison, and is familiar-feeling but unlike anything I've bump-slashed prior!

This game is the Ocarina of Time of the 2020's.
Yes, I know what I just said. No, I'm not hyperbolizing.

This game is like if you threw early Ys, Mario 64, Zelda, and Earthbound in a blender while then filtering it all through a PS1/Dreamcast aesthetic. The devs truly nailed the vibe here and there is so much personality to be found in this title...but most importantly, it's super fun! 

I have absolutely loved my time with this fantastic and creative game so far, and I can highly, HIGHLY recommend that you check this gem out! 

P.S. The music is GODTIER!!!

Angeline Era brought me immense joy and made me laugh out loud more times than I remember. It made me feel like I had returned to childhood, playing games for the first time. While the long-winded ending sequence left me feeling disappointed, it also provoked bittersweet thoughts on life and art.

The game is messy and imperfect, but also - confident, surprising, delightful, and not to be missed.

Note that offline play seems to have deflated my playtime; my save file lists 20 hours.

Angeline Era is ridiculously good. It feels like a classic from the early 2000s that only recently hopped over from a parallel timeline, one of those rare games that has an unwavering sense of the developer's passion and vision.

Gameplay-wise, it's a 3D platforming/puzzle/action adventure game set in a surreal world where the lore is slowly pieced together throughout the main story and side quests. The core "bump combat" mechanic is seemingly simple, but it requires you to think on your toes and figure out how to handle each combat situation, providing a satisfying level of challenge from start to finish. There are also side weapons like an uzi-style gun and things like grenades and land mines to add additional strategy and variety to the fights. 

The overworld exploration is amazing for people who love to explore the little nooks and crannies of an in-game world. It has a sense of adventure that's nearly on par with the greats like the N64 Zeldas or old school Final Fantasy games. The graphics are decidedly not technically impressive--essentially what I imagine a PS1.5 game would look like--but its intentional low-poly styling is actually quite nice to look at, and elevates the game's already surreal atmosphere. 

Overall it's a very well made, unique, and fun game, and if you're curious or on the fence, I strongly recommend checking this one out.

An enjoyable experience overall, though it felt like the end of the game was a bit of a mess. Also the relative scarcity of money combined with not getting your food back if you die after eating some made me not want to engage with the mechanic. I still think this game is absolutely worth a play. It has great exploration and bump combat and so on and so forth.

This game's got the sauce. Low poly Ys-like should sell itself, and it has this like quirky pithy writing style that feels so minimal and clean yet expresses so much emotion. No other game I've played reads like this. Music rules, too. Everything's on-point. 👍👍👍

Angeline Era probably has the best approach to bump combat I've seen in a game. Everything about the world is designed to encourage exploration. Exploration occurs on a large scale, there are tons of secret areas hidden on the world map and within every level. However, exploration also exists on a smaller scale as you discover new environmental interactions through bumping into everything and find hidden NPC's, small rooms, and simple moments which often serve no tangible purpose other than to further bring the world to life in unexpected ways. Tonally, this game is a rollercoaster as it is often weird and funny, but just as often heartfelt and deeply melancholy.

If you enjoy bump combat, good writing, and/or exploration for the sheer sake of it, I highly recommend Angeline Era.

Angeline Era is a strikingly unconventional action-adventure that confidently embraces experimentation, curiosity, and player-driven discovery. Developed by Melos Han-Tani and Marina Kittaka under Analgesic Productions, the game immediately sets itself apart through its refusal to hold the player’s hand, instead inviting exploration, interpretation, and mechanical mastery on its own eccentric terms. From the opening moments, it becomes clear that this is not a game interested in traditional structure or familiar genre rhythms. Instead, it offers a dense, unpredictable world where movement, combat, and exploration are intertwined in ways that constantly challenge expectations.

The setting of Era is presented as a fragmented, myth-soaked landscape inhabited by humans, angels, and strange fae beings, all existing under a sense of looming unease. You play as Tets Kinoshta, a former soldier whose journey is shaped less by explicit exposition and more by surreal encounters, cryptic dialogue, and dreamlike imagery. The narrative unfolds indirectly, often through environmental storytelling and optional discoveries rather than clear story beats. This approach can feel disorienting at first, but it gradually reveals itself as intentional, encouraging players to piece together meaning through observation and reflection. The result is a story that feels personal and interpretive, lingering in the mind rather than resolving neatly.

Combat in Angeline Era is one of its most distinctive features, built around a bump-based attack system that removes the traditional attack button entirely. Tets automatically strikes enemies when colliding with them, transforming movement and positioning into the primary combat skills. This seemingly simple mechanic evolves into a surprisingly deep system, demanding precise control, awareness of enemy patterns, and smart use of space. Later encounters introduce multiple threats, projectile hazards, and environmental dangers that turn battles into kinetic puzzles. Supplemental tools, such as a limited-ammo firearm and various artifacts, add tactical layers without undermining the purity of the core design.

Exploration is just as central as combat, if not more so. The world of Era is expansive and largely unmarked, filled with hidden areas, secret levels, and unexpected transitions that reward players willing to experiment. A dedicated search mechanic allows players to reveal invisible paths, unlock concealed spaces, or trigger strange events, reinforcing the game’s emphasis on curiosity over linear progression. Some discoveries yield powerful upgrades or narrative insight, while others exist purely to surprise or unsettle, reinforcing the sense that the world has its own logic independent of player expectations. This design philosophy makes exploration feel genuinely adventurous rather than checklist-driven.

Visually, Angeline Era embraces a low-poly, retro-inspired 3D aesthetic that evokes the early days of polygonal gaming while feeling entirely modern in intent. The environments are varied and memorable, ranging from quiet rural spaces to ominous ruins and surreal dreamscapes. Character designs and enemy encounters lean into abstraction and symbolism, enhancing the game’s otherworldly tone. This visual style pairs seamlessly with the soundtrack, which shifts between atmospheric, energetic, and contemplative moods, reinforcing both tension and wonder as players move through the world.

Tone is another area where the game excels. Despite its often dark themes and enigmatic storytelling, Angeline Era frequently surprises with moments of humor, warmth, and emotional sincerity. Quirky dialogue, unexpected character interactions, and playful design choices prevent the experience from becoming overly bleak or self-serious. These tonal shifts give the world a sense of life and unpredictability, making it feel like a place that exists beyond the player’s immediate objectives.

While the game’s nonlinear structure and cryptic storytelling may not appeal to everyone, they are integral to its identity. Players seeking clear objectives, constant guidance, or tightly scripted narratives may find the experience challenging or even frustrating. However, for those willing to embrace ambiguity and self-directed exploration, the game offers an unusually rich and rewarding journey. Its occasional rough edges and unconventional pacing feel less like flaws and more like the natural consequences of a project that prioritizes creativity and personal expression over mass-market polish.

Ultimately, Angeline Era stands as a confident and memorable indie achievement that rewards curiosity, patience, and mechanical engagement in equal measure. It is a game that trusts its audience, offering depth without explanation and meaning without insistence. Through its inventive combat, layered exploration, distinctive aesthetic, and emotionally resonant world, it delivers an experience that feels deeply personal and refreshingly unorthodox. For players who value originality and are eager to get lost in a world that refuses to fully explain itself, Angeline Era is a rare and compelling journey worth undertaking.

Rating: 9/10
felt like Mario Galaxy mixed with Zelda exploration and some cool philosophical thoughts to boot. Fun!

Charming, worthwhile, unique, lush, heartfelt. 
The encounter design can tend a little more pinball-danmaku-panic than deliberate-thoughtful-clever-spacing-puzzles, which began to frustrate me at times, but maybe that's a skill issue on my part. The story can often feel like it puts its own concepts and questions and thoughtful pensive moments before the player might actually understand what is going on or what the questions are referencing? but the broad thematic strokes aren't exactly hidden, at least.

Delightfully charming with a dreamlike vibe only Analgesic games have, this game has some issues in terms of gameplay (mainly finding secrets in levels can rapidly turn into "search every single tile" if you're lost, and useful items cost an obscene amount compared to the amount of money you find) but they're really minor compared to just how intoxicating and engrossing the world is.
Top 5 prettiest games of all time. Dense with jokes and poetry. Loved all the scenes and moments. Didn't enjoy the fighting but worth it to see the world. A really loved it in the end!

Neat gameplay, tons of easter eggs and secrets, Soundtrack is awesome. 

worth a buy for sure.

Honestly it took me a lot of time to fully digest the game after I was done with it. I wasn't 100% sure of what I played; it took me time to properly process how the game sticks the landing. It also didn't help that the shoulders it stands on were kind of an unknown to me, both gameplay and story wise. The closest experience was probably getting a bunch of Saito Games demos as a kid that had that early 3D shareware quality, but obviously none of those games had some mythological framework that I should've probably been aware of.

Regardless, you don't need any previous knowledge or experience to enjoy this game and have a lot of fun with it. The gameplay loop is really unique, and you will always find things that feel personal and very hidden. It plays great, it's never boring.  

What made this so memorable to me was how the very deliberate gameplay choices and design restrictions are so good at developing the main character's worldview. As the game goes on and, especially in the latter half, all this information hits you so comically fast. It's what still has me talking about the game with the handful of people close to me that have also played it through.

I wouldn't recommend this game to most people, but if you have some inexplicable urge to check it out then it's probably something you're gonna get a lot out of.

Fun top down exploration based action game with bump combat. Story was full of religion based stuff, not quite sure I understood it all, but it was interesting and poetic. Exploring the overworld, and the levels was fun. You could often tell where a secret was if you were looking hard enough.

It's funny. When I'm playing the game I become bitter, but when I'm thinking about the game after, I get warm and positive memories.

In terms of 'understanding the game', I would say from a story sense, it makes a lot more sense when thought about after having reached its end.

This is a very strange game, I think I can say that. It's strange all the time, and sometimes it gets even weirder. Your constant throughout the game is you, the main character Tets. So you meet a many many characters on your way, as you go by.

The game playing is fun, I guess. Personally it might not have been for me. Searching for secrets makes me sometimes overzealous and I spam the search function in one too many places. Enemies are diverse, there is a wide range of casual strategy in 'combat'. There is a focus around the game's combat, it is a large core of the game, and you're not going to be engaging with the rest of the game without engaging with the combat. At least there is a very nice system of difficulties.

You play levels which you go into from a world map, which is compartmentalised into a few zones. A big island. Cliffs, a lot of countryside, mountains, the mines.

The soundtrack is good, you can find it on Bandcamp.

Loved this! I wish more retro-style indie games took this approach. Instead of regurgitating comfortable styles of gameplay from decades ago, Angeline Era is unashamedly weird. Its bumpslash combat is unlike anything I've played before (although I hear the ys games have something similar?), and its story really took me by surprise. Its much more dense and impenetrable than I would have expected, but I haven't been able to stop thinking about it! Definitely recommended.
the lovely mix of melancholy and sincerity that infuses all of Analgesic's games, but in a very idiosyncratic bumpslash package. if only i could level up in my dream house!

Straightforward gameplay that's rewarding on higher difficulties, gorgeous retro visuals, and a story I'll be thinking about for a long time (even if I'm too dumb to grasp some of the finer points).

I'm so glad I jumped into this game.

This is the fun weird ps1 game with Bump-Slash gameplay and endless little secrets and easter eggs for the sake of fun or a laugh. I'm near end game and enjoyed it. 

I've heard the ending is currently a let down due to potential plot & DLC plans, but imo its a treat and I want to see the dlc/next game! These are the Anodyne game devs
The combat, exploration and music are all really good. The story wasn't for me, and veered into nonsensical / personal near the end. Overall I would strongly recommend it.

Something I said in my review for Anodyne 2: Return To Dust (a prior game by Analgesic productions) was something along the lines of, “the only people who could have made this game were Melos and Marina (the co-developers of both titles),” and while I think it's a succinct way to praise a piece of art for a particular brand creativity that I find endearing, it's a bit of a nothing statement at the end of the day.  Of course nobody else could have made Anodyne 2, because if somebody else tried, it wouldn't have been Anodyne 2 at all.  Every piece of art that's ever been created, even this very review, couldn't have been made under any other circumstances besides the exact ones which led its creator(s) to be creating it in the first place.  Of course, nothing epitomizes this more than Angeline Era, which hosts a unique blend of influences and ideas delivered in an equally unique way.  

If I were forced to describe what Analgesic’s overall “style” is, I would say that no matter what the context is, whether that be the gameplay, music, narrative, or what have you, the games exude so much confidence in themselves that they become pleasantly uncompromising.  Random NPC’s will have dialogue that ranges from nonsense jokes to legitimately thought provoking ideas, and they might be standing right next to each other. The gameplay can swing wildly from one tone, style, or influence to another, but it's done with so much confidence that you don't really have time to question it, you just have to stay in the moment and soak it all in. Even if you aren't exactly following along with the story at any particular moment, the game has the confidence to tell you that not only are you following along, you're actually one step ahead of the curve, and by the end, it will probably have been right to think so.  

This extends to all facets of the game, including the marketing and all the subsequent materials, first and foremost the concept of BUMPSLASHING.  All throughout the newsletters and the steam page (again you wanna talk about confidence, Melos has the characters roleplaying an introduction to the game lol) the term bumpslash appears again and again, and while it is obviously an integral part of the game, the confidence to basically will this term into existence makes me laugh every time I see it.  Anytime I've talked about this game with a friend, a huge smile forms on my face as the word ‘bumpslash’ exits my mouth, and I can't help but feel drawn in by that uncompromising vision of what Angeline Era has to offer. I mean, one of the two main creators is out there in the steam community hub writing multiple guides with tips on bosses he, himself made and making demands in the achievement descriptions asking for players to send in screenshots if they earned the super hard ones. It's infectious in a way, and even though I wouldn't say the game is a 10/10 or whatever, it definitely punches above its weight through sheer force of will alone, though it obviously helps that the game is pretty solid to begin with.  

I think that if they were even slightly less confident in the game, it would probably be a total mess.  It's not hard to imagine this kind of project being done by somebody else and it totally missing any number of marks, soiling the whole package.  But Angeline Era was made by Melos and Marina, who carried out their specific vision and brought it into reality in only a way that they can, and for that, I'm thankful.

Enjoyed the vast majority that the game had to offer. Really liked exploring for levels in the overworld and enjoyed the combat thoroughly. My only gripe with this game is the ending stretch. Took me about an hour and a half to get through it, and I didn't have much fun with it. Took the exploring for secrets and most of the combat away for exposition and an ending boss that was just annoying to fight. Still would recommend playing the game, especially if you enjoy exploring for secrets both in the overworld and inside of levels and unique combat system.

Typical high quality music, gameplay, leveldesign and mood from Analgesic Productions but with an impressively deeper narrative told in little bits and serious engaging with christianity, the bible and the people who got touched by them.
Only played on normal and it was still a good challenge. There are numerous other difficulties above.
Fantastic piece and very fun game.
It's weird, confusing, beautiful and, above all else, confusing. It also has bumpslash combat, which by itself is enough of a reason to play it.
Very cool retro zelda-like. Surprisingly solid platforming, too.

Another banger from Analgesic Productions. As an Irish person I think the use of Irish mythology in this game is really striking and well done, amd the combat has a lot of depth and complexity considering attacking is automatic with the bumpslash mechanics.

Wow. This is a beautiful, complex game that I'm still absorbing. As of writing this I've completed the main story. The vibe of this game is that of a lost hidden gem from the Dreamcast or Gamecube era, like playing a far more expansive Lost Kingdoms by FromSoft. "Searching" is such a fun mechanic that I wish it were in every game! In terms of the story, it's a compelling tale about conflict in the world that reflects the inner conflict of the protagonist, and it is extremely thought-provoking. I highly recommend Angeline Era to anyone up for a rewarding challenge in both action and narrative!

Angeline Era is an incredible achievement! It’s great as a game, with world class encounter design, but also manages to be a narrative work for adults. I’ll be thinking about this game for years to come!

As a fan of the developers' previous games Anodyne 1 & 2 as well as bump combat games like Ys, I thoroughly enjoyed Angeline Era. The game is filled with many secrets to find and the bump-slash 3D combat was wonderful. Really good bosses, too.

I love everything about Angeline Era, the look, the movement, the writing.  It’s a cohesive singular vision, easy to pick up and play and always pulling you onwards to discover more and more secrets. 

It’s a delight, you’ll love it , it’ll charm your socks off!
Too many games ask you to press buttons. This game is the warm, certain kindness of a fist bump. It will stay with you long after credits roll, and I'm so excited to come back to it again.

A classic JRPG modernized classically. 

A great example of creating a nostalgic game for a modern age. The theme, music, character design, and combat are all great examples of taking something old and building on it in a new way. The combat especially was interesting in that you don't have to attack but move into enemies to deal damage. It took a second to get used to but was really satisfying once I got the hang of it. 

I recommend this if you have a nostalgia for early Playstation JRPGs but want a little modern twist added in.

absolutely fantastic exploration/secret-hunting game. it surprises constantly, both mechanically and narratively, and does so all the way until the very end... and the combat is great, too! it really takes bump combat to depths i never thought the genre could support, being used to ys 1/2.

i really hope to see more indie games in the future that respect things that are often considered "outdated" nowadays, like the 4:3 aspect ratio or the very 2d-based gameplay design.

(note: my playtime is so low because i originally bought the game on itch, then decided to buy it here too once i finished it lol.)

Solid spooky cute fae-cursed atmosphere (*pristine* environment art) and solid gameplay, or it least it was for me on the automatically suggested 'High' setting. The story is excellent, it isn't always delivered in a digestable way or in a way that immediately strikes the heart, but often enough, it is. Generally, it's about struggling to lead a good life in a world that makes that difficult.

Some mild antispoilers to save you from various disappointments: [spoiler]Tets does not lead a triumphant life, and justice is not done in the end, nor is the injustice of any of it really properly discussed. The protagonist wanders too and fro under the unfluence of various ideological spells and curses. He has no beliefs of his own. If the player has beliefs, they will have to play against one belief or another to finish the game. The ending feels quite true, though. It do be that way sometimes.
There doesn't seem to be a way to do anything with the withered otherworld portals you find in various places in the world, which in many ways is effective, it makes the portal remnants more poignant as monuments to the earth-bound fairies' condition, they truly can't return. But I guess it reminds me... the game loves the idea of *secrets* but there don't seem to be any really great secrets in this game, nothing like the secrets of, eg, void stranger (which I've always found fascinating, but cruel and miserable, but if you're reading this, you may, somehow, like it, so give it a look) or Ooo (which I recommend highly) or any of the metroidbranias (eg Animal Well). It seems like deeper secrets might have been planned, (or dare we hope, might already be present, undiscovered), given that the player does at one point receive an item that can reopen the portals, but it doesn't amount to anything. Maybe it just got cut and that's the end of it.[/spoiler]

As someone who’s been following this game for a while now and is a huge fan of the devs other projects, I feel like it lives up to what I was hoping for from it in a lot of ways, while leaving me just a bit unsatisfied in others. 

To start off with the positives, I think it’s awesome that a game as bizarre and experimental as this one exists. It truly aims to deliver an experience unlike anything you’ve ever played before with its constantly shifting gameplay styles and its inventive takes on combat and exploration. In a way it almost feels like the kind of game you would see made for an internet arg that’s wouldn’t actually be playable by the general public.

I think my one major criticism for this game is that they could have done a better job of tying the story beats into the overall gameplay progression. It feels like the game switches randomly from delivering parts of an overarching story in each level to then doing nothing to progress it for long stretches at a time. I found a lot of these story heavy segments to be incredibly compelling and can’t help but feel like it would have enriched the experience of exploring the world if there was a more consistent focus on them.

I was also personally not a fan of the ending and how the game drags on for an extra hour and a half after you’ve reached a point where you’ve prepared yourself to be done with the game. I won’t spoil anything specific but was kind of just going “why are we doing this now” the longer it went on. 

One other issue I had which can’t really be helped is that the games secrets are a little too obtuse for a game this small. It sucks not having any kind of guide to help me find the things that I missed, there were certain areas I could see we’re locker behind some kind of hidden pathway that I just never found the answer to. 

In spite of my criticisms though I would still strongly recommend checking this game out. None of them subtract from how magical of an experience this game is overall, and I would love to see unique ideas like this get more support.

Very interesting game, it takes elements of PS1/N64 era games and lets you loose on a world that presents mystery after mystery. The bump combat may make it seem simple, but this game is anything but. It expects you to experiment and try lots of different things. There is a lot of discovery and wonder in this game.

Great and fun game. Rolled credits and going back in to get some stuff I missed. Great soundtrack, very mechanically fun, and a well built world. My only critique is that I wish they had taken even longer in development because it feels like there's even more here.

Few games capture the very old-school sense of discovery as well as [i]Angeline Era[/i]. It's almost like booting up an NES game you know nothing about. I'm often not sure what I'm supposed to do and might miss some things, but am simply excited to poke around. This has more QOL features than those classics, but the rewarding feeling is the same.

I don't think it would feel so compelling if the writing was boring. Thankfully, there's some genuinely reflective and powerful storytelling here. There's some quirky dialogue like you'd expect in modern indie RPGs, but then you get hit with an unusually direct confrontation of points of faith. That sincerity feels earned, as quests naturally weave those bigger ideas into their stories along with smaller moments of quiet reflection. 

Unfortunately, having an old school game emphasizing simplicity does cause some frustrations. The "Bumpslash" combat is a fun game of positioning, but often makes you feel like an uncontrollable pinball. And some of the areas that required a level of platforming didn't feel great. But I'm not here for the combat or platforming, so it didn't bother me too much.

There's a power to experiencing something so committed to a lost design ethos with genuine introspection. If you have memories of excitedly exploring old games with little to no context, this is definitely for you. This is going to stick with me for a while.

this is an analgesic joint so you already know the level design and the writing are going to be off the chain, so when i tell you this one is even better than that pedigree...

I finished the game and truly, this is one I will surely continue to think about. It’s a strange, moving, poetic, and deeply philosophical game that is absolutely worth seeing all the way through. I’ve never played anything quite like this before and I simply cannot recommend it enough!

this game is something really special. i usually am glued to google playing games bc im a pleb, but the way this game rewards exploration had me finish the whole thing without googling anything once. cannot recommend more.

When playing Angeline Era, you can almost imagine the creators watching over you from the gradient sky, giggling and smirking. From the opening moments you wake up on a ship cabin, bump the door off its hinges, leap over cargo and battle an exponentially increasing number of fish with lasers. Sixty-three fish later, the camera pans to the mainland, and the title card fades in. Whatever just happened, I'm in.

Bump-based combat sounds easy, but you have to be facing the correct direction and will bounce back off whatever you hit. Frequently you'll be put in arenas with 5-10 enemies with their own movement, damage and projectile patterns - forcing you to really consider positioning. On top of this is the unique exploration mechanics of a dedicated search button. On the world map, all levels are hidden until searched! Inside levels, searching suspicious spots nets you items, passages, environmental changes, or... more levels?

The game's tone is hard to pin down; initially optimistic, full of wonder, but increasingly melancholic. That's reflected really well in the soundtrack too. For most of the game major story beats are minimal, there's a constant sense that you're missing something. I don't think this always lands, especially when twists are heavily back-loaded and feel obtusely foreshadowed, if at all. The devious, almost trolling level design makes it hard to build the 4th wall back up again leading to the finale - but it is very fun.

The ending sequence takes a deeply personal, sometimes disturbing turn. It's a 3 hour stint past the point of no return, but one that will stick in my head for a long time.
I’ve only scratched the surface, and I can already tell this one is special.

An incredible repository of ideas and inspirations. Standout among its peers of modern action games for its restraint not limiting creativity.

Exploration and interaction with the world are two sides to the same bumpslash coin. You do not simply enter levels, you find them, and they are extremely varied in the goals and outcomes they provide. Bumpslash through several screens of enemies to make a path in the overworld. But you also intuit enemy patterns and gamestate behaviors that will reappear and reinvent themselves.

Throughout the playthrough (of which I took several breaks!) I immersed myself in the mythology and philosophy of Angeline Era. It was familiar in its themes (humans vs. the divine vs. the old ones) but refreshing in its presentation and carefully crafted setting. A fantasy adventure in a strange land that simultaneously takes place in the 50s, a time of rapidly accelerating progress. This game also rapidly accelerates, and its ending leaves you in suspended hang time. it was honestly pretty awesome. The game sets up "Find the 7 Mystical Items" and then puts you in freefall. A bold twist on expectations that puts the rest of the narrative into perspective.

Whatever you [i]think[/i] this game is, dispel that notion.  This game feels like a PSX game that you, in a fever dream, vaguely remember playing as a child but, in the present, realize that you cannot remember its name or find anyone else that knows what you're talking about.  It ventured to places that I couldn't have possibly predicted, [i]and then kept going[/i].  

The gameplay is deceptively simple: bump into things to interact with them.  There are a whole host of weapons and artifacts that enhance that basic mechanic, and at the end of my 14 hour playthrough (on Normal; there are several further difficulty levels) I realized I had somehow missed roughly [i]50% of them[/i].  This is a game that is absolutely teeming with secrets that I barely even scraped the surface of, so if you're a true explorer you'll likely be invested in this game's world for many more hours than I was.

On its face it may appear deceptively cute, but just as dreams can be whimsical, they can also swiftly become nightmares.  There will be moments of that strange, unsettling horror that you can only find in non-horror low-poly games, where the lack of detail lets the mind wander to dark places to fill in the blanks.

The soundtrack is phenomenal, and runs the gamut from upbeat and catchy, to exciting thrills, to uneasy atmospheric tension.

I struggled on the Normal difficulty, so if you're looking for proper retro challenge, you've come to the right place.

What a ride.  I could keep going, but I just finished the game and needed to express these feelings while they were hot.  This is a game that will stick with me...and maybe years from now I will remember it in fever dream, but this time realize that it did, in fact, exist.

Angeline Era is a game about the joys of discovery.

It's a brilliant game that certainly has novelty through its bump combat system (which hasn't been seen since what, Lagoon in 1990?), but is filled with so many smart decisions and personal touches that it's hard not to smile when you're playing it.

Its main claim to fame is the search mechanic, through which a single button reveals any nearby secrets. It's a simple, genius mechanic that gives the game that sense of wondrous discovery people romanticize in Metroid or The Legend of Zelda on NES, but neither without being obtuse nor too obvious. You start with (almost) everything you need to reveal all of the game's various secrets; finding them is just a matter of perspective. By simplifying the process of searching for secrets, there's no reason not to indulge your curiosity by poking around any stray odds or ends you find: it COULD just be something worthwhile. The search button is easily the smartest inclusion in Angeline Era, and what propels it higher up my list of 2025 indie releases.

Because Angeline Era is so focused on player discovery, I can't say too much else without spoiling the experience outright. The bump combat is simple, yet clever and honest; the presentation is charming and evocative of various N64, PS1, and PSP titles all at once; the core gameplay loop is addictive and perfect for short, bite-sized play sessions; the enemy design is extremely varied, with clever use of enemy formations to amp up difficulty; and dialogue is very down-to-earth, with a personal touch filled with sincerity - be it whimsical or serious.

It's not for everyone, but those who cut your teeth on games like old-school 90s RPGs, Zelda, or Ys should absolutely play Angeline Era. Those yearning for a game that affords them the freedom to explore and simply discover will find a lot to love here.

Really interesting Bump Combat game (there aren't many)! I really appreciate the way this game feels like the developers had a ton of fun ideas and just wanted to go with it at every point. There's sometimes some structural fatigue from this format, especially as it bumps (haha!) up against a more linear and backloaded narrative further in, but it's so distinctly itself that it still comes across as a success. I love the surrealist tones, and the mixture of Christian, Buddhist, and Celtic imagery is really fascinating. Always happy to see more traditional Fae, especially represented in this charming low-poly style.

This game evokes the specific vibe of early 2000s PC gaming in me, a time when consoles and home computers were still practically distinct markets. I'm not sure how to describe it to someone who wasn't there, but it felt like its own space separate from console gaming, with different conventions in convergent evolution. In that way Angeline Era eschews a lot of modern game design wisdom to deliver a sense of surprise and discovery I haven't felt from a game in years.
