[i]Angeline Era[/i] enters the canon of works in my little pad under the So Good It's Funny tier of personal favorites and declarations of greatness. See also Paul Thomas Anderson's [i]There Will Be Blood[/i], William Gibson's [i]Pattern Recognition[/i], Sergio Leone's [i]Once Upon a Time in the West.[/i] [i]GOD HAND.[/i] Works that, at nearly every turn, are so perfectly attuned to my scale of "greatness," doing things both just as I'd like and subverting expectations and showing me things I didn't know I wanted, to the extent that all I can do is laugh in the face of something that entertaining, endearing, wrenching, etc.

Beware the Pussle Master.

I'm a longtime Analgesic fan, the second [i]Anodyne[/i] being one of the inaugural games christening my shift to PC gaming. That game captivated me in ways few still do, elaborating on its predecessor and cementing my love for Melos and Marina's work. Since then their brand of cerebral genre fare has ~stimulated~ both the craving for ergonomic gaming platforming and distinct visuals as well as filled the void of impersonal works in gaming. Even amongst indie studios composed of single-digit teams, games so often lack the confidence to be presented by the artists behind them; a distinct detachment despite the heaps of work and creativity demanded. 

You have your "celebrity devs" like Toby Fox and Rami Ismail, and it feels as if we ought to be able to rattle off the names of more the way directors (and ~cinematographers~, if you're [i]really[/i] apt; bonus points if you can talk [i]production designers[/i]) and musicians are discussed. For me, Melos and Marina at Analgesic feel so present in [i]Angeline Era[/i] due to the consistent threads embedded in their work. The narrative themes of the Asian-American experience, loss and loneliness, and the rally of nature in a world stricken by mankind and its meddling. There are quietly unsettling forces and lucid reflection aplenty in [i]Angeline Era[/i].

[i]Only, this time, they're all out of bubble gum.[/i]

So, having bested Han-Tani's Challenge (see how forward the devs are!) I have spent probably more time on first pass with [i]Angeline Era[/i] than your average player. The harder difficulty is [i]brutal[/i] on several counts, yet I would have had it no other way. More than past Analgesic games, [i]Angeline Era[/i] engages with fine-tuned encounter design and combat mechanics that beg to be pushed to their limits by a stripped-down higher difficulty. It's like playing [i]Super Mario Sunshine[/i] without F.L.U.D.D. or something else suitably spartan. And by George it is beautiful.

I recommend Han-Tani's Challenge to everyone.

I wish Han-Tani's Challenge upon no one.

If dying over 200 times to the penultimate boss (PAIN) is really not your idea of fun (give it a try!) that's okay. We got more hits! 

"So Good It's Funny." 

[i]Angeline Era's[/i] structure, the world on display both aesthetically and narratively--as well as mechanically--is an absolute treat to live in. The world map's "plain-sight levels" are a part of the shtick on the Steam page and are something to which you're likely already privy. However, consider now poking around these levels, the little towns and hovels across Era's verdant Gallic continent, searching about with your literal magnifying glass only to warp elsewhere.

Mischief! Magic!

Consider the wide variety of endearing and honestly really quite insane denizens, both human and otherwise, you'll encounter. Strange butlers and shopkeepers who love your healthy bones. Deviant miners. Bug bears! [i]Angeline Era[/i] boasts a rogues gallery of cute and grotesque critters to chat with and murder (often in the same breath) that immediately recalled the incredible fauna across the [i]Pikmin[/i] games. The variety of standard bumpslash-fodder and towering bosses is impressive, many enemies twisting the game's mechanics and tugging your kneejerk reactions in different directions so as to keep you on your toes.

And really, that's what [i]Angeline Era[/i] does, in the simplest way of expressing it. It keeps you guessing. Very funny that it is so clearly reverent towards From Software games while eschewing the trends in modern action games hammered by [i]Dark Souls[/i], inhabiting an adjacent reality where every Nintendo [i]Zelda[/i] is just as radically strange in comparison to its predecessor as [i]Majora's Mask[/i] was to [i]Ocarina of Time.[/i] The bumpslash, a bloodletting art I think I can say with a shred of immodesty I am [i]now quite the goddarn journeyman in[/i], rewires the fundamentals of action gaming, freeing the player from inputting commands and instead demanding focus on things like spacing and angling relative to enemies. The use of Tets' gun as a purely vertical tool, calling to mind vertical STG's like [i]Ikaruga[/i], again provides the player with a familiar dish, asking them to try it in a new arrangement.

Try that pizza with a knife and fork! You might find you enjoy it more..!

There are many 2025 releases I enjoyed. There are many more I likely would enjoy. I have a difficult time imagining that there is a video game from two-thousand and twenty-five, [i]quite[/i] the year, that I would enjoy anywhere near as much as the time I spent with [i]Angeline Era.[/i]

Oh!

And I didn't even mention the score! The sweet shredding tones to which you may or may not hurl yourself time and again at the scourge of Era to, or the mysterious whimsy of stomping about the countryside. Music in games is very often an easy point to compliment, not least because the medium attracts a wide variety of extremely talented musicians given freedom of expression where films often require more restraint, and popular music more structure. [i]Angeline Era's[/i] tunes are off the goddarn charts. I can't elaborate more, so do yourself a favor and pop into your favorite non-warmonger-owned digital music outlet for a sampling of some of those sweet beats.

With a little patience, I truly believe this is a game that can speak to such a vast pool of video game players, even outside of the (hopefully growing) niche that identifies as Analgesicheads. Even having just today beat it, my draconian run of the game leaves me ready to start fresh and explore at a more languid pace, sampling all of the cute items and accoutrements that would have literally made me combust had I dare tried to use them during my Challenge playthrough. A brief respite, for a Bumpslasher's work is never done, and remember:

To shop, bump something, then bump me.

Angeline Era is the latest Analgesic Productions game, but if that doesn't mean anything to you that's perfectly okay- I think it might be the most widely accessible game they've made yet. However, if you're a returning fan, fear not; this does not mean the game is any less weird or surreal than their previous titles. For the TL;DR of how I felt, I think this is by far the best [i]game[/i] they've created so far, and is absolutely worth picking up for the gameplay alone, even if I still don't know how I felt about the game's story and ending. The graphics and music are all fantastic yet again, like other Analgesic works. More on all that below...

I played Ys I & II once for like twenty minutes at my friends house. Despite the short time with it, it still left an impression on me: since then i've occasionally asked myself "why don't more games try bump combat?". It's a fun system where all you need to do is walk into your enemies to attack them. Angeline Era's gameplay revolves around this system, and it is a joy to play. Melos Han-Tani (the game designer) really understands what makes bump combat interesting, from having to mitigate knockback so you don't get slammed into dangerous situations, to needing to plunge yourself headlong into peril and bullets since your "attack" doesn't have any range. The levels are very well designed, with plenty of opportunities to flex your skills both in 3D platforming and massacring the various enemies you'll face along the way. Everything about the design feels like something they added just because they thought it was a fun idea... at one point there is an hour or so long mining minigame where you bump rocks to get ore. 

The second huge aspect to the gameplay that is a little less advertised is the "Search" system. To find any level on the overworld, you need to hold Triangle (or the equivalent button) to initiate a "search", which will reveal a level if one is hidden on the tile you're on. However, this also can be used inside levels. So if you find a suspicious place while bumping and slashing around, you can once again hold the button and uncover something. Well, maybe "uncover" is the wrong word... sometimes it'll teleport you, spawn things, change the level, it can pretty much do anything. Because of these systems, I can definitively say that if you love secrets and exploration [b]this is the game for you![/b] It feels really rewarding to notice a strange patch on the ground, and having it teleport you to a completely different secret area after searching it. All of Analgesic's games that i've played have exuded this explorative energy (Anodyne 2 has you clipping out of bounds for collectables), and i'm glad the trend continues here. One criticism with this however, is that the secrets go too far sometimes... A lot of the secrets are "secrets for secrets sake", which is totally fine, but when you've been teleported to a secret area from inside a secret area from inside a secret area from inside a secret area, and all there is inside of it is an NPC that says a line about trees, you start pressing the search button on every tile in the room like you're trying to find a hidden door in DOOM. 

The story is... interesting. I'm pretty used to Analgesic's writing style at this point, so reviewing it is kind of like trying to gauge how spicy a hot sauce is to somebody who has never tried it before... but it feels very tangental and sometimes unfocused despite having enjoyable parts. I really enjoyed Anodyne 2's story and emotional themes, didn't really get anything out of Sephonie's at all, and Angeline Era is smack dab in the middle. Analgesic's storytelling style is very fragmented, with focus on specific interesting worlds rather than the greater picture as a whole- an example of this would be walking into a level and suddenly getting a long backstory about a blacksmith out of nowhere. Both Anodyne and Sephonie do this, but never to the point of muddying what the core story is about. Angeline Era kind of goes all over the place, particularly in the final act, to the point where I didn't really know or understand what was going on by the end of it. When the credits rolled, all I could really say to myself was "sure, OK". I wasn't mad or disappointed, I was just sort of confused and underwhelmed. I don't even remember if I actually got all of the [strike]crystal stars[/strike] Bicones, I just stumbled upon "the point of no return" fight while exploring and suddenly was entrenched in plot development after plot development for two hours. I think the story was maybe just bogged down with too many moving parts and sudden story shifts to stay cohesive. The writing itself is totally fine though and on par with other Analgesic works- funny, surreal, quirky, sometimes scary. A bunch of jokes made me laugh out loud, and there's a lot of them in Angeline Era... so even if you find the writing overly quirky or weird to start I think you'll eventually find something to enjoy here. You can even see the "writing" in the level design too, with lots of pretty good gags in various traps, level names, and secrets (loved the chest guy).

All in all, I think this game is a fantastic entry into the Analgesic catalogue, even with my few criticisms. If you've never played one of their games before, this is a fantastic start. It's fun and nonlinear, rewards exploration, has great combat, and is deeply funny at times. High recommend, just prepare yourself to expect the unexpected! This game is extremely confident and never strays away from what it wants to do, even if it's weird or off-beat. For that, I have nothing but respect.

PS. Anodyne 2 had a decently large secret postgame, and I guarantee I haven't found everything there is to find in Angeline Era. Just thinking about going back has me excited, it'll be fun to explore what I missed!

one of the most glowing recommendations i can possibly give, this game is for everyone who likes stuff that’s a little bit out there (perhaps even so compared to other analgesic games). i’ve waited years for this and it’s everything i imagined it would be and so, so much more. the visual style is so unique yet familiar, so breathtaking, at times even literally stunning. the ost is comfortably melos’ best soundtrack yet, they continuously outdo themself in this game alone. marina’s characters and story telling are as always, cryptic but comfortable, familiar yet fable, warm and welcoming yet so, so very distant. so very cold. everything feels as though something is missing, purposefully of course, to the point that even at its most beautiful you are never quite at ease. you start with the hand of those above guiding your actions until you slowly, horribly realize your actions are your own. this world was made for you to explore until it does everything in its power to push you out. play angeline era. a beloved bible verse passed down through families that now with time seems ominous. heaven is waiting for you. can’t you see it?

Absolutely wild RPG with some great graphics, music, and combat through the fun "bumpslash" system. Although I couldn't get into the story that much, I did really enjoy the gameplay - it has some of the best exploration of any indie game all year.
this game is a monumental achievement of combat and level design. deep, beautiful and complex but also very simple and easy to pick up. do yourself a favour and play it

They basically made a new Dreamcast game, and I'm not talking about some arcade port or shoot-em-up. Like, the aesthetic and gameplay is pitch-perfect. It's got a bit of Ys in it with the "bumpslash" combat (basically you attack enemies by bumping into them). Each stage is a bit like the recent 3D Mario games, these somewhat short levels that explore one idea really, really well. It's impressive how much variety there is. It makes discovering new levels and secrets really rewarding. It's definitely the most "game design-y" game they've made. The script has this quality of having been translated from Japanese into English even though English is the original language, it's very fun and authentically weird. But also dark! There's some surprisingly emotional stuff in this game, especially in the later half of the game.

I think theres a lot of strong emotion about religion in communities like this that will cause people to vilify religious ideas (namely christian) on sight and i think its important for queer media to actually engage with it all in earnest.

I enjoyed the various mini games within the game very much. I only got 3 endings before what I'm assuming is the "main win" ending, and don't intend to play for more (though I did do *one* more out of curiosity, it was frustrating because I had to replay an entire level from the start to get to it, and the additional ending wasn't even fun), but it was 4.5 hours that I enjoyed. At this point I'm confident that I would enjoy it *less* if I continued to play for additional endings, but I don't have to, and if I'd found them organically I'd probably have appreciated them, so yay.

The inspirational dark humor of ending 2 appealed to me very much, and the general overarching theme of "don't spend your whole life doing things you don't enjoy" is a delight (maybe it inspired me to not continue playing for additional endings when I don't want to!)

Peppered is an experience. It’s wacky, it’s over the top, and the choices matter—almost too much or too little at the same time. The butterfly effect is in full effect with this one.

I can’t say I’ve played any game quite like Peppered, which makes the experience of playing this game worth the purchase price alone. The sprites look beautiful, and the characters are likable, charming, and unique. The platforming is tight and responsive. Levels are not too difficult, and each has its own mechanics, keeping every level feeling fresh. The game has multiple endings, which adds replayability. You’re going to want to replay this one—the endings are vastly different, and certain decisions can lock or unlock entire portions of the game.

Peppered is worth the full price tag, but get it on sale if you can. If you got it from the recent Playful Platformers Humble Bundle, then I’m convinced you damn near stole this game value-wise.

P.S. — I ran into no performance issues while running Peppered on my LED Steam Deck.

PEPPERED: an existential platformer is a cleverly subversive indie title developed by Mostly Games and published in collaboration with Black Soup, blending traditional side-scrolling platforming with branching narrative design and sharp existential satire. At first glance, it presents itself as a colorful pixel-art adventure about saving the world, but beneath its playful exterior lies a surprisingly layered exploration of failure, agency, and the absurdity of heroism. Rather than simply challenging players to overcome obstacles with precision jumps and boss fights, PEPPERED asks what it actually means to try—and what it means to fail.

The premise is immediately unconventional. You play as an underappreciated office intern who, in a moment of impulsive bravery, attempts to prevent the end of the world after the designated hero mysteriously vanishes. Armed with a powerful artifact known as the Life Star, your character sets out to confront looming catastrophe. However, your actions quickly spiral into unintended consequences, and instead of being celebrated as a savior, you are misunderstood, misrepresented, and sometimes outright vilified. This narrative framing establishes the tone early: this is not a straightforward hero’s journey, but rather a chaotic examination of intention versus outcome.

The most distinctive feature of PEPPERED is its one-shot structure. Unlike conventional platformers where failure simply resets the player to a checkpoint, this game incorporates outcomes—including defeat—into the evolving storyline. Losing a boss fight does not necessarily mean retrying until you succeed; it can instead send the narrative down an entirely new path. Characters react differently, environments shift, and the broader plot adapts to your choices and missteps. This design philosophy transforms every moment into a meaningful branch in the timeline. As a result, no two playthroughs feel identical, and experimentation becomes central to experiencing the full scope of the story.

Gameplay combines classic platforming mechanics with occasional stealth segments, puzzle-solving, and interactive story beats. Movement feels responsive, with traditional jumping and hazard navigation forming the foundation of exploration. Boss encounters often mix reflex-based action with situational decision-making, reinforcing the idea that outcomes depend as much on player judgment as skill. Some segments introduce unusual mechanics or unexpected shifts in gameplay, maintaining unpredictability and reinforcing the game’s surreal tone. While certain platforming sections may occasionally feel mechanically simple, the variety of narrative consequences ensures that even routine challenges carry weight.

Visually, PEPPERED embraces vibrant pixel art infused with expressive character animation and exaggerated design. Environments range from mundane office interiors to surreal landscapes populated by eccentric NPCs. The aesthetic complements the writing, which is sharp, self-aware, and frequently humorous. Dialogue leans heavily into satire, poking fun at corporate structures, media narratives, and the idea of predetermined destiny. The humor is not merely decorative; it supports the game’s thematic exploration of existential anxiety and societal expectation. The soundtrack and sound effects further enhance this mood, alternating between energetic tracks and more subdued tones depending on the path you take.

The branching narrative system provides substantial replay value. With multiple endings and numerous alternate routes, players are encouraged to revisit earlier decisions to see how different outcomes unfold. Some paths may be unexpectedly mundane or anticlimactic, while others escalate into dramatic or bizarre scenarios. This unpredictability reinforces the game’s central idea that life—and by extension, gameplay—rarely follows a single linear trajectory. Importantly, the writing ensures that even less heroic outcomes feel narratively satisfying rather than punitive.

While the ambition of its design occasionally results in uneven pacing or experimental mechanics that may not resonate with every player, the overall execution remains cohesive. The occasional mechanical awkwardness is offset by the strength of its narrative vision. The game understands that its core appeal lies not in technical complexity but in the interplay between player choice and consequence.

Ultimately, PEPPERED: an existential platformer stands out as a creative fusion of genre conventions and narrative experimentation. It takes the familiar framework of a pixel-art platformer and transforms it into a meditation on identity, responsibility, and the unpredictability of outcomes. By treating failure as an integral part of storytelling rather than a setback, it challenges traditional design expectations and invites players to embrace uncertainty. For those seeking an experience that blends humor, branching narrative, and platforming fundamentals into something both playful and introspective, PEPPERED delivers a memorable and refreshingly unconventional journey.

Rating: 9/10

I had high hopes for this game after seeing it at the OTK Indie Game Expo, but after finishing the first chapter, I mostly walked away confused.

[spoiler]The premise is strong on paper: A hundred years ago, the God of Death was chained and must be periodically fed stars to remain bound. These stars are manufactured by a corporation and delivered by a guy named Theodore. The game starts at the 100th immortality day but Theodore has gone missing and, unless they are found, the God of Death will return and everyone becomes mortal again. Enter the main protagonist, who is just interviewing for a junior coffee-making position at the company that manufactures said stars.

Right at the start, the game lets you choose between "getting a job" and "saving the world". Choosing the former leads to a short but genuinely funny sequence where you rapidly climb the corporate ladder, become CEO, and are later guillotined by Death's henchmen for incompetence. This segment is part of the demo and suggests a level of reactivity and that unfortunately doesn't really reappear anywhere else in the first chapter. Maybe later chapters offer more meaningful choices. Maybe this was simply a polished PR moment for the demo. Either way, it sets expectations the rest of the chapter does not meet.

Once you commit to saving the world, the protagonist steals a star and escapes toward the site of the feeding ceremony through a series of platforming sections, environmental puzzles, and occasional character interactions. Credit where it's due: the presentation is excellent. The pixel art is detailed and expressive, and the chiptune soundtrack has a surprisingly strong identity. The core gameplay loop feels solid and responsive. I also liked the worldbuilding idea that the environment is full of lethal traps because death is meaningless in this world -- dying just leads to instant respawning, and the traps exist to stave off boredom in eternal life.

[/spoiler]The early part of the chapter leans heavily into satire of corporate culture and complacency, and this is where the game is at its most coherent thematically. Unfortunately, things start to fall apart once the chapter's main antagonist is introduced[spoiler]: a police officer trying to arrest the protagonist for stealing the star.

This character is framed as goofy comic relief and as commentary on the toothlessness of law enforcement in a late-capitalist dystopia where all meaning has been reduced to work and entertainment. There's an interesting question here: what even is justice in a deathless world where mortality no longer underpins morality, and mere existence feels like its own punishment? The game clearly wants this officer to be a sympathetic figure -- probably someone who will eventually join the protagonist against greater evils in later chapters.

The problem is that the character simply isn't likable or compelling enough for that arc to land. Their motivations are unclear, their behavior is more irritating than endearing, and the writing doesn't do enough to make their role intelligible. By the end of the chapter, the game seems to expect the player to feel sympathy for them, but I mostly felt annoyed and confused.

The chapter concludes with the God of Death escaping (potentially preventable, leading to an early ending), and mortality returns to the world. This is a genuinely interesting turning point. From a gameplay perspective, it raises the stakes. From a narrative perspective, it suddenly makes the previously indifferent inhabitants a lot more relatable (and a lot more mortal). But as a first act, the chapter leaves us knowing almost no one. The one character we've spent the most time with -- the police officer -- disappears, and the overall shape of the story remains frustratingly vague.[/spoiler]

This left me pretty bummed. The presentation is stellar, even if heavily inspired by Undertale. But there is far less actual substance here than the presentation suggests. Characters are underdeveloped, themes are introduced but not meaningfully connected to each other or to the plot, and the storytelling feels scattered. The premise is compelling; the delivery is not.

It's possible that later chapters retroactively strengthen this opening, but I'm not sure I am going to stick around and find out. Regardless, I appreciate the game's ambitions. It has high production values and a surprisingly large scope -- five chapters in total -- all developed by a small team of four. Even if the execution stumbles in my opinion, the game still manages to sketch out some interesting ideas, and for that reason alone I'd like to recommend it.

Overall a great game and can't wait for more from these developers. The Art, Music, Concept, and Gameplay are pretty great. It is a shame this game hasn't gotten as much attention as I find it to be a hidden indie gem. 

The games biggest issue (arguably a good issue to have) is that it left me wanting for more from some of the paths that you can take. I would have loved to explore some storylines deeper but instead they just end. This leaves the characters feeling a little underdeveloped and me wanting more. I think this could have been fixed if a select few endings (8, 3 and maybe 10) were expanded upon like ending 9 which has two whole sections dedicated to it and it alone. (Granted this is a big ask and honestly unreasonable for a very small dev team).

Also I wish ending 9 didn't lean so much into [spoiler] the golf mini game. I'm fine with that chapter existing and I found it fun (but wished its levels were more easy to replay) but having the final boss and final section being all golf a poor choice for game that is supposed to be a platformer. Although I will say that the golf remains fresh throughout and isn't a terrible time if you are worried about that. [/spoiler]

Don't let my criticism, detract you from buying the game. I still think it is great and I can't wait for future games. 

I'm glad that Manly has once again been a great advertiser for what indie games to take a look at.

PEPPERED: an existential platformer is a cleverly subversive indie title developed by Mostly Games and published in collaboration with Black Soup, blending traditional side-scrolling platforming with branching narrative design and sharp existential satire. At first glance, it presents itself as a colorful pixel-art adventure about saving the world, but beneath its playful exterior lies a surprisingly layered exploration of failure, agency, and the absurdity of heroism. Rather than simply challenging players to overcome obstacles with precision jumps and boss fights, PEPPERED asks what it actually means to try—and what it means to fail.

The premise is immediately unconventional. You play as an underappreciated office intern who, in a moment of impulsive bravery, attempts to prevent the end of the world after the designated hero mysteriously vanishes. Armed with a powerful artifact known as the Life Star, your character sets out to confront looming catastrophe. However, your actions quickly spiral into unintended consequences, and instead of being celebrated as a savior, you are misunderstood, misrepresented, and sometimes outright vilified. This narrative framing establishes the tone early: this is not a straightforward hero’s journey, but rather a chaotic examination of intention versus outcome.

The most distinctive feature of PEPPERED is its one-shot structure. Unlike conventional platformers where failure simply resets the player to a checkpoint, this game incorporates outcomes—including defeat—into the evolving storyline. Losing a boss fight does not necessarily mean retrying until you succeed; it can instead send the narrative down an entirely new path. Characters react differently, environments shift, and the broader plot adapts to your choices and missteps. This design philosophy transforms every moment into a meaningful branch in the timeline. As a result, no two playthroughs feel identical, and experimentation becomes central to experiencing the full scope of the story.

Gameplay combines classic platforming mechanics with occasional stealth segments, puzzle-solving, and interactive story beats. Movement feels responsive, with traditional jumping and hazard navigation forming the foundation of exploration. Boss encounters often mix reflex-based action with situational decision-making, reinforcing the idea that outcomes depend as much on player judgment as skill. Some segments introduce unusual mechanics or unexpected shifts in gameplay, maintaining unpredictability and reinforcing the game’s surreal tone. While certain platforming sections may occasionally feel mechanically simple, the variety of narrative consequences ensures that even routine challenges carry weight.

Visually, PEPPERED embraces vibrant pixel art infused with expressive character animation and exaggerated design. Environments range from mundane office interiors to surreal landscapes populated by eccentric NPCs. The aesthetic complements the writing, which is sharp, self-aware, and frequently humorous. Dialogue leans heavily into satire, poking fun at corporate structures, media narratives, and the idea of predetermined destiny. The humor is not merely decorative; it supports the game’s thematic exploration of existential anxiety and societal expectation. The soundtrack and sound effects further enhance this mood, alternating between energetic tracks and more subdued tones depending on the path you take.

The branching narrative system provides substantial replay value. With multiple endings and numerous alternate routes, players are encouraged to revisit earlier decisions to see how different outcomes unfold. Some paths may be unexpectedly mundane or anticlimactic, while others escalate into dramatic or bizarre scenarios. This unpredictability reinforces the game’s central idea that life—and by extension, gameplay—rarely follows a single linear trajectory. Importantly, the writing ensures that even less heroic outcomes feel narratively satisfying rather than punitive.

While the ambition of its design occasionally results in uneven pacing or experimental mechanics that may not resonate with every player, the overall execution remains cohesive. The occasional mechanical awkwardness is offset by the strength of its narrative vision. The game understands that its core appeal lies not in technical complexity but in the interplay between player choice and consequence.

Ultimately, PEPPERED: an existential platformer stands out as a creative fusion of genre conventions and narrative experimentation. It takes the familiar framework of a pixel-art platformer and transforms it into a meditation on identity, responsibility, and the unpredictability of outcomes. By treating failure as an integral part of storytelling rather than a setback, it challenges traditional design expectations and invites players to embrace uncertainty. For those seeking an experience that blends humor, branching narrative, and platforming fundamentals into something both playful and introspective, PEPPERED delivers a memorable and refreshingly unconventional journey.

Peppered is an experience. It’s wacky, it’s over the top, and the choices matter—almost too much or too little at the same time. The butterfly effect is in full effect with this one.

The sprites look beautiful, and the characters are likable, charming, and unique. The platforming is tight and responsive. Levels are not too difficult, and each has its own mechanics, keeping every level feeling fresh. The game has multiple endings, which adds replayability. Peppered is worth the full price tag, but get it on sale if you can. If you got it from the recent Playful Platformers Humble Bundle, then I’m convinced you damn near stole this game value-wise.

P.S. — I ran into no performance issues while running Peppered on my LED Steam Deck.

I had high hopes for this game after seeing it at the OTK Indie Game Expo, but after finishing the first chapter, I mostly walked away confused.

[spoiler]The premise is strong on paper: A hundred years ago, the God of Death was chained and must be periodically fed stars to remain bound. These stars are manufactured by a corporation and delivered by a guy named Theodore. The game starts at the 100th immortality day but Theodore has gone missing and, unless they are found, the God of Death will return and everyone becomes mortal again. Enter the main protagonist, who is just interviewing for a junior coffee-making position at the company that manufactures said stars.

Right at the start, the game lets you choose between "getting a job" and "saving the world". Choosing the former leads to a short but genuinely funny sequence where you rapidly climb the corporate ladder, become CEO, and are later guillotined by Death's henchmen for incompetence. This segment is part of the demo and suggests a level of reactivity and that unfortunately doesn't really reappear anywhere else in the first chapter. Maybe later chapters offer more meaningful choices. Maybe this was simply a polished PR moment for the demo. Either way, it sets expectations the rest of the chapter does not meet.

Once you commit to saving the world, the protagonist steals a star and escapes toward the site of the feeding ceremony through a series of platforming sections, environmental puzzles, and occasional character interactions. Credit where it's due: the presentation is excellent. The pixel art is detailed and expressive, and the chiptune soundtrack has a surprisingly strong identity. The core gameplay loop feels solid and responsive. I also liked the worldbuilding idea that the environment is full of lethal traps because death is meaningless in this world -- dying just leads to instant respawning, and the traps exist to stave off boredom in eternal life.

[/spoiler]The early part of the chapter leans heavily into satire of corporate culture and complacency, and this is where the game is at its most coherent thematically. Unfortunately, things start to fall apart once the chapter's main antagonist is introduced[spoiler]: a police officer trying to arrest the protagonist for stealing the star.

This character is framed as goofy comic relief and as commentary on the toothlessness of law enforcement in a late-capitalist dystopia where all meaning has been reduced to work and entertainment. There's an interesting question here: what even is justice in a deathless world where mortality no longer underpins morality, and mere existence feels like its own punishment? The game clearly wants this officer to be a sympathetic figure -- probably someone who will eventually join the protagonist against greater evils in later chapters.

The problem is that the character simply isn't likable or compelling enough for that arc to land. Their motivations are unclear, their behavior is more irritating than endearing, and the writing doesn't do enough to make their role intelligible. By the end of the chapter, the game seems to expect the player to feel sympathy for them, but I mostly felt annoyed and confused.

The chapter concludes with the God of Death escaping (potentially preventable, leading to an early ending), and mortality returns to the world. This is a genuinely interesting turning point. From a gameplay perspective, it raises the stakes. From a narrative perspective, it suddenly makes the previously indifferent inhabitants a lot more relatable (and a lot more mortal). But as a first act, the chapter leaves us knowing almost no one. The one character we've spent the most time with -- the police officer -- disappears, and the overall shape of the story remains frustratingly vague.[/spoiler]

This left me pretty bummed. The presentation is stellar, even if heavily inspired by Undertale. But there is far less actual substance here than the presentation suggests. Characters are underdeveloped, themes are introduced but not meaningfully connected to each other or to the plot, and the storytelling feels scattered. The premise is compelling; the delivery is not.

It's possible that later chapters retroactively strengthen this opening, but I'm not sure I am going to stick around and find out. Regardless, I appreciate the game's ambitions. It has high production values and a surprisingly large scope -- five chapters in total -- all developed by a small team of four. Even if the execution stumbles in my opinion, the game still manages to sketch out some interesting ideas, and for that reason alone I'd like to recommend it.

ADEN is a bold and eccentric side-scrolling action game developed and published by takayakata that thrives on unpredictability, expressive motion, and a playful disregard for genre boundaries. At first glance it presents itself as a colorful pixel-art action title with anime sensibilities, but it quickly becomes clear that ADEN is built around rhythm, spectacle, and personality rather than conventional combat systems. The result is an experience that feels energetic and theatrical, as if every fight is meant to be performed rather than simply won.

You play as Toaka, a mysterious girl who quite literally falls into the world with little memory of who she is or why she is there. Guided by an equally strange mentor and empowered by the magic of dance, she sets out to confront bizarre enemies known as purgers, whose existence threatens the stability of the world. The narrative unfolds in fragments, blending absurd humor, surreal imagery, and occasional dark undertones. Rather than grounding itself in a serious heroic arc, the story leans into dreamlike logic and eccentric character interactions, allowing tone and emotion to shift freely from playful to unsettling without warning.

The core gameplay revolves around movement and timing rather than traditional attack strings. Toaka’s combat style feels closer to a dance than a brawl, with dashes, evasive maneuvers, and rhythmic positioning forming the backbone of every encounter. Enemies are defeated not simply by dealing damage, but by maintaining flow and building momentum until a climactic release occurs. This emphasis on rhythm encourages players to stay constantly in motion, weaving through attacks and hazards in a way that feels fluid and expressive rather than aggressive or rigid.

Boss battles are where ADEN truly shines. These encounters abandon subtlety in favor of spectacle, transforming the screen into a vibrant storm of color, sound, and movement. Patterns often resemble bullet-hell designs, demanding sharp awareness and instinctive dodging while staying in sync with the underlying rhythm. Success comes from learning patterns, trusting timing, and embracing the game’s dance-like logic. These fights are memorable not only for their difficulty, but for how theatrically they present themselves, often feeling like climactic performances rather than conventional boss encounters.

Outside of boss fights, standard stages serve as rhythmic playgrounds that gradually introduce new ideas and challenges. Early sections are approachable, easing players into the movement system and visual language, while later stages layer complexity through faster pacing and denser enemy patterns. While some regular encounters can feel comparatively simple next to the bombast of boss battles, they act as effective build-ups, reinforcing mechanics and maintaining a sense of flow between major set pieces.

Visually, ADEN embraces expressive pixel art that balances cuteness with chaos. Character animations are lively and exaggerated, environments burst with color, and effects are used liberally to emphasize motion and impact. The art direction reinforces the game’s whimsical tone while ensuring that even the most visually intense moments remain readable. This visual energy is matched by a strong soundtrack that leans heavily into rhythm and melody, often syncing directly with gameplay. Music doesn’t merely accompany the action; it actively shapes it, reinforcing the sensation that combat is meant to be felt as much as played.

Progression is light but meaningful, focusing on mastery rather than stat accumulation. As players advance, they unlock new abilities and options that subtly expand how Toaka can move and respond to threats. These additions don’t fundamentally alter the game’s core identity, but they add variety and encourage experimentation, especially for players interested in replaying stages or refining their performance. The game rewards familiarity and rhythm awareness more than brute force, making improvement feel personal and skill-driven.

ADEN’s tone and structure may not resonate with everyone. Its surreal storytelling, unconventional combat logic, and frequent tonal shifts can feel disorienting to players expecting a more traditional action game. However, these same qualities are precisely what give the game its identity. It embraces strangeness rather than smoothing it out, and its rough edges feel intentional rather than accidental, reinforcing the sense that this is a deeply personal creative project.

Overall, ADEN is a confident and imaginative indie title that prioritizes expression, rhythm, and spectacle over convention. It blends action, music, and surreal storytelling into a cohesive experience that feels playful, intense, and unapologetically strange. While it may not appeal to players seeking straightforward mechanics or grounded narratives, those drawn to experimental design and energetic presentation will find ADEN to be a memorable journey—one that feels less like playing a game and more like dancing through controlled chaos.

I played YumeCore a while back and bought this because I liked that one, even if it was somewhat janky. It had a lot of heart.

This is much the same, except with a lot more polish and a weird plot that kept my interest and somehow managed to actually surprise me with a few twists. Also, pretty much every endgame stage is masterful in presentation.

I have to state this, however: playing it on hard mode is mandatory. It's not as hard as it sounds. It really is more of a normal mode, and normal is more like easy. On normal, you're not getting the intended experience and the game will not last you nearly as long. Normal lets you just immediately continue at any point in the middle of a fight with no real penalty, and nothing hits hard enough to justify caution; Hard works on a checkpoint system and is balanced to provide some semblance of challenge. The end result is that normal feels more like a VN where you're there for the plot. In any case, if you can't "git gud" there's an item shop that lets you buy permanent upgrades, so hard really is not as bad as it sounds.

ADEN is a bold and eccentric side-scrolling action game developed and published by takayakata that thrives on unpredictability, expressive motion, and a playful disregard for genre boundaries. At first glance it presents itself as a colorful pixel-art action title with anime sensibilities, but it quickly becomes clear that ADEN is built around rhythm, spectacle, and personality rather than conventional combat systems. The result is an experience that feels energetic and theatrical, as if every fight is meant to be performed rather than simply won.

You play as Toaka, a mysterious girl who quite literally falls into the world with little memory of who she is or why she is there. Guided by an equally strange mentor and empowered by the magic of dance, she sets out to confront bizarre enemies known as purgers, whose existence threatens the stability of the world. The narrative unfolds in fragments, blending absurd humor, surreal imagery, and occasional dark undertones. Rather than grounding itself in a serious heroic arc, the story leans into dreamlike logic and eccentric character interactions, allowing tone and emotion to shift freely from playful to unsettling without warning.

The core gameplay revolves around movement and timing rather than traditional attack strings. Toaka’s combat style feels closer to a dance than a brawl, with dashes, evasive maneuvers, and rhythmic positioning forming the backbone of every encounter. Enemies are defeated not simply by dealing damage, but by maintaining flow and building momentum until a climactic release occurs. This emphasis on rhythm encourages players to stay constantly in motion, weaving through attacks and hazards in a way that feels fluid and expressive rather than aggressive or rigid.

Boss battles are where ADEN truly shines. These encounters abandon subtlety in favor of spectacle, transforming the screen into a vibrant storm of color, sound, and movement. Patterns often resemble bullet-hell designs, demanding sharp awareness and instinctive dodging while staying in sync with the underlying rhythm. Success comes from learning patterns, trusting timing, and embracing the game’s dance-like logic. These fights are memorable not only for their difficulty, but for how theatrically they present themselves, often feeling like climactic performances rather than conventional boss encounters.

Outside of boss fights, standard stages serve as rhythmic playgrounds that gradually introduce new ideas and challenges. Early sections are approachable, easing players into the movement system and visual language, while later stages layer complexity through faster pacing and denser enemy patterns. While some regular encounters can feel comparatively simple next to the bombast of boss battles, they act as effective build-ups, reinforcing mechanics and maintaining a sense of flow between major set pieces.

Visually, ADEN embraces expressive pixel art that balances cuteness with chaos. Character animations are lively and exaggerated, environments burst with color, and effects are used liberally to emphasize motion and impact. The art direction reinforces the game’s whimsical tone while ensuring that even the most visually intense moments remain readable. This visual energy is matched by a strong soundtrack that leans heavily into rhythm and melody, often syncing directly with gameplay. Music doesn’t merely accompany the action; it actively shapes it, reinforcing the sensation that combat is meant to be felt as much as played.

Progression is light but meaningful, focusing on mastery rather than stat accumulation. As players advance, they unlock new abilities and options that subtly expand how Toaka can move and respond to threats. These additions don’t fundamentally alter the game’s core identity, but they add variety and encourage experimentation, especially for players interested in replaying stages or refining their performance. The game rewards familiarity and rhythm awareness more than brute force, making improvement feel personal and skill-driven.

ADEN’s tone and structure may not resonate with everyone. Its surreal storytelling, unconventional combat logic, and frequent tonal shifts can feel disorienting to players expecting a more traditional action game. However, these same qualities are precisely what give the game its identity. It embraces strangeness rather than smoothing it out, and its rough edges feel intentional rather than accidental, reinforcing the sense that this is a deeply personal creative project.

Overall, ADEN is a confident and imaginative indie title that prioritizes expression, rhythm, and spectacle over convention. It blends action, music, and surreal storytelling into a cohesive experience that feels playful, intense, and unapologetically strange. While it may not appeal to players seeking straightforward mechanics or grounded narratives, those drawn to experimental design and energetic presentation will find ADEN to be a memorable journey—one that feels less like playing a game and more like dancing through controlled chaos.

First, the music is awesome! I love the challenge and silly characters, hope to see more of this type of game later on! Try it on hard mode too!!!
Short and sweet game about the power of friendship and dancing. This was honestly amazing and its worth a go.

The atmosphere and artistic direction of this game are truly memorable. I was surprised to find myself enjoying the story so much, and the gameplay is fun and easygoing.
If the trailer intrigues you even just a bit, don't hesitate to give it a try, the story is captivating.

I played YumeCore a while back and bought this because I liked that one, even if it was somewhat janky. It had a lot of heart.

This is much the same, except with a lot more polish and a weird plot that kept my interest and somehow managed to actually surprise me with a few twists. Also, pretty much every endgame stage is masterful in presentation.

I have to state this, however: playing it on hard mode is mandatory. It's not as hard as it sounds. It really is more of a normal mode, and normal is more like easy. On normal, you're not getting the intended experience and the game will not last you nearly as long. Normal lets you just immediately continue at any point in the middle of a fight with no real penalty, and nothing hits hard enough to justify caution; Hard works on a checkpoint system and is balanced to provide some semblance of challenge. The end result is that normal feels more like a VN where you're there for the plot. In any case, if you can't "git gud" there's an item shop that lets you buy permanent upgrades, so hard really is not as bad as it sounds.

Would recommend.
a unique beat-'em-up with simple but compelling combat, a thought-provoking story, and distinct audio and visual design

game_name: ENDER MAGNOLIA: Bloom in the Mist
review_text:
Pros :
- Story is a lil bit different from the first series, more complicated and LONGER but its okay, still understandable (they improving the length storyline, but still in good narrative)
- Mechanics same as the first series, easy to learn and build characters or allies, but this time with MORE various allies skill (some of them really broken in end game content btw).
- Character design MUCH BETTER than before! Thanks for the artist who improving the artstyle of the every characters like MC, allies, enemies, and NPC!
- The OST very touching as always! 
- The price is almost 2 times compared with the first series, but its really WORTH IT! Overall got improvement.

Cons :
- Hope in the next sequel (if there are), the characters have VA (voice actor), i will not hesitate to give BETTER score!

Thanks for the great experience dev, all ender series never disappoint me. ❤️

Very recommended! (9 of 10)

game_name: ENDER MAGNOLIA: Bloom in the Mist
review_text: Still 10 hours in at review time, playing at 'hard' preset from beginning since i already familiar with ender lilies. 40% better "game" (QoL, NO COLLISSION DAMAGE, more fun map and bosses, MC can talk, etc.) than ender lilies, but 20% worse "vibes" than EL (For me, the post apocalyptic theme execution is rather cliche compared to "Innocence in the collapsing world" EL gives). Still, it's a better game and I approve it as a successor of the previous one.

game_name: ENDER MAGNOLIA: Bloom in the Mist
review_text:
Yes, yes and yes, 100% get it if you are a metroidvania or a fairytale enjoyer like me.
I wont write a long review about this because its just insanely better in everything sequel to Ender Lilies. 
Just like prequel its a wonderful fairytale in steampunk mixed with magic aesthethics. 
The backgrounds and graphics are now insanely beautiful and detailed, I've spent few hours looking at details (One of my favourite ones is the fact you sit on giant excavator bucket at one of rest spots inside magic mine).
Once again OST is made by Mili so there's absoluteny no point of saying anything (Every single track from both games are essential parts of my playlist).
Gameplay-wise as I said above its Ender Lilies but polished and with new several additions.
P.S. Bless my all-time favourite gaming chair user Lorna Frost (Love her design).

Once again, thank you, Binary Haze, for making amazing sequel to an amazing fairytale.

game_name: ENDER MAGNOLIA: Bloom in the Mist
review_text: Great metroidvania game. Music, gameplay and map/character design are very good (especially if you like Mili's music like me). Has slight souls-like elements to it but is more on the easier side in terms of difficulty.

game_name: ENDER MAGNOLIA: Bloom in the Mist
review_text:
I honestly didn't like the first game (Ender Lilies, which I wrote a review for, too), but this one: Ender Magnolia, I LOVED. It became one of my favorite games of recent years and one of my favorite metroidvanias of all time!
Magnolia does everything right. All my complains about the first game are totally gone.
In Magnolia, characters have actual personalities that make you care for them, especially the protagonist. The story starts strong and intriguing and gets better the more you know, until the very end.
Graphics are insanely beautiful and everything visual is polished to the bone, with flawless optimization. Sound and music are amazing and a big step up from the first game as well. This is one OST for the ages! Mixing epic tones and moving themes with exotic instruments and even some electric -rocking- beats when and where it fits SO nicely (not to mention, vocals!).
Gameplay is a 10/10. Smooth, fun, challenging, but never abusive. And just when you think that's it: surprises and variation come around, refreshing things more than enough to keep you fully engaged at all times.
Magnolia's map is BIG and exciting, with different areas capable of taking your breath away. It's not dauntingly giant, but it always feels GENEROUS and inspired, with many secrets to uncover. I never got lost in the game and if I did, the game itself was smart and ingenious enough to point me where to go without making it obvious. All things exploration feel very well thought and refined, as Magnolia provides enough tools and quality of life improvements over the first game to keep you going, at your own pace. Comparing Lilies to Magnolia in this aspect is practically night and day.
Bosses are CRAZY GOOD! Each and every one of them are a thing of their own, with different levels of challenge that will overflow your dopamine. They're hard: BUT FAIR, never feeling forced or too gimmicky (and NO secret extra phases that will obliterate your patience).
Difficulty is very well balanced overall, which is very welcome as it makes the game more accessible for a wider audience. That said, if you feel it's too easy for you: dare to try the hard difficulty and live to tell the story.
Extra content is great, too! Unlockable outfits, a gallery with beautiful artwork and characters' profiles that add a lot to your knowing of the lore... and BOSS RUSH, where you can master your abilities against them and continue to earn points to unlock everything the game has to offer.
New Game+ is very well thought and feels very satisfying, as you can go everywhere much sooner (you keep your abilities) and reach the end early, BUT don't underestimate enemies and bosses, as they get tougher and even have some different moves! Sad you can't go NG++ or more after you beat NG+, though. :-(
In short, Magnolia is an artistically gorgeous, rock-solid game and a completionist dream. It respects your time, skill and patience. Also, it has regional prices and goes on sale pretty often so there's ABSOLUTELY no reason to not get it. Wishlist it, get it, get a copy for a loved one.
This is Binary Haze's true masterpiece. 9.9/10.

game_name: ENDER MAGNOLIA: Bloom in the Mist
review_text: Out of all the games I’ve played in my life, Ender Lilies and Magnolia are some of the very few where i’ve loved absolutely everything about them. To me, they’re just perfect. No doubt they’re on my favorites list

game_name: ENDER MAGNOLIA: Bloom in the Mist
review_text: I really enjoyed Ender Magnolia.  The music is fantastic, the sweet tracks with Cassie Wei will live in my head for a while.  The art and story are both a delight.  I appreciate having a map that tells you when you are done with the area so much.  I often have to revert to the internet for games like this to figure out what I've missed and where it might be.  I didn't need to do that here because the map was so helpful, it always seemed like a quick look over the map and I had a direction to go.  The characters and conversations at rest sites was fun.  I was playing this on the oled steam deck and I found the controls to be loose.  I would often have Lilac just suddenly face the other direction even if my thumb was away from the stick, or I would go to parry and she would dash instead.  I ended up turning on the press down and LT to parry because getting her to not dash was a problem.  The bosses totally suck but at least there is usually a rest area right before a boss fight so you can bash your head against the wall without getting annoyed at the hike back.

game_name: ENDER MAGNOLIA: Bloom in the Mist
review_text:
An improvement on Ender Lilies in every way. 

Still shoves in bonfires and unecessary souls elements, but there are accessibility sliders for difficulty.
My only gripe with the game is the way it tells its story. Just a ton of skits, interchangeable NPCs with no personalities sputing word salad lore, and absolutely no emotion from any dialogue whatsoever. It honestly interrupts the gameplay.

But a minor complaint in the grand scheme of things.  

Overall, this is one of the best metroidvanias out there and well worth playing. Awesome combat, great physics, wonderful map system, fast travel, and all the basic stuff is done well here that so many other games get wrong.

And of course, its gorgeous and has an amazing OST. Highly recommend!

game_name: ENDER MAGNOLIA: Bloom in the Mist
review_text: This was a very good game. The music was fantastic and had it's own feel while still matching the tone in a similar way as the first game. The mechanics have gotten more involved in a good way, and by the end of the game moving through the map becomes quite a joy. The tone and plot of the game worked well to add to the atmosphere. I would recommend this to anyone who's into metroidvania games where the feel of exploration drives them in progression.

game_name: ENDER MAGNOLIA: Bloom in the Mist
review_text: This a good follow-up to Ender Lilies, with pretty much everything refined well beyond the first game. Even so, I did feel bored in some areas - they felt big without relevant content in them. It also gets a bit weaker toward the very end, but it is still one of the better metroidvanias out there.

game_name: ENDER MAGNOLIA: Bloom in the Mist
review_text: perfect metroidvania for me & this game improving a lot from the previous game. good job Adglobe & Live Wire. cant wait to play another ENDER

game_name: ENDER MAGNOLIA: Bloom in the Mist
review_text:
Game fun, me enjoy. Absolutely loved the first game and didn't realize there even was a second one for quite a bit.
So far, I DO enjoy the first game more, even if it's been awhile. That's not to say this game isn't good.
If I had to rate the first game a 9/10 then I would rate this one an 8/10

Can I back that up with detail and examples? No.
Would I say you should play this game if you liked the first one? Yes, absolutely, even when it's not on sale.

game_name: ENDER MAGNOLIA: Bloom in the Mist
review_text: Amazing game. Story, pacing, music, character development, mechanics and art; there isn't anything to criticize about. This coming from an indie studio really put at lot of veteran studios to shame. A pure example of crafts that solely focused on making good game minus all the ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ agenda.

game_name: ENDER MAGNOLIA: Bloom in the Mist
review_text: I feel like its a step back from Ender Lilies, as combat is not as tight nor does it seem to be the main focus of the game. It's honestly very, very easy till the end. Towards the end of the game some of the enemies' hitboxes will have you taking damage and dying before your character is even hit but it's not that bad. There was a boss that had several attacks like that and it took me a 3 or 4 tries just to get the timing down because of it. Other than that this game is much, much easier than the first one. I still liked it though.

game_name: ENDER MAGNOLIA: Bloom in the Mist
review_text:
Platinum achieved, overall a fun experience. The game captures the essence of a PSX-era Metroidvania. It really looks and plays like a sequel to Symphony of the Night. The game also has great QOL features for the genre, that I wish were more commonplace. No runbacks, no currency loss, refillable potions, and a map that is intuitive and makes back-tracking less of a brain!@#$. 

Unfortunately, that means it also looks, controls, and plays like a PSX-era game. Controls and animations are very stiff, unnatural feeling, though the bindings and abilities are flexible. It can be hard to shake the flat and drab feeling of the game. There's also little character sound, such as no battlecries or even grunts. The story is sparse and presented as a mere afterthought. The game feels like an unfinished prototype for everything EXCEPT the gameplay and game systems, which they totally nail. So that's awesome!

game_name: ENDER MAGNOLIA: Bloom in the Mist
review_text: This is a very solid improvement over the first game, combat is now smooth and fast, a complaint I had with how clunky and sluggish the first ones movement was.  Well polished, strong in all areas.  Doesn't really bring anything new to the table (neither did ender lilies), but instead polishes what it had to make it more enjoyable.

game_name: ENDER MAGNOLIA: Bloom in the Mist
review_text:
the game is pretty much a re-skin of Hollow Knight. The story is pretty good but also very similar to Hollow Knight. 

What is interesting is the various abilities and the fact that you can use these to practically break the game. Tons of different playstyles for different abilities and each of them are fun and powerful.

game_name: ENDER MAGNOLIA: Bloom in the Mist
review_text: Kind of a side grade for me when compared to the first game. I felt like the story was less compelling, the game was less atmospheric, and the music wasn't as good. On the other hand, the mini map was way better, the bosses were more challenging, the skill/item progression felt more interesting, and the movement was slightly better I'd say. One think I do really like that both games stayed constant on was the fact there wasn't any arbitrary achievements like in most games where you have to go out of your way or look up a lot of guides. If you wish to 100% these games it is very straightforward (just play the game). Overall I'd give this game an 8/10 and worth trying if you are a fan of the metroidvania genre or you enjoyed the first game.

game_name: ENDER MAGNOLIA: Bloom in the Mist
review_text:
Probably my favorite metroidvania series I've played so far. I found out after beating both games it was executive produced by Hiroyuki Kobayashi (Resident Evil) 

This game as a souls-like combat heavy style does a great job of having you strategize and utilize most of your abilities, gear for specific areas and bosses keeping it fresh and engaging.

Unlike the first game this one has a difficulty tuner, all I did was change the preset at the beginning to hard and forget about it.

10/10 metroidvania looking forward to another one

game_name: ENDER MAGNOLIA: Bloom in the Mist
review_text: I never played anything by this dev so I didn't know what to expect. Great game. Would reccomend to anyone that likes metrodvanias

game_name: ENDER MAGNOLIA: Bloom in the Mist
review_text: Great game, better then Silksong, because it doesn't give me migraine.

game_name: ENDER MAGNOLIA: Bloom in the Mist
review_text:
This is an excellent sequel to Ender Lilies. The abilities feel like a near direct progression from the first game (especially how you can dash from the beginning of the game), and there are plenty of new movement options that create different and fun ways to think about and traverse each of the levels. The attack abilities are a mix of familiar ones from the previous game, and a host of new ones. For my first playthrough, I tried to use each ability a decent amount, and I felt that each one felt powerful; there were no abilities that felt overpowered or useless, especially with the way you can aid certain ability setups through the relic system. 
     The music and art was what drew me into the series, and it's excellent here as well. Ender Lilies already had a distinct style, though some parts DID feel a bit Hollow Knight-ish (especially the design of certain elements like the experience bar). I think everyone knows that Hollow Knight was likely a big influence on the game, but in Ender Lilies I couldn't help but think about that every time I played, even if the games are notably distinct in gameplay and art and mood in many ways. In Ender Magnolia, though, the game really feels like it has a unique style: cleaner, more crisp, maybe even mechanical in a good way? Which fits with the more steampunk-y theme of the game. The music reflects this as well, building off of and at times directly quoting melodies from Ender Liles, but with more synths and effects that fit the more futuristic setting. I could go on and on, but tl;dr the game is awesome!
     Oh one other thing: the deflect feels really good. And you can apply it in a LOT of situations. Might feel like a small thing, but a good parry can make or break a game for me.

game_name: ENDER MAGNOLIA: Bloom in the Mist
review_text:
Really good sequel to Ender Lilies, I had quite a lot of fun with it and I still love the gimmick of a Metroidvania where the character is actually summoning creatures to fight for them rather than being the one doing the fighting. It's a small detail and functionally meaningless, but it plays well into the theme of the story, like it did in the first game. 
I don't know if I can be objective about the game's difficulty, as Metroidvania is my favourite genre and so I'm very used to it if only by virtue of having played so many, but I mostly breezed through it. 
Which isn't necessarily bad nor good, but it may be important to some. 
I also appreciated that much less text had the "mocking Spongebob" alternate casing that made some moments, even dramatic ones, feel much goofier than intended. But I may just be too online myself.
Highly recommended, especially to people who liked the first game

game_name: ENDER MAGNOLIA: Bloom in the Mist
review_text: Excellent art and atmospheric background music. After I finished it, I was left with a lingering sense of void.

game_name: ENDER MAGNOLIA: Bloom in the Mist
review_text:
Man man man what a game! 
the devs did so well like ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ this is how a sequel should be it adds and improves on the original formula instead of taking away ( looking at you cv2 removing coop >:c )  
The combat feels a lot smoother 
The inventory certainly an improvement
the art style is simillar but honestly i wish it had more songs like the one in the first area
also the CAMEO of certain character was ...♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ i got soo excited when i saw it 

You don't necessarily need to play the first game to understand this game but it helps a lot to be like "ohhh i member" with certain things.

If i had a complaint that would be that the last spirit felt a bit underwhelming compared to the first ones so i hardly changed my build 
if you're grabbing this one be sure to grab ender lilies! it's a great introduction

game_name: ENDER MAGNOLIA: Bloom in the Mist
review_text: More of the last game. Extra plot in a different but similar world with familiar like characters. It took me 30 hours to complete everything in this game, which i think is the perfect amount of time.

game_name: ENDER MAGNOLIA: Bloom in the Mist
review_text: One of the best metroidvanias i've ever played. The combat imo is up there together with silksong's. Unlike lilies here in magnolia there are enough bosses and the game generally has a lot more content, the only downsides compared to lilies are how streamlined the exploration is, the level design is much less convoluted too except in a few areas and the abilities upgrade system. Also you have the complete difficulty slider unlocked since the start of the game after the first boss, so you can customize the game however you want, i've played on hard and some moments were tedious rather than hard but most of the time it was challenging and engaging. Other than that the game is really solid and it offers a lot of replayability, you can make a lot of varied builds and the rpg mechanics are quite well done.

game_name: ENDER MAGNOLIA: Bloom in the Mist
review_text:
This is an enjoyable game, to me it's average, a solid 7/10. 
I really didn't like the first one, but this I got into. The combat is nothing special but it gets the job done. Navigating the map this time around is much improved, I was able to quickly find my way most of the times. The story didn't really grab me but that may just be on me, the brain has rotted beyond repair probably.
But holy moly the music goes [i]hard[/i] in this game! Like, unreasonably so, banger after banger. For that alone it's worth the buy.

game_name: ENDER MAGNOLIA: Bloom in the Mist
review_text:
It's good. Movement is great and boss fights are fun. Not as hard as first but can still get spicy.
Better than the first 9/10 IMO.

game_name: ENDER MAGNOLIA: Bloom in the Mist
review_text:
Absolutely amazing game. I didn't think the OST can improve from ender lilies, but it has and it is phenomenal. 

Gameplay was excellent. Challenging but fair. Each ability and combo is very satisfying.

Play the game, you won't regret it.
