game_name: ENDER MAGNOLIA: Bloom in the Mist
review_text:
Peak from start to finish, gameplay so good it potentionally ruin how I play the first game. 

Outside of the glazing. 

I do found the exploration to be on the "meh" side compared to the original, the verticality is good but the wolf is barely faster especially with how long you need to charged it, the story is ok but I found the characters to be mostly unmemorable even the one the players can use in battle, and some of the cutscene can hit uncanny valley tho idk if this is either positive or negative. 

Overall great game, could've been my GOTY of 2025.

game_name: ENDER MAGNOLIA: Bloom in the Mist
review_text: The First thing, the map, it should be the industry standard. Having arrows and question marks all over inform you along the way. Still got stuck here and there. Beautiful game, tough bosses, tight as nails controls.

game_name: ENDER MAGNOLIA: Bloom in the Mist
review_text:
One of the greatest Metroidvanias of our time

The first one was one of my favorite games, and the second one did not disappoint, even though I prefer the first game

game_name: ENDER MAGNOLIA: Bloom in the Mist
review_text:
Can't say that some parts weren't a bit annoying (tells for bosses were too vague most of the time so a trial and error to learn patterns, not really able to flow into it from scratch) but mostly my own fault as the modular difficulty i put at glass cannon everything (enemies do more damage and less hp, they weren't as easily stunned or affected by status condition)

Had to put it down for final boss ending B to regular levels, a bit too hard to dodge some stuff for me and down to just both endings to finish so felt satisfied to clear last boss without going hard for me.

Overall an excellent game, map shows when you have fully cleared a room (though does so a bit early at a few places with hidden rooms, so you might mistakenly think it's cleared but a doorway is left to enter)

Moveset on most abilites was really fun, i found a few i liked and stuck to them for most of the game, tried any new i got and eventually settled on the Parry, shotgun and dash slash (names kept vague to not spoil)

Story was fine, and maps beautiful.
The checkpoints evenly staggered so no long run backs and no death penalty besides being set back to a save point so less stressful.

Only thing i didn't really like was the flashbangs it did every cutscene :/
But given they were always at the same part and had a prompt to trigger so no surprises i could deal with them (peek through fingers to watch cutscene, close them when it's about to flash)

Might get back to it someday for another run, likely after doing a Ender lily playthrough, looking forward to any next in series. 
9/10, wholeheartedly recommended.

game_name: ENDER MAGNOLIA: Bloom in the Mist
review_text:
For the context of this review, I am comparing this game to its predecessor, "Ender Lilies Bloom in the Mist."
I'll start it off by saying this game is a lot of the same. And for that I mean it has amazing art and music with a great gameplay experience to back it up. The improvements are across the board. If you liked Ender Lilies, you'll almost definitely like this one even more.
However, this game does disappoint me in one nitpick and one bigger area. The nitpick is that the exploration feels a bit too streamlined. Any blank part on your map get filled in in real time, removing a deeper sense of discovery,  and every room has a clear roof and walls, so any empty space is just telling you that you need something to get there. Also, any thing that needs an ability to get through is marked on your map in red, and will turn blue when you get the ability to get past it. It makes backtracking have less moments of it clicking in your head and more like checking boxes off a list. As for the bigger problem, it is the characters. In Lilies, you had a very small cast of characters and most of them only had a couple lines, leading to only 3-4 characters that really mattered and had good effort and writing. And it really worked, making the game really stand out to me among the dozens of other Metroidvanias I've played. Ender Magnolia however, has a much larger cast, but ends up disappointing with its lackluster execution. And it's a real shame, because the cast that follows you throughout the game had so many chances to be better characterized and share the strength that Ender Lilies had, but Magnolia just never does and ends up with what feels like a crowded but hollow cast that is never realized.
To wrap it up, the game is a clear improvement on almost all grounds, with only some missed potential that the previous game had, making it feel like it's missing something.

game_name: ENDER MAGNOLIA: Bloom in the Mist
review_text: Absolutely love this game, I played the first game as well and had a blast. I kind of hope there is another one.

game_name: ENDER MAGNOLIA: Bloom in the Mist
review_text: Ender Peak never misses. This game manages to be both very similar yet different enough to Ender Lilies. One notable difference is that there are much fewer spirits now, but each of them has 3 different skills instead, so the total amount of skills is about the same. There's also a lot of NPCs in the world now, making it feel rather different from that of the first game.

game_name: ENDER MAGNOLIA: Bloom in the Mist
review_text: finally got the chance to play it, oh boy.. i can't deny that Lilies feels more deep but damn, Magnolia is indeed beautiful. I enjoyed both games.

game_name: ENDER MAGNOLIA: Bloom in the Mist
review_text:
Wonderfull game as always from these devs.

From the moody soundtrack to the interesting and varied bosses. You never have a truly dull moment in the ender series!

game_name: ENDER MAGNOLIA: Bloom in the Mist
review_text: Another great soul-like / metroidvania game in the Ender series. Don't be fooled by the first few bosses, the boss are hard and all about pattern learning. I enjoyed (and infuriated against some boss) my time playing this game. I do recommend it!

game_name: ENDER MAGNOLIA: Bloom in the Mist
review_text: The game is not called Ender Lilies 2, it's Ender Magnolia so don't come expecting Ender Lilies 2

game_name: ENDER MAGNOLIA: Bloom in the Mist
review_text:
Very well made metroidvania. Different in some ways, not nearly as difficult as Silksong or similar. Tons of customization and secrets. Defintiely worth a play if you like this genre. 

Would rank it in the Top 10 for metroidvanias imo.

game_name: ENDER MAGNOLIA: Bloom in the Mist
review_text: bigger and better! great sequel especially the build variation in this game is very nice

game_name: ENDER MAGNOLIA: Bloom in the Mist
review_text:
Yup. Overwhelmingly positive is more than accurate. After a lifetime of metroidvanias (starting with the original metroid and original castlevania, all the way up to super meat boy, celeste and hollow knight), Ender Magnolia is a masterpiece of the genre. The art, the music, the combat, the pathing, the progression, the weapon system, the equipment, the writing, the characters. The only fault I can find with this game is that it ends.

If you enjoy platformers or combat platformers (like super guacamelee), you owe it to yourself to play this game. 100% at a slow pace took about 25 hours, with new game+ as an option. I never played the first game, but I intend to 100% that game next.

It's well worth it at full price, but on sale? Auto-buy. I'd even gift it on sale.

game_name: ENDER MAGNOLIA: Bloom in the Mist
review_text: A little hard and frustrating at times but its a fun game

game_name: ENDER MAGNOLIA: Bloom in the Mist
review_text: Just as the previous one, beautiful music, mechanics, and some not very clear story.

game_name: ENDER MAGNOLIA: Bloom in the Mist
review_text:
I completed it 100%. I liked it, but sometimes it was a bit hard. In the end, after I got the true ending, I had to use a map to find every item in each area. Up to that point, it was normal. However, in this game I had to farm money to buy every item in the store, which took around 4 or 5 hours. I prefer Ender Lilies because in that game there is no shop.

PD: I really enjoyed those “big puzzles” with the new mechanics. I had to think about how the map worked and how I could open new zones, unlock blocked areas or find hidden items. Thanks to those mechanics, I completed around 95% of the map on my own before finishing the game.

game_name: ENDER MAGNOLIA: Bloom in the Mist
review_text: Amazing sequel with smoother gameplay, but less interesting world compared to lilies.

game_name: ENDER MAGNOLIA: Bloom in the Mist
review_text:
A good metroidvamia, easier than Ender Lily because the parry is fixed in this version.
Normal play through is about 20hours.
Save game + available.

game_name: ENDER MAGNOLIA: Bloom in the Mist
review_text: It's basically just a more polished version of Ender Lilies. If you liked that game, or Metroidvanias in general, it's worth a shot.

game_name: ENDER MAGNOLIA: Bloom in the Mist
review_text: Great game. Very much worth playing. Each map is wildly unique, so you won't feel lost much, and the fact that you're able to tell when you're done with a room really helps a lot. I found the game harder than most of the reviews I've seen here but the gameplay is really tight, smooth and it never felt like it wasn't my fault when I died. The exploration is amazing. The map quite clearly lays out where each blockade is and the ability you can use if you have it. You can very easily explore this world without getting lost. You also get 15 markers to lay down anywhere on the map. Once you get an upgrade that classic Metroidvania "Oh yeah I can use this for the blockade I couldn't pass through before" hits. And all of this is done very well. The only downsides I can say is that some rooms are just way too overfilled with enemies, but the bosses are quite fun. Some of em were quite annoying because the closest respite was kinda far from their boss room, but its only one or two of em that are like that. Overall, this is a great game.

game_name: ENDER MAGNOLIA: Bloom in the Mist
review_text:
Honestly I'm glad that this is the way the devs wanted to take this series. No forced elements from other genres for the sake of it or for targeting the hip kids. We get something that I expect from a sequel and that is evolution of previous mechanics and building upon the foundation of the series. The best example of this is movement which is one of THE most important things in a metroidvania game which makes your character traverse a large area filled with different biomes. The movement went through a significant overhoul from the first entry were it felt floaty and without weight. Here you have a feeling you actually do control a body and not a paper doll. Great stuff devs, great stuff! Worth mentioning are also the animations which I have a feeling went trough a touch up themselves. We get quite a lot of fidelity here and you can see that when idling or during your stay at safeplaces, where homunculi do poses and take care of themselves (like the shackled beast smoking).
Music stayed top notch and added a bunch of vocal tracks which I dont remember in the first one and they are some of my favourites. It makes the atmosphere more esotheric and well melancholic. Which itself may not be for everyone. The second entry also decided to increase the number of NPCs you meet and interact with which I think is good and makes the game come to life. Not only do we get lore info from notes and diaries but also from living people. I think this is a good direction.

So as for myself I give this game a 8/10. Really solid game with improvemnts across the board not only from visuals to audio but also to controlls and technical stuff. The few things that hold it back from a perfect 10 is the story department and the combat. As for the story this one didn't challenge you to come up with a conclusion to the story nor the different endings it did (the first one wasn't that hard with it also but did a bit better than this one). I feel it also didn't use the npcs as well as it could. They didn't offer much exposition nor did they have any important role in quests. This could use more work and a bit of a more complex approach to the npcs or to the way we can influence the story itself.
As for the combat it has improved but there are still areas it lacks in. The aforementioned animation cancelling comes to mind but also the abilities and team members themselves. They do have different uses between them but not as much as you could mix and match to different situations. The abilities themselves could change a bit more with upgrades. 

All in all I think it is an excellent addition to the Ender series and the metroidvania genre. So if you are a fan of that genre and are looking for something new to play Ender Magnolia is a solid one to pick.

game_name: ENDER MAGNOLIA: Bloom in the Mist
review_text:
Incredible. Just absolutely incredible. A masterwork in game design and cohesive vision.
The only thing I disagree with are the presence of difficulty sliders, but that's nitpicking at best.
It is astounding that the game is an improvement over Ender Lilies in every way, except for the story and atmosphere, albeit that's my personal taste.
The closest a game can get to 10/10 in my opinion. I cannot wait for whatever their next project might be.

game_name: ENDER MAGNOLIA: Bloom in the Mist
review_text:
Absolutely loved this game, it's a perfect sequel to Ender Lilies!! From the music and visuals to great boss fights I enjoyed every moment and just kept coming back for more! The story is so well written, and just pulls you in!

10/10!

game_name: ENDER MAGNOLIA: Bloom in the Mist
review_text: After Silksong, this might be the best sequal to a metroidvania ever. Improves upon Ender Lilies in every way. Gameplay is more fluid, there's way more lore thanks to the protagonist being able to speak (but the story is still non linear in true metroidvania fashion), and the soundtrack might be some of Mili's best work, just banger after banger. Must play metroidvania along with the original, praying for more from this series, 9.5/10.

game_name: ENDER MAGNOLIA: Bloom in the Mist
review_text: Very nice game, good responsive controls, nice combat. Okay writing.

game_name: ENDER MAGNOLIA: Bloom in the Mist
review_text: Better than the first game in nearly every aspect. More fluid, easier UI, better map system, clearer art. The story is good too, just a little less interesting than the first, but still engaging. Play the first game too, not a requirement, but I'd recommend it because you'll enjoy the little references.

game_name: ENDER MAGNOLIA: Bloom in the Mist
review_text: Very fun metroidvania. one of the most incredible ace attorney/danganronpa style investigation games i have ever played, hands down. incredibly stylish and dynamic despite VN limitations, well fleshed out and nuanced characters, this game put me in a thriller genre chokehold i havent experienced since playing the ace attorney trilogy for the first time. more, even, which feels insane to say. toxic yuri is always a plus too. and with the extremely thoughtful cyberpunk worldbuilding on top of it all? this game is more than a hidden gem, its a whole cavern of them. and its not even done yet.

After years of searching for a game that really manages to emulate the case-solving and wild twists mid-trial craziness the Danganronpa and Ace Attorney series has - I've finally found something that I feel surpasses both.

The character writing is beyond good (Morgan's one of the best VN protags I've ever seen), being hard-hitting, funny, and heartwrenching all at once. Visually and audio-wise, the entire game absolutely BLEEDS style, with substance to match. Soundtrack is very reminiscent of DR and Zero Escape but in a good way, but the real star of the show is visual presentation - lots of impactful lines are paired with impactful visuals to match and it's a really unique way of presenting dialogue I haven't seen much in VNs previously. 

Gameplay is where it really feels like a proper evolution of DR; at its core, it's still "select the correct piece of evidence to disprove the claim presented", but with a wonderful gambling twist. Inspecting evidence grants you chips which you can use to raise 'Bets' during the trial. Real confident you've got the smoking gun? All-in. Not so sure? Hedge your bets, or make a crazy all-in play that you KNOW will land even if you're not exactly sure why. Later on in a trial, you'll be playing for pretty high stakes and sometimes the game will have you do some crazy ♥♥♥♥ beyond just evidence matching and prodding witnesses [spoiler]like making an ass out of the court solely to stall for time,[/spoiler] for example.

Sadly, the game isn't "complete" yet - there's 5 episodes total (6 counting Episode 0 as a prologue), but only 2 are out currently. Each case is standalone for the most part, and they're all well-crafted (Episode 2's case is up there with some of the more crazy Danganronpa murders in terms of complexity), but we've got a LONG way to go before the tale of the Heartbreak Killer reaches a close.

That being said - usually, I wait for games to be fully finished before playing them. I am STILL waiting on the last part of Your Turn To Die. I broke that rule for this game after reading reviews (and yes I do love toxic yuri so what), and I IMPLORE you to do the same. Episode 0 is free and it should be all you need to convince you if you're willing to stay on this wild ride. It was more than enough to convince me.

This has to be the best game in its genre I've ever played. The writing is snappy, the characters are interesting, the world-building is enthralling and the presentation is really cool. A lot of thought has been put into what the world will become with advances in technology, and how that will effect truth-seeking and morality. The protagonist is pragmatic and disarming with a hidden edge that just works with the setting so well. 

The writing accomplishes something that really great works do which is I am always torn between wanting to sit back and think about what just happened but also want to rush ahead and see what comes next. It's a wonderful problem to have. 

The major caveat - this is unfinished and coming out in parts, but it has been a delight so far so I recommend it. The chapters are pretty long and worth the money.

Solid writing, great cast of characters, and above all an elite sense of style that propels the game far up my list of recommendations. The game is also not afraid to show its influences proudly while differentiating itself from them. Will be eagerly awaiting the rest of the episodes!

I think I haven't enjoyed a VN/adventure game this much since Somnium Files. It abuses legal procedure to a degree I'm sure is matched only by Ace Attorney, but I can put up with it 'cause I love the characters, and I'm really invested in the unfolding plot. My only real complaint is the lack of voice acting. I'd love to stream for my friends but I can't narrate all that aloud :C

this game commits vengeance on my nostalgia for ace attorney, which i played as a child and will always roost in my heart. it almost feels dirty, like finding a new favorite toy. its writing is sincerely and uncommonly intelligent. i'm not usually shocked by twists -- i'm a persistent reader, curious and/or hypervigilant enough to be an observant audience member -- but this game took me off-guard for the first time in a long time. what an awesome experience!

i'm 30 and had been growing to accept that games probably wouldn't keep pace with my own maturity, with few exceptions. feeling like you need to put away your idyllic, childish sentiment sucks booty. now i feel vindicated. i'm grateful that i can still delight in a hobby that's kept me company through troubling times, and grateful to the devs for giving that gift to me. please keep sharing your art with us!

In a world where capcom hasn't given us a genuinely new ace attorney in nearly 10 years, it's up to the fans to not only make their own, but beat them at their own game. Morgan stands as an absolutely fascinating protagonist in a well fleshed-out cyberpunk world. Storytelling is punchy, interesting, and moves forward with incredible aplomb. Dynamics and tropes from its inspirations are not merely simply recreated, but improved upon in thoughtful ways, and despite its main conceit falling very easily into empty edge, it walks that tightrope with genuine heart. Started referencing Crime and Punishment to my friends immediately after finishing episode 1. Absolutely stellar and a must-play if this style of game is at all your wheelhouse.

The writing for the characters in this game is unbelievably good. The characters feel like real actual people more so than any other game I've ever played. The world building is so interesting and cool but feels so grounded in reality in spite of it being futuristic. It's so aggressively competent, even excellent at what it sets out to do that it brings tears to my eyes as I am typing this. Please try the demo at the very least. Chapter 0 is free as a demo, I'm sure it will convince you to buy the rest of the episodes.

The story and worldbuilding are top notch, the cases themselves are pretty interesting with satisfying conclusions and the prose is good. Why the bad score then? Because the gameplay itself is just mid. Not tedious but I don't really feel very engaged. I recommend you play the prologue, the worldbuilding and story in the chapters is far better than in the prologue, so, if you are at least ok with the gameplay I recommend you buy Chpt. 1, if you find the gameplay a dealbreaker then don't buy it

From interesting characters to great art, to divine writing and concepts, I cannot sing this game's praises enough. Hell, I don't write reviews, but this is a game that desperately deserves praise.

I wish I could buy this game multiple times. I wish I could support it more.
This is the best I can do for now. Please play this game.

(This review takes into account Episodes 0, 1, and 2. Not because the rest are bad, they just aren't out at the time of posting) Accomplishes the herculean task of being a point-n-click investigation and trial defense game and being consistently, genuinely, good in by every metric. Fascinating world, powerful characters, intriguing mysteries, stellar writing, etc. etc. etc. Fantastic game, highly recommend.

Here's my pitch: You are Morgan, esquire. A psychopathic defense attorney in a not-quite Orwellian cyberpunk future who always manages to be a little too involved in the crimes she defends people for (but not always in the same way). You navigate her ♥♥♥♥♥♥ up world and her ♥♥♥♥♥♥ up self and explore the interactions between technology, humanity, ▮▮▮▮, or a lack thereof. 

Episode 0 is a fantastic short teaser, and the episodes themselves are well worth the price of admission and more. I'm not the biggest fan of the episodic release format in general, but of the Devil is good enough to warrant the periodic drops, and it makes thinking about the story more interesting to chew on. It's not like the original Sam & Max released (no matter how much I adore them) where it adds very little to the overall game (because the overarching plot is too ridiculous and goofy to theorize about).

Initially went with a bit of skepticism into this game because the art style didnt really impress me when looking at this game on the Steam page, a friend of mine then suggested I at least give Episode 0 a shot since it IS free and I thought; "Why not? Worst thing that could be is is that I waste my time." So I downloaded the game and got into it and the very first scene where you get to interact with how this world is, even if basic, had me hooked, especially with the sass and humor of its Main protagonist, Morgan and as I carried on to dwelve into the game further and further it dragged me in more and more with the mysteries it sets up early on about the case as well as about the main characters backstory. With that I became more and more emamored with the style this game oozed with its CGs, animations and character sprites! 


[h1]SPOILERS BELOW[/h1]

[spoiler]Also ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ that like reveal about Morgan at the tail end of Episode 0 really took me for a spin, I was fricking flabbergastered! Absolutely beautifully done. I finished up Episode 1 at the time of this review and I am really curious where the journey of these characters will lead to! Especially in regards to Serra and Morgans relationship, knowing what Morgan has done and how it will impact everything. [/spoiler]

This game does a good job of immersing you into the depressing corporate dystopian world it takes place in, as well as the morally grey characters that live in it. Some are selfish idealists and others are just trying to get by. The mystery itself is well written for each episode so far, with each new story beat always making sense regardless of how much of a twist it is. Like any good mystery, the story beats never feels like an illogical leap from the presented evidence and clues, and the keen eyed may even find themselves able to piece out key moments early on. 

The actual gameplay is entertaining as well with its casino/gambling aesthetic, with the art and music being very stylish as well.

A more complete review to come when all episodes are released.

Okay I played the first (free) case and.... What... the ♥♥♥♥?!  This is incredible!

So the obvious comparison here is Phoenix Wright and... yes.  It's basically that but it's a bit cyberpunk so your character smokes and gambles and the game doesn't feel a need to hold your hand so stinkin' much.

And.  AND!!  Navigating the evidence is framed as a high stakes game of poker, where you can raise or fold when the prosecution pushes back on your assertions.  Is it like... the best fit mechanically?  I'm not actually sure yet, but it immediately makes the game stand out as it's own unique thing, as well as gives Morgan some immediate character charm.

Finally, the colors and presentation are delightful even if I personally find the art style a bit of a turnoff.

Literally one of my favorite ongoing games. I am always excited for the next installation; I've been hooked since the demo and don't see that changing. I love the characters to death, and the balance between comedy and tension is always perfect. Please play of the Devil.

If Phoenix Wright, V41 Hall-A, and Danganronpa all had a love child, it would feel pretty similar to the gameplay you can expect here. A visual novel first and a courtroom thriller second, this game will take you to places and situations that explore a dystopian future where corporations are treated as beings and humanity takes a far back seat to profits.
The social commentary is not very subtle.

What I love about this game, having completed the prologue, Episode 1, and Episode 2 on High Roller mode, are the quirky characters, the references, the music, the backgrounds, and the subject matter.
You play as Morgan, a defense attorney who loves to argue and loves to win. Because she is your MC, her perspective is never quite hidden from you and yet every chapter reveals more layers to why she is the way she is and surprises you. There is a major story beat at the end of the prologue that is hinted at all along but flips the script on its head once known that I won't spoil here, but it makes you see things in a different light from that point on.

I want to say that I have no complaints about this game but I did find that while I enjoyed every second of it, the gameplay is definitely on the easy side. The gambling themes and imagery during court cases give a very similar vibe to how Danganronpa uses bullets and guns - very cool aesthetically and in implementation, but ultimately is a pretty shallow disguise to track progress. The stakes, even on the High Roller mode, never seem truly that high and it is still quite forgiving when you make mistakes. While there apparently is a Game Over state, I never came close to seeing it.
Despite how easy the trials were, though, I was eager to progress the story and see what twists and turns were next.

The exploration sections of the game are very simple and act as vehicles to both world-build and give you information. I did like there was a hotkey for clicking on objects that were present that you may not have moused over as you point and click.

All-in-all, I am very thoroughly entertained and await the release of further Episodes with great anticipation.
My chips are all on the table and I want to be dealt in.

Also I've never chuckled at an attempt of humour in a game before but did a few times with this and all without any cringe moments. The character design I saw in screenshots before I played worried me that the devs might try too hard to make the characters "edgy" but no, it's all good here.

The plot offered up enough that I actually felt I noticed a few key parts of the evidence without anything feeling too glaringly obvious. I liked the characters and the art style a lot. Seeing as how Episode 0 is free to play and how many positive reviews there are, I can't see how this game isn't way more popular.

I played on Steam Deck OLED and it worked flawlessly. It defaults to 30fps and high graphics but you can set that to ultra straight away with no issues and 30fps is just fine for a game like this as there's not a lot of on screen movement.

Like so many other reviews have said...

TRY THIS GAME!

[h2] Gentlepersons of the jury [/h2] 
I'm here to plea for this game to be [b] GUILTY [/b] of being a gem, asking for the maximum punishment of [b] being bought and played and enjoyed by the most ppl possible [/b] (starting from you gentlepersons of the jury). So that's my case:

[h3] The motives [/h3] 
How well is it written! Drawed! Played! The themes which are explored give the player an interesting view on some classics of the cyberpunk literature, while keeping them engaged thanks to the inspired courtroom mechanics and intriguing story. I wonder, gentlepersons of the jury, if you like lawyers-and-detectives stories, if you like VNs, Attorney-like games, and comics-like art, if that's the case will you be complelled to try this game? That's what I'm here for.

[h3] The means [/h3]
The noir setting and character roster are nuanced but memorable, with their unique quirks and backgrounds that do not make them all absolute heroes or villains. Au contraire, we cannot but try to understand their emotions, their pasts and what drove them to be who they are. All this intriguing and, yes, complicated web of lives and beings is lightened (sometimes even better delivered) by pixel-perfect comic reliefs: no character is only serious or only facetious, but each part of this game contributes to a beautiful story which keeps you engaged and curious, even attached to your favorite characters.
And what can I say about the art-direction, the music, most honorable jury, the personality of this game is perfectly expressed in every detail: the mixing of 3d environments and 2d, comic-like characters is not only an exercise in style, but it's well balanced and gives a surprisingly good result. They integrate very well one another and are smartly used in the narrative, setting the tone of the scenes, delivering all the emotions and suspense and high stakes challenges in and outside the courtroom.

[h3] The Opportunity [/h3]
While I get to the conclusion of my humble review, I must say every moment is an opportunity for this game to hit; I was lucky enough to see Lena Raine's contribution to the game, so being a humble person of culture I went here. And everyone of you will be lucky too, since episode 0 is [b] free [/b], giving you a taste of what's to come in the rest of the game. Or the series, if you want: each one is a case, and it gives you plenty of time to enjoy the atmosphere and the crafting of this hidden gem.

I can now rest my case only by yes, suggesting you this game, remembering to check the TW (especially [spoiler] episode 2 [/spoiler], and believing that the jury will make the right choice.

I'm not really a ~visual novel~ guy but who cares about that, this game whips. I've never played Phoenix Wright games so I don't know where this falls on that spectrum but for the skeptics out there, the gameplay is thus: you are Morgan, cyberpunk defense attorney with some... rough edges. You are presented with a case, you investigate, ask questions, chase down leads, all in service of building up your cache of credibility in the "boss fights" that are the actual trials which test your understanding of the narrative you've just read. If I say it's basically like that part of the SATs where you read a thing and then get quizzed on it, that would be underselling the appeal. The world is cool and weird and gross and fun to learn about. The characters are amazingly written, and their interactions are genuinely hilarious. Here's a little bait for a certain gamer: if you like the Queen from Deltarune chapter 2 you're gonna love the Robot Judge in Episode 1. 

The writing is great, the world is interesting, the moral explorations are deep without being ham-fisted... it's just an incredible piece of media. Oh and are you mad it's not finished? You silly goose? It means you get to have multiple fully satisfying self-contained adventures within this perfect world, each a delicious surprise! Learn to yearn for once!!

Of the Devil is now one of my favorite games.

Remarkably engrossing story so far. I enjoyed Ace attorney quite a lot but other than that and a quick jaunt through Tyrion Cuthbert, never stuck much to the subgenre. Successfully, much like AA, every character you meet is a fun and memorable experience with a distinct depth to their personalities, even the less important ones that you might have had low expectations for initially, or that you don't even talk to for very long. But I think even AA suffered from a comparatively weak protagonist, and yet here, Morgan, our MC, is an intriguing and thoroughly witty character in her own right. While there's a million instances of media today involving outright autistic characters to explore the idea of "humanity" with, she makes for an exceptional case by 1. being the main protagonist rather than just a side character (although you certainly have others... quite significantly so, in fact) and 2. being [spoiler] a born psychopath [/spoiler].  

You're /really/ not supposed to actually project yourself on Morgan at all. Unless you fit the criteria, I suppose. Yes, haha, hot woman, I know; but I have a great many concerns if you think of her as "literally you". There are ways to question what it means to be human or object to societal expectations, without being... her. A certain character she comes to closely affiliate with seems perhaps specifically intended to demonstrate this.

Speaking of hot woman though, the artstyle [i]is[/i] really charming. Rough, but colorful, expressive, readable... to say nothing of the added visual flairs sprinkled in at points to spice it up. Some of which admittedly feels sliiightly overdone, or overused, but just slightly. Easily looked past. I'd say it's already among my favorite indie artstyles I've seen in general; hell, I almost want to use Morgan as a pfp, although, see above, might go a little bit against my point.

I was hooked and I don’t even think of myself as a boomer shooter guy but there was just something different about this game. The trap with all good fps games is attempting to describe how they feel. Its hard to escape the vapid definitions of moving and shooting apart from fun and satisfying. You have a grappling hook I guess, and it's your tongue because you're a frog, so that's good. You are bouncy and fast but you are always in control so there is little else you can ask for. The arsenal and charms are wonderful but more or less what you’d expect. The major shake up is that you have equipable spells that act as your only source of healing. 
	I saw a post from the dev on twitter that was showing off a boss and that's what really got me interested. I haven’t played a game that executed the voxel artstyle to this kind of scale. The art itself is amazing but it also seems to be incredibly efficient. The lighting is just incredible and there are no loads in this entire game. I’ve heard people call this a metroidvania, but aside from a couple instances of new tools getting you to the immediate next area I would disagree. I think what makes people feel that way tho is the fact that you can walk from one end of the world to the other without a break in the gameplay. It's still tightly designed levels; they just flow into each other quite naturally. 
	But as much as I like wandering thru this world, the boss and encounter design is what really kept me going. 20 unique bosses doesn’t sound totally mind blowing until you play the game and experience just how different they are. The boss design in this game puts the rest of the industry to shame. Bosses all have their own unique areas, most have their own gimmicks or even entire mechanics and they all have multiple phases. They are rewarding to fight and even more so to master because some of these guys are complete bastards. Really this whole game is. I struggled in sections of this game more than any other. Even some of the normal enemy encounters gave me more trouble than some bosses, but it always felt doable. I had plenty of ways to experiment and I had enough leeway. I always felt like I was just one idea or one attack pattern away from surviving. 
	What put this game over the edge is that beyond an adventure it really felt like fighting in a mythical struggle. There is little in the way of text or story but what is there does track. There are some rules to this world and you can observe them in action. Everything else just feels strange and magical. These lands don’t make sense for living in but they are also too strange to just feel like video game levels either. The closest comparison I can think of is wonderland. You can open doorways into places across the map. A lot of spaces don’t make sense geographically or spatially. It feels really weird to go on about this when you play as a frog with a gun, but there is still a sense of magic here.

I've been thinking about this game a lot over the last several months, and I'm overdue to say some things. I have a lot of thoughts about it and I'm not particularly good at structured writing, so my thoughts are going to be all over the place, but feel free to take it as it is (or not).

(As a disclaimer: after playing through this game, I have since worked with the developer on releasing an item randomizer mod for this game through Archipelago. I do not have a neutral relationship with this developer, so measure my opinions accordingly. I don't feel like I held any punches in this review, though.)

In short, this game is an indie gem. It's a one-person pet project at the end of the day, and it's got all the benefits and drawbacks that comes with that. It's got some rough edges, but it's got a lot of places where it really shines, most obviously so in the meat-and-potatoes of the gameplay itself.

The core gameplay experience wears its inspirations on its sleeves. You've got Boomer Shooter-style gunplay attached to a metroidvania-style world exploration, blocked out with learn-or-die soulslike bosses. The end result is a gameplay loop that, as far as I know, doesn't really exist anywhere else. I think that's a great shame, because these mechanics work together in a way that is much greater than the sum of their parts, and Frogmonster puts it to full effect. I do find myself wishing that the game set itself up to allow more of the backtracking and "getting lost" that metroidvanias are known for, but I think that's a limitation of genre-fusion like this. You have to strike a balance between the features that can appeal to fans of both parent genres, so I don't fault Ben for this. Despite the obvious inspiration from Team Cherry's work in this game, please know going into it that you're not getting Hollow Knight With Guns when you pick this up; this game is trying to do something else. 

That something else, in my opinion? It's the boss fights. It's what makes the game what is is. I think there's somewhere in the range of 40 of them as of time of writing, when I think any shooter game would be noteworthy if it had 5 of this caliber. Shooter games as a whole have historically struggled to create interesting setpieces and challenge fights that extend much beyond "shoot the thing until it dies". Frogmonster's focus on management of your movement options and ammo/mana creates an interesting back and forth dance that feels more at home in a Dark Souls game than it does in something in this genre, but it works beautifully. Each of the bosses is memorable, and most of them are genuine challenges that will force you to keep coming back until you figure them out. There's a couple of them that turn the rules on their head entirely and have "gimmicks" you have to solve like puzzles to beat them, if the more "Zelda" style bosses are more to your liking. There's so much variety with comparatively few tools and you're not gonna find that variety in other shooters. Oh, and yes, there IS a post-game boss rush mode. This game's got the Hollow Knight "Pantheons" thing going on, and it adds a lot of replay value to this game and gives you space to really master your movement. Technically a spoiler, but I don't care. That's a dealmaker for a lot of people, so I'm mentioning it.

The visual design is something else. The entire world is hand-crafted from the ground up out of these large, chunky voxels, and yet they've been sculpted out into these large, flowing shapes. The world is full of caves, rivers, trees, mushrooms, and other structures that all still feel impressively organic given the limitations of the medium. Ben has gone the deranged indie developer route of building his own game engine from scratch, and it pays off here in a lot of subtle ways that aren't obvious if you aren't familiar with the limitations of off-the-shelf engines. Of course, custom engine also means custom bugs. There are definitely some sticky geometry, performance hiccups, or weird graphical glitches here and there, especially around what I've since learned are the game's loading zones, but I hardly consider them a dealbreaker.

This game doesn't take itself too seriously, but I mean this in the best way possible. The game is extraordinarily goofy. Basically all of the sound effects in this game ultimately amount to Ben or some friend of his grunting into the microphone. The sound effects of your guns and spells are plosives and whistles from somebody's mouth. The NPCs you encounter babble nonsense at you in funny accents. Most of the enemy death/pain sound effects are just cries and screams pitched up or down to comic effect. And yet, it remains artistically cohesive. Combine this with the many goofy designs of friends and enemies alike (I will NEVER forget the wall-eyed stare of Yanoy as he lunges at me from across the room), and also combining it with the boss design (see above), and you have a very unserious but ultimately very charming package that you're not gonna experience anywhere else. You certainly would never get this from an established game studio, at the very least.

The storyline is... present. It's enough to justify the action of the gameplay, but that's the only real commendation I can give it. Again, the game doesn't take itself too seriously, you're here to get your ass kicked by a mastiff-sized screaming cicada with a gatling gun mounted on the back for 30 straight minutes.

I also have to talk about the music. I'm an audio guy, professionally speaking, so I'm going to be a lot more critical here than I think most people expect or want. The game is distributed with two separate soundtracks. There's the original soundtrack, which I believe was composed by Ben himself (EDIT: Nope, a college friend!), and then there's the "new" soundtrack, which was composed by Viho!. The classic soundtrack is a lot more minimalist and simple (as you would expect from solo dev music), and generally contributes a bit of a broodier atmosphere. The newer soundtrack, by contrast, has a lot more range in both the emotional and mechanical senses; it has much higher highs and much lower lows. There are several tracks which are individually excellent tracks, many of which provide a much-appreciated boost of energy to frantic, violent gunplay, and on average the soundtrack has a quirky and fun spirit that closer matches the tone of the rest of the game that the original lacked. However, there are a handful of places in the soundtrack where there are clear audio execution errors (miscut loops, audio pops) that I find very distracting, and a few individual tracks that are a bit disjointed from the adjacent tones/styles, or I otherwise am not sure I understand the vision on. But, hey, flipping between the two soundtracks is very easy, so if you want my recommendation for the best experience, switch to Classic when in the Lost Swamp (the first region) as well as Old Road and the areas immediately past it ([spoiler]Old Wood, Estate, Fog Garden, and the combats therein. Well and Thickness can stay New. [/spoiler]) (sorry Viho, I love a good polyrhythm but your inner pianist is showing. Those drums ain't it!). Switch to the New soundtrack for everything else.

All in all, Frogmonster has shaped up to be one of my favorite videogames of all time. I'm comfortable calling it my GOTY 2025. I've got enough issues with it that I think I can't comfortably call it a "masterpiece" by any real means, but the vision is absolutely there, and the bones of the game are strong. Ben Jungwirth has vision and a lot of other strengths which shine through in this game, and I would love to see what he could be able to do if had a small team of talent to shore up some of his weaker skills.

After recently playing this game again thanks to an Archipelago randomizer, I found myself having so much fun on my second playthrough that it inspired me to write a review. Frogmonster is a cutesy, comfy fps metriodvania, with bullet hell and especially heavy on boss-rush elements that genuinely puts AAA games to shame with their bosses. There are 30+ bosses, all of which that have truly unique concepts, attacks patterns, mechanics and design that makes no two feel even remotely alike. This concept is only pushed further with the Boss Soup mode near the end of the game, which cooks up those same bosses with extra phases, more attacks, and twists. Between boss battles, you explore the atmospheric world uncovering the story, platforming and doing small puzzles, all while collecting bugs that act as modifiers to create unique playstyles that change how each area is approached.

Huge shoutout to the solo dev who created this gameplay masterpiece, achieving what big studios struggle with, and then doing it a dozen more times with each unique boss added.

This has better bosses than any other FPS I've played, and there are a ton of them.

Super atmospheric and charming. This game is severely underrated.

My friend gifted this game to me in about June of 2025, solely because he liked it a lot and wanted to share it. I hadn't heard of the game before that, and couldn't run it well until November, so I didn't really play it until then.

I really liked Frogmonster. It's creative and consistently feels off kilter, which serves to make it experientially unique. I particularly like the world the game presents, both in terms of its narrative premise and worldbuilding, and the exploratory aspect of the game. Despite the somewhat open spatial progression, it has a coherent and deft tonal progression that fed into my interest in the world. Although the music tends to be comprised of short loops, usually coming across as a bit rudimentary, it too is artistically coherent. Really that's true of the game as a whole: it's a bit rough and rudimentary, but that quality helps build the unique artistic experience that the game provides.

The writing also follows this trend, although it falters in certain ways. I'd like to be unspecific so as to not unduly prime anyone's opinion on the game, but that was the only part that I felt notably let down by.

Gameplay-wise, Frogmonster is rather simple moment-to-moment. Complexity of play mostly lies in build strategy - which there isn't a ton of - and boss novelty - which there [i]is[/i] quite a bit of. While I don't love every boss fight, there's an impressive variety and creativity to them which at least makes things interesting if nothing else. The game does really well in exploring its simple combat, and the ratio between that and exploration is just about perfect for my tastes.

To sum it up... Frogmonster is an exploratory first-person shooter with a focus on bosses, set in a neat, strange little world. It's basic, but incredibly deft at making the most of limitations. It also has official Archipelago support which is very cool, the game is very well suited for it c:

There's a reason why this game is rated so high. Exploration, boss fights and the aesthetic are the game's main highlights and it does all of these extremely well. Every time I would feel that a boss was unfair and impossible, I would eventually figure it out. Such a rewarding experience.

Tight gunplay, unique bosses and enemies, a fun skill selection mechanic, and interesting weapon upgrades.

This is a really solid boss battle game. The frog aesthetic is fantastic, and all the boss designs and gimmicks are a lot of fun. Frogmonster, developed and published by Ben Jungwirth, is an audacious and deeply personal indie project that feels unconstrained by genre expectations. At a glance, it presents itself as a quirky first-person shooter set in a colorful voxel world starring an unlikely amphibian hero. Spend any real time with it, however, and Frogmonster quickly reveals itself as a dense hybrid of Metroidvania exploration, Soulslike boss design, and methodical FPS combat, all woven together by a strong sense of experimentation and creative confidence. It is a game that constantly surprises, not only in what it attempts, but in how successfully it brings those disparate ideas together.

Exploration forms the backbone of the experience. Frogmonster’s world is fully three-dimensional and non-linear, encouraging players to roam freely through interconnected regions rather than follow a rigid sequence of levels. New areas often loop back into older ones, and progress is frequently gated by abilities, weapons, or tools acquired later in the journey. This structure gives exploration a strong sense of purpose, as revisiting familiar spaces with new capabilities unlocks shortcuts, hidden encounters, and optional challenges. The absence of heavy hand-holding reinforces the feeling of discovery, making progress feel earned rather than prescribed.

Combat is where Frogmonster asserts its identity most strongly. Although it uses a first-person perspective, encounters rarely devolve into simple run-and-gun chaos. Enemies demand awareness, positioning, and timing, especially as the game introduces more complex attack patterns and environmental hazards. The boss fights are the clearest expression of this philosophy. With a large roster of unique bosses, each encounter feels deliberately crafted around distinct mechanics that must be learned and respected. These battles often punish impatience and reward observation, echoing Soulslike design principles while still maintaining the immediacy and intensity of an FPS. Victory against a difficult boss brings a genuine sense of accomplishment, not just because of mechanical difficulty, but because success comes from understanding the encounter rather than overpowering it.

Progression systems add further depth to this loop. Frogmonster offers a variety of weapons and spells that cater to different approaches, allowing players to adapt their playstyle based on preference or necessity. Firearms coexist with magical abilities, and the interplay between them encourages experimentation rather than strict optimization. One of the game’s most distinctive mechanics involves collecting insects using the frog’s tongue, which then provide passive bonuses or modify gameplay in subtle but meaningful ways. This system blends humor with strategy, reinforcing the game’s playful tone while giving players additional layers of customization and decision-making.

Visually, the game’s voxel art style walks a fine line between charming and unsettling. Environments are built from simple blocks, but strong lighting, creative enemy design, and varied color palettes prevent the world from feeling flat or repetitive. The contrast between the cute, almost toy-like presentation and the often intense, punishing combat creates a distinctive atmosphere that feels uniquely Frogmonster. It is a world that invites curiosity while simultaneously warning that danger is never far away.

The tone of the game is equally distinctive. Frogmonster doesn’t take itself too seriously, but it also never undermines its own challenge. Humor exists alongside genuine tension, and moments of levity are often followed by demanding encounters that require focus and discipline. This tonal balance keeps the experience engaging over long play sessions, preventing it from becoming either exhausting or frivolous. The world feels strange, playful, and occasionally ominous, which suits the unconventional protagonist and setting perfectly.

There are, inevitably, rough edges. Navigation can sometimes feel vague due to limited mapping tools, and certain audio elements, particularly music repetition, may wear thin for some players over extended sessions. The Metroidvania elements, while effective, may feel lighter than genre purists expect, with fewer dramatic ability-based transformations than in more traditional examples. However, these shortcomings rarely overshadow the game’s strengths, and many players view them as natural trade-offs in a project of this scope created largely by a single developer.

What truly elevates Frogmonster is the sense of authorship behind it. Every system, encounter, and design choice feels intentional, as though the game exists because its creator wanted to explore these ideas without compromise. Post-launch support and additional content further reinforce this impression, extending the game’s lifespan and demonstrating a clear commitment to its community.

In the end, Frogmonster stands as a bold and memorable indie achievement. It may not fit neatly into a single genre, but that refusal to conform is precisely what makes it compelling. By blending first-person combat, exploration-driven progression, and demanding boss encounters into a cohesive whole, it delivers an experience that feels both challenging and deeply original. For players who enjoy discovery, mastery, and games that dare to experiment, Frogmonster offers a journey that is as rewarding as it is unconventional.

Rating: 9/10

The sheer creativity on display in this game's boss design is genuinely insane. To put things into perspective, there's a boss where partway through, another of the same boss joins and you have to fight two at once. This is on the lower end of creativity and surprise for Frogmonster.

Surprise is a great word, because your expectations are constantly being challenged in this game, not in a bad way. Sometimes in games you're surprised and it's bad because what you were expecting was better than what you got, like hyping up a boss just for them to die in one hit. In Frogmonster you're surprised by the new ways enemies and bosses move and attack, forcing you to adapt.

This is the type of fps that's more concerned with letting you encounter new enemies than building a roster of about a dozen enemies that are used throughout the game. The focus is more on exploration and discovery. The game also doesn't break new ground with the weapons but each is customisable to suit how each individual player uses them.

Where the game really gets interesting is with its spell and upgrade system. Your spells will heal when they hit an enemy, basically doing away with the need for an 'estus flask'. Meanwhile the upgrades work very similarly to Hollow Knight's, except each upgrade is a bug you have to find and eat in the world, you know, because you're a frog.

Overal I'd rate it 6/6 mysterious eyes on a door, I thoroughly enjoyed playing this game and would recommend it to anyone who likes shooters and soulslikes.

By virtually every conceivable measure of "is this a good game" I can think of, Frogmonster punches far, far above its weight. It's fun, charming, challenging. It's a pinnacle example of video games as interactive art - the relatively straightforward story serves as the perfect vehicle to make you into a frog, give you some guns, and send you out into a vibrant world to turn a bunch of bad guys into psaladogu. I got to experience Ben Jungwirth's creativity in real time through an immersive experience, and it was awesome.

Some specific things I'd like to highlight:
[list]
	[*] [b]Guns[/b] - Varied, creative, impactful. All are fun to use and I had memorable clutch moments with each one. The upgrade system actually makes meaningful changes to how a given gun works rather than just "same gun but more damage", and there are situational pros and cons to the upgrades that add a layer of strategy when selecting loadouts for different scenarios.
	[*] [b]Sound design[/b] - Largely human-made noises, goofy in a way that contributes to the tone. I felt like a little action figure dropped into the heat of battle, championed to victory by a passionate and powerful child, except in the context of Frogmonster the action figure is a frog with a gun and the child is a bird demigod with yellow eyes.
	[*] [b]Music[/b] - Always complimentary of the areas, bosses and vibes. Some tracks are genuine bangers and some honestly helped me lock in ಠ_ಠ during the harder boss fights
	[*] [b]Movement[/b] - Nailed. It's fast, precise, and simple, forcing you to lean on your fundamentals to overcome challenges. It's just you, your timing, and your spacing. As a bonus, your tongue eventually becomes a grappling hook, and I am unabashedly a little slut for a grappling hook.
	[*] [b]Bosses[/b] - Rightfully highlighted in many reviews as a standout feature. The game description itself presents them as soulslike but I say they greatly outperform that moniker. My thoughts here are nuanced but for brevity's sake I'll say that I don't fully disagree with the idea of rollslop; if we're being honest, a great many soulslike bosses can be broken down to pressing the dodge and attack buttons at the right times until the bad guy is dead. With enough pattern mixups that can still be fun, but it can also be a little one-note. Frogmonster's big bads are much more interactive and mechanically creative; they're built like action puzzles, incorporating bullet hell-ing and platforming into a combat system that emphasizes constant shmoving and mshooting over the pseudo-turn-based tilt that soulslike often implies. These ingredients are mixed and matched and then plated with an immense variety of visual designs to make 30+ unique stews (somewhat literally) of bossy goodness to slurp through, and not a single one is anywhere near bad.
	[*] [b]Level design[/b] - Art and level design is truly great. So many different biomes, moods, variations on gameplay, etc. Each individual zone is more or less linear with some deviation, but I think it's ok for the game's scope to focus more on finely-tuned combat encounters and atmosphere than intricate labyrinthine zones.
	[*] [b]NPCs[/b] - By and large just so damn charming. Funny lines, fun voices, distinct personalities, good sidequests.
	[*] [b]Supo[/b] - Supo
	[*] [b]Frog[/b] - Monster
[/list]
Thanks for making this game, Ben. I had a lot of fun and I'm excited to pick my way through the soup challenges. Looking forward to playing whatever you decide to create next!

I've been thinking about this game a lot over the last several months, and I'm overdue to say some things. I have a lot of thoughts about it and I'm not particularly good at structured writing, so my thoughts are going to be all over the place, but feel free to take it as it is (or not).

(As a disclaimer: after playing through this game, I have since worked with the developer on releasing an item randomizer mod for this game through Archipelago. I do not have a neutral relationship with this developer, so measure my opinions accordingly. I don't feel like I held any punches in this review, though.)

In short, this game is an indie gem. It's a one-person pet project at the end of the day, and it's got all the benefits and drawbacks that comes with that. It's got some rough edges, but it's got a lot of places where it really shines, most obviously so in the meat-and-potatoes of the gameplay itself.

The core gameplay experience wears its inspirations on its sleeves. You've got Boomer Shooter-style gunplay attached to a metroidvania-style world exploration, blocked out with learn-or-die soulslike bosses. The end result is a gameplay loop that, as far as I know, doesn't really exist anywhere else. I think that's a great shame, because these mechanics work together in a way that is much greater than the sum of their parts, and Frogmonster puts it to full effect. I do find myself wishing that the game set itself up to allow more of the backtracking and "getting lost" that metroidvanias are known for, but I think that's a limitation of genre-fusion like this. You have to strike a balance between the features that can appeal to fans of both parent genres, so I don't fault Ben for this. Despite the obvious inspiration from Team Cherry's work in this game, please know going into it that you're not getting Hollow Knight With Guns when you pick this up; this game is trying to do something else. 

That something else, in my opinion? It's the boss fights. It's what makes the game what is is. I think there's somewhere in the range of 40 of them as of time of writing, when I think any shooter game would be noteworthy if it had 5 of this caliber. Shooter games as a whole have historically struggled to create interesting setpieces and challenge fights that extend much beyond "shoot the thing until it dies". Frogmonster's focus on management of your movement options and ammo/mana creates an interesting back and forth dance that feels more at home in a Dark Souls game than it does in something in this genre, but it works beautifully. Each of the bosses is memorable, and most of them are genuine challenges that will force you to keep coming back until you figure them out. There's a couple of them that turn the rules on their head entirely and have "gimmicks" you have to solve like puzzles to beat them, if the more "Zelda" style bosses are more to your liking. There's so much variety with comparatively few tools and you're not gonna find that variety in other shooters. Oh, and yes, there IS a post-game boss rush mode. This game's got the Hollow Knight "Pantheons" thing going on, and it adds a lot of replay value to this game and gives you space to really master your movement. Technically a spoiler, but I don't care. That's a dealmaker for a lot of people, so I'm mentioning it.

The visual design is something else. The entire world is hand-crafted from the ground up out of these large, chunky voxels, and yet they've been sculpted out into these large, flowing shapes. The world is full of caves, rivers, trees, mushrooms, and other structures that all still feel impressively organic given the limitations of the medium. Ben has gone the deranged indie developer route of building his own game engine from scratch, and it pays off here in a lot of subtle ways that aren't obvious if you aren't familiar with the limitations of off-the-shelf engines. Of course, custom engine also means custom bugs. There are definitely some sticky geometry, performance hiccups, or weird graphical glitches here and there, especially around what I've since learned are the game's loading zones, but I hardly consider them a dealbreaker.

This game doesn't take itself too seriously, but I mean this in the best way possible. The game is extraordinarily goofy. Basically all of the sound effects in this game ultimately amount to Ben or some friend of his grunting into the microphone. The sound effects of your guns and spells are plosives and whistles from somebody's mouth. The NPCs you encounter babble nonsense at you in funny accents. Most of the enemy death/pain sound effects are just cries and screams pitched up or down to comic effect. And yet, it remains artistically cohesive. Combine this with the many goofy designs of friends and enemies alike (I will NEVER forget the wall-eyed stare of Yanoy as he lunges at me from across the room), and also combining it with the boss design (see above), and you have a very unserious but ultimately very charming package that you're not gonna experience anywhere else. You certainly would never get this from an established game studio, at the very least.

The storyline is... present. It's enough to justify the action of the gameplay, but that's the only real commendation I can give it. Again, the game doesn't take itself too seriously, you're here to get your ass kicked by a mastiff-sized screaming cicada with a gatling gun mounted on the back for 30 straight minutes.

I also have to talk about the music. I'm an audio guy, professionally speaking, so I'm going to be a lot more critical here than I think most people expect or want. The game is distributed with two separate soundtracks. There's the original soundtrack, which I believe was composed by Ben himself (EDIT: Nope, a college friend!), and then there's the "new" soundtrack, which was composed by Viho!. The classic soundtrack is a lot more minimalist and simple (as you would expect from solo dev music), and generally contributes a bit of a broodier atmosphere. The newer soundtrack, by contrast, has a lot more range in both the emotional and mechanical senses; it has much higher highs and much lower lows. There are several tracks which are individually excellent tracks, many of which provide a much-appreciated boost of energy to frantic, violent gunplay, and on average the soundtrack has a quirky and fun spirit that closer matches the tone of the rest of the game that the original lacked. However, there are a handful of places in the soundtrack where there are clear audio execution errors (miscut loops, audio pops) that I find very distracting, and a few individual tracks that are a bit disjointed from the adjacent tones/styles, or I otherwise am not sure I understand the vision on. But, hey, flipping between the two soundtracks is very easy, so if you want my recommendation for the best experience, switch to Classic when in the Lost Swamp (the first region) as well as Old Road and the areas immediately past it ([spoiler]Old Wood, Estate, Fog Garden, and the combats therein. Well and Thickness can stay New. [/spoiler]) (sorry Viho, I love a good polyrhythm but your inner pianist is showing. Those drums ain't it!). Switch to the New soundtrack for everything else.

All in all, Frogmonster has shaped up to be one of my favorite videogames of all time. I'm comfortable calling it my GOTY 2025. I've got enough issues with it that I think I can't comfortably call it a "masterpiece" by any real means, but the vision is absolutely there, and the bones of the game are strong. Ben Jungwirth has vision and a lot of other strengths which shine through in this game, and I would love to see what he could be able to do if had a small team of talent to shore up some of his weaker skills.

After recently playing this game again thanks to an Archipelago randomizer, I found myself having so much fun on my second playthrough that it inspired me to write a review. Frogmonster is a cutesy, comfy fps metriodvania, with bullet hell and especially heavy on boss-rush elements that genuinely puts AAA games to shame with their bosses. There are 30+ bosses, all of which that have truly unique concepts, attacks patterns, mechanics and design that makes no two feel even remotely alike. This concept is only pushed further with the Boss Soup mode near the end of the game, which cooks up those same bosses with extra phases, more attacks, and twists. Between boss battles, you explore the atmospheric world uncovering the story, platforming and doing small puzzles, all while collecting bugs that act as modifiers to create unique playstyles that change how each area is approached.

Huge shoutout to the solo dev who created this gameplay masterpiece, achieving what big studios struggle with, and then doing it a dozen more times with each unique boss added.

My friend gifted this game to me in about June of 2025, solely because he liked it a lot and wanted to share it. I hadn't heard of the game before that, and couldn't run it well until November, so I didn't really play it until then.

I really liked Frogmonster. It's creative and consistently feels off kilter, which serves to make it experientially unique. I particularly like the world the game presents, both in terms of its narrative premise and worldbuilding, and the exploratory aspect of the game. Despite the somewhat open spatial progression, it has a coherent and deft tonal progression that fed into my interest in the world. Although the music tends to be comprised of short loops, usually coming across as a bit rudimentary, it too is artistically coherent. Really that's true of the game as a whole: it's a bit rough and rudimentary, but that quality helps build the unique artistic experience that the game provides.

The writing also follows this trend, although it falters in certain ways. I'd like to be unspecific so as to not unduly prime anyone's opinion on the game, but that was the only part that I felt notably let down by.

Gameplay-wise, Frogmonster is rather simple moment-to-moment. Complexity of play mostly lies in build strategy - which there isn't a ton of - and boss novelty - which there [i]is[/i] quite a bit of. While I don't love every boss fight, there's an impressive variety and creativity to them which at least makes things interesting if nothing else. The game does really well in exploring its simple combat, and the ratio between that and exploration is just about perfect for my tastes.

To sum it up... Frogmonster is an exploratory first-person shooter with a focus on bosses, set in a neat, strange little world. It's basic, but incredibly deft at making the most of limitations. It also has official Archipelago support which is very cool, the game is very well suited for it c:

There's a reason why this game is rated so high. Exploration, boss fights and the aesthetic are the game's main highlights and it does all of these extremely well. Every time I would feel that a boss was unfair and impossible, I would eventually figure it out. Such a rewarding experience.

Its hard to escape the vapid definitions of moving and shooting apart from fun and satisfying. You have a grappling hook I guess, and it's your tongue because you're a frog, so that's good. You are bouncy and fast but you are always in control so there is little else you can ask for. The arsenal and charms are wonderful but more or less what you’d expect. The major shake up is that you have equipable spells that act as your only source of healing. 
	I saw a post from the dev on twitter that was showing off a boss and that's what really got me interested. I haven’t played a game that executed the voxel artstyle to this kind of scale. The art itself is amazing but it also seems to be incredibly efficient. The lighting is just incredible and there are no loads in this entire game. I’ve heard people call this a metroidvania, but aside from a couple instances of new tools getting you to the immediate next area I would disagree. I think what makes people feel that way tho is the fact that you can walk from one end of the world to the other without a break in the gameplay. It's still tightly designed levels; they just flow into each other quite naturally. 
	But as much as I like wandering thru this world, the boss and encounter design is what really kept me going. 20 unique bosses doesn’t sound totally mind blowing until you play the game and experience just how different they are. The boss design in this game puts the rest of the industry to shame. Bosses all have their own unique areas, most have their own gimmicks or even entire mechanics and they all have multiple phases. They are rewarding to fight and even more so to master because some of these guys are complete bastards. Really this whole game is. I struggled in sections of this game more than any other. Even some of the normal enemy encounters gave me more trouble than some bosses, but it always felt doable. I had plenty of ways to experiment and I had enough leeway. I always felt like I was just one idea or one attack pattern away from surviving. 
	What put this game over the edge is that beyond an adventure it really felt like fighting in a mythical struggle. There is little in the way of text or story but what is there does track. There are some rules to this world and you can observe them in action. Everything else just feels strange and magical. These lands don’t make sense for living in but they are also too strange to just feel like video game levels either. The closest comparison I can think of is wonderland. You can open doorways into places across the map. A lot of spaces don’t make sense geographically or spatially. It feels really weird to go on about this when you play as a frog with a gun, but there is still a sense of magic here.

Frogmonster, developed and published by Ben Jungwirth, is an audacious and deeply personal indie project that feels unconstrained by genre expectations. At a glance, it presents itself as a quirky first-person shooter set in a colorful voxel world starring an unlikely amphibian hero. Spend any real time with it, however, and Frogmonster quickly reveals itself as a dense hybrid of Metroidvania exploration, Soulslike boss design, and methodical FPS combat, all woven together by a strong sense of experimentation and creative confidence. It is a game that constantly surprises, not only in what it attempts, but in how successfully it brings those disparate ideas together.

Exploration forms the backbone of the experience. Frogmonster’s world is fully three-dimensional and non-linear, encouraging players to roam freely through interconnected regions rather than follow a rigid sequence of levels. New areas often loop back into older ones, and progress is frequently gated by abilities, weapons, or tools acquired later in the journey. This structure gives exploration a strong sense of purpose, as revisiting familiar spaces with new capabilities unlocks shortcuts, hidden encounters, and optional challenges. The absence of heavy hand-holding reinforces the feeling of discovery, making progress feel earned rather than prescribed.

Combat is where Frogmonster asserts its identity most strongly. Although it uses a first-person perspective, encounters rarely devolve into simple run-and-gun chaos. Enemies demand awareness, positioning, and timing, especially as the game introduces more complex attack patterns and environmental hazards. The boss fights are the clearest expression of this philosophy. With a large roster of unique bosses, each encounter feels deliberately crafted around distinct mechanics that must be learned and respected. These battles often punish impatience and reward observation, echoing Soulslike design principles while still maintaining the immediacy and intensity of an FPS. Victory against a difficult boss brings a genuine sense of accomplishment, not just because of mechanical difficulty, but because success comes from understanding the encounter rather than overpowering it.

Progression systems add further depth to this loop. Frogmonster offers a variety of weapons and spells that cater to different approaches, allowing players to adapt their playstyle based on preference or necessity. Firearms coexist with magical abilities, and the interplay between them encourages experimentation rather than strict optimization. One of the game’s most distinctive mechanics involves collecting insects using the frog’s tongue, which then provide passive bonuses or modify gameplay in subtle but meaningful ways. This system blends humor with strategy, reinforcing the game’s playful tone while giving players additional layers of customization and decision-making.

Visually, the game’s voxel art style walks a fine line between charming and unsettling. Environments are built from simple blocks, but strong lighting, creative enemy design, and varied color palettes prevent the world from feeling flat or repetitive. The contrast between the cute, almost toy-like presentation and the often intense, punishing combat creates a distinctive atmosphere that feels uniquely Frogmonster. It is a world that invites curiosity while simultaneously warning that danger is never far away.

The tone of the game is equally distinctive. Frogmonster doesn’t take itself too seriously, but it also never undermines its own challenge. Humor exists alongside genuine tension, and moments of levity are often followed by demanding encounters that require focus and discipline. This tonal balance keeps the experience engaging over long play sessions, preventing it from becoming either exhausting or frivolous. The world feels strange, playful, and occasionally ominous, which suits the unconventional protagonist and setting perfectly.

There are, inevitably, rough edges. Navigation can sometimes feel vague due to limited mapping tools, and certain audio elements, particularly music repetition, may wear thin for some players over extended sessions. The Metroidvania elements, while effective, may feel lighter than genre purists expect, with fewer dramatic ability-based transformations than in more traditional examples. However, these shortcomings rarely overshadow the game’s strengths, and many players view them as natural trade-offs in a project of this scope created largely by a single developer.

What truly elevates Frogmonster is the sense of authorship behind it. Every system, encounter, and design choice feels intentional, as though the game exists because its creator wanted to explore these ideas without compromise. Post-launch support and additional content further reinforce this impression, extending the game’s lifespan and demonstrating a clear commitment to its community.

In the end, Frogmonster stands as a bold and memorable indie achievement. It may not fit neatly into a single genre, but that refusal to conform is precisely what makes it compelling. By blending first-person combat, exploration-driven progression, and demanding boss encounters into a cohesive whole, it delivers an experience that feels both challenging and deeply original. For players who enjoy discovery, mastery, and games that dare to experiment, Frogmonster offers a journey that is as rewarding as it is unconventional.
