I felt like a little action figure dropped into the heat of battle, championed to victory by a passionate and powerful child, except in the context of Frogmonster the action figure is a frog with a gun and the child is a bird demigod with yellow eyes.
	[*] [b]Music[/b] - Always complimentary of the areas, bosses and vibes. Some tracks are genuine bangers and some honestly helped me lock in ಠ_ಠ during the harder boss fights
	[*] [b]Movement[/b] - Nailed. It's fast, precise, and simple, forcing you to lean on your fundamentals to overcome challenges. It's just you, your timing, and your spacing. As a bonus, your tongue eventually becomes a grappling hook, and I am unabashedly a little slut for a grappling hook.
	[*] [b]Bosses[/b] - Rightfully highlighted in many reviews as a standout feature. The game description itself presents them as soulslike but I say they greatly outperform that moniker. My thoughts here are nuanced but for brevity's sake I'll say that I don't fully disagree with the idea of rollslop; if we're being honest, a great many soulslike bosses can be broken down to pressing the dodge and attack buttons at the right times until the bad guy is dead. With enough pattern mixups that can still be fun, but it can also be a little one-note. Frogmonster's big bads are much more interactive and mechanically creative; they're built like action puzzles, incorporating bullet hell-ing and platforming into a combat system that emphasizes constant shmoving and mshooting over the pseudo-turn-based tilt that soulslike often implies. These ingredients are mixed and matched and then plated with an immense variety of visual designs to make 30+ unique stews (somewhat literally) of bossy goodness to slurp through, and not a single one is anywhere near bad.
	[*] [b]Level design[/b] - Art and level design is truly great. So many different biomes, moods, variations on gameplay, etc. Each individual zone is more or less linear with some deviation, but I think it's ok for the game's scope to focus more on finely-tuned combat encounters and atmosphere than intricate labyrinthine zones.
	[*] [b]NPCs[/b] - By and large just so damn charming. Funny lines, fun voices, distinct personalities, good sidequests.
	[*] [b]Supo[/b] - Supo
	[*] [b]Frog[/b] - Monster
[/list]
Thanks for making this game, Ben. I had a lot of fun and I'm excited to pick my way through the soup challenges. Looking forward to playing whatever you decide to create next!
Incredibly creative boss fights

The enemies feel pretty plain at times, and it can take a little bit to get used to the tongue controls, but the main course here is truly something else. An easy recommendation to anyone who doesn't mind goofy voxel graphics and the occasional hallway simulation.

It's a masterpiece.

I've been thinking about this game a lot over the last several months, and I'm overdue to say some things. I have a lot of thoughts about it and I'm not particularly good at structured writing, so my thoughts are going to be all over the place, but feel free to take it as it is (or not).

(As a disclaimer: after playing through this game, I have since worked with the developer on releasing an item randomizer mod for this game through Archipelago. I do not have a neutral relationship with this developer, so measure my opinions accordingly. I don't feel like I held any punches in this review, though.)

In short, this game is an indie gem. It's a one-person pet project at the end of the day, and it's got all the benefits and drawbacks that comes with that. It's got some rough edges, but it's got a lot of places where it really shines, most obviously so in the meat-and-potatoes of the gameplay itself.

The core gameplay experience wears its inspirations on its sleeves. You've got Boomer Shooter-style gunplay attached to a metroidvania-style world exploration, blocked out with learn-or-die soulslike bosses. The end result is a gameplay loop that, as far as I know, doesn't really exist anywhere else. I think that's a great shame, because these mechanics work together in a way that is much greater than the sum of their parts, and Frogmonster puts it to full effect. I do find myself wishing that the game set itself up to allow more of the backtracking and "getting lost" that metroidvanias are known for, but I think that's a limitation of genre-fusion like this. You have to strike a balance between the features that can appeal to fans of both parent genres, so I don't fault Ben for this. Despite the obvious inspiration from Team Cherry's work in this game, please know going into it that you're not getting Hollow Knight With Guns when you pick this up; this game is trying to do something else. 

That something else, in my opinion? It's the boss fights. It's what makes the game what is is. I think there's somewhere in the range of 40 of them as of time of writing, when I think any shooter game would be noteworthy if it had 5 of this caliber. Shooter games as a whole have historically struggled to create interesting setpieces and challenge fights that extend much beyond "shoot the thing until it dies". Frogmonster's focus on management of your movement options and ammo/mana creates an interesting back and forth dance that feels more at home in a Dark Souls game than it does in something in this genre, but it works beautifully. Each of the bosses is memorable, and most of them are genuine challenges that will force you to keep coming back until you figure them out. There's a couple of them that turn the rules on their head entirely and have "gimmicks" you have to solve like puzzles to beat them, if the more "Zelda" style bosses are more to your liking. There's so much variety with comparatively few tools and you're not gonna find that variety in other shooters. Oh, and yes, there IS a post-game boss rush mode. This game's got the Hollow Knight "Pantheons" thing going on, and it adds a lot of replay value to this game and gives you space to really master your movement. Technically a spoiler, but I don't care. That's a dealmaker for a lot of people, so I'm mentioning it.

The visual design is something else. The entire world is hand-crafted from the ground up out of these large, chunky voxels, and yet they've been sculpted out into these large, flowing shapes. The world is full of caves, rivers, trees, mushrooms, and other structures that all still feel impressively organic given the limitations of the medium. Ben has gone the deranged indie developer route of building his own game engine from scratch, and it pays off here in a lot of subtle ways that aren't obvious if you aren't familiar with the limitations of off-the-shelf engines. Of course, custom engine also means custom bugs. There are definitely some sticky geometry, performance hiccups, or weird graphical glitches here and there, especially around what I've since learned are the game's loading zones, but I hardly consider them a dealbreaker.

This game doesn't take itself too seriously, but I mean this in the best way possible. The game is extraordinarily goofy. Basically all of the sound effects in this game ultimately amount to Ben or some friend of his grunting into the microphone. The sound effects of your guns and spells are plosives and whistles from somebody's mouth. The NPCs you encounter babble nonsense at you in funny accents. Most of the enemy death/pain sound effects are just cries and screams pitched up or down to comic effect. And yet, it remains artistically cohesive. Combine this with the many goofy designs of friends and enemies alike (I will NEVER forget the wall-eyed stare of Yanoy as he lunges at me from across the room), and also combining it with the boss design (see above), and you have a very unserious but ultimately very charming package that you're not gonna experience anywhere else. You certainly would never get this from an established game studio, at the very least.

The storyline is... present. It's enough to justify the action of the gameplay, but that's the only real commendation I can give it. Again, the game doesn't take itself too seriously, you're here to get your ass kicked by a mastiff-sized screaming cicada with a gatling gun mounted on the back for 30 straight minutes.

I also have to talk about the music. I'm an audio guy, professionally speaking, so I'm going to be a lot more critical here than I think most people expect or want. The game is distributed with two separate soundtracks. There's the original soundtrack, which I believe was composed by Ben himself (EDIT: Nope, a college friend!), and then there's the "new" soundtrack, which was composed by Viho!. The classic soundtrack is a lot more minimalist and simple (as you would expect from solo dev music), and generally contributes a bit of a broodier atmosphere. The newer soundtrack, by contrast, has a lot more range in both the emotional and mechanical senses; it has much higher highs and much lower lows. There are several tracks which are individually excellent tracks, many of which provide a much-appreciated boost of energy to frantic, violent gunplay, and on average the soundtrack has a quirky and fun spirit that closer matches the tone of the rest of the game that the original lacked. However, there are a handful of places in the soundtrack where there are clear audio execution errors (miscut loops, audio pops) that I find very distracting, and a few individual tracks that are a bit disjointed from the adjacent tones/styles, or I otherwise am not sure I understand the vision on. But, hey, flipping between the two soundtracks is very easy, so if you want my recommendation for the best experience, switch to Classic when in the Lost Swamp (the first region) as well as Old Road and the areas immediately past it ([spoiler]Old Wood, Estate, Fog Garden, and the combats therein. Well and Thickness can stay New. [/spoiler]) (sorry Viho, I love a good polyrhythm but your inner pianist is showing. Those drums ain't it!). Switch to the New soundtrack for everything else.

All in all, Frogmonster has shaped up to be one of my favorite videogames of all time. I'm comfortable calling it my GOTY 2025. I've got enough issues with it that I think I can't comfortably call it a "masterpiece" by any real means, but the vision is absolutely there, and the bones of the game are strong. Ben Jungwirth has vision and a lot of other strengths which shine through in this game, and I would love to see what he could be able to do if had a small team of talent to shore up some of his weaker skills.

After recently playing this game again thanks to an Archipelago randomizer, I found myself having so much fun on my second playthrough that it inspired me to write a review. Frogmonster is a cutesy, comfy fps metriodvania, with bullet hell and especially heavy on boss-rush elements that genuinely puts AAA games to shame with their bosses. There are 30+ bosses, all of which that have truly unique concepts, attacks patterns, mechanics and design that makes no two feel even remotely alike. This concept is only pushed further with the Boss Soup mode near the end of the game, which cooks up those same bosses with extra phases, more attacks, and twists. Between boss battles, you explore the atmospheric world uncovering the story, platforming and doing small puzzles, all while collecting bugs that act as modifiers to create unique playstyles that change how each area is approached.

Huge shoutout to the solo dev who created this gameplay masterpiece, achieving what big studios struggle with, and then doing it a dozen more times with each unique boss added.

This has better bosses than any other FPS I've played, and there are a ton of them.

Super atmospheric and charming. This game is severely underrated.

First I thought I just bought a game for only kids and considered refunding, but it's good. Music is chill. Boss's are what provide the challange. The levels are well designed, too easy to find secrets. Wish there was a hard mode.
There are over 30 bosses, one even has more than 5 phases. Some are quite hard.

My friend gifted this game to me in about June of 2025, solely because he liked it a lot and wanted to share it. I hadn't heard of the game before that, and couldn't run it well until November, so I didn't really play it until then.

I really liked Frogmonster. It's creative and consistently feels off kilter, which serves to make it experientially unique. I particularly like the world the game presents, both in terms of its narrative premise and worldbuilding, and the exploratory aspect of the game. Despite the somewhat open spatial progression, it has a coherent and deft tonal progression that fed into my interest in the world. Although the music tends to be comprised of short loops, usually coming across as a bit rudimentary, it too is artistically coherent. Really that's true of the game as a whole: it's a bit rough and rudimentary, but that quality helps build the unique artistic experience that the game provides.

The writing also follows this trend, although it falters in certain ways. I'd like to be unspecific so as to not unduly prime anyone's opinion on the game, but that was the only part that I felt notably let down by.

Gameplay-wise, Frogmonster is rather simple moment-to-moment. Complexity of play mostly lies in build strategy - which there isn't a ton of - and boss novelty - which there [i]is[/i] quite a bit of. While I don't love every boss fight, there's an impressive variety and creativity to them which at least makes things interesting if nothing else. The game does really well in exploring its simple combat, and the ratio between that and exploration is just about perfect for my tastes.

To sum it up... Frogmonster is an exploratory first-person shooter with a focus on bosses, set in a neat, strange little world. It's basic, but incredibly deft at making the most of limitations. It also has official Archipelago support which is very cool, the game is very well suited for it c:

There's a reason why this game is rated so high. Exploration, boss fights and the aesthetic are the game's main highlights and it does all of these extremely well. Every time I would feel that a boss was unfair and impossible, I would eventually figure it out. Such a rewarding experience.

Tight gunplay, unique bosses and enemies, a fun skill selection mechanic, and interesting weapon upgrades.

My only criticism is the lack of schmovement. This is a game that would have benefited greatly from more robust movement mechanics.

The only strange thing about that is that I only played it a couple of times. Yep only a couple of sessions that accumulated into 22 hours of gameplay. I was hooked and I don’t even think of myself as a boomer shooter guy but there was just something different about this game. The trap with all good fps games is attempting to describe how they feel. Its hard to escape the vapid definitions of moving and shooting apart from fun and satisfying. You have a grappling hook I guess, and it's your tongue because you're a frog, so that's good. You are bouncy and fast but you are always in control so there is little else you can ask for. The arsenal and charms are wonderful but more or less what you’d expect. The major shake up is that you have equipable spells that act as your only source of healing. 
	I saw a post from the dev on twitter that was showing off a boss and that's what really got me interested. I haven’t played a game that executed the voxel artstyle to this kind of scale. The art itself is amazing but it also seems to be incredibly efficient. The lighting is just incredible and there are no loads in this entire game. I’ve heard people call this a metroidvania, but aside from a couple instances of new tools getting you to the immediate next area I would disagree. I think what makes people feel that way tho is the fact that you can walk from one end of the world to the other without a break in the gameplay. It's still tightly designed levels; they just flow into each other quite naturally. 
	But as much as I like wandering thru this world, the boss and encounter design is what really kept me going. 20 unique bosses doesn’t sound totally mind blowing until you play the game and experience just how different they are. The boss design in this game puts the rest of the industry to shame. Bosses all have their own unique areas, most have their own gimmicks or even entire mechanics and they all have multiple phases. They are rewarding to fight and even more so to master because some of these guys are complete bastards. Really this whole game is. I struggled in sections of this game more than any other. Even some of the normal enemy encounters gave me more trouble than some bosses, but it always felt doable. I had plenty of ways to experiment and I had enough leeway. I always felt like I was just one idea or one attack pattern away from surviving. 
	What put this game over the edge is that beyond an adventure it really felt like fighting in a mythical struggle. There is little in the way of text or story but what is there does track. There are some rules to this world and you can observe them in action. Everything else just feels strange and magical. These lands don’t make sense for living in but they are also too strange to just feel like video game levels either. The closest comparison I can think of is wonderland. You can open doorways into places across the map. A lot of spaces don’t make sense geographically or spatially. It feels really weird to go on about this when you play as a frog with a gun, but there is still a sense of magic here.
Easily one of the best games i played in 2025. Such a cool game and all the characters and enemies are so fun to see. And the music is great, too! Easy recommendation.
Alot of creativity in the boss fights and the story was simple but engaging 10/10

Genuinely one of the best games I've played. This game really deserves more visibility and people playing it. Absolutely unique and challenging gameplay. 

Starting the game sure it's a very basic and kid friendly seeming FPS, but the further in the challenge escalates and the bosses, oh man the bosses are so unique and fun to take down. Not a single boss in this game is a retread of another and the creativity is absurd. 

Play this game if you want something new. I think about this game often and wish I could play it with fresh eyes again.

This is a really solid boss battle game. The frog aesthetic is fantastic, and all the boss designs and gimmicks are a lot of fun. All the collectible bugs with different effects really emphasized the amphibian focus. Bosses ranged from annoying to peak design. Monsters are all a little too fast and end up face ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ you which is frustrating. Final two bosses rival Souls design in terms of enjoyment. A great selection of weapons with split upgrade paths really let you choose how you want to play the game. Very enjoyable game for ~$10-20, but didn't feel motivated to get all the achievements after the credits rolled.

Frogmonster, developed and published by Ben Jungwirth, is an audacious and deeply personal indie project that feels unconstrained by genre expectations. At a glance, it presents itself as a quirky first-person shooter set in a colorful voxel world starring an unlikely amphibian hero. Spend any real time with it, however, and Frogmonster quickly reveals itself as a dense hybrid of Metroidvania exploration, Soulslike boss design, and methodical FPS combat, all woven together by a strong sense of experimentation and creative confidence. It is a game that constantly surprises, not only in what it attempts, but in how successfully it brings those disparate ideas together.

Exploration forms the backbone of the experience. Frogmonster’s world is fully three-dimensional and non-linear, encouraging players to roam freely through interconnected regions rather than follow a rigid sequence of levels. New areas often loop back into older ones, and progress is frequently gated by abilities, weapons, or tools acquired later in the journey. This structure gives exploration a strong sense of purpose, as revisiting familiar spaces with new capabilities unlocks shortcuts, hidden encounters, and optional challenges. The absence of heavy hand-holding reinforces the feeling of discovery, making progress feel earned rather than prescribed.

Combat is where Frogmonster asserts its identity most strongly. Although it uses a first-person perspective, encounters rarely devolve into simple run-and-gun chaos. Enemies demand awareness, positioning, and timing, especially as the game introduces more complex attack patterns and environmental hazards. The boss fights are the clearest expression of this philosophy. With a large roster of unique bosses, each encounter feels deliberately crafted around distinct mechanics that must be learned and respected. These battles often punish impatience and reward observation, echoing Soulslike design principles while still maintaining the immediacy and intensity of an FPS. Victory against a difficult boss brings a genuine sense of accomplishment, not just because of mechanical difficulty, but because success comes from understanding the encounter rather than overpowering it.

Progression systems add further depth to this loop. Frogmonster offers a variety of weapons and spells that cater to different approaches, allowing players to adapt their playstyle based on preference or necessity. Firearms coexist with magical abilities, and the interplay between them encourages experimentation rather than strict optimization. One of the game’s most distinctive mechanics involves collecting insects using the frog’s tongue, which then provide passive bonuses or modify gameplay in subtle but meaningful ways. This system blends humor with strategy, reinforcing the game’s playful tone while giving players additional layers of customization and decision-making.

Visually, the game’s voxel art style walks a fine line between charming and unsettling. Environments are built from simple blocks, but strong lighting, creative enemy design, and varied color palettes prevent the world from feeling flat or repetitive. The contrast between the cute, almost toy-like presentation and the often intense, punishing combat creates a distinctive atmosphere that feels uniquely Frogmonster. It is a world that invites curiosity while simultaneously warning that danger is never far away.

The tone of the game is equally distinctive. Frogmonster doesn’t take itself too seriously, but it also never undermines its own challenge. Humor exists alongside genuine tension, and moments of levity are often followed by demanding encounters that require focus and discipline. This tonal balance keeps the experience engaging over long play sessions, preventing it from becoming either exhausting or frivolous. The world feels strange, playful, and occasionally ominous, which suits the unconventional protagonist and setting perfectly.

There are, inevitably, rough edges. Navigation can sometimes feel vague due to limited mapping tools, and certain audio elements, particularly music repetition, may wear thin for some players over extended sessions. The Metroidvania elements, while effective, may feel lighter than genre purists expect, with fewer dramatic ability-based transformations than in more traditional examples. However, these shortcomings rarely overshadow the game’s strengths, and many players view them as natural trade-offs in a project of this scope created largely by a single developer.

What truly elevates Frogmonster is the sense of authorship behind it. Every system, encounter, and design choice feels intentional, as though the game exists because its creator wanted to explore these ideas without compromise. Post-launch support and additional content further reinforce this impression, extending the game’s lifespan and demonstrating a clear commitment to its community.

In the end, Frogmonster stands as a bold and memorable indie achievement. It may not fit neatly into a single genre, but that refusal to conform is precisely what makes it compelling. By blending first-person combat, exploration-driven progression, and demanding boss encounters into a cohesive whole, it delivers an experience that feels both challenging and deeply original. For players who enjoy discovery, mastery, and games that dare to experiment, Frogmonster offers a journey that is as rewarding as it is unconventional.

Rating: 9/10

Quite a novel concept of a FPS mixed with metroidvania. Game is incredibly fun to play, never a dull moment during exploration or combat. Speaking of combat, boss fights are the main appeal. Very creative and varied, with cool gimmicks that will make you think "oh, that's a pretty neat idea"

Mechanics wise, Frogmaster got a very robust loop of resource juggling that rewards you doing well in combat. Exploration of the levels is not exactly non-linear, you're expected to take a specific path with a few detours, but there are places and stuff gated by having movement abilities, so you will backtrack a bunch.

Music is nice, I really dig the artstyle and voice acting is pretty funny. It does feel influenced by Hollow Knight in some way, but also stands out on its own.

Recommend the game very much, definitely a little gem to discover.
Fun combat. Great design. Even better music. 10/10.
Movement and aiming both feel fantastic

game_name: Teddy's Haven - A Fantasy Inspired Shop Simulator
review_text: This is truly a relaxing game. It's the only thing I've been able to have the energy to do while I'm in the process of mourning a loved one. I can't thank the developer enough for creating this safe place that let's me quietly remember the one that I miss.

game_name: Teddy's Haven - A Fantasy Inspired Shop Simulator
review_text:
This game has become a game that I come back to repeatedly. The simple task of running a small shop of curiosities for adventurers I thought would be engaging for a bit; however I have found that I return to this game time and time again and have since I've purchased it. Every new update brings me sheer joy and I can't stop myself from updating and booting it up to play. If you are looking for a cozy game you can get lost in, I highly recommend. 

Pros: 
- Mounts fit for a fairy! (One is a boulder and my absolute fav. You can ride it for miles.)
- NPCs that give buffs to the shop! They vary day by day. 
- Resources vary by season and can be used in a variety of crafting. 
- Loads and loads and loads of animals to engage with and gather from. (You can rename them!)
- Constant and consistent updates and additions as well as quality of life improvements. (The dev is a one man team but is extremely regular with fixes and updates.) 
- You can absolutely scuttle out of bounds and doodle about. 
- Decorations to make your shop cozy. 
- Plenty to craft!
- Expansions and other options to expand your products and space. 

Cons:
- None in my opinion. This game is the epitome of cozy gaming. 
- I keep forgetting to pay my bills on a daily basis and the book tells me to pay up D: 

And to the dev: Thank you. This game has become such a meditative vessel for me as a gamer who wanders from genre to genre. I find myself slowing down and enjoying the passing of time as I sit with Teddy behind the counter, watching our employees. The specific theme of remembrance has given me a space to process grief instead of avoid it like the plague. I will continue to return to this game for the foreseeable future. 

 I cannot recommend this game enough.

game_name: Teddy's Haven - A Fantasy Inspired Shop Simulator
review_text:
A wonderful game where you are a shop keeper of whatever you feel like selling. Only fruits and jams? Sure. Weapons and potions? Go for it? You want to line your walls with nothing but stone? Hell yeah.
I have approximately 20,000 bottles of milk to sell please help

game_name: Teddy's Haven - A Fantasy Inspired Shop Simulator
review_text: Imagine playing World of Warcraft, but being a shopkeeper! There is no rush for anything, all the mobs are friendly, and everyone is happy! You get to stock your store with what YOU want and the game rewards you for being kind. I love this game, it's my new hyperfixation!

game_name: Teddy's Haven - A Fantasy Inspired Shop Simulator
review_text: This is such a soothing, calming, charming game. There's no time pressure; time only moves when you light a candle to open the shop, and pauses again when it burns down until you go to bed to advance to the next day. You're free to roam around and harvest and collect, and meet the named characters that stop by to visit you and your little shop. There's a thread of hope and melancholy and remembrance that weaves through their stories. It's the perfect game to chill out to, or to soothe a broken heart. It's being updated regularly and has an active discord. Best comfort game on my list.

game_name: Teddy's Haven - A Fantasy Inspired Shop Simulator
review_text:
This is the game I've been looking for. A shop simulator with free building. And I mean truly free building. There's some bugs here and there, but overall, I am enjoying the heck out of this game. 

The only thing that would make this game perfect is if they made it fully compatible with Steam Deck. Right now it's only "playable" and it's not great, lol.

game_name: Teddy's Haven - A Fantasy Inspired Shop Simulator
review_text:
Went into this a little skeptical because as much as I enjoy shopkeeper simulators - Something about this game gave me pause.

I've been playing for like an hour or so and as 6 geese walk into my shop to buy... swords. I realize I'm playing the unity asset store fever dream version of Recettear. 

And I'm here for it. 

You got me dev.

game_name: Teddy's Haven - A Fantasy Inspired Shop Simulator
review_text: I like how peaceful the game is. there is no combat (that I have seen) and no fall damage. the only "timed" part is when you open the shop part for the day and the customers come in to buy stuff. otherwise, before and after the shop is open, you can take as long as you want to go tend animals and gather stuff or just wander around.

game_name: Teddy's Haven - A Fantasy Inspired Shop Simulator
review_text: My neurodivergent brain cannot get enough of this game! I adore the fantasy elements and being rewarded for exploring and experimenting. It is very obviously in early development, but that just makes me even more excited for what's to come. It's a very fun twist on the classic shop simulator games we've all grown used to seeing and so very refreshing!

game_name: Teddy's Haven - A Fantasy Inspired Shop Simulator
review_text: It's charming, cozy, and a little weird! The surprises keep it interesting, and the lack of a clock(except when your shop is open) takes away the pressure. Sometimes I just spend a day picking berries. If you love shop simulators but want more freedom and less pressure, look no further.

game_name: Teddy's Haven - A Fantasy Inspired Shop Simulator
review_text:
Very enjoyable game as far as I can tell from my short gameplay session.

Of course it is early access, as you notice immediately ingame due to things like the rudimentary interface or unfinished areas. And its core gameplay is also basically a copy of all the other "shop simulator" type games.

However, where this game already excels at is atmosphere and chill gameplay. Unlike many other similar games you don't have to micromanage super boring tasks like "put boxes on storage shelfes" or "walk back and forth to order stuff after you had a visual look". This game outright tells you what your storage looks like and it basically has an endless bag as storage that your employees just take stuff out to refill.

This leads to a very chill experience where you actually feel like you are managing a store instead of simulating a minimum wage retail employees work. All put into a lovely fantasy scenario where you actually can explore and do stuff. If you wanted you could just run around all day and gathering stuff to sell in your store.

I really don't know why this isn't more popular. It is clearly better than basically all the other "shop simulators" out there.

My personal highlight was the weird little mask guy that was one evening sitting in the tree before my house. It was a little creepy, lol. Also, some of the shoppers are ... interesting, to say the least. Also, ghost bear cubs. :(

Some things that I hope get improved:
- The game feels like it wants you to explore, but there isn't actually much of relevance to find aside ressources. Or at least it seems like that.
- More random events would be neat. I enjoy some of the stuff that happens and wish there was more. It already feels quite magical, but there is potential.
- Not sure if it is intended that the cats follow you non-stop. :D
- The actual gameplay is maybe a little too chill. It basically comes down to "press button on object" and thats about it. Gathering from a cow is basically no different than gathering from a stone.
- Some more secondary goals would be neat. There are already some, like building that statue, but I feel there is much more potential.

If you don't mind clearly unfinished early access this is definitively the "shopping simulator" type game I highly recommend.

game_name: Teddy's Haven - A Fantasy Inspired Shop Simulator
review_text: Impressively addictive- With how much bulkier the product models are, it's much more satisfying to have full shelves than some other shop sims

game_name: Teddy's Haven - A Fantasy Inspired Shop Simulator
review_text:
This is the only game I can keep coming back to no matter what.
It somehow makes everything feel less heavy.

game_name: Teddy's Haven - A Fantasy Inspired Shop Simulator
review_text: It’s a very enjoyable and relaxing game. I had a great time exploring and relaxing within it. I would appreciate the addition of a music rotation option, as I enjoyed the background music from different areas. Additionally, I had a goat that purchased a few swords along with several stamina potions (funny as hell). Overall, I rate it a 10 out of 10.

game_name: Teddy's Haven - A Fantasy Inspired Shop Simulator
review_text: I have never posted a review but this game was exactly what I was looking for. It's relaxing, beautiful to see, no time frame pressure, no zombies or skeletons chasing me down. This game is the embodiment of cozy. It feels like a safe space for your soul - and I mean this in the best way possible.

game_name: Teddy's Haven - A Fantasy Inspired Shop Simulator
review_text: This is an amazing little game, and so far is my favorite "shop" type one.  There are lots of things to do, the graphics are really nice, the music is peaceful, and the geese buy weapons.  I'm really glad I stumbled across Teddy's Haven, and I can't wait to find out more of the secrets it has...in store.  Keep an eye on your geese folks, they might shank you, lol :p

game_name: Teddy's Haven - A Fantasy Inspired Shop Simulator
review_text: Yes, the game is cozy — you'll read that in every review, and they're right. But it's more than that. I love the stories woven into the land by its ancients, the scenery that makes me stop walking just to look around, and the cats — I can't help myself, I have to pet every single one. But what doesn't get talked about enough is the developer behind it. I've reported bugs and had them fixed the same day. I've posted feedback and gotten a real response — not a form letter, not silence, but an actual conversation. That kind of engagement from a developer is rare, and it shows in everything about this game. He shows up, he listens, and he delivers. If you're on the fence, you're not just buying a charming shop sim for fifteen bucks — you're supporting the kind of independent developer the industry could use a lot more of. Thank you!

game_name: Teddy's Haven - A Fantasy Inspired Shop Simulator
review_text:
I am a sucker for cozy shop simulators so I bought this game with tempered expectations, thinking that it's probably one of many such games that flood the market currently, nothing spectacular, just something to kill time.

Oh how wrong I was.
The best and most important feature of Teddy's Haven is something that even huge established franchises with billion dollar budgets have hard time grasping - it always makes the player want to see what's around the next corner. When I explore I just want to see whats over this hill, and then over this bridge, and then... when I manage my shop I just want to see what the next upgrade is, and then what decorations I can unlock, and then... well, you get the idea. That sense of curiosity is what makes you think "okay, just one more day..."  and suddenly it's 5 hours later and way past your bedtime.
Is it still a little rough around the edges? You bet. Does it use assets that you've probably seen in one or two other games? Absolutely. But it's just so, so NICE. I dont remember playing a game that is so genuinely welcoming in a long time. I cannot even properly explain it, it's just the feeling that the dev loved making this world ans wants you to feel good in it.

game_name: Teddy's Haven - A Fantasy Inspired Shop Simulator
review_text: I picked it up for Teddy and to show some love to the creator, and it ended up being way more enjoyable than I imagined. Now I’m just relaxing and having a great time. Cheers to Teddy. 🥂

game_name: Teddy's Haven - A Fantasy Inspired Shop Simulator
review_text:
This game saved my life.

I am 39.

My ex-wife and I have a daughter together, and adopted our son together. They are now both 4 years old.

When we were going through our separation, I found myself lost and miserable. I was self destructive. I got so mad one day from everything spiraling out of my control that I punched some concrete in a moment of overwhelming emotion. That caused me to break my 5th metacarpal in my right hand... my working hand... my games hand.. the hand that I held and carried my children to bed with.. The hand I desperately needed to make sure I could continue to provide.

After learning of the severity of my self-inflicted damage, I was borderline suicidal. Keep in mind that just a few months before this, I was the happiest man with no history of depression or anxiety. I have never had fits of rage, or been one to break down and cry, but I was in a low spot that just really buried me from being able to see the light on the other side.

Having nothing better to do, I searched for a game I could play, ONE HANDED while I recovered. I somehow stumbled upon this game and read some of the reviews. I decided that it had to be worth a shot... I must admit, I didn't beat the game, or play nearly as long as some of you. In fact, I may have only played this game a day or two.. With that being said, after doing so, I had a new found joy and hope for life. I was able to put behind me the pain and suffering that had been cast over me. I was able to experience other peoples joy and happiness. I was able to see the fruits of my "labor". I relaxed for 5 ♥♥♥♥ing minutes to this music long enough to realize that I was going to be okay.

After coming to that realization, I turned the game off, and I went back to work. It hurt my hand like hell but I was motivated. I stopped feeling so damn sorry for myself, and I became the father I needed to be in that moment, not the weak boy I was behaving as.

Today, I am close friend with my kids mother. We don't fight, or argue, or say hurtful things to each other. We are parents, and friends.

I now have 3 kids. My third child is, wait for it, ALSO 4 YEARS OLD. The woman I am with was going through a very similar situation at the time of my own separation, and we just stumbled in to each others life unexpectedly. We have been in a relationship for a year now, and are very happy together.

Moral of the story, you never know what life holds in store for you, and if I would have given up when all the odds were stacked against me, I wouldn't be where I am today. This silly little game helped me realize that.

Thank you.

The zip line im not a fan of. Id rather have a teleport I could place somewhere..id walk or fly faster then being able to use the zip properly. I would add different background musics. The two gets a bit much after awhile. Customer requests for particular items? Maybe some negative events? I would also like to see more customers like little goblin customers. review_text:

While there is some traditional clunkiness with some things (which is expected in early access), they are minor inconveniences at best. If you love shop simulators (or cozy fantasy games) this is definitely worth a try! 

Also, heads up about the demo, I think it's an older version of the game. I didn't realize that going into the demo and I still loved it. But when I purchased the full version, I truly felt as if the world opened up to me. Cannot recommend this enough!

game_name: Teddy's Haven - A Fantasy Inspired Shop Simulator
review_text:
This game is a breath of fresh air.
- just pure relaxation
- no heavy grind
- no timers
- there is already a large amount of different npc skins added which is great
Legit put a smile on my face within the first 2 hours , and just sat there grinning like an idiot whilst I played it. 
Fantastic to play after a long day of work and you just need a bit of relaxation while you game. 

Things I would love to see added in the future : 
- some more music tracks 
- more ways of interacting with NPCs, perhaps customers who come in one day and can't find something, only to leave you a quest and return in a few days looking for the item they ordered
- more rare/legendary items to find that would go for a large sums ( maybe some sort of bidding system for very rare items )
- change the name of the coins from $ to something magical ( breaks the immersion )
- extra ways to spend the earned money ( maybe add a new type of branch to the store, very exclusive and only for VIPs , where you keep the most expensive items , separate luxurious decors for it )

All in all, if you want to relax while you game, this is it. 10/10

review_text: I opened the game for the first time and played for 6 hours straight. I like how the actual store runs, I love that you can go out collect resources and refine them, I adore my little blue and pink glowing winged friends I've found that now follow me everywhere. The little events and bonuses you get from interacting with the different characters that pop up are great. I'm OBSESSED with "outofboundsium", whoever had that idea is a genius and I love you please marry me omg. As for things I'd like to see (Mind you I'm only on day 8 in my very first year) a more complex economy with fluctuating market price. Maybe it would be due to different events happening in far off lands. Have a "news guy" come by and tell me about magical wars, droughts, competitor stores. A complex-ish character creator for myself, not limited to just humanoid forms (I really want to be a multicolored goose ngl). The ability to craft clothes for teddy... and myself (see character creator previously mentioned). If I walk away from my register and I've hired register staff then I would like for customers to not queue up in my line, and remove themselves from my line if they're already there when I walk away. Beyond that it's a cute little cozy game, the fact that I immediately put in 6 hours my first time opening it speaks for itself.

game_name: Teddy's Haven - A Fantasy Inspired Shop Simulator
review_text:
I think it's fair to say that I pick up, and more or less enjoy, most games that pop up on Steam in this genre. Cozy sims where I can harvest, grow, and craft things? Say no more. But this game said more: what if all of that, AND you can participate in an extremely satisfying and laid back gameplay loop while putting in as much or as little effort as you'd like in order to customize your adorable little fantasy shop?

I was fortunate enough to escape the clutches of retail hell in my very early 20s. Never in a million years did I expect to enjoy counting out change for strangers from behind a cash register... but I find that aspect  of this game the most flow state inducing of all. 

And here is my only gripe, and it's not really a gripe worth griping: the customers who wander in to buy things from you are just a randomized mishmash of UE5 assets, which is kind of distracting because I've seen them in a handful of other games. Although I wish there was maybe a little more to them, it also doesn't matter much. They're just there to fund your shop decoration habit.   

There are no time limits, there is absolutely no pressure. All paths in the game are viable. The few little monologues you get from various (extremely large) mythical creatures are lovely and poetic, telling little stories that feel like they suggest much more. It feels like being a shopkeeper at the crossroads of a thousand worlds, assisting adventurers who happen to walk through the right portal to find you when they really seem to need that dubious concoction you made using honey and bloodberries. 

This game is such a delight. Even if the dev didn't add anything else to it, I'd still recommend it in its current state. But from what they're setting up, there's so much potential for this game to grow into something that keeps me coming back to it whenever I need a couple hours to zone out, rest my brain, and throw berries I harvested and then turned into jam into a customer's magical (ostensibly reusable) shopping bag. The shop I run is fantastical... but this change still won't count itself. 

PS: Please please please at least add fishing and cooking, I *need* to bake pies using jam I made out of berries I harvested.

game_name: Teddy's Haven - A Fantasy Inspired Shop Simulator

Something I really enjoyed was seeing my little shop quickly grow more magical. I placed down crafting stations that glow in really nice hues when loaded with resources, giving that "fantasy" look to my shop -- and letting me know that my efforts are going to turn into profit! Wizard capitalism!

game_name: Teddy's Haven - A Fantasy Inspired Shop Simulator
review_text:
Teddy's Haven was a genuinely pleasant surprise. What looks like a simple shop management game in theory turns out to be thoughtfully designed, deeply polished, and easy to lose hours in - which I would say is a serious rarity in games still in Alpha phase, you can tell this developer has significant experience.

The biggest strength is how the game handles time and choice. You fully control how your day unfolds, with no real pressure to "fail" or optimize every second. That freedom makes the experience relaxing without ever feeling empty. It encourages experimentation rather than punishing mistakes.

The mechanics are smooth and well-integrated, the inventory has real variety, and personalization makes your shop and world feel uniquely yours. Visually, the game is charming and detailed, and it runs impressively well - stable, clean, and clearly well-tested.

Most surprising is how engaging it remains over long sessions. Hours pass easily, which is not something  I expected from a shop management game. Teddy's Haven shows a high level of care in both design and execution, it's absolutely worth your time, and ultimately is a beautiful tribute. A+

game_name: Teddy's Haven - A Fantasy Inspired Shop Simulator
review_text: This game, even as an early release, has more to it that most finished games out there!  You want quests, mining, buy/selling, decorating, collecting, organizing, crafting, tending, boons, etc. this game has it all and its not even done yet!  There a mention for additional crafting, fishing, new portals/quests that are planned along the way.  The developer(s) are really about the fun and quality of the game.  There are little surprises everywhere and everything is so fun and amazing.  This game will keep for busy for days, weeks, years even.  There is constant upgrades and improvements being made which makes you appreciate all the hard work that is being put into the game.  The cozy feel to it relaxes you and lets you just enjoy the environment and have fun.  No enemies, no battles/fighting, you can even jump off a mountain and not get hurt! Good lil game that teaches you the ropes of Pathfinder 2nd edition, especially the infamous 3 action per turn rule that the Church of Pathfinder™ talks so much about on internet forums like Reddit. According to 
 them, "pathfinder 2e fixes everything wrong with D&D". Amen to that, my brethren...
The story is silly and light hearted, but the tactical turn based combat is competent and enjoyable.

7.5/10

I have to stop praise this phenomenal little game. I originally bought it to sate my curiosity about how the Pathfinder 2E combat system plays out in practice before I start to play it for real with friends. I almost bounced off, at first, as I dove too deeply into the various classes and ended up making a pretty ineffective party comp that stopped me from getting through fights on hard.

But I came back with a new party. Figured out how to shore the gaps with buffs, debuffs, consumables, and CC. And it was a phenomenal time - absolute steal for a $15 bundle to get all the DLCs. More than the combat, the general humour, charm, writing, and voice acting is top notch. The Dawnsbury Four are a loveable little group of adventurers. The expansions took the core and only made it better, and combat has gotten more fun as my options have expanded. 

Really hope we see more from this team in the future!

For what it costs, it's a decent bargain. It would be much more appealing if it were possible to use player-generated adventure paths. I liked it and am likely to do a replay, but not right away. I tried it on hard and failed terribly, so maybe there is room for more than one playthrough in my horizon.
So far so great. Lovely game. Works great with the PF2e ruleset. Interesting encounter setups. Cute story. Fun meta humor. Worth it.

I'm loving the game. There appears to not be a HUGE story for the base game but with the ability to pick so many different classes backgrounds etc. there's so much replayability. You can tell a lot of love was put into the game by the people involved and I really enjoy playing it.

At first, the default manga portraits turned me off, but the developer added optional "realistic" portraits and also made it very easy to add more portraits or even to replace any images or icons in the game (you simply drop a PNG image in the correct folder).  I also added a mod (Steam Workshop) that improves (in my opinion) all the NPC portraits too.

Here is my party for example: https://steamcommunity.com/sharedfiles/filedetails/?id=3663552795

- Character creation, in terms of choosing your "stats" (class, heritage, feats, etc.), is the most efficient and user-friendly design I have ever seen, and I have played tons of cRPGs.  You can make changes to your party members freely before adventuring (unlike all other games I have played) and you can even retrain (re-spec) during Long Rests, so it is a really user-friendly way of discovering the Pathfinder 2 system. 

- The voice acting is surprisingly good, which is rare for small indy games.  The narrator, notably, is excellent. 

- Difficulty: there are four difficulty settings and the Medium (normal/standard) one is challenging enough.  Even experienced D&D/Pathfinder players can feel the risks on Medium difficulty.  I won all the battles, but it felt risky a lot of times in the second half of the main campaign.  

- Humor : your party lives in a world where there are four dimensions, and so they constantly go through the fourth wall when they speak.  Your characters openly talk about their stats and refer to rules in their role-play. :P  It is not what I would prefer, but you have to take it with a smile. 

- Time: people claim it took them 4 to 11 hours, but I took 13 hours: I am methodical and kick ass. That said, it was 21 encounters, plus leveling-up, buying gear, narration, so maybe most folks under-report their time.  The claimed 4 hours would be 10 minutes per encounter and spending zero time levelling-up, shopping, and ignoring narration.  

Since the game is only about 5 USD, and each extra campaign DLC is also about 5 USD, it is a pretty fair deal ; and that is without a rebate.  There are three campaigns in all (2 DLCs) and about four mods add more adventures (there are a lot more mods, but the others are classes, feats, etc.).

Dawnsbury Days does an outstanding job of translating the PF2E ruleset to the CRPG format and then leverages that to give you a great tactical combat sim for the ruleset that provides clear and fair difficulty settings based on the encounter budgets.

If you like or are interested in the PF2E system, this is a great low cost entry to learning to use those rules effectively as a party.

Due to the timing of the remastered rules launch, some of the class options are out of date, and some concessions are made to making certain utility options function in what is essentially just a tactical combat game, but this is a very well done translation and a lot of fun.  The workshop has many mods to expand options or update things to the newer remastered setting.

Recommended. 

The gameplay is (modified) Pathfinder Second Edition, very cool.

The characters and the majority of the story narration are voiced, a feature I greatly appreciate.

You can also add your own character portraits for added personalization. 

The game consists of two parts: Management and Encounters. 

In the Management section, you can read (or listen to, ) story information, level up your characters, buy and sell items, and manage your inventory. From here, you’ll select the next encounter. 

Encounters are the combat part of the game, you will generally have an intro introducing a character, and giving you your combat parameters. Encounters always involve combat, but they will some times add a variable to keep it interesting, something to interact with, or protect, etc. There are difficulty options to ensure you find the challenge level that best engages you.


The story is straitforward, we want to have a lot of encounters in interesting environments, so we find a reason to move around a lot. 

The writing often uses 4th wall jokes related to the tabletop game, 

The game seems like it is mostly designed to be a tech demo for trying out the game system, but they have somehow managed to make it a susrpisingly charming experience despite that. 

Overall I have had a lot of fun trying out the pathfinder system here and hope the devs go on to further expand on this concept in the future.

Great game! Fun story and good characters in the campaign, and also a great way to mess around with pre-remaster Pathfinder 2nd Edition as a system!

[h3] Pros [/h3]
[list]
    [*]  It's a faithful representation of PF2E. As an encounter simulation, it does well. For a tactical game, this makes it feel pretty fun.
    [*] Profane Barrier did hit the difficulty curve the best, where encounters usually felt fair.
    [*] Good Little Children started to give actual skill usage, along with separated encounters in the same level. This gives more freedom in choices, and made buffs and other spells feel a lot more viable when you don't need to waste turns mid combat.
    [*] The free encounter mode provides a nice place to experiment and challenge yourself with additional scenarios.
    [*] The UI is easy to understand. This makes it pretty easy to play and understand what's going on.
[/list]

[h3] Cons [/h3]
[list]
    [*]  While the base game has a basic story, which lent itself to light hearted dialogue, the DLC's could have story potential, but the dialogue and (lack of) character interaction makes the story a bit unengaging. The current style of dialogue, with the fourth wall breaking, often seems to have a feeling of levity, which makes the serious parts of the DLC storyline feel less significant. In addition, a lot of the effects on people don't feel developed. For the main redemption arc in Profane Barrier, while it hit all the major points, we didn't get much of an opportunity to talk to her or flesh out her character, which makes it feel a bit more like reading Spark Notes on the character.
    [*] There isn't much out of combat at the moment. I think the most I saw was 2 conversations, and multiple chests to loot. This makes most skills that don't apply to combat rather useless. This also means that there isn't downtime, which makes it hard to play more than a few encounters at a time due to fatigue.
    [*] Some encounters are too difficult. It's fine in free encounter, but the idea of an "honor mode playthrough" would be pretty much pointless due to a few bad dice rolls or lack of specific preparation would wipe the run. The latter would be less of an issue if it was foreshadowed, but currently it requires a failed attempt to know what's next. On the final boss for the second DLC, as an example, required me to prepare a specific spell and make a few attempts to get a 30% fail to remove reactions. While my party wasn't completely optimal, it was still fairly balanced. Needing to depend on a lucky hit on a specific spell that I didn't know I needed to prepare doesn't feel nice.
[/list]

[h3] Hopeful Future Changes [/h3]
[list]
    [*] AOEs show failure chance while hovering.
    [*] The dialogue tone better matches the adventure. It might be nice if Dawnsbury was a real place, to allow for dialogue opportunities and flesh out the characters, so it feels more engaging. Along with this, some lore, along with hints on future enemies being spread out in the encounters would make it feel like an actual world.
    [*] I would prefer if there was a short rest option while out of combat, and everything between each long rest was connected in one level. This would make it start to feel more like a proper CRPG.
    [*] It's on pre-remaster due to OGL. A bit unavoidable since Paizo hasn't rereleased everything under ORC yet. Once Paizo republishes everything to ORC, it would be nice if the game got rereleased under ORC with the remaster changes.
[/list]

This is an excellent adaption of the Pathfinder 2nd Edition rules to a computer roleplaying game.  The layout is near identical to most virtual tabletops.  The ruleset is largely still pre-remaster, with alignment-based damage still being a thing, though there are options to allow for specific remaster updates, such as not linking cleric bonus spells to charisma modifier.  There's also a toggle option for free archetype if you want to explore certain builds.  (Do bear in mind that this game takes place in a homebrew setting with its own pantheon of deities; there will be no greatsword-totting clerics of Gorum casting enlarge in this game unless you mod for it.  Skill feat selection is also much more limited than in tabletop, and you don't get skill feat slots to take them in, so you'll need to spend general feat slots for them if desired.)  The base game's story is enjoyable, so it fully works as a single-player game.
Very solid pf2e implementation with good enough story flow.

Fun little implementation of the PF2E rules. It is narrow in scope but executes well. The story is twee and meta (characters refer to the rules as if they are the laws of the universe) which I don't know I found pretty goofy but fun. Overall I found it very enjoyable.
It isn't a huge game, but its not expensive either. The writing isn't complicated but it's intentional and fun.  Great way to learn the PF2 system, which is implemented very cleanly.

Even thought this is a game based of the premastered rule books for Pathfinder 2nd edition it really scratches that itch for the game.  Its a bit short and the character are always breaking the fourth wall but it hit most.  I have been really enjoying the game and the mods have brought a lot more classes and ancestry options.  I'm very happy with this purchase.

This is basically a Pathfinder 2nd Edition combat engine that comes with implementations of many but not all of the games classes, archtypes, feats, and spells and a short campaign (levels 1-4). The DLC add a level 5-8 campaign and a somewhat more fleshed out level 9 adventure. If you aren't familiar with the system, this is an excellent way to learn it. If you are, it's a good way of experimenting with character builds and tactics.

As for the campaign, the style of presentation isn't especially appealing to me but it's not obnoxious. The characters act as though they're being controlled by players outside the game who're enjoying a fluffy adventure with friends where they're playing their roles to the hilt however these players don't actually exist within the games fiction. Similarly, what is happening in the fiction feels a lot like what would be come up with by a gamemaster who wants to have heavier themes and thus generates some fairly dark content but instinctively approaches this darker content like the fluffier adventure that the non-existent players are expecting.

With that said, as this is an engine, any kind of content can be created for it. There's already a roguelike mode, several user made campaigns, and implementations of many of the classes and other character building options not included by default.
Hands-down the best PF2 game out there. Lots of cool mod support as well to keep the game fresh with new classes.
This game deserves a lot more credit than it gets. It absolutely nails the spirit of Pf2e, with but a few minor changes necessary to adapt the ruleset to a different medium.

This game is exactly what it feels like to play in a very tactical group of PF2e players.
Whenever I want to play the system, but my friends aren't avaiable, a round of Dawnsbury Days is just what fills the void :P

If you love Pathfinder 2nd Edition and can't find enough excuses to try out new character builds and party compositions, this is THE game for you. The story is simple and endearing, and feels very authentically like a group of friends earnestly roleplaying as a new adventuring party. The game uses the PF2E legacy ruleset (for legal reasons) but has an extensive modding scene that adds a ton of the classes and mechanics that have come out since the PF2E remaster. It also gets regular updates and occasional story expansions, and I'm always genuinely excited when I see a Dawnsbury Days update in my Steam notifications. Lovely game all around, I wish the devs well.
Honestly for just being a little indie RPG game, it's pretty good and fun. I love the PF2e system and I've wanted a video game in this system for a long time.

Story is not the main draw here, the mechanics of Pathfinder 2nd Edition are, and I look forward to possibly seeing more projects like this one.

Dawnsbury Days was a really pleasant surprise. It captures the feel of Pathfinder 2nd Edition far better than I expected, from the action economy to how encounters encourage smart positioning and tactical choices. If you’re familiar with PF2e, it feels immediately recognizable rather than a loose adaptation, which is honestly its biggest strength. The game respects the system instead of simplifying it into something generic.

On top of that, it’s excellent value for money. There’s a surprising amount of content, replayability, and polish for the price, especially considering how niche and rules-faithful it is. It’s clear a lot of care went into making something that Pathfinder fans would actually enjoy. If you like turn-based RPGs and PF2e in particular, Dawnsbury Days is absolutely worth picking up.

A simple in aesthetic but very charming little crpg that uses the Pathfinder 2e system faithfully. The story is very basic, but the encounter design is very good and the voice acting adds enough to the characters to make them grow on you. 

For anyone who likes PF2e and wants to get more of it, this is a very worthwhile investment. Also very good to introduce someone else to PF2e.

Really hope to see more from the creators!

The implementation of the Pathfiner 2 ruleset works great as a video game, and the original tabletop rules have been followed pretty faithfully. There is a ton of variety  in available character options to try and pretty flexible allowances for changing at a few points during the game which makes exploring the character creation quite fun. I like that you can individually replay encounters with custom parties just to try new things.

Brilliant idea for a game, and very fun to play. 

I'm inexperienced as a shader programmer though. While I can largely brute-force my way through puzzles, it's unsatisfying knowing that I'm not solving them "the right way." The in-game hints feel a little too abstract for me to learn a lesson from them. For example, on the one with the cute dog, it gives a hint about using max() but I honestly can't figure out how to use it in that puzzle for good results and just fall back to something like "if abs(x) < 2 and ...". This ends up being how i'm solving most of the puzzles and predictably, I'm no where close to the optimal code size or core cycles. 

I've started watching the developer's youtube videos where he solves some of the puzzles, and have picked up a few tricks here and there, but I still don't feel like the game is teaching me to be good at it. 

So all this is to say: If the Replicube somehow gamified the learning process of writing shader code more efficiently, it would elevate the overall design to a whole new plateau. For now, I'll continue learning what I can from the dev's youtube channel. 

Overall though, still a very excellent and ingenious game, well worth the price, and despite it's shortcomings in doing so, is an invaluable resource for improving your technical skills.

Replicube is an unusual and compelling hybrid of programming puzzle game, voxel art tool, and creative sandbox, developed and published by Walaber Entertainment LLC. Rather than relying on traditional mechanics, Replicube invites players to think like engineers and digital sculptors. Its premise is deceptively simple: you’re given a 3D voxel model and must write a script that procedurally reproduces it. If your script generates a shape that matches the reference, you win. Everything revolves around that loop — write, run, observe, refine — a cycle that mirrors real programming in miniature. The result is a uniquely introspective puzzle experience that rewards experimentation over brute memorization, and creativity over singular “correct” solutions.

The heart of Replicube lies in its freedom. The game never tells you how to solve a puzzle or which coding philosophy to adopt. Because each voxel challenge can be solved in dozens of ways, your code becomes an expression of your thinking. You might approach a shape mathematically, using symmetry, loops, and conditionals to carve out a pattern; or you might take a more brute-force route, mapping each voxel explicitly. The game judges only the final result, not the elegance of the technique, which gives the experience a liberating sense of openness. Instant visual feedback after every script execution makes learning feel natural — every tweak is visible, every mistake becomes a discovery, and every improvement is immediately satisfying. Even beginners with limited coding experience can find joy in seeing their logic come to life as a tangible, three-dimensional object.

Outside the core puzzle mode, Replicube expands into a creative toolset that enhances its appeal. A full sandbox allows you to build custom voxel objects, generate artistic shapes, or simply play with procedural logic without a goal in mind. A 2D editor lets you produce images and animated GIFs, turning the game into a lightweight platform for digital art experiments. This dual identity — puzzle challenge and creative utility — gives the title real longevity. Some players will focus on solving structured puzzles, optimizing their scripts, or climbing the leaderboards for code efficiency; others will spend hours crafting voxel sculptures or exploring the boundaries of procedural art. The experience becomes whatever you choose to make of it.

However, Replicube’s openness also shapes its limitations. Because puzzles can often be solved with improvised or inelegant logic, some challenges risk becoming exercises in trial-and-error rather than clever reasoning. More experienced coders may find that certain problems can be bypassed using shortcuts inherent to the scripting language (Lua), making a few puzzles feel too flexible for their own good. Conversely, newcomers may occasionally feel overwhelmed by the abstract nature of coding without a heavily guided tutorial system. The game provides the tools, but it does not teach coding fundamentals extensively — those unfamiliar with loops, conditions, and coordinate systems may need patience or external guidance to fully enjoy the experience.

Another aspect worth considering is structure. Replicube doesn't follow a traditional progression curve or narrative arc; there is no story, no character development, and no overarching campaign. The experience is modular and self-directed, which will delight players who enjoy creative freedom and intellectual play, but may leave those seeking clear goals or escalating difficulty wanting more. Even with the puzzle sets and community challenges, the focus remains on personal expression rather than completionist milestones. In this sense, the game behaves as much like a coding playground as it does a puzzle collection.

Despite these quirks, Replicube succeeds because it understands exactly what it wants to be: a procedural generation toybox built for curiosity, creativity, and problem-solving. Its low system requirements make it widely accessible, and its clean, minimalist interface ensures the focus stays on logic, geometry, and iteration. It is a rare game that encourages you to think spatially and structurally, to test theories through code, and to embrace the joy of discovery that comes with results emerging onscreen from your own reasoning.

Replicube will appeal most to players who enjoy coding puzzles, math-based creativity, voxel art, or open-ended experimental tools. It’s not a game you “beat” in the traditional sense; rather, it’s something you learn from, play with, and grow into. For those with a curious mind — whether seasoned programmers or complete novices — it offers a refreshing and intellectually rewarding experience that stands apart from typical puzzle design.

How to train your dragon: Umamusume edition

That's by far the best way for me to describe this rollercoaster of a game. But what is Drapline? A game about forging a bone with your dragon child? Saving the world? 

No.

It's that Coo is such a hopeless glutton that a missclick made her eat an entire neighbouring village. And that's the essence of the game.

While I won't go too much into the story to prevent spoilers, and also given that we only have a part of it, but despite the sometimes awkward translation and typos here and there, the English version is more than usable, especially for a Japanese early access game. As for the gameplay, anyone familiar with the loop of Uma will definitely feel right at home. A random set of NPCs will be available at every game turn, where you can then pick and choose what path you want your gluttonous child to go down.

But as with all Early access games, the edges may require some polishing, such as random events being quite limited in terms of number, making the randomness of them disappear quite quickly. Having played on the Steam Deck, I fully expected many bugs, but it ran great out of the box with minimal issues, except for a few graphical glitches over my three runs.

So do I recommend the game? Yes, by quite a lot in fact, the product in it's current stats easily can give your 20-40h of gameplay and given the developers hard work, whether it'd be with the adorable pixel art style or quite intricate battle system due to the sheer amount of skills possible, I can only expect this game to improve as time goes on.

Also, you can eat people, ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ Coo...

[h2]It's Not What You Might Think[/h2]
I feel the need to put this out there. If you look at this game, see a young girl in dragon form, and immediately jump to inappropriate comparisons or Dragon Maid style sexualization, curb your fetish or temper your bias.

Drapline is not that.

This is a story about an interdimensional universe of worlds slowly being overrun by the sheer power and devastation of Divine Dragon girls. Some go berserk. Some isolate themselves. Some try to fight their insatiable urge to feed on powerful enemies, ultimately destroying themselves and their worlds in the process.

What does this mean for you? You are their guardian.

In the fractured remains of these ruined worlds, you are there to guide her toward a new path. Will you treat her well? Teach her how to live among humans, or let her stay wild and unruly, the way she was born to be?

It is really, really well done. For a solo project, this says more than enough. The overwhelmingly positive reviews are earned. There is depth here. Tension. Regret. Fear. Emotion. Connection. You want to help this girl. You want to save both the world and her from herself.


[h2]Gameplay[/h2]
My main issue is the time investment required to unlock most of the game’s content. If you want everything, you are looking at 20 plus hours to access all the lore, upgrades, and extreme difficulty modes.

That grind is what holds the experience back for me.

Runs can take two to three hours, and when you only walk away with one or two upgrades, it starts to feel repetitive. This is true even with the RNG-driven variety. One run might push you toward a mage build, the next toward melee. You could stack defensive relics or go full glass cannon. You deal with minor calamities while maintaining the dragon’s affinity as Wild, Neutral, or Rule.

You can deepen relationships with everyone in town, the problem here is that you're on a time limit, one event per week, and you only get a full year to make your choices, so replaying the game is sort of mandatory. Every choice feeds back into the dragon herself.

It is an excellent concept. I just wish progression respected the player’s time more.

[h2]Conclusion[/h2]
If you enjoy story-driven games with turn-based combat, strong replayability, and the promise of future content through early access, you should already be playing this.

I loved every moment of it. The pixel art is fantastic. The writing is strong with very few grammatical issues. The music is catchy and memorable.

This is a must-play.

How to train your dragon: Umamusume edition

That's by far the best way for me to describe this rollercoaster of a game. But what is Drapline? A game about forging a bone with your dragon child? Saving the world? 

No.

It's that Coo is such a hopeless glutton that a missclick made her eat an entire neighbouring village. And that's the essence of the game.

While I won't go too much into the story to prevent spoilers, and also given that we only have a part of it, but despite the sometimes awkward translation and typos here and there, the English version is more than usable, especially for a Japanese early access game. As for the gameplay, anyone familiar with the loop of Uma will definitely feel right at home. A random set of NPCs will be available at every game turn, where you can then pick and choose what path you want your gluttonous child to go down.

But as with all Early access games, the edges may require some polishing, such as random events being quite limited in terms of number, making the randomness of them disappear quite quickly. Having played on the Steam Deck, I fully expected many bugs, but it ran great out of the box with minimal issues, except for a few graphical glitches over my three runs.

So do I recommend the game? Yes, by quite a lot in fact, the product in it's current stats easily can give your 20-40h of gameplay and given the developers hard work, whether it'd be with the adorable pixel art style or quite intricate battle system due to the sheer amount of skills possible, I can only expect this game to improve as time goes on.

Also, you can eat people, ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ Coo...

game_name: HIDDEN CATS: The last of cats
review_text:
Not much to be said. This is just a simple and extremely short hidden object point and click game. 

There are three or four nice songs that loop non-stop and 101 cats to find. That's literally it.

Game also has a tool that provides help with the searching, if you are having trouble (hints). I never used it, though (was almost tempted for the last one).

I like the feature that turns both the cats and the map from white to colored. All the doodles are also pretty well drawn.

game_name: HIDDEN CATS: The last of cats
review_text:
Hidden Cats: The Last of Cats is a cozy and relaxing hidden-object game with charming artwork and detailed scenes. Searching for the hidden cats is satisfying, and watching the environments gradually fill with color adds a really nice sense of progress.

One of the best features is how each room becomes fully colored once you find all the cats in that specific area. It makes completing each section feel rewarding and keeps the experience engaging. Overall, it’s a simple and enjoyable game, perfect for unwinding at your own pace.
