Game also has a tool that provides help with the searching, if you are having trouble (hints). I never used it, though (was almost tempted for the last one).

I like the feature that turns both the cats and the map from white to colored. All the doodles are also pretty well drawn.

game_name: HIDDEN CATS: The last of cats
review_text:
Hidden Cats: The Last of Cats is a cozy and relaxing hidden-object game with charming artwork and detailed scenes. Searching for the hidden cats is satisfying, and watching the environments gradually fill with color adds a really nice sense of progress.

One of the best features is how each room becomes fully colored once you find all the cats in that specific area. It makes completing each section feel rewarding and keeps the experience engaging. Overall, it’s a simple and enjoyable game, perfect for unwinding at your own pace.

game_name: HIDDEN CATS: The last of cats
review_text:
Un platino de 24 min.

Tranquilo y relajado como debe ser, mas con el soundtrack dandole mas tranquilidad
As someone with anxiety and difficulty interacting with others, this is a great way to get the experience of an MMO without the overwhelming dread of having to find strangers to group up with.
i almost dropped this after about 2 hours, but once i hit the main island and the game opened up to me I got sucked in and fell in love
Its crazy to think that a game thats essentially a fake MMORPG is going to end up being more successful in the end, then an ACTUAL MMORPG (Ashes of Creation)  ERENSHOR IS FOR THE REAL MMORPG LOVERS!!

Oh My Gods! It's like playing an MMO back in the days, just without all the suck!
Recommended for anyone suffering from mild cases of nostalgia and no time to waste on guild and server drama.

Super cool idea that ends up paying off very well. Feels very much like a classic mmo and masters the illusion of being "online." Open "chat" is hilarious. Had a random run in and help take down a boss. Watched a Druid harass a GM in shouts until getting put in jail. 10/10

I played a lot of the old MMOs, starting with EQ about 2 months after launch and then eventually FFXI and WoW.  EQ always had my heart, TBH.  It still does through various emulators and private servers, but it's tuned a bit too grindy and while the majority of the players are helpful and fun, there are quite a few people on those servers who are just annoying or too eager to show off how much time they've sank into a nearly 30yr old game by being being elitist or exclusionary.

There's nothing wrong with finding the thing you like and enjoying it, which is why I keep coming back to EQ, but Erenshor scratches the MMO itch in a different way.   It gives me the sense of fun I miss from the old games, but has enough modern quality of life changes for it to stand out.  I wish the classes were a little better fleshed out, however.  There aren't any "pure" classes like in the old MMOs.  No Cleric, no Wizard, no Warrior.  There's a Druid who's the closest to the pure healer, a Paladin who is a healer/tank but mostly a tank, an Arcanist who is a smattering of wizard and enchanter, a Windblade(?) that's kinda a mix of rogue and ... something else(?), and a Ranger-like class.  The Reaver is a new class added recently and I get the feeling it's modeled on the Deathknight from WoW, but I haven't played it yet.  The game would really benefit from adding dedicated classes that let the hybrid classes stand out more.

Still, for being in early access this is VERY good.  I think it shows serious promise.  I think expanding the classes would be a good idea.  Also the beginning zone is kinda meh.  There's a quest that needs to be done to get through it, but it's a little muddled and I had a little bit of difficulty figuring out what to do next.  The wiki helped, but the in-game hints weren't very helpful.  Maybe the other non-tutorial starting hub is better, but once you're in newb island you can't just leave :(

For the price, this is a gem.  I am eager to see the progress and TBH I would help support it in other ways if I can.

If you want to scratch the MMO itch and want to play the game at your pace without having to worry about finding other players to complete content, then this is the game for you!

The game can be buggy, the game does use store bought assets and the game still is quite unknown so the community is small which means not many player guides or build guides. 

However, the gameplay is good enough to hold my attention. Which, is the most important thing, really. Also, the wiki and the tutorials managed to guide me through the island.
So, I wasn't completely lost.

I do wish the game used original assets as there's only so much you can do with store bought assets as you have to make do with what you've got. The lore has to mold to the assets instead of the other way around. The design of the zones can only ever be generic.
But, this game is being made only by 1 person. Maybe he can hire an artist/modeler if this game pops off.

Looking forward to seeing the game expand and improve.

The most important thing to know about this game is that the developer listens to the community. Join the journey with the rest of us.

Do not forget the beer, pretzels, and your battle snuggie.

Erenshor captures a specific charm that early MMOs had. It's hard to describe. "A grass spider" in flashy bright text above a cartoonish giant spider that moves and attacks slightly comically too fast is just not something you'll see in modern MMOs. The game goes out of it's way to drive home the MMO feel, and you'll have fleeting moments where you forget everyone else are bots. Yea it looks jank, feels jank, sounds kind of jank, but that is, again, part of the charm. The core systems are solid, the stats are interesting yet straight forward, and there is enough gameplay now to warrant the price tag. If the game gets fully fleshed out and continues to get developed beyond that, I would be more than happy to purchase $10-$15 expansions in the same vein as EverQuest/WoW.
This game is a love letter to OG MMOS.  It feels like early Everquest.  I am really looking forward to playing this game a lot more.
This game sucks me right in after a bit of playing, its a SINGLEPLAYER game that is simulating the old school MmoRpg's and its good at doing so.

What an incredible game, at first I was put off by aesthetics but the gameplay and the world building just threw it all out of the window. This game is incredible and so addicting, it really grows on you. So fun, so immersive and so carefully crafted. Guy who made this truly adores mmos and you can really tell. Just, well done. Highly Recommended!

As someone who has played Everquest for over 20 + years, this game very much has the same feel. and its SINGLE PLAYER OFFLINE, but feels more alive then a lot of the Dieing MMO servers in most of the mainstream MMO's. also you can tailor your experience with the admin panel. not hard enough? make the mobs hit harder or have more hp. do you want exp and loot pinatas ? crank up exp gain and loot drop chance. This game has so much and is only getting better and better. its in early access 0.3 . the dev talks to us daily listening to our ideas, adopting many to add in. the games a labor of love, and well worth full price.

so i started with the demo i thought eh ill play for a hour see what its like yes the graphics are basic but dam did it drawn me in i brought the game and having a blast the sim players can be jerks just like a real mmo but there kinda cool too and very needy XD but the feeling you get from adventuring is great well worth your time if your into this kind of thing. my advice try the demo finish the starter island then decide

It brings back the old MMORPG feeling. Definitely worth your time and money spent 👍
Hope for the next good update, cheers for the dev!

Here is my 2 cents!

Ive multiboxed EQ2 with 11 accounts and multiboxed Wow with 9 accounts. It has a feel like you are multiboxing while running bot scripts for the other "players". I loved Multiboxing, Nothing better than running an X2 or greater Raid all on your own. The loot is all yours. So that being said. Its the same feeling.. All the loot is yours. Dont like a player in your group. Give them some of the loot so they too get better with you!

The game is rough around the edges. Listen... there is no game out there that is perfect. Most AAA games in my opinion Suck ass. This is not AAA but i can almost get the feel that i am playing with real people if you can immerse yourself into the game play.

For me, I have so missed any GOOD MMORPG without being Eq2 or Wow. and it is neither but has the feel of being a live service game. Complain all ya want or not. The game is great. Ill go as far as to say, i think about it when i wake up, at work, and cant wait to get home to play it. NO game in the past 7 or 8 years has done that for me.

Awesome game. Exactly the experience it bills itself as. It is a very good simulation of an Everquest-style OG experience.  You can control your group a bit like a classic-EQ pet.  You can click 'Guard' and they'll Hold that area, then you can Manage roles, set up a Main Tank, Assist, etc...and a Puller. Then you can set up in a dungeon with a group and simulate the exact grouping experience. You can set when the puller pulls (what percentage of mana should group be at), you can set what percentage the healer should heal each group member.
 Pretty cool. Been having a lot of fun.
Updating after finishing the full tutorial island (did every quest).  Absolutely in love with the game.  There are some direct EQ references (D'Vinn, lookin at you).  Keep in mind you can add friends and you can gear them too, you can trade them and inspect them and set their gear with stuff they own/given them.


The Sims (NPC players) have personalities. You will find yourself drawn to some more than others. Some are clearly toxic, some are clearly naive pure-hearted souls. You can group with either. I find the banter in all chat fairly accurate, in terms of balance of toxic people and kind people.

It is still in early access but here's some things I hope to see in the future:
-Ability to make macros. 
-Ability to set keybinds for the Group actions. 
-A little bit more of a customizable UI (every MMO enjoyer gotta have their custom UIs)...you can move all the windows around, but I wanna resize my chats, I wanna have more hotbars at once, not just two alternate hotbars (press a button to switch between them).  
-And with that, ability to keybind alt+1 alt+2 etc etc.
-[strike]Have the Sims (friendly 'player' NPCs) respond to words. Like if one whispers you asking to group, and you respond with 'not right now group is full', they would say okay or whatever.  Just to sell the simulation a little more.  Because I had one send me 'wanna group?' I said "cant right now group is full" ~ and the sim just saw the keyword 'group' and was like OK OMW, even though I didnt want them too. [/strike] (and with this, i want a /r added in for replying to last whisper).  **Update: after more gametime, they DO respond to certain things. The example i described I dont think the current system of finding keywords can handle...but I did ask where to level at lvl 15 and someone in guild chat gave a suggestion.  but they DO respond.
-Very minor stuff like linking in chat, or NPCs link in chat (items or quests maybe, or just items) and can click and see the item theyre talking about.  If someone whispers you, you can click their name (or you can click any name) and itll bring up a /w name immediately.
-Make it so NPC names reflect their level if aggressive.  Not just default yellow names for everything and have the little target circle under reflect the color...wish the name did too.
-After you group with someone for a bit, theyll start sending you whispers asking to group in various ways.  The problem is, I grouped with some people on tutorial island but now at level 20, I have the same guys (who are still level 5) begging me to group.  They don't understand chat replies that might shut it down.  Would be nice if they only asked if within a certain range of your level.
-Also would be cool if people had a 'Likeability score' or something.  This would be hard to implement maybe, but it's an idea, that the simplayers would have a certain scale of how much they liked you, based both on time grouping AND messages sent.  So if you cussed out someone, they would obviously not like you much (not that you would, it's just an easy example to demonstrate the concept).
-For MODDERS, hope for a EQ reskin lol. To reskin everything, and make it EQ style polygon graphics.  I know the dev cant do this because that's a lawsuit, but modders sure could.

A lot of these suggestions are little gripes, but would be very nice.  Overall enjoying the game a lot, I was literally starting to feel an MMORPG itch.  The Hero's Journey got shut down due to a lawsuit, and WoW released TBC again recently (but I refuse to give that company any $$$), and was feeling that itch again.  And this does scratch that itch in a surprisingly wonderful way.

HIGHLY suggest purchasing if you enjoyed Everquest original.  Especially worth it on sale! I could see people not liking it if they have expectations of a more modern type of gameplay.  I genuinely am not sure if people who never played oldschool MMOs would like it, but I imagine people looking for a relaxed game, that happens to hit the progression dopamine of the old Skinner Box style MMORPGs, would enjoy this. For the same reason original EQ appealed to certain gamers, I can see this catching people the same way.   Anyway, well worth it for a guy like me!

Absolutely fantastic game. To keep things short before I go into it a bit more, if you're even remotely interested in the concept of a solo MMORPG style of game, taking quests, exploring, going through dungeons, and grinding, this is the game for you. It really provides an amazing sense of progression early on, and hooked me in quickly after the initial tutorial.

For reference, I never played Everquest, I played Vanilla WoW and Runescape, so if you think "well, I never played Everquest or Ultima, maybe this isn't for me" just try the demo, and it could still click for you. The NPCs are useful/helpful, it's also nice having to read the text to find out what to do for a quest and where to go rather than clicking through it and relying on markers to guide you. That said, you CAN turn markers on, and set the entire experience to your own liking. Want more XP from mobs? You can increase that. You can make it as grindy, casual, or somewhere in the middle as you'd like, and you can do this on the fly without starting a new game.

I didn't even notice I had spent 5hrs in game already all at once. But so far, it ticks all the boxes for someone who loves MMOs, but is looking for something a little more chill and something I can play at my own pace, no worries of a subscription or anything.

Give this one a shot.

EDIT/UPDATE: I want to add some things now that I have over 40hrs of game time. First of all, this is shaping up to be one of my recent favourites of the past 5-7 years. This is early access, it shows it at times with a fair bit of incomplete quest lines, but there is still SO much content here, quality content at that, content that makes you feel like you're grinding and progressing in an MMO. The amount of bosses, and cool hidden areas, and things to do really is amazing and I've been logging in nonstop to grind new gear, find new things, etc. 

As for performance, I haven't had a single issue. I run on what's probably a below average or at best, average system, and it's been smooth sailing on very high/ultra. This being 0.3 in early access is super impressive with how much is already there. 

P.S. Please dev, if you see this, I hope you keep ai/LLMs out of this. The game is fantastic right now without it. Amazing work overall.

Cannot recommend this fun, cozy MMO enough. Especially with the guild update. Being talked down to by an npc in the top guild on the server made me feel like a teenager in 2004 playing WoW on Mal'Ganis again :)
i didnt realize it was single player for like 4 hours. pretty great mmorpg simulator.

This is a great game. It reminds me in some ways of games like Everquest, but single player with a party that manages themselves in battle. It's well worth picking up. This is one of those rare gems imo.

If your a fan of Everquest or more classic MMO's from the early 2000's and you want to play the game completely solo with AI friends or "Simulated Friends", then Erenshor is your cup of tea to enjoy. 

This game is basically singleplayer Everquest with simulated AI players that you can group up with for all the different roles you normally would expect in an MMO environment and those AI players can join your guild as well as create their own guilds and complete with you. They also can buy your wares from the auction house as well as grow strong alongside with you. (The AI is pretty unique for what it offers but dont expect anything deep from a communication standpoint, its deep from a systems standpoint, meaning the AI won't reply to just anything you say unless your using certain commands)

Keep in mind, Everquest by Itself on private servers or even older MMO"s on private servers take tons of time and energy to develop your character as well as require lots of communication and effort.  while Erenshor allows you to jump right in and have fun and set your difficulty to whatever you want it to be.

That being said, I give an Erenshor an 8/10 for what it offers. It has an interesting take using AI or Simulated players to group with alongside it being a classic single player MMO. I just wish the AI was more deep from a communication standpoint in terms of being able to have deep conversations with the sim players as one aspect of classic MMO's was communicating with your party and getting to know them even during down times with hard fights.

For us aging MMO fans, fans of EQ and Runescape, all the love none of the player hate, quest, join/create your own guild, quest with SIM players, loot, gear up, learn new spells, and raids are in the works, so if any of this appeals to you, then why not try it out. Also, for those who love to glam out a base/house, something for you too.

This review is for the 0.3 version of the game, the Rising Shadows update.
Erenshor is a faux-MMORPG. It's a single player game that uses mechanics and systems from classic MMOs alongside NPC simulated players, giving the feel of a populated world. It does not use generative AI, the NPC simplayers are programmed and their 'conversations' are prewritten.

In no particular order, the main takeaways for me are these:

-It's fun, it plays exactly like an old-school MMO (think Everquest), but with some quality of life options you can turn on. You can tweak a number of server settings, like difficulty and progression speed. The game has controller support, though I play primarily with mouse and keyboard.

-As of the latest update there are six classes, and each feels unique and interesting, with different roles and specialities. You can customize your appearance a little, mostly colors and hairstyle. No transmog options right now without mods.

-Quests are handled in a classic (not modern) way, where you need to read and explore. No quest markers. But a vast majority of XP is earned through defeating enemies.

-It's in early access, but there's currently a lot of content to play through, with more being added consistently.

-Simplayers are able to group with you and be given directions, as well as joining guilds. Simplayers in your group will occasionally request loot, though the player has the final say and can even control what they equip. Other simplayers will be out in the world 'playing' the game or hanging out in settlements.

-The in-game chat is designed to both parody MMO chats and give the feeling of being in a shared world with other players. It does this reasonably well, though given the nature of prewritten dialogue, it improves as more variety is added to it to prevent too much repetition. One thing I would love to see in a future update is the ability to separate party chat from the regular chat box, though right now you can limit the chatter with a few options.

-The art style is fine for this kind of game and even feels like the kind of old-school graphics classic MMOs had, though it does lack a distinctive visual identity. The main critiques for me are that certain textures, like some building walls can feel a bit out of place, and that there isn't enough variety in the appearances of worn items like armor.

-The UI is basic and adequate, but could be improved by adding more flair. There are a small handful of instances in the game world where the font that's used feels out of place (like one of the shop signs in the major city).

-You technically do not need the wiki to play this game, but it will improve your ease of finding items, solving some quests etc. There's been improvements recently where simplayers in the chat box will talk about these things, and over time it's possible that you may be able to pick up the same information you'd get from the wiki from the in-game 'community.' As of the latest update, you can ask simplayers about the locations of specific items and enemies by name. The game contains rewarding secrets and hidden items that feel like those from classic MMOs.

-The discord community has a pretty active modding scene, including a lot of common modern MMO add-ons or equivalents.

-I haven't delved deep into the lore, but from what I've seen and read, there's interesting history about the gods, the land and the effects still being felt by various groups around the land of Erenshor.

-I have not reached endgame with any of my characters yet, but there are plans to add raids soon so I've got to keep leveling. Based on the cadence and consistency of updates since early access launch, I have faith that the developer will continue to improve and work on the game. 

This game is very impressive for a single developer as is clearly a labor of love. If you like the gameplay of MMORPGs but being single player isn't a deal-breaker, it's worth a look. Check out the demo.
Very fun game with a lot of potential. I just wish the dev would add mounts. Running everywhere gets tedious.

THIS GAME IS INCREDIBLE! Its a single player mmo and an absolute masterpiece! It has an awesome story filled with hidden quests and stuff to explore. A world filled with hilarious NPCS. A love letter to EQ that will steal hundreds of hours of your free time away.
The most unrealistic thing about this game is how incredibly fast it is to find a group / party.

Really interesting game and idea. I'm recommending it because it succeeds at its goals: solid early mmo experience, fun combat, interesting quests, npc grouping is solid and party play feels good. The problem I ran into is that there is a constant feeling in the back of my head that I would be having more fun if I was playing with people, even if it was just having a connected chat where every player was in their own instance with NPC groups still, or even if you could host a small world that your friends all play in. If it sounds at all interesting to you, give it a shot and see if it clicks. I'll probably revisit it further down the line.
Found it after the downfall of AoC. Very interesting and unique. AI players are entertaining as they banter. Highly recommended.

This is honestly a new genre of game. Being an introvert it is hard to get into MMOs once you get to the point where you have to have a party. This is the first game I have seen that solves that problem, but also plays in a manner similar to the original EverQuest mixed with some old Elder Scrolls. If this gets updated regularly with expansions similar to those found in other MMOs. I can see this game being a major hit if it doesn’t fall into early development hell.

I was able to get this game to run on Android via the mobile port of Proton that the community is working on. This is just a bonus :D
This is a neat game with interesting mechanics. Definitely a nice time sink when feeling that old school mmorpg itch.

Best MMO out and it isn't even multiplayer. It scratches that old itch that we grinders all have. Completely customize your game experience with server settings that allow for anything from casual gameplay to a zone out and grind, or if you want hard hitting and punishing combat you can do that too with sliders and options that affect how the game is played. Simplayers you encounter can act like your own personal mercenaries that you can call on and gear up or when left alone will return with their own upgrades or if you'd like you can stick to pugs. You may come across a well geared simplayer who can help carry you through tough situations or ones wearing noob trash. Get trained in a dungeon, or gain experience by saving a simplayer in a tough spot. Sit at camp and let the puller pull or roam in a dungeon or field. The game is good and it isnt even that far along. Do quests that require a few more than 2 braincells or look at the wiki and play with quest icons on. Simulated market where you can browse to sell high and buy low. The dev is passionate and active in the friendly and helpful community of players. The game is worth at least a try. It will get you hooked.

Erenshor is amazing and the Developer is active in the community with suggestions and feedback to improve the game.  Old school MMO gamers need to try Erenshor.  I think the game is a steal even at full price.

Great game, tons of fun, I'm always hyped for the updates.
The GM's really need to step it up tho, there's a whole guild called the Friends Club and every single one of those guys are botting. I swear my reports are going straight into the trash.

Very enjoyable game for those with an itch for old school MMOs. The Dev is probably the most active I've ever seen, with small updates/bug fixes coming out seemingly every day or every other day, and bigger updates planned for the future (Patiently waiting for raids). The sim players are something I haven't experienced before, and they definitely add life and immersion to the world. You can play as casually as you'd like, take breaks while your group farms for you, and see how the Sim players banter with each other. It also has a very customizable difficulty, allowing you to change EXP gain values, Health/Damage of NPCs, and drop rates, a massive QoL feature for those either having trouble, or those who may not have as much time as others to play. At 145 hours I have completed every zone, almost every available quest, nearly full BiS gear for myself and everyone in my group, and am hunting for my last achievement. If you enjoy old school MMO's (Everquest/Classic Wow), and/or are on the fence about this game, please give it a try.

A singleplayer "MMO" where the other players (called SimPlayers) are all controlled by the game. Default settings are modelled after Everquest era MMO's and at times can be nostalgically inconvenient (no fast travel without mods at the moment), though there are a few settings you can tweak to your liking.

Brian, what the hell? You made the game too good and an old EQ addict lost several weeks of his life. Seriously though, this is an amazing love letter to the original Everquest. Any fan of the franchise will have an absolute blast. You can make it easier or harder with the admin panel but I suggest either Recommended or Extended for the EQ experience!

Fantastic game, made by one developer. Has a great community in his discord! I've always dreamed of a Single player MMORPG, I can't believe it became a reality. It was made by someone who wanted the same game I wanted, the game just didn't exist yet! I expect this game will go far, it's already looking great.

Haven't had much time to get around to playing this but God I love every bit of it. 

it's obviously still in the early stages and really shows that through things like the exploration, lack of story and the very limited response of the simplayers but what's here is amazing and it really does feel like playing a typical MMO.

reading up on the wiki is cool too, I found out the game will essentially keep playing for a few hours after you've quit out and essentially simulate the world moving and the simplayers levelling as you're gone, sorta like that game that takes 400 days to beat.

really cool stuff and I'm beyond excited to see where it goes.

it's also surprsingly well optomised considering everything it's running. hopefully we see more optomisations in the future because it has a tendancy to crash for me randomly whenever I'm in a dungeon
Great game, although its the only one that crashes under my overclocking/undervolt settings

I have already gotten my money's worth out of Erenshor and have only just scratched the surface! It gives major Everquest nostalgia vibes. The game doesn't hold your hand but gives options to tweak the difficulty, and there are fun little secrets to uncover for yourself if you don't look it up and spoil them. The simulated players are a fun addition for making the world feel alive. There's also an easily installed mod for multiplayer. The combat so far has not been overly complicated and it's calming to just have something straightforward when I want to relax my brain. The game is very relaxing. No microtransanctions, no FOMO. Playable on the Steam Deck is a major bonus. I am usually hesitant about recommending early access games but this one is worth it. The dev is friendly and active in the discord and the game receives frequent updates. There's also a demo to see if you like it. I would definitely recommend this game!
I blinked and 7 hours went by....  That hasn't happened in a really long time, almost two decades.  This game is special.

I tried the Demo for about an hour bought the game, and I can honestly say I am grateful a game like this exists. It not only fills that MMO itch of Eq esque type game play, I dont have a monthly fee, and I dont have to deal with trolls.  Keep it up, Please Finnish this game and Add content, There is tons already, wander around Die a lot. Do it again.
So far its good, tons of potential. I can imagine the game only getting better if there is a larger team.

This game is an amazing gem and really harkens back to the times of early EQ camps. 

While there may be a few bugs and your groups pathing may be a bit wonky (specifically when Shift+6 near a wall to make the group stay in place while I'm pulling), it really gives you that sense of discovery and danger that might be around every corner, especially in dungeons.

With the exception of the Underspine Hollows, every dungeon is tuned well, and offer different areas depending on your level, which extends the life of it, and there's a great sense of achievement when you are able to kill a new boss.

Keep in mind, some of the stronger items may take some farming (damn you Astra!), but once it finally drops, it's so nice. 

My main gripe is that I've ran out of content. I've done nearly everything. I've killed almost every boss. I've ran every dungeon, and I still want more!

Really looking forward to the new Reaver class, v0.3, and all future updates!

An excellent throw back to older MMOs. A ton of cool secrets to explore, NPCs to talk to, quests to do, and loot to be found, but all done at your own pace. Even has sliders to make the game as easy or as hard as want. Discord has a very helpful community for if you get stuck and need some advice, or just want to talk about the game.
The amount of content in this game is amazing! It's basically a single player Everquest, anyone who enjoyed EverQuest THJ should defintiely try this out.

This game is easily worth the price to check it out.  Ill be honest, it wasn't love at first sight.  I reckon it probably took a good 6-8 hours in 2 or 3 sessions.  But, then you start figuring out where everything is and how the zones connect.  You start getting carrots and setting goals.  Before you know it you are at work planning what you are going to do that night.  Where am i going?  Which quests should i do?  Which bots am I using tonight?  Where does that ring drop that I saw my friend Cyndara wearing?  

There is enough here already to keep you busy for a while, even without rolling alts, and there is more content and quality of life stuff on the way.  Everquest fans there are quite a few easter eggs in here for you.  Everyone else, there is plenty for you too.  Try it out.

If you are thinking about buying this and havent. This is your sign.

It's great, its going to itch that mmorpg itch with grouping, a puller and being able to give loot to your group members. It's still early but theres a lot of fun to be had not to mention all the coming updates. 

Get it!
Thank me later.

Started with the demo, got hooked, kept playing and forgot I was still in a demo. Bough the game, progress was saved still. I was hesitant about the "players" being bots with no choice for actual multiplayer but this game really delivers. It's a straightforward MMORPG, not simple enough to feel boring but not terribly complex. Love the art style, music is great.

There are quests, monsters to grind, and a world to explore. I am excited to see what comes in the future of this game as it is clearly in the earlier stages of development.

The game is certainly on the right track. It's impressive what Brian has accomplished.

I'm excited to see what else is to come.

For example, I was stuck looking for the next step on a quest, and a 'player; in my guild asked about it and the other sim players helped him, helping me in the process! Amazing! An interesting game, 
My first 30 minutes were make or break. I didn't enjoy/ understand much. Now this is completely on me, folks getting into this game have to read the dialog. If you skim through you will miss key prompts telling you what needs completed. (Like I did when first starting) Now beyond that, the game is rather fun. I took me about 150 hours on a x1 scale to reach level 35, and do the majority of quests at a relaxed pace. Quests, Sim players, and other stuff made the game feel fun and alive despite its single player aspects. 

I would like to see a better AI engine, like inworld with meta data. That way the ai are even more productive and seem more realistic. Yes, the Ai in the current state will follow, and help you complete combat tasks. However it would be nice to have trade between the ai.  For example I find myself constantly buying gear from the auction house or drops and giving it to the team members. If I could trade new stuff over for older obsolete gear I'd enjoy that better. 

Now that I'm in the end game, I find myself grinding ascension levels, money, and upgrading gear/ equipment/ completing all quests. 

I would highly recommend folks to pick up the demo and give the game a try.

This game just takes me back to teaming up with my boy Slyder in Asherons Call (the first one) and tackling dungeons, waiting for spawns and looting something over and over again to get that one specific drop... good times baybe!
The Best MMO Experience for Single Player you can ever get without annoying other friends to play with!

Ironically enough, the best comparison I can come up with for what it feels like to play [i]Erenshor[/i] right now is soloing in an oldschool MMO: running around mostly doing your own thing following quest breadcrumbs while guild and local chats scroll by. Even if you're partied up with "SimPlayers," there's not a lot of interaction outside of giving commands either via chat or the GUI interface, though they do have flavor text in response. The programming on them behavior-wise is in a pretty good state: as an Arcanist, I haven't yet run into any level-appropriate content that I can't clear with a party of a Paladin tanking, a Druid healing, and a Stormcaller and Windblade providing DPS and support, particularly after helping gear up my favorite "friends."

The wiki is extensive, which is good, because it's sometimes mandatory for getting a grasp on the game's basics, which aren't always thoroughly explained in the game itself (sometimes by design; sometimes by oversight; sometimes because it's presumably just not implemented yet). It's also helpful for looking up details about things you may have missed or that you're getting frustrated trying to figure out on your own, though asking in chat can sometimes give clues that will help guide you, if you'd prefer to stay in-game as much as possible. The fact that you have a choice is, I think, really important for keeping the game as broadly appealing as possible, where people can choose their own levels of challenge.

Right now, the biggest changes I would like to see are:

1. In-game notes and map markers. As far as I know, there's no way to take your own notes inside the game on anything NPCs mention that doesn't trigger a quest journal entry, there's no way to add your own notes to existing journal entries, and there's no way to place a flag on a map to remind yourself of where an NPC or other point of interest is located.

2. More NPC dialogue, but [i]without[/i] using LLMs in any capacity. I do realize that doing this all by hand takes time, so I'm not at all bothered by the current level of dialogue that exists, which I think would be impressive even if this weren't still an Early Access game. I just hope that more will gradually be added, and that the developer will stick by his current strategy of not using AI. Directly integrating LLMs in any way would almost certainly result in me dropping the game and never coming back. (If people want to have "fun" with stolen work that gives outputs that are unpredictable in every way except being guaranteed to routinely give incorrect information, they can mod that in.)

Speaking of mods, there's already a pretty robust modding scene for the game, and the dev is enthusiastic about supporting it. He's also very clearly listening to player feedback during the EA period, as evidenced by the addition of QoL features, particularly the "Server Admin" panel that lets you customize elements of game experience such as XP gain, drop rate, and respawn times (the sort of things someone would be able to customize on a private server for an MMO).

Overall, even with just $12 hours of playtime, I'm quite happy I bought it on sale, and I think it's worth the current full EA price of $20.

Good simulation, flight is close to reality. The drone models fly very close to how they fly in real life. You can definitely practice and get real world skills here. You can test new rate settings before making changes to your own drone. It is the best simulation for tiny whoops currently. It suffers from what real world pilots also say about even the best flight simulators: It feels a bit like flying through oil. No big deal, as I said, this game is good enough to practice real world skills.

Differences to reality: 

Real world drones 
1) fall out of the air faster when loosing throttle
2) suffer more from turbulence (especially with brushed motors)
3) get out of control faster in angle mode
4) are not perfectly aligned and will thus drift (all drones in Liftoff hover politely on the y-axis)

The real world has
1) wind outside
2) air movement such as drag

I think the lack of turbulence and lack of drifting are the biggest differences to reality in regards to learning to fly drones. If you are already familiar with flying drones, compensating for turbulence and drifting is something you automatically do when flying, so it is not a big draw back for experienced drone pilots. As for beginners, well, this is still *the* drone simulator and it will get you from 0 to flying in a great, risk free environment.

I think Liftoff is best used when you already have a drone. It will greatly improve your appreciation for and use for Liftoff. I wouldn't buy Liftoff to see if drone flying is interesting. Buy a drone for that. Liftoff is fun because you are practicing with your drone and have a real world point of reference. Without that point of reference, I think Liftoff could feel a bit empty or abstract.

Liftoff has a good selection of popular drones. New products such as Lava II and Mobula6 2024 would be nice, as well as old favorites such as Tinyhawk II and Meteor65.
Some levels and graphics work really well even on my non-gaming laptop with Intel 2.5 GHz i5-6300U and integrated graphics.
Love this game, awesome purchase and good for beginner on fpv who want to practice tiny whoop before actually buying one, works awesome with radiomaster boxer controller.

Just getting into flying drones and every youtuber recommended this sim. Took about 8 hours of frustration learning, but suddenly the controls clicked for me and it all just started making sense. I could fly wherever I wanted without issue.

I'm hoping this translates to flying in the real world.

If you're a newbie to FPV (like me), would recommend using the balloons section in acro mode (any map). Acro doesn't hold your hands but the balloons gives you a specific task so you can focus on (rather than just blat it around). I've noticed a significant improvement in my fine control doing this.

Graphics are pleasant, music is nice. Would be nice to have some buttons preconfigured to navigate the menus without switching to mouse/keyboard (e.g. reset/exit to menu etc).

There is a bit of a weird optimisation issue with the cinema map (might be the whole game, but start to notice stuttering after flying for 10m+) Specs: 7700x/32gb/9070xt
Great for practice! Good configuration and tweaks for your controller and drone. But... The maps to get a bit dull after a while, wish for more larger maps, and more realistic maps...

MASSIVELY dislike how little maps there are. The diversity of the maps are also not that great in an attempt to keep the locations more realistic. Just give us the liftoff maps too?!?! Custom map builder helps this a bit.

I don't know how good the physics are, but as soon as I got my first drone (tinywhoop air75) I could fly it because I had practiced well in the simulator.
However, this simulator doesn't have wind.  Unless you go to the sealand (night) map.  
I spent almost all my time on the Melon-pan Park map, the playground.  I learned a lot in this simulator and I think that for tinywhoops, there's a lot here for them.
It also has a lot of easter eggs and features that most might not find.  For example, in the high school prom map (or the rock wall map, I believe, maybe one of the factories), bumping the fan and flying into it will give you an achievement.  Going to the house (day) map and going to the top room and pushing the radio throttle stick will give you the "Play liftoff in liftoff" achievement.
The reason I am saying these is because there is not a lot of people who have said how to get these achievements.
Another, if you go and tap 4 vending machines' keypads, you get an achievement.
Less about achievements and more about community: Community maps and quads work fine.  I recommend you try some.  The level editor works fine.

One negative thing I will say is performance.  My $450 laptop doesn't really handle it at 1080p well (25-30fps normally there).
However, eventually I figured out that by setting the game to the same quality as my analog goggles would be, I got 60fps smoothly and it didn't even look that bad.

Anyway, great simulator, I think.  Lots of heart went into the small details and I think the physics are pretty good.

Liftoff: Micro Drones is a slick and focused FPV sim that brings the micro-quad experience indoors, your living room becomes a legit freestyle playground. The flight physics feel very realistic for whoops and tiny drones, and customization is deep: you can tweak frames, motors, and more.

Using my RadioMaster Pocket with this feels just as good as on the regular Liftoff, the controls map cleanly, and I get very tight, precise responses. But unlike Liftoff: FPV Drone Racing, Micro Drones has a more intimate scale: tighter spaces, more indoor-style tracks, and a refined UI that’s lighter and faster.

If you liked the realism of the original Liftoff but want something that emphasizes micro-drone agility, lower system requirements, and creative indoor flying, this is a very worthwhile pick.

[b]I have completed the game at 100% within 10 hours of gameplay (excluding multiplayer).[/b]

Driftwood has been a great experience in the casual-descender-like game genre. It encompasses a perfect balance of relaxing gaming and exciting action. The levels of the game have great variety as well as achievable goals. Game did not feel easy neither hard, the perfect balance.

While the game has been made (presumably?) by a solo dev, bugs are minimal (at least for my experience) and it is very rare that some things may break. Minor bugs have been mostly related to ragdoll physics and some outfit visuals, but those have been mostly patched on recent updates.

My favourite thing about this game has been the tracklist. It is such a great addition to the game, where it makes you feel immersed into the skateboarding experience, just like real life. The game's sound tracks are very replayable and I cannot imagine myself playing the game without the integrated playlist playing in the background. Not sure how the dev managed to integrate the songs in the game, but they fit very well.

I have not played the multiplayer format yet, but from what I've seen in videos and clips, it looks very fun and casual, packed with plenty of action. However, the single player version with all the maps is super replayable.

In summary, I do recommend the game, even at full price.

Everything about this game just clicks. From the smooth gameplay to the cute graphics and chill music, it's a total vibe. Highly recommended! 

Dev, please... can I have more?

So, I really do like the idea of it and the "vibes", chill tunes, cute artstyle - overall really an enjoyable ride. I can't wait to give it a try on multiplayer. So generally - try it, especially at this price. 

I do find the difficulty spikes after each bridge maybe a couple degrees too high and the way you unlock the next levels CAN be a bit tedious. I don't really know how you would be able to fix that - I understand that classic skating games had goals to unlock levels too, it's been a thing. Maybe give a "wheel" upon first completion of a course or something along those lines. 

TL:DR: 7.5/10 Chill game, which you CAN elevate to a difficult game if you want to - excited to see where it goes from here, hope to find players for multiplayer one of these days.

I don’t usually write reviews, but this game deserves one. I’ve been looking for a good downhill/cruising skate game for a long time and this is easily the best one out there. The physics feel great, the music and vibes are on point, and the board variety + wheel mechanics are just really well done. As a skater, this game feels right. If you’re into downhill skating, this is 100% for you. It’s a beautiful indie game and even after 100% completing it, I still come back to it every now and then.

I don't ususally leave reviews, but this game was great. The controls are simple but super tight and feel great; the music is good and matches the overall vibe; and I never felt like enjoying the game took a backseat to beating the game. The progression of the courses and goals was challenging the whole way through, and I absolutely felt my skill growing as I learned new ways to manage increasing speeds and tighter corners. I've only ridden one of the roads included in the game, but it was immediately recognizable. I'm not sure if it was 1 to 1, but the turns matched my memory closely enough that I leaned wide out one corner to see if the gate was open - if you know you know.

My only complaint is that there isn't more. Please, may I have some more?

Sloths on longboards! Funny game idea with brilliant game design and mechanics. Loved the character designs! Map designs were fantastic, and came across no glitches or bugs. Great game for a Sunday off!

Nice chill game, once you get used to the controls it's pretty easy to get into a groove. Plenty of challenge if you want to try get all goals at once or just pick one each run to be able to make consistent progess.

Driftwood is genuinely a blast! The game keeps you motivated and makes you want to keep improving. The graphics are beautiful and create a relaxing, feel-good atmosphere while drifting through scenic, well-designed tracks. The routes are varied, fun to explore, and a joy to drive.

There’s so much room to experiment, try new lines, and approach challenges differently — it keeps the gameplay feeling fresh every time. The missions are tough but fair, delivering a great sense of achievement without becoming frustrating. The replay value is especially strong, making it a game you can return to again and again without it losing its charm.

gives me THPS 1 vibes. fun and relaxing game with a fantastic chill soundtrack

I love how relaxed this Driftwood is. The visuals, the music and the way the game just flows makes for a great experience. The game can be challenging but it's rarely frustrating and going down some of the longer courses can feel sublime.

The controls take a bit getting used to because it does not work like most "racing" or sports games. Mastering the left and right slides is pretty key to not flying off the road every 30 seconds and you don't necessarily have a way to hard stop. Once you get past that though, the game just opens up.

There's a good selection of courses here and the challenges all seem fair and doable. I especially like how most of the courses have multiple routes you can take and they're fairly long detours so there is lots to explore.

[b]A few things I wish were better:[/b]

I do wish it was a little more forgiving when you go off the road and land on a big rock or other hard surface that you could in theory keep rolling on. It's a little quick to force you back to a checkpoint, especially when you can just push yourself back onto the road.

Dropping you at the last checkpoint can be a fraught thing. Sometimes you're placed directly in front of a jump without the speed to clear the jump so you immediately crash again.

I also wish there was split-screen co-op. I'd love to play this with the family on the couch. It's just begging for it.

So overall, an excellent game. If there's a Driftwoods 2: Downhill Boogalo, I'm definitely buying it.

[b]Driftwood[/b] is a game developed by a team of 2 (designer and developer) where you control a sloth that drives a van through the map searching for tracks to stop by, grab the board and get some drifting while enjoying both beautiful backgrounds and really chill music. Every track has some challenges to unlock new gear:
there's a nice variety of [u]boards[/u], [u]wheels[/u] and [u]cosmetics[/u] like helms/hats, shirts, pants, shoes, gloves, etc.
Earn coins by completing challenges, and unlock new areas by reaching a certain amount of them.

Whether you're looking for
[list]
[*]a chill game to relax after work/school without any rush (alone or in [b]multiplayer[/b] with your friends or players around the world)
[*]or you're a stubborn player who loves a challenge (unlocking all gear & cosmetics, getting in them [u]leaderboards[/u] by speedrunning/highscores)
[/list]
this game has you covered.

Run from the Cops game! — It's an explosive mix of adrenaline, humor and crazy fun! The controls are simple, but this only makes police racing more exciting. Each level features new traps, unexpected turns, and crazy chases through the city, parks, and even rooftops! The graphics are stylized, but very expressive, and the physics of movement adds a chaotic charm. The game is great for quick sessions when you want to take your mind off things and get a little "crazy." Light, dynamic and very addictive — thanks to the developers for the great action!

Run from the Cops! is one of those games that feels a bit like my real life… except here I actually manage to escape my problems.
You jump in, grab the cash, hop in your car, and start drifting so hard the neighbors downstairs can probably hear the cops screaming after you. Maps change, cars unlock, but my driving skills? Yeah, those stay in 2008.
Half of my escapes end with me kissing the nearest streetlight at full speed — but damn, it’s fun. Short runs, simple concept, and somehow it sucks you in until “one more run” becomes an hour of your life. Different cars, different tracks, same angry police.
If you want a game where you can legally cause chaos, steal money, and outrun the cops faster than an ex leaving your life — this is it.
Cool top-down racing game where you have to evade the police. The graphics are cool, the controls are awesome, I was hooked for hours.

Steal cash, upgrade speed/handling, outrun pursuits: loop hooks you. Smooth controls, vibrant visuals. Finished unlocks fast, now grinding scores – simple joy done right!
Last night solo rush: pulled off heist, jumped in car and ditched cops on streets! Corner drifts—adrenaline pumping! Pure thrill!

The game offers a good balance of challenge and fun, making it suitable for casual players and those seeking a lively, action-packed experience. Fast, chaotic chases with cops always on your tail. Tight turns, risky shortcuts, and that rush when you barely slip away.

VRacer Hoverbike is, IMHO, the finest VR-native sci-fi racer on the market. Immersive controls, amazing speed and varied thrilling environments make this game a great time every time you fire it up.
I have owned VRHB since the Early Access, pre-multiplayer days when I played on my Rift CV2 and have watched its development closely. These devs have worked diligently on steadily improving VRHB over the years, implementing about every feature standard to a top-tier racer (MP, leaderboards, playback/photo modes, livery editor, etc.). The full version is a testament to that hard work, and a real gift to VR racing lovers!
I'm glad to see VRHB is getting more love since the excellent Meta Quest version was released. I have it there as well, but the sharper PC visuals always bring me back to my PC version if I'm home.
This is the most fun racing ive had in VR so far, fast paced lots of tracks and options just great riding fun

I'm not really one for anti-grav racers (or their physics based learning curves), but giving this one a try as I was intrigued by the lean steering mechanic because it's like a call back to arcade moto racers. Because of my lack of experience with the genre I'll stay away from aesthetic/feel talking points.

Rather, some settings to make the game feel better: personally I keep the steering sensitivity/lean-camera sensitivity all the way up because I feel it helps keep up with demanding tracks while feeling more natural. Like although it might help some folks and is default having my head gyroscopically always pointing straight rather than leaning with the bike made me feel more sick in than if the bike leaned as I turn (Might also just be the console "tilting the control to steer harder" placebo lol)

General Racing game player tings: Only gripe so far is the race lines are finicky and "overly optimistic" so it's better to leave them off and follow your gut on trail-braking. Also the bike shows you what gear you're in but there's no manual mode as far as I can tell, which would be a nice addition to making me feel like I have more agency in acceleration/turns. 

All in all pretty decent, there's an extensive cosmetic livery customization, little career, quickplay, and online modes, having fun so far! I always try to check out all the new VR racing games and see what people are up too, since it's a much smaller community these days, this ones pretty solid/realized.

FAIRY TAIL: Beach Volleyball Havoc is a deliberately offbeat spin-off that takes the familiar faces of the FAIRY TAIL universe and places them into an intentionally absurd setting built around magical beach volleyball. Rather than attempting to extend the series’ main storyline or deliver a traditional action RPG experience, the game fully commits to being a lighthearted party title, prioritizing chaotic fun and fan service over depth or realism. From the moment a match begins, it’s clear that the game’s goal is not to simulate volleyball accurately, but to create fast, silly encounters where magic, explosions, and exaggerated character abilities take center stage.

The core gameplay is built around two-on-two volleyball matches played on compact beach courts, with controls that are extremely easy to grasp. Movement, jumps, passes, and spikes are all simplified, making the game accessible even to players with little experience in sports titles. What distinguishes the gameplay from standard volleyball is the constant presence of magical elements. As rallies continue, magical crystals drop onto the court, and collecting them fills a meter that allows characters to unleash special abilities. These attacks can drastically alter the flow of a match, summoning additional balls, distorting trajectories, or overwhelming opponents with visual chaos, turning what could have been a straightforward rally into an unpredictable spectacle.

Character variety is one of the game’s biggest draws, especially for fans of the franchise. With a large roster of mages pulled from across the FAIRY TAIL world, each character brings unique magic that subtly changes how they play. While the basic mechanics remain the same regardless of who you choose, these special abilities give matches personality and help prevent the roster from feeling purely cosmetic. The game’s pixel-art presentation complements this well, offering expressive animations and colorful effects that emphasize humor and exaggeration rather than precision. The beach setting, bright colors, and playful sound design all reinforce the sense that this is a game meant to be enjoyed casually rather than competitively.

That said, the simplicity that makes Beach Volleyball Havoc approachable also limits its long-term appeal. Beyond the initial novelty of magical volleyball and character abilities, the mechanics do not evolve much over time. Player control over ball placement and strategy is fairly loose, which can make matches feel chaotic to the point of randomness. Success often depends less on careful positioning or tactical play and more on timing special attacks or benefiting from unpredictable interactions. For some players, this randomness adds to the fun, while others may find it undermines any sense of mastery or skill development.

The single-player experience offers only modest longevity. While it provides a way to unlock characters and artwork, there is little in the way of structured progression, narrative framing, or escalating challenge. As a result, solo play can begin to feel repetitive once you’ve seen most of what the game has to offer. Multiplayer is where the game shines most, particularly in local couch play. With friends, the chaotic physics and flashy magic can lead to laugh-out-loud moments, sudden turnarounds, and entertaining matches that feel more like a party game than a competitive sports title. Remote Play Together support helps extend this appeal, even if the lack of traditional online matchmaking limits broader multiplayer engagement.

Overall, FAIRY TAIL: Beach Volleyball Havoc succeeds by embracing its own silliness and staying true to its identity as a casual, fan-focused spin-off. It is not a deep or technically refined sports game, nor does it aim to offer a long, content-rich experience. Instead, it delivers short bursts of chaotic fun powered by recognizable characters, flashy magic, and an intentionally unbalanced sense of humor. For FAIRY TAIL fans looking for a playful side experience or groups of friends in search of a lighthearted party game, it can be an enjoyable diversion. For players seeking depth, precision, or long-term competitive appeal, it’s best approached as a novelty rather than a fully fleshed-out volleyball title.

From the moment a match begins, it’s clear that the game’s goal is not to simulate volleyball accurately, but to create fast, silly encounters where magic, explosions, and exaggerated character abilities take center stage.

The core gameplay is built around two-on-two volleyball matches played on compact beach courts, with controls that are extremely easy to grasp. Movement, jumps, passes, and spikes are all simplified, making the game accessible even to players with little experience in sports titles. What distinguishes the gameplay from standard volleyball is the constant presence of magical elements. As rallies continue, magical crystals drop onto the court, and collecting them fills a meter that allows characters to unleash special abilities. These attacks can drastically alter the flow of a match, summoning additional balls, distorting trajectories, or overwhelming opponents with visual chaos, turning what could have been a straightforward rally into an unpredictable spectacle.

Character variety is one of the game’s biggest draws, especially for fans of the franchise. With a large roster of mages pulled from across the FAIRY TAIL world, each character brings unique magic that subtly changes how they play. While the basic mechanics remain the same regardless of who you choose, these special abilities give matches personality and help prevent the roster from feeling purely cosmetic. The game’s pixel-art presentation complements this well, offering expressive animations and colorful effects that emphasize humor and exaggeration rather than precision. The beach setting, bright colors, and playful sound design all reinforce the sense that this is a game meant to be enjoyed casually rather than competitively.

That said, the simplicity that makes Beach Volleyball Havoc approachable also limits its long-term appeal. Beyond the initial novelty of magical volleyball and character abilities, the mechanics do not evolve much over time. Player control over ball placement and strategy is fairly loose, which can make matches feel chaotic to the point of randomness. Success often depends less on careful positioning or tactical play and more on timing special attacks or benefiting from unpredictable interactions. For some players, this randomness adds to the fun, while others may find it undermines any sense of mastery or skill development.

The single-player experience offers only modest longevity. While it provides a way to unlock characters and artwork, there is little in the way of structured progression, narrative framing, or escalating challenge. As a result, solo play can begin to feel repetitive once you’ve seen most of what the game has to offer. Multiplayer is where the game shines most, particularly in local couch play. With friends, the chaotic physics and flashy magic can lead to laugh-out-loud moments, sudden turnarounds, and entertaining matches that feel more like a party game than a competitive sports title. Remote Play Together support helps extend this appeal, even if the lack of traditional online matchmaking limits broader multiplayer engagement.

Overall, FAIRY TAIL: Beach Volleyball Havoc succeeds by embracing its own silliness and staying true to its identity as a casual, fan-focused spin-off. It is not a deep or technically refined sports game, nor does it aim to offer a long, content-rich experience. Instead, it delivers short bursts of chaotic fun powered by recognizable characters, flashy magic, and an intentionally unbalanced sense of humor. For FAIRY TAIL fans looking for a playful side experience or groups of friends in search of a lighthearted party game, it can be an enjoyable diversion. For players seeking depth, precision, or long-term competitive appeal, it’s best approached as a novelty rather than a fully fleshed-out volleyball title.

While the basic mechanics remain the same regardless of who you choose, these special abilities give matches personality and help prevent the roster from feeling purely cosmetic. The game’s pixel-art presentation complements this well, offering expressive animations and colorful effects that emphasize humor and exaggeration rather than precision. The beach setting, bright colors, and playful sound design all reinforce the sense that this is a game meant to be enjoyed casually rather than competitively.

That said, the simplicity that makes Beach Volleyball Havoc approachable also limits its long-term appeal. Beyond the initial novelty of magical volleyball and character abilities, the mechanics do not evolve much over time. Player control over ball placement and strategy is fairly loose, which can make matches feel chaotic to the point of randomness. Success often depends less on careful positioning or tactical play and more on timing special attacks or benefiting from unpredictable interactions. For some players, this randomness adds to the fun, while others may find it undermines any sense of mastery or skill development.

The single-player experience offers only modest longevity. While it provides a way to unlock characters and artwork, there is little in the way of structured progression, narrative framing, or escalating challenge. As a result, solo play can begin to feel repetitive once you’ve seen most of what the game has to offer. Multiplayer is where the game shines most, particularly in local couch play. With friends, the chaotic physics and flashy magic can lead to laugh-out-loud moments, sudden turnarounds, and entertaining matches that feel more like a party game than a competitive sports title. Remote Play Together support helps extend this appeal, even if the lack of traditional online matchmaking limits broader multiplayer engagement.

Overall, FAIRY TAIL: Beach Volleyball Havoc succeeds by embracing its own silliness and staying true to its identity as a casual, fan-focused spin-off. It is not a deep or technically refined sports game, nor does it aim to offer a long, content-rich experience. Instead, it delivers short bursts of chaotic fun powered by recognizable characters, flashy magic, and an intentionally unbalanced sense of humor. For FAIRY TAIL fans looking for a playful side experience or groups of friends in search of a lighthearted party game, it can be an enjoyable diversion. For players seeking depth, precision, or long-term competitive appeal, it’s best approached as a novelty rather than a fully fleshed-out volleyball title.

Not every game is for everyone, nor should they be. The game is a true labour of love for the developers who have a proven record of supporting the scene for the prequel to this gem, so you can expect a nice long tail of content and features yet to come.

Lonely Mountains: Snow Riders is a downhill skiing game where you carve your way down snowy mountains, either casually or with a focus on clean runs and tighter challenges. It builds on the original Lonely Mountains formula, adding snow physics, new perspectives, and multiplayer options that let you tackle the slopes solo or with friends.

Pros
✔️ Comforting or challenging, depending on how you play — Casual mode is relaxing, while regular mode still asks you to use more risky paths and improve.
✔️ Seeing your previous runs is a great touch — The snow trail showing past attempts is simple but really effective for learning and self-improvement.
✔️ The new POV option is a big win — It made the game noticeably more enjoyable for me and helped the runs feel clearer and more engaging.

Cons
❌ I’m not into the character style — Totally personal preference, but it just didn’t click for me visually.
❌ The music didn’t stick with me — Nothing bad, just not memorable.
❌ Landing physics can be frustrating — Some falls or wipeouts didn’t feel consistent, which took away from the flow at times.

Conclusion
I liked this, and I had fun with it. Once you get past the learning curve, it’s easy to play and unwind. That said, I kept feeling like it was just missing something — not anything obvious or broken, just that extra spark that would’ve pushed it from good to great for me and make me keep coming back long term. Still, it’s an enjoyable time on the slopes, and I can absolutely see myself coming back to it in short bursts.

Score
7/10 Fresh Tracks — A solid downhill ride that just doesn’t quite reach the summit.

Physics and controls are top notch and the visuals are just a joy to behold. Snow tessellation tech is awesome. Mountain paths are well designed but also feel organic. Usually you crash because you are going too fast or aren't lined up to give yourself time and space to react. It's a diorama style presentation and clearly designed for a fixed camera. A first or third person camera wouldn't work at all. There are other games designed for that - try Steep if that's what you're after.

Really cool examples of how they balance the difficulty - Restarts from crashes are almost instantaneous. Crashes reset if you wipe out before the first checkpoint, saving you a manual restart. Checkpoint restarts are from a dead stop, but optional side paths account for this so you can hit level goals even though you lost momentum.

Ammo Bounce is a fast-paced, neon-soaked arcade game that reimagines the classic brick breaker genre. I played Ammo Bounce thinking it was going to be a simple breakout game.
Yeah, sure.
Five minutes later, I was already talking to the flying saw like it was my pet:
“Please, come back to the platform… NOT THAT WAY!!!”
The enemies are something else too. Some move like their engines were lubricated with caffeine, others put up shields faster than I can remember where the pause button is.
One of them built a barricade right in front of my saw. I felt like I was working construction, just without the paycheck and with more emotional damage.
Special thanks to the developer for the power-ups.
Picked up a “mysterious item” — lost half my platform.
Picked up another — lost a life.
I’m starting to think this game is secretly plotting against me.
But despite all the chaos, I got hooked.
Every level feels like a geometry exam, except instead of rulers I have a hyperactive circular saw living its best life and ignoring the laws of physics.
In the end:
10/10 — I yelled, laughed, suffered, and want more.

This is a fun game.  I haven't played the previous years games.  
I am confused why all the bowlers seem to be exactly the same except for their animations.  
Why are there no stats for the bowlers?  What's the point in licensing all of them when they all play exactly the same with the only difference being that they have different balls and whether they are left handed or right handed?  What really confuses me is a career mode in most sports games consists of improving your bowlers stats over time, with this game you start out just as good as the pros and the only point in the career mode is to collect more balls and cosmetic items that do nothing.  
Also why do we not get to select different weights for the balls?  
There is also some really weird animations that the bowlers do that make them look like they are imitating a pregnant Frankenstein's monster.  
I wish I could recommend to only buy this only on sale.  I don't think it is worth the full price.  Like I said the game is fun, but I feel this is a very minimum effort from the developers.  
I hope PBA 2028 or 2029 continues to improve and actually makes every bowler unique with different stats and a career mode where you have to build character up by improving stats.  Developers please check out Ten Pin Alley for the Playstation.  That game gave each bowler three ratings: power, accuracy, and spin.  Also you could choose your ball weight and type.  Please add some professional female bowlers next time.
I think if FarSight Studios adds these things to the game and continues to expand on the career mode this game could end up being the best bowling game series ever.

As someone who has bowled on these PBA patterns often and an aspiring PBA Regional bowler, these are the best bowling physics in a video game. And the selection of pros who did their own motion capturing adds a nice touch to the game, especially those who had not appeared in previous PBA Pro Bowling games.

In my career character, I have purchased all the bowling balls in the game that I own in real life, and treating it as a simulator rather than just a game. I would love to try to take my time experimenting in this game onto the lanes and use it as a tool to improve my own bowling skills. The real-time oil breakdown is a really nice touch and helps show why professional bowling isn't as easy as it seems.

Shoutouts to Keven Williams for having his music on the main menu, I've taken a deeper look at his music because of this game's release. And I gotta support my fellow lefties!

One thing I'd love to see implemented in a future update or installment is a greater distinction between solid/pearl/hybrid coverstock types. Like instead of just a 1 to 10 overall strength rating, display how early/late the ball wants to change directions down-lane, and how fast/slow the ball reacts to friction. For example, the Jackal Ghost and the Black Widow Mania are two very different kind of balls in real life, but both have a 9.0 hook rating in the game, and they feel virtually identical. Which is not a bad thing, but it's a suggestion to give that extra bit of realism and gameplay depth.

Thank you to the dev team for being super passionate on the project, and for hosting the bowling community while hyping the game up on Discord. I hope this game gets the love it deserves.

10 / 10

As a district bowler, transitioning from real life to the game feels surprisingly good. The mechanics capture a lot of what makes bowling enjoyable, and once you understand how the system works, shots begin to feel rewarding and skill based rather than random.

There is definitely a learning curve, especially when it comes to the controls. On PC, the default keybindings feel kinda awkward, and the lack of remapping options makes adjusting harder than it needs to be. Because of this, the game feels like it was primarily designed for console play. It registers inputs as an Xbox or PlayStation controller, and unfortunately, neither PC nor console currently allows keybinding customization.

For the Devs:

That said, the core gameplay is solid and shows a lot of potential. It’s impressive how well real world bowling concepts translate into a video game. Since the game just launched, some rough edges are understandable. With future updates, especially improved control customization O_O this could become an even better experience. Keep up the hard work!

PBA Bowling 2026 is a nice looking bowling game with a good core, but right now it feels a bit like throwing a perfect ball… and watching it gently leave a single pin standing.

Let’s start with the good stuff. Visually, the game looks solid. The lanes are clean, lighting is pleasant, and the bowling physics are doing their job. Pin action feels believable, shots react logically, and when you mess up, it usually feels like it is your fault, not the game’s. That alone already puts it ahead of some bowling titles. Character creation is also a highlight. I created a character with a massive mouth, no chin, tiny eyes, and a big nose, and every time he appeared on screen, it felt like my bowling career had taken a very strange turn. In a good way.

Where the game suddenly drops the ball is in its league system. You start playing in a team league, which sounds exciting, but there is no table, no rankings, no standings, and no clear idea of how your team is actually doing. Are we champions? Are we terrible? Are we even in a league, or is this some kind of bowling fever dream? A proper league overview with rankings and stats would add a lot of motivation and structure.

Team identity is also a bit… confused. You cannot properly name your team or give everyone matching uniforms, so it feels less like a team and more like a group of strangers who met five minutes before the match and agreed to bowl together anyway. In a sports game, being able to build a real team identity is important, even in bowling.

Match pacing brings its own small frustrations. Sometimes you can skip your opponents’ turns, sometimes you cannot, especially when they get a strike. The game clearly wants you to admire these “big moments,” but after a while, being forced to watch yet another opponent strike feels less like suspense and more like being politely trapped in front of someone else’s highlight reel. Letting players always choose whether to watch or skip would be much better.

If the developers happen to read this, the good news is that these problems feel very fixable. Adding real leagues, rankings, better team customization, and consistent match flow would not require reinventing the wheel. Right now, the game feels a bit too safe and a bit too quiet, which makes it risk becoming just another bowling game you play for a few evenings and then forget.

In the end, PBA Bowling 2026 has a solid foundation. The visuals are good, the physics are reliable, and the character creator alone deserves a small trophy for unintentional comedy. But without deeper leagues, visible rankings, stronger team identity, and better pacing, it currently feels more like a decent bowling game than a standout one. With the right updates, it could easily turn a spare into a strike.

I've been bowling for 8+ years, with experience in local tournaments along with league.

(For people who have experience in competitive bowling)
If you want a solid game that captures competitive bowling, maintaining the shim/ball this is the game for you.  IMO, they could have gone a little more in depth in terms of asym vs. symetric, including coverstocks but overall, it captures how most of the balls react in real life.  I generally bowl with SPI products and this game covers that with new as well as old balls.  The lines are fairly accurate as well per ball.  What I will say in regards to balls, is that urethane acts a little different, and doesn't push oil down the lane as consistent as it should.  The game also has a good amount of Brunswick and Motiv balls, and is not as limited as some reviews say.  Of course, with any career mode you have to unlock balls, with the later tiers providing a great amount of variety.  If you're reading this section you likely have experience in league, or tournaments, it is a must you play on the hardest difficulty to have any real challenge.  If you're not, when you make the PBA in the career mode, they will not average over 200.

(For casual bowlers)
Yes, the graphics are dated, we know.  However, if you focus on the gameplay, it does a great job at introducing new people to bowling, giving a brief overview on how people progress from local tournaments into the PBA.  While it may be overwhelming at first, try and mess around with different balls to see how they react in game.  My advice, focus on getting the ball to the right of that hash mark that sits around board 10.  Once you get there, speed up or slow down.  Have fun, watch a few videos to help supplement your learning of the game, and you'll quickly figure out that this game does an amazing job at capturing the physics of real bowling.

Overall, PBA Pro Bowling 2026 is a solid game that lacks in the graphics department, but delivers a great product both in regards to audio and gameplay.  It is 100% worth a buy.
