PBA Pro Bowling 2026 has quickly become one of those games my son and I keep coming back to, even when we tell ourselves we’re “just doing one quick set.” It hits that sweet spot where it’s easy to pick up and have fun right away, but there’s enough depth that we can actually work on shot-making, timing, and strategy instead of it feeling random.

What we love most is how it captures the rhythm of bowling. When you’re locked in, it feels smooth—like you can really see the difference between a confident shot and one you rushed. And when you miss, it doesn’t feel unfair… it feels like bowling: you got it a little right, a little soft, a little fast, and now you’re paying for it. That’s the part that makes it fun for both of us—celebrating the big strikes, then laughing when one of us leaves something ugly and has to figure out a spare.

Playing together is the best part. We’ll hype each other up on doubles, talk a little trash when someone strings a few, and then suddenly we’re both focused like it’s a real match. My son loves chasing strikes and building streaks, and I like that I can slow it down and play smarter—making moves, changing lines, and trying to stay consistent. It turns into this perfect mix of competition and bonding time.

Overall, PBA Pro Bowling 2026 is a blast if you love bowling, and even better if you’ve got someone to play with. For us, it’s not just a game we own—it’s become a go-to “dad and son” thing we genuinely look forward to.

Runebound Bastion is a strategic tower defense game set in a fantasy world. Build and upgrade your bastion, summon heroes, and defend against waves of enemies. Engaging gameplay and captivating graphics make it a must-play.

Welcome in this casual action game of Runebound Bastion. You are an adventurer who has discovered magical crystals deep within ancient lands. These crystals are your only hope to stop the hordes of monsters that have awakened in the darkness.

Your main mission is to pass succesful  all the waves. To create more crystals and strengthen them, you must collect special runes scattered across the world. A clever fox will assist you in this task — but he only works in exchange for a few runes.

Tower defenses with roguelike mechanics or vice versa, but the second is more pronounced here. Cool toy, there are upgrades and tower transfers, as well as assistants. Pixel graphics and a low price, as well as not the most boring gameplay are the main advantages of the game.
The fussy game of the “tower defence” genre will not let you relax. You will have to constantly move in level and collect in order to stop powerful enemies.

The art and design are visually stunning, immersing players in a vibrant fantasy world. The mechanics are well-balanced, providing a mix of exploration, combat, and resource management that keeps the gameplay dynamic and exciting. However, new players might find the learning curve a bit steep. Overall, it’s a captivating addition to the Runebound series, perfect for fans of adventure board games.

A simple pixel-art roguelike that brings to mind Stardew Valley and Realm of the Mad God. While it lacks originality, the game is worth checking out as a casual evening pastime. The gameplay revolves around collecting runes and upgrading crystals.

Runebound Bastion is a fantastic game with immersive gameplay and well-crafted mechanics. The fantasy atmosphere is rich and detailed, heroes are unique, and the combat system offers great variety. Perfect for solo play or with friends.
Exciting pixel-art game! Collect runes, upgrade crystals, and fend off monsters. Strategy and quick reflexes are key to saving the world!

The player's main task is to defend ancient lands from hordes of monsters by collecting runes and using them to create and enhance magic crystals. These crystals serve as the primary weapon and can be upgraded to increase damage and attack speed. Gameplay focuses on surviving multiple waves of enemies, requiring strategic planning for effective defense.

The game stands out with its appealing pixel-art style and simple but engaging gameplay. It is aimed at a broad audience looking for a relaxing yet addictive gaming experience.

Runebound Bastion is ideal for fans of casual action games, pixel graphics, and resource collection/upgrade mechanics for defending against incoming enemies.

Two ancient citadels, divided by centuries of hatred, have finally collided in a brutal final clash. Now, only one banner will remain flying in the blood-soaked wind of war.

Your objective is clear: destroy the enemy banner before they bring yours down.

This is a strategic auto-battler where every choice matters. Build deadly fortifications, recruit ruthless mercenaries, and lay out devious traps to stop the enemy’s advance. But don’t expect an easy fight — your opponent will strike back with overwhelming forces, clever tactics, and even warriors who rise from the dead.

One wrong move, and your flag will fall. Use your resources wisely, think ahead, and adapt on the fly. The last banner standing will decide the fate of the war.

game_name: Automobilista 2 - Circuit des 24 Heures du Mans
review_text:
Incredible track I recommend  this dlc (when it's on sale becuase I gotit for 4.49 bucks instead of 8) because if you have all of the endurance dlcs you can recreate the 24 hours of LeMans by racing with Lmdhs and GT3S. One of my favorite track in the game : )

Great DLC.

game_name: Automobilista 2 - Circuit des 24 Heures du Mans
review_text: One really long straight line, broken up by 2 chicanes but hey, they go the opposite way so you can't get bored. And it all ends in a right angle corner just at the point where you started to doze off. Then a trip through the back streets and some squiggly bits to get you back home so you can do it all again. I've never been to France but I knew a real Frenchman once. He told me the French weren't very nice. I have eaten some Croissants as well. They were very nice And I have driven this track which also seems to be very French in almost every way.

Welcome back Punk!
Excellent DLC, the first one in a long time where i actually want to use all of the new wrestlers in my Universe mode.

If you have blood turned on in the settings during Sandman's entrance he will bust himself open on the way to the ring like in RL, what a train wreck of a human being. I love that they thought to add this detail.
No Enter Sandman entrance theme music though sadly.

Only have it because I have the Wrestlemania edition. Personally, this pack is worth it whether you just want actual CM Punk or love classic ECW. Only downside is no Tommy Dreamer. How could you have the Sandman and not Tommy Dreamer?

Would've got it, even if it was not included in my purchase.

The best wwe DLC ​​ever came
Thanka to the creators who chose the best DLC for 2k24, and CM punk is definitely the best
Just expected a couple of characters for this update/dlc, very much pleased to have also the inclusion of new weapons! Hope the other dlcs include added features!

It's a great expansion pack. 
I compared the old entrance scenes of the characters in the DLC with their new entrance scenes. 
This can give you an idea of what has changed: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=gpInltyp5nA

Is it worth 10 dollars? Debatable, but out of all the first few packs for this game, this is definitely the one that's most worth that price regardless. You've got your classic hardcore icons, several new weapons you can have under the ring like a door or a tube light, you can now add Cult Of Personality to the songs that play when you're navigating through menus. Hell, The Dudley Boys even have a specific unique tag team champs entrance made for them. Regardless of what mode I'm playing, it feels like having these additions to the roster actually spice up gameplay choices in a fun way.

Now, if only they'd done this with the two packs that followed this and things would be okay right now, but that's just not how it worked out, was it?

One of the most refreshingly unique games I've played in ages.  As I have mainly been playing story mode I will review it from that perspective.

Do you like over the top sports anime?  School themed slice of life shows?  Do like your character designs eccentric?  Do you enjoy a good training arc?  Well then boy howdy, do I ever have a game for you.  This is 100% a slice of life sports anime simulator, and I am here for it.  

It's got it all.  Charming characters, silly humor, over the top dramatic moments, character bonding, and some intense anime soccer with all the secret moves you could ever ask for.  Oh, and it's a full on turn based JRPG too.  This game wears many hats, and wears them well.

I can't really compare it to anything as I've never played anything like it.   A wholly unique and refreshing experience.  It even allows you to keep the japanese names in tact, rather than the cringy english names.   Or you can keep the englishittified names for the sub if that's somehow your thing.  The point is, the game respects player choice and that is a good thing!

Don't miss out!

I had never played an Inazuma Eleven game before but I kind of had a hunch that I'd like it because I like RPG games, character building and over the top football anime (Captain Tsubasa anyone?). Well, to make a long story short I find the game to be very fun. I finished the story mode which was not too long but also not too short either. I enjoyed the story of Unmei and the introduction to the world of Raimon and the Endos. Now I'm playing the routes in the Chronicle Mode which is taking me through the stories of the older games and older teams/characters while building my own team at the same time. Geniunely a great way to continue the legacy of the 2010 anime, the story feels fresh, the gameplay (after a while in story mode of playing rock paper scissors) is genuinely fun, chronical mode is a blast and the amount of time and effort spent into crafting this game is more than any EA sports title will give you.

If you're a fan of inazuma eleven, or just want to play an animé football game with absurd ways to score goals with an RPG system, this is for you.

Inazuma Eleven: Victory Road

Inazuma Eleven: Victory Road delivers a far more substantial and emotional Story Mode than I expected. The campaign takes around 30–40 hours to complete depending on whether you stick to the main story or also explore the side quests spread throughout the chapters. The Story Mode is divided into nine chapters, each functioning like its own narrative arc, and the overall pacing is one of the game's biggest strengths.

A Strong, Accessible Beginning (Chapters 1–3)

The first three chapters focus heavily on world building and introducing the cast. You meet Unmei (dub name: Destin), the protagonist, and a variety of characters who will become important parts of the team. The game explains everything clearly, making it extremely welcoming to newcomers, you don't need any prior knowledge of the Inazuma Eleven franchise to follow the story.

It's worth noting that the first three chapters contain no football matches. The focus here is on forming a club from scratch, which means your main gameplay mechanic early on is the rock paper scissors style minigame used for encounters. Opinions will vary: some players might find it fine, others may find it repetitive. Thankfully, you can fully automate these encounters if you want to skip them. But I loved it.

Importantly, once you finish Chapter 3, this mechanic goes to the background since you will start playing football games. From Chapter 4 onward, the Story Mode shifts into full football matches, and that's where the game truly takes off.

Great Pacing and Tons of MAPPA Animation

The pacing throughout the entire Story Mode is excellent. There's never a point where the story drags or loses momentum. Every chapter feels purposeful, and something interesting is always happening.

A huge part of this is thanks to the incredible number of high-quality animated cutscenes from MAPPA Studios. The amount of animation in this game is honestly wild, it feels like watching an entire full season of a high quality anime. The animations elevate the emotion, intensity, and stakes of every major story beat.

A Cast Full of Personality – Best Inazuma Game Yet

One of the biggest highlights is the main cast. Every character has their own story, personality, and struggles. It's almost similar to how Persona handles its cast: everyone is likable, distinct, and brings something unique.

The game also gives you plenty of chances to learn about them, connect with them, and see their development. You will genuinely care about this team, and maybe even relate to some of their struggles and dreams.

Honestly, of all the Inazuma Eleven games I've played, this one is by far the best. From gameplay to story to characters, it just hits every mark. For me, it's the number one Inazuma Eleven experience to date.

Extremely Accessible – Even If You Don't Like Sports, RPGs, or Anime

Another strength of Victory Road is how accessible it is.
You absolutely do not need to:
like football
have played previous Inazuma Eleven games
enjoy anime games
or be familiar with RPG systems
The game is designed to invite players from across the entire gaming spectrum. The mechanics are gradually explained during the Story Mode, and nothing is overwhelming or complicated. It's easy to learn, fun to master, and refreshingly different from your typical sports game. Even if you've never watched a match of football in your life, the game makes sure you understand everything you need to enjoy the experience.

Cinematic Matches and Outstanding Music

Matches in Victory Road are extremely cinematic.
The presentation, camera work, special moves, and MAPPA style flair turn even regular matches into exciting set pieces.

The soundtrack is phenomenal as well. Many tracks sound like a full live orchestra performance, elevating both the emotional scenes and the intensity of gameplay moments. It's easily one of the best musical scores the series has ever had.

The Final Match – A Truly Epic Conclusion

The final match deserves its own spotlight.
It lasts over an hour long if not more, and it's everything a finale should be:
Insane music (I am not kidding. 10/10)
Insane animation
Insane special moves
Insane emotional payoff
And the main cast absolutely shines
It checks every single box you want from a final showdown. The final part of the game is so over the top, emotional, and beautifully animated that it genuinely feels like a series finale or anime movie.

Let me say it one more time: the final match is amazing. Best final match in inazuma eleven history.

Voice Acting – Japanese Dub Is a Must

A very important recommendation:
Play with Japanese voices, English subtitles, and Japanese names.

The Japanese voice cast is phenomenal. They bring real emotion, charm, energy, and personality to every scene. It matches perfectly with the animation and elevates the experience.

The English dub, on the other hand, is honestly bad. It lacks emotion, feels flat, and simply does not do justice to the characters or the dramatic moments. If you want the intended experience, Japanese dub is the way to go.

About Performance – Smooth on PC, Steam Deck, and Consoles

I also want to touch on performance, because the game runs exceptionally well. On my PC, I was able to play at 144 FPS on the highest graphics settings without any issues. Even on my Steam Deck, it ran smoothly at 60 FPS.

I've also heard very positive reports from people playing the PS5 and Switch versions, the game seems to run great across all platforms. No matter what system you're on, the experience is solid and stable.

READ THIS IF YOU ARE GOING TO PLAY THE GAME, MIGHT PROTECT YOU FROM SOME SPOILERS

After finishing Chapter 6, a new opening starts playing, featuring scenes from the last three chapters. I highly recommend skipping it, as it spoils some surprises.

The new opening also plays every time you start the game, so make sure to skip it immediately to get to the main menu. Trust me, these scenes give away some of the fun surprises you don't want to see beforehand!

TL;DR / Final Verdict

Inazuma Eleven: Victory Road surprised me in every way. The Story Mode is long, well paced, and full of personality, with a cast you'll genuinely care about. The gameplay is easy to pick up, accessible to everyone, and refreshingly different from typical sports games.

MAPPA quality animation, cinematic matches, and a phenomenal soundtrack make it feel like a full anime season. The final match is an absolute highlight, and the Japanese voice cast elevates the experience even further.

Of all the Inazuma Eleven games, this one is my number one, from story to gameplay to characters, it just hits every mark.

If you like football, RPGs, anime, or just want something fun and heartfelt,

Ok im split on this game. On one hand my nostalgia would love to give this game a 10/10 but it would be stupid to overlook this games massive flaws.

Story Mode:
The first thing i played was the story mode. I like the new characters and i like that its the first time the protagonis team is not Raimon. The world looks beautiful even tough it is sad to see that it is not one continous world but multiple smaller areas that have loading screens between them. The first big flaw with the game is the time the story mode needs to get going. Why do i have to play for 4h before i get my first soccer match? They should have utlilized the 5v5 matches way earlier and much more in the story mode. Its also ridiculous to have to do the same minigame 15 times IN A ROW to "scout" the new players. But all said and done the story mode was quite enjoyable at the end.

Chronicle Mode:
Cool idea but way too grindy. The chronciel mode was the second mode i tried after finishing the story mdoe. Who tought it was a good idea to force players to do every battle twice to progress? I get why because of the way the story is built around it but i would have prefered if they did it in a different way. Maybe have some mandatory matches with our chronicle team in between the story matches against invaders or something. But yet again the chronicle mode is cool for fans of the series but not really for newbies. It only gives a brief overview of the storylines and it doesnt do them real justice. I hope they will remake the older games and storys in the story mode style of Victory Road.

Online:
Playing competetiv is quite fun when you have a fully fledged team. It isnt quite balanced yet but im sure they will get there. My biggest problem is that I (Europe) can get paired with Japanese players which results in horrendous ping problems and lag for both players resulting in a sort of "Ghost Match" where you never know where the enemy players, the ball or your players truly are. They really should restrict matchmaking to the same continent or something.

Achievements:
Id say 80% of the achievements are fine. Some require to play a few online matches but its nothing too annoying. But then the grinding for the awakenings and the emblems and uniforms is quite tedious.

All in all i think i would give this game an 8/10. Its maybe a bit pricey for players who are not fans of the franchise yet but i would still say that there is enough content to enjoy, especially with all the free updates they did so far.

It’s the first time in 10+ years that I bought a game when it was not on sale, and it’s certainly worth the price. 

Core Gameplay Loop - the primary soccer game is a nice mix of RPG-style stat battle that makes you feel the farming is worth it, and reaction and strategy-based outcomes that let you feel you get better at the game itself. 

Story mode - as someone with ZERO prior knowledge in the franchise, the story mode provided a nice self-consistent setting around basic soccer rules that let you suspend your disbelief and immerse yourself in the story. 

Chronicle mode - the game came with a full-on historical mode to let you experience decades worth of past contents in the franchise without needing extra background knowledge. 

Multiplayer - the fact that you can compete against anyone in the world gives a lot of replay value, and a great sense of accomplishment as you continue to strengthen your team and get better at the game. 

Customizability - the fact that you can create your own avatar any way you want, and build a team of 11 out of any of 5400+ characters, means you can customize your own team to your heart’s content. You can play this team against ANY team in the story mode, history of the franchise, and multiplayer, scratches an itch in RPGs to make your own story that could only be found in DnD campaigns otherwise.

Setting - the special moves and story adds just the right level of ridiculousness, like a funny DM that lets you have your own team kick literal black holes and galaxies as shots against the goalkeeper. 

I encourage you to try the game, and enjoy it for what it offers.

Rating: ★★★★★ (5/5 God Hands)

Look, I’m going to be honest. I have waited for this game. And when I say "waited," I mean I have aged significantly. I have watched entire civilizations rise and fall in the time since Level-5 first announced a new Inazuma game on the consoles. My hair has gone gray waiting for the next kickoff.

But folks? It’s finally here. And it is glorious.

The "Dream" is Real (and it has penguins)

As a long-time fan who still gets chills every time Endou Mamoru (Mark Evans) screams about not giving up, I can confidently say: This is the Dream Game.

It’s like Level-5 looked into my brain, saw the chaotic mess of characters I wanted to put on a single team, and said, "Yes. Let’s do that." You want to put Tenma, Endou, and Asuto on the same field? Done. You want to recruit that one obscure scout from Inazuma Eleven 2 because you liked his mohawk? Go for it. The roster is absolutely massive. It is the Avengers: Endgame of anime soccer.

Gameplay: Superpowers > Physics

If you are looking for a realistic football simulation like FIFA or PES, please leave the room immediately.

In Victory Road, we do not respect the laws of physics. We spit on gravity. If I want to summon a giant majestic fire god to block a shot, I will. If I want to kick the ball so hard it turns into a literal penguin battalion, that is my right as a sovereign citizen of the pitch.

The controls feel snappy, the tension gauge system adds a nice layer of strategy (so you can't just spam Fire Tornado until your leg falls off), and the animations? Chef's kiss. Seeing the new high-def versions of classic Hissatsu techniques brought a tear to my eye. A manly, burning tear.

The Story: Clouds, Drama, and Soccer

I won't spoil the new story mode, but Unmei Sasanami is a breath of fresh air. It’s nice to have a protagonist who doesn’t immediately want to play soccer, only to inevitably realize that SOCCER SAVES THE SOUL. The drama is high, the stakes are absurd (as they should be), and the vibe is immaculate.

The Verdict

Is it perfect? I don't care. It’s Inazuma Eleven. It’s colorful, it’s loud, and it’s full of heart.

Pros:

Sakka Yarouze! (Let's Play Soccer!)

Recruiting literally everyone.

The "Focus" battles are surprisingly fun.

Emperor Penguin No. 2 is in HD.

Cons:

I have forgotten to eat or sleep for three days.

My neighbors are concerned because I keep shouting "GODO HANDO!" at 3 AM.

Final Score: 11/11.

Tl;dr from someone who’s never played an Inazuma Eleven game prior to this – [i]A promising intro and strong story that stumbles hard in the second half between overly formulaic writing, and AI that has to cheat drastically to stay competitive, and way too much leaning on references to previous titles. Get it on sale if you’re interested, but I can’t recommend it at full price unless you’re already invested.[/i]

Long version:

Inazuma Eleven is a franchise I knew basically nothing about going in, but Victory Road dropped just at the moment I was craving a sports game to play. Having messed with Captain Tsubasa: Rise of New Champions in the past, I figured I’d be getting a similar experience to that. Imagine my surprise, then, when the game I got was both more and less than what I anticipated.

To avoid any ambiguity, this review is based entirely on my experience with the game’s Story Mode. Chronicle Mode is its own thing, but from my understanding it’s very gacha-based and requires a lot more familiarity with previous games in the series – familiarity which I don’t have. Even so I suspect a lot of my issues with the game’s balance will be applicable there as well.

To start with, Story Mode sets an incredible first impression. The amount of animated cutscenes is obscene, and the story actually touches on some nice points. Instead of just being about playing football, it’s a Yakuza-ish open world for you to travel around and get in fights with people. These initial fights are fun and simple but there’s enough room for them to expand into something interesting.

I also found myself really liking the protagonist, Unmei Sasanami (or Destin Billows if you’re playing the dub). He’s a genius who has a plan for everything, and some of his big tricks were really clever writing that I have to respect. He’s quiet but not shy, and when Chapter 1 needs him to get emotional he REALLY delivers.

All in all the first few chapters are really strong. The world slowly expands as you build up your initial roster, unlocking new training techniques and skills. You have interesting conversations, the sidequests are fresh, the combat is still appealing. I was scared the actual football would be overwhelming after a rough experience playing the demo on switch, but I actually found it much more approachable this time around. The first couple of matches are challenging but still fun.

So if I’ve got this much good to say about it, why am I not telling you to buy it immediately? Well, it’s because a lot of this opening potential isn’t followed through to a satisfying conclusion.

For one, once you’ve unlocked the full 11 man squad, the plot becomes extremely formulaic. 

“Here’s a new team that’s stronger than anything we’ve faced so far! Time for some intense training to come out on top along with a master strategy that Unmei won’t reveal until the absolute last minute!”

I understand that this is a shonen sports series, and to that end you should expect it to follow a lot of the tropes involved. But there’s such a thing as following the formula TOO rigidly, and Victory Road is very guilty of that.

Of course, to really embrace the shonen sports narrative, our heroes have to come back from impossible odds. Which leads into my second issue with the game, and my experience playing against the AI.

At a fundamental level, the AI isn’t very good at playing the game. In friendlies against opponents around my level, I could consistently win by 6 or 7 goals without the other team getting a shot in. The AI can only really threaten you in one of two ways; by overpowering you with sheer stats, or by cheating with disturbing frequency.

The Story Mode battles tend towards the latter case. When the AI wants to score for narrative reasons, it will score. No ifs, ands, or buts. Your stats don’t matter, your skills don’t matter – hell, sometimes the rules of the game don’t matter. They’ll run the clock instead of picking an option on offense and win what’s meant to be an auto-loss for the attacker. They’ll take shots your keeper should be perfectly capable of blocking only for plot-induced incompetence to kick in and give them the goal. Whenever the words ENEMY BOOST! pop up on screen, you may as well put the controller down and make a sandwich until you’re allowed to play again.

This is the most blatant instance of cheating, but a subtler case is built into the AI from the start. See, in order to activate skills, you need to build Tension by winning 1-on-1 bouts against the enemy. Steal the ball away from them, break through their defense, that sort of thing. But if anyone uses a skill in these clashes, nobody gets any Tension from it.

I bring this up because the AI clearly does not have the same Tension restriction the player has. They can use skills whenever they want, and if you don’t have the Tension to counter with a skill of your own it’s basically a guaranteed loss. Even if you do use a skill to break past a defender, the next player down the field will do the same thing and get the ball back anyway.

This leads to really bizarre gameplay where you just let the AI walk over you, hoping it’ll run through its skill spam and let you maybe build some Tension so you can make some counter-shots. And the further you get into the game the worse this habit becomes. (Mention the name [spoiler]Royal Academy[/spoiler] to anyone who’s played this game and watch them spontaneously froth at the mouth!)

If that’s not enough, the last few encounters have some truly monstrous level gaps between them. Even if you play around the AI’s scam tactics, you’d better have the stats to compete or you'll just lose the old fashioned way. The only way to combat this is to spend hours repeating the same training exercises over and over again, long after they’ve stopped being interesting because the game ran out of ideas halfway through.

The later story doesn’t really connect either. There are some nice plot points, but in the latter half they lean really hard on references to earlier games as a source of story. Aged-up versions of former characters are all over the place, and you’re expected to just know who they are. This is the problem with referential storytelling – it works fine if you’re familiar with the material, but newcomers are just left scratching their head wondering what the heck is going on.

And at the end of it all there’s the Extended Story, which I assumed would be some sort of playable postgame. Imagine my horror to discover it’s [spoiler]a superboss battle against a level 60 team that’d take multiple hours of grinding for me to even consider competing in.[/spoiler]

If you can pick this game up for cheap, I think there’s enough content in the first half to get your money’s worth. Just be prepared for a drop in quality from the midpoint onwards, and for a lot of references to fly right over your head.

This game is a mixed bag. I've been a fan of Inazuma Eleven since 2014, and I've been waiting over 8 years for this game. I'll separate this into three parts, the single player, the online, and the post-launch experience.

The single player modes, Story and Chronicle mode, are both fantastic. The writing for Story mode is out of this world, and it does a great job of building a cast you get attached to and has a well structured journey of rags to riches. For me, it surpasses almost every other arc of Inazuma Eleven, minus IE2, even comparable to other sports anime like Haikyuu!! or Blue Lock (lmao). Chronicle Mode, is a watered down version of the original games/shows by only having the player experience the main matches, omitting majority of the plot. I would personally recommend a newcomer watch the anime or play the games on the DS, but in 2025, that can be a challenge, so Chronicle mode will unfortunately have to do. This mode is a bit grindy, but through post launch updates, it has been made easier.

Online mode is actually miserable. Inazuma Eleven has historically made the community figure things out, and this game is no different. It will give you a general explanation of all the mechanics, but there is a lot of depth that isn't explained. Focus battles are so wacky that, even when you think you understand it, certain interactions will make you even more confused. Match that with latency/general online nonsense, stat gaps between you and the other player that can only be closed with an exorbitant amount of grinding, Justice existing as an archetype, RNG in passive farming/spirit farming/gacha (yes, this game has a gacha), online just ends up being a "who grinded harder/who got luckier" match. 

The post launch of this game has been interesting. The game has a lot of bugs the player will easily encounter, and every update they push to fix bugs, more bugs appear. The devs listen to player feedback very closely, and implement changes very quickly, for better or worse. Even though this game has been delayed for 8 years, the state the game was in at launch was honestly unacceptable, and it definitely needed some more time in the kitchen to have a fully polished experience at launch.

I started the Chronicles mode, just to relieve the original story, and building my team with my favorite characters.
You'll start creating your custom avatar, and setting his traits. You'll be able to select a starting team and you'll be ready to relieve all the storyline.
I love Macha Green's feet.
The first challenge will be the canon story of the games, and after that you'll have to win against the opponents with your custom team you built. Every match will give your characters EXP, and you'll eventually unlock new special techniques. You can even battle against the same opponents in other 2 difficulties, that grant rewards like new team jerseys and rarer characters.

If you don't want to build a team and level it up, you can play in an arcade mode selecting a team, and you can even play online or unlock 3 new teams for this mode.

It's not enough? In the ranked section, you can play online (ranked or unranked), unlocking characters from the opponents just by participating.
This mode has a singleplayer path, that gives you the chance to play against all the teams in the stories from all the games, in 4 different difficulties.

The main story and the chronicles mode, has really cool anime cutscenes made by studio Mappa, and even an anime intro.

I have no more things to say, so go and buy this, the devs deserve this.

You'll love it if you are fan of the franchise, but a warning, the first 10 hours of gameplay in story mode are awful, very repetitive and a rock paper scissors minigame that is repeated A LOT of times, and the interviews to scout more team members are even worse, you have to repeat the same minigame 15 times.

Past that, it turns in a great game, almost 30 hours of pure football, full Inazuma Eleven style, great story, charismatic characters, overall a great, fun game with a very bad beggining.

I love football, and Inazuma Eleven was one of those anime series I watched when I was a little kid, so being able to finally play a proper game with all these characters is honestly an amazing feeling. Everything about it hits that nostalgic spot - the music, the animations, the special moves, even the vibe of the teams and story. It really feels like stepping back into something that was a big part of my childhood.

And as a game, Victory Road is probably one of the best anime-based games I’ve ever played. It has so much charm, so much energy, and it genuinely captures the spirit of Inazuma Eleven perfectly. The matches are super fun, building your team is addictive, and just seeing all these characters come to life in gameplay makes the whole experience even better.

It is pretty grindy, you’re going to spend a lot of time leveling, recruiting players, and improving your team. But honestly, the grind fits the vibe of the series: working your way up, training hard, and forming your own ultimate squad. If you’re a fan of the anime, it’s hard not to enjoy that.

Overall, Inazuma Eleven: Victory Road is a blast. Nostalgic, energetic, full of charm, and absolutely worth playing if you love football, anime games, or both. For me, it’s easily one of the best anime games I’ve ever experienced!

For what is basically a silly game that's FF10's blitzball stretched out into an entire game about teenage soccer with superpowered kicks, this game does astonishingly well in many aspects, and the story was incredibly well tuned to my tastes with a solid, down-to-earth story about characters pursuing their passions.

And the cherry on top? With every update i feel like the devs listened to the playerbase and addressed issues and added features that specifically reduced frustrations i had with the game.

This game feels like a labour of love to me, and it's a damn blast to play. If you ever think "man this looks like a silly anime game for kids" or "this is just for soccer fifa heads right?", reconsider. It's an anime game made with love and with RPG mechanics that don't demand you like soccer.

Pros:

* Comes across as a labor of love because it is far better-made than any sports game aimed at children from a quarter-century ago has any right to be, with surprisingly full and sound mechanics that make it a genuinely satisfying arcade baseball experience

* 28 kid versions of real-life bygone MLB stars to select - it was 31 in the original version but Frank Thomas, Barry Bonds and Ken Griffey Jr. declined to return. Luckily they are still in the game, visually changed to more generic kids but with the same player ratings, meaning you can hit nuclear Griffey homers even though he now goes by the alias "Luke Lopez"

* Nostalgic to play as the MLB teams of that era after decades of relocation and rebranding (one notable exception: the Cleveland Indians have been changed to the Guardians, other renamed/relocated teams like the Montreal Expos are left as-is)

* Strong replayability with good staying power - no match ever plays the same way twice and the game stays charming and engaging, I have 14 hours at the time of this review (and countless more from my childhood) and still remain always eager for more

Neutral:

* All the effort put into almost every facet of the game, then its creators saw fit to only record a handful of voice lines of players taunting during matches, then programmed them to be repeated absolutely constantly at high volume

* HEY BATTER BATTER BATTER, SUH-WING BATTER

* WE WANT A BATTER, NOT A BROKEN LADDER

* The above phrases will be seared into your brain permanently if you play this game for any length of time, but fortunately they can be turned off (using the "chatter" setting under options, the existence of which is why I'm not putting this under cons), and I suggest you do so to preserve your own sanity

Cons:

* Some technical problems with this version: sometimes there is noticeable lag when pitching, plus occasional graphical errors such as the ball sometimes appearing to split in two briefly as it approaches home plate when batting. Not ideal, but not game-breaking

* The AI is very far from perfect - not surprising for a game of its age, but opposing players too often take unnecessary outs by doing foolish things like leaving base when they have no real opportunity, or trying to steal home for no particular reason, and it's easy to abuse these weaknesses intentionally

* Not much difficulty - again not surprising given its age and original target audience, but even on maximum difficulty settings (difficulty set to hard, fully manual pitch tracking when batting, errors enabled) the game is only really challenging if one intentionally selects a team of inept players

* In-game player ratings can be deceptive because they are presented as four simple 1-10 categories (batting, pitching, running, fielding) when in fact each of the four stats is governed by multiple subcategories rated on a 100 point scale, but these are undisclosed. For example, one Backyard kid is rated 10/10 in batting, but underneath that she has a perfect hidden contact rating with a poor hidden power rating. She is also unusually slow, meaning she mostly sends out weak hits that can become double plays and is usually thrown out at first herself, making her "perfect" batting rating feel like an outright lie

The verdict:

In my opinion this is the apex of the Backyard Sports series and an example of how much quality even sports games for kids could have once upon a time. I'm delighted to see it on Steam, once again made available through legitimate channels, and it's easily worth $10 and the time to try it even if one does not have the nostalgia factor from playing it back in the day. Pablo Sanchez is a legend but he's far from the only thing Backyard Baseball '01 has going for it. It's legitimately good.

Don't let the old graphics and the old looking gameplay fool you. There is actually alot of skill involved in this game as each player you can pick has stats you can see in game but also each character has a set of hidden stats that are not listened and the only way you can learn about these hidden stats is by going online and doing research. 

For example the stats you can see in-game are batting, running, pitching and fielding.

But the hidden stats for each kid that you can't see in the game also include bat power, bat contact, actual speed, coordination, arm strength, arm accuracy, eye, stamina, heat, slow ball, left hook, right hook, height, intelligence, attention and aggression.

Thanks to people over the years diving into the code they have found these hidden stats and there exists various websites that can tell you all about the hidden stats and what they mean and how important they are. Such as height determining whether a small kid will have a easier time catching a ball thrown to them compared to a tall kid. Or attention dealing with whether a kid will be able to know exactly where a ball is going to come down and land or whether that kid will have a larger area to guess from as to where the ball will land. And so on and so on.

Despite having to look online for information about the hidden stats of each character the game still holds up to this day and is a solid game to pick up and enjoy.

Really solid game, and the support team is excellent. There are a few minor bugs, but the devs are active on Discord and quick to address issues. Mods are also available, including ones that replace the ‘fake’ names with real ones, which adds to the experience. Gameplay wise, it’s very much Football Manager meets Speedway if you enjoy those kinds of games, this will be right up your alley.

I’ve already put 20 hours in over just 4 days, and it’s proving to be seriously addictive. I also agree with another reviewer about the language switching, but it’s easy enough to sort out through the menu.

I really like this kind of climbing game. I think it's related to my son's recent enrollment in a climbing class. Children are quite flexible and fun to climb, but adults can't. This game makes up for this regret.

This game is very interesting. I found that once I climbed to the top of each building, there would be a roaming module in the air, just like walking on the glass walkway. At first, I was a little afraid to walk, afraid of falling from high altitude, and gradually I got used to it. Can this make people no longer afraid of heights?

Extreme Climbing is a VR-focused experience that builds its entire identity around one powerful sensation: the fear and exhilaration of height. Developed and published by HFRK, the game places players at the base of massive, recognizable landmarks and asks them to do something instinctively uncomfortable—climb them by hand, one grip at a time. There is no elaborate story, no complex progression systems, and no heavy abstraction; instead, the game relies on presence, scale, and the player’s own physical reactions to sell the experience. From the first ascent, it becomes clear that Extreme Climbing is less about traditional game challenge and more about confronting vertigo in a controlled but immersive virtual space.

The core gameplay loop is deliberately straightforward. Players use their VR controllers to grab surfaces and pull themselves upward, slowly ascending towering structures that stretch far above the ground. The mechanics are intentionally accessible, allowing players to focus on movement and spatial awareness without juggling stamina meters, complex physics, or strict failure conditions. This simplicity makes the game easy to pick up, even for those with limited experience in VR climbing titles, and ensures that the primary challenge comes from the mental tension of height rather than mechanical difficulty. Looking down mid-climb can be genuinely unsettling, and the game leans heavily into that reaction.

One of Extreme Climbing’s most effective design choices is its use of real-world landmarks. Climbing familiar structures adds psychological weight to the experience, as players instinctively understand just how high they are supposed to be. Even though the models are stylized and not hyper-realistic, the sense of scale is convincing enough to trigger hesitation, sweaty palms, and that unmistakable drop in the stomach when peering over the edge. Reaching a resting point or platform high above the ground often feels like a small victory, not because of complex gameplay, but because of the emotional relief it provides.

That said, the game’s mechanical simplicity can also feel limiting over time. While the climbing is functional and responsive, it lacks the physical nuance found in deeper climbing simulators. Movement can feel more like pulling yourself along predetermined surfaces than truly navigating a body in space. There is little emphasis on leg placement, balance, or varied handholds, which can make longer sessions feel repetitive once the initial thrill wears off. Players looking for a physically demanding or highly technical climbing simulation may find the mechanics too lightweight to sustain long-term engagement.

Visually, Extreme Climbing does just enough to support its core goal. The environments prioritize clear shapes and readable surfaces over fine detail, ensuring that players always understand where they can climb next. While textures and geometry are serviceable, they don’t always hold up under close inspection, and occasional visual roughness or clipping can break immersion. However, these issues tend to fade into the background once the sense of height takes over, as the player’s attention shifts from surface detail to the dizzying drop below.

Content variety helps keep the experience from feeling too narrow. Each structure offers a different climbing rhythm and visual profile, preventing the game from becoming monotonous too quickly. Some climbs emphasize vertical endurance, while others require navigating around protrusions or changing angles. While the core interaction remains the same, the visual and spatial differences between landmarks give each ascent its own character, encouraging players to try multiple climbs rather than stopping after a single completion.

Ultimately, Extreme Climbing succeeds because it understands its purpose and doesn’t overcomplicate it. It is not a deep simulation, nor does it aim to be a mechanically rich sandbox. Instead, it delivers a focused VR experience that taps into a primal human fear and turns it into a source of excitement. For players who enjoy VR experiences that test their nerves, provoke strong physical reactions, and offer memorable moments of tension and relief, Extreme Climbing provides a compelling climb. While there is room for refinement and added depth, its core achievement lies in making height feel real—and that alone is enough to leave a lasting impression.

Rating: 8/10

There is no elaborate story, no complex progression systems, and no heavy abstraction; instead, the game relies on presence, scale, and the player’s own physical reactions to sell the experience. From the first ascent, it becomes clear that Extreme Climbing is less about traditional game challenge and more about confronting vertigo in a controlled but immersive virtual space.

The core gameplay loop is deliberately straightforward. Players use their VR controllers to grab surfaces and pull themselves upward, slowly ascending towering structures that stretch far above the ground. The mechanics are intentionally accessible, allowing players to focus on movement and spatial awareness without juggling stamina meters, complex physics, or strict failure conditions. This simplicity makes the game easy to pick up, even for those with limited experience in VR climbing titles, and ensures that the primary challenge comes from the mental tension of height rather than mechanical difficulty. Looking down mid-climb can be genuinely unsettling, and the game leans heavily into that reaction.

One of Extreme Climbing’s most effective design choices is its use of real-world landmarks. Climbing familiar structures adds psychological weight to the experience, as players instinctively understand just how high they are supposed to be. Even though the models are stylized and not hyper-realistic, the sense of scale is convincing enough to trigger hesitation, sweaty palms, and that unmistakable drop in the stomach when peering over the edge. Reaching a resting point or platform high above the ground often feels like a small victory, not because of complex gameplay, but because of the emotional relief it provides.

That said, the game’s mechanical simplicity can also feel limiting over time. While the climbing is functional and responsive, it lacks the physical nuance found in deeper climbing simulators. Movement can feel more like pulling yourself along predetermined surfaces than truly navigating a body in space. There is little emphasis on leg placement, balance, or varied handholds, which can make longer sessions feel repetitive once the initial thrill wears off. Players looking for a physically demanding or highly technical climbing simulation may find the mechanics too lightweight to sustain long-term engagement.

Visually, Extreme Climbing does just enough to support its core goal. The environments prioritize clear shapes and readable surfaces over fine detail, ensuring that players always understand where they can climb next. While textures and geometry are serviceable, they don’t always hold up under close inspection, and occasional visual roughness or clipping can break immersion. However, these issues tend to fade into the background once the sense of height takes over, as the player’s attention shifts from surface detail to the dizzying drop below.

Content variety helps keep the experience from feeling too narrow. Each structure offers a different climbing rhythm and visual profile, preventing the game from becoming monotonous too quickly. Some climbs emphasize vertical endurance, while others require navigating around protrusions or changing angles. While the core interaction remains the same, the visual and spatial differences between landmarks give each ascent its own character, encouraging players to try multiple climbs rather than stopping after a single completion.

Ultimately, Extreme Climbing succeeds because it understands its purpose and doesn’t overcomplicate it. It is not a deep simulation, nor does it aim to be a mechanically rich sandbox. Instead, it delivers a focused VR experience that taps into a primal human fear and turns it into a source of excitement. For players who enjoy VR experiences that test their nerves, provoke strong physical reactions, and offer memorable moments of tension and relief, Extreme Climbing provides a compelling climb. While there is room for refinement and added depth, its core achievement lies in making height feel real—and that alone is enough to leave a lasting impression.

This pong game is very reminiscent of childhood. When you watch some people play, you think it's very easy, but it's not. The game is ideal for both short gaming sessions and long hour-long sessions.

An exciting game that combines the genre of ping pong and action, but unlike the standard game, where players just kick the ball, here we will face a variety of unique enemies and bosses in a variety of colorful locations. Our main goal is to score a goal, while protecting our side from enemy attacks, and our main character has not only standard skills, but also special throwing daggers, which will allow us to make powerful strikes on the ball, providing incredible strength and accuracy.

Extraordinary Ball is a fun and challenging game that really tests your reflexes and precision. The gameplay is smooth, and the levels are creatively designed, keeping you engaged for hours. It’s a great choice for anyone who enjoys skill-based games with a simple yet addictive concept. Highly recommended!

He has to work too hard to do anything against mixup gods and pressure gooners while having an obnoxious lack of safe luanchers that come out in time to punish anything like every other character can. Most of these issues arent even the character himself, its the very base of what Tekken 8 has become, which is why this game has such mixed reviews. Season 2 is an overtuned mess, and no matter how broken your non-tier greedy characters are, there will always be the Bryans, the Hworangs, the Lydias, the Ninas, Anna, PAUL. There are characters that are broken and there are characters that are silver spoon fed privileged and people have serious issues understanding and accepting that difference. 

Armor King is pretty dope and beginner friendly as he plays what is considered "Small Tekken" He's not at all complicated to learn and his Bad Jaguar stance while cool, completely pales in comparison to every one else that has a stance, and the parry window on stance is very small. Hope season 3 completely erases what season 2. Wouldn't recommend spending money on this game till they wake up and listen to everyone begging them to not run it further into the crust of the earth.

game_name: Revenge of the Boxer: Moscow Criminality
review_text:
Revenge of the Boxer: Moscow Criminality, developed by Alligator Friends Software and published by Strategy First, is a gritty, retro-inspired beat-’em-up that transports players into the harsh underworld of Moscow’s criminal scene. Originally released in the mid-2000s and later reintroduced on Steam, the game embraces an old-school action formula built around straightforward combat, urban environments, and a revenge-driven storyline. While it does not attempt to reinvent the genre, it offers a nostalgic, arcade-style brawling experience that leans heavily into atmosphere and raw momentum.

The story centers on a professional boxer whose life is shattered when he is falsely accused of murder. Stripped of his career and freedom, he is thrown into prison for a crime he did not commit. Refusing to accept his fate, he escapes custody and embarks on a violent quest through Moscow’s criminal underworld to clear his name. This simple but effective premise fuels the action from beginning to end. Rather than presenting a deeply layered narrative, the game uses its revenge plot as a catalyst for continuous confrontations with gang members, corrupt figures, and a colorful cast of street-level adversaries.

Gameplay unfolds in a side-view perspective reminiscent of classic arcade brawlers. Revenge of the Boxer: Moscow Criminality, developed by Alligator Friends Software and published by Strategy First, is a gritty, retro-inspired beat-’em-up that transports players into the harsh underworld of Moscow’s criminal scene. Originally released in the mid-2000s and later reintroduced on Steam, the game embraces an old-school action formula built around straightforward combat, urban environments, and a revenge-driven storyline. While it does not attempt to reinvent the genre, it offers a nostalgic, arcade-style brawling experience that leans heavily into atmosphere and raw momentum.

The story centers on a professional boxer whose life is shattered when he is falsely accused of murder. Stripped of his career and freedom, he is thrown into prison for a crime he did not commit. Refusing to accept his fate, he escapes custody and embarks on a violent quest through Moscow’s criminal underworld to clear his name. This simple but effective premise fuels the action from beginning to end. Rather than presenting a deeply layered narrative, the game uses its revenge plot as a catalyst for continuous confrontations with gang members, corrupt figures, and a colorful cast of street-level adversaries.

Gameplay unfolds in a side-view perspective reminiscent of classic arcade brawlers. Players guide the protagonist through various urban environments, engaging in hand-to-hand combat against waves of enemies. The mechanics are intentionally accessible: punches, blocks, dodges, and basic combinations form the core of the combat system. Unlike modern fighting games that emphasize intricate combo trees or layered mechanics, Revenge of the Boxer focuses on timing and positioning. Success often depends on reading enemy movements, choosing when to strike, and maintaining control of crowd situations. The pacing keeps fights energetic, with a steady rhythm that encourages aggressive play while still rewarding cautious defense.

The protagonist’s boxing background influences the feel of combat. Strikes carry weight, and the emphasis on close-range fighting reinforces the character’s identity as a trained athlete thrust into street warfare. While enemy AI may not be particularly advanced, encounters gain tension through numbers and varied enemy types. Some opponents rush aggressively, while others hang back, forcing the player to manage spacing and movement carefully. Though the AI can feel simplistic at times, the sheer volume of confrontations maintains engagement.

Visually, the game reflects its era with stylized 3D character models and environments presented from a side-on perspective. The art direction captures a gritty urban atmosphere, featuring dim alleyways, industrial zones, and rundown neighborhoods that reinforce the crime-driven narrative. Character designs lean toward exaggerated archetypes, adding personality to the adversaries encountered throughout the campaign. While the graphics do not meet modern standards of detail or fluidity, they carry a distinct charm rooted in early 2000s action titles.

The game offers multiple modes to extend replay value. Career Mode drives the narrative forward through successive stages, while standalone battle modes allow players to jump directly into combat scenarios. A local multiplayer option adds further appeal, enabling two players to engage in head-to-head fights. Though the absence of online multiplayer limits its competitive longevity, the inclusion of varied modes ensures there is more than just a linear campaign to experience.

One of the game’s defining strengths lies in its accessibility. Controls are straightforward, and players can quickly understand the mechanics without extensive tutorials. This simplicity allows the focus to remain on the visceral satisfaction of landing punches and clearing groups of enemies. At the same time, extended play sessions may reveal repetition in enemy behavior and stage progression. The game does not introduce significant mechanical evolution over time, so enjoyment largely depends on appreciation for its straightforward design.

Despite its limitations, Revenge of the Boxer: Moscow Criminality manages to carve out a unique identity. Its setting distinguishes it from more common Western crime narratives, offering a Moscow backdrop that adds cultural texture. The combination of revenge story, street-level combat, and exaggerated characters gives the game a slightly pulpy tone that borders on camp at times. For players who enjoy retro beat-’em-ups and straightforward brawling action, this tone can be part of its appeal.

Ultimately, Revenge of the Boxer: Moscow Criminality is a product of its time, delivering a focused and uncomplicated action experience. It may lack the polish and complexity of modern fighting titles, but it compensates with energetic pacing and a clear sense of purpose. For those seeking a nostalgic return to arcade-style combat infused with a gritty urban revenge narrative, it provides a surprisingly engaging ride through Moscow’s criminal underbelly.

Rating: 8/10
