Really fun racing game. Really good indie music. Really pretty art style. Great vibes overall. Definitely recommend. Loved the multi-speed bikes personally. They make the game feel more grown. 

My beef: Game is so easy that is has to cheat to be challenging. Cars and other bikers constantly swerve straight into you. Your bike will just randomly run you into things. Camera will make races harder by bugging out when you get too close to objects or other racers or when too many things are on screen or when you make a turn less than 90 degrees.

Very good sense of humor about cycling culture. If you love bicycles, you will love this game. Beautiful art style, great world building. Physics feel a bit off, but in a way that's consistent with racing games from the 90s. Sometimes frustrating if you're trying to order a cheesecake from Home Depot.

I would leave a neutral review if i could but i do think this game's pros overwhelm the cons.

What the game gets right:
1 Moebius artsyle is great 
2 Music is amazing
3 Riding a bike is very fun and feels distinct (not like you are riding a motorcycle)
4 Different race tracks are all fun in their own right
5 The vibes are great in world 1 and 3, you can almost feel the wind in your face

Where the game falls short:
1 It is way too easy, most of the time you will come first and beat tom's time without really trying
2 The physics are straight up jank, you can clip something tiny and still get sent to the shadow real
3 Enemies' AI is bad, a lot of the time they will randomly turn into you (well and just be too slow)
4 Collecting letters in each race is not fun, it makes you think of how to collect the letters in a way that will still allow you to beat tom's time instead of trying the lowest time possible. And yeah technically you can first collect then try actually racing but considering how easy the races are there is no challenge in them
5 Second world feels uninspired, while in world 1 for some tom's times you had to use shortcuts, here you just race and get it without trying. It looks meh, it's all a green blob and yeah, people ruined it blah blah blah, still not great to look at. The races become even easier, if in first world you had to change your bike to suit the track style, in world 2 i changed absolutely nothing and still won most races first try (the retries came from dying on a random cardboard box)

Overall it's a great "artists game" where visuals are king and the gameplay is okay enough so that the game doesn't feel annoying. I personally enjoyed the game you might too, if you have a spare 10 bucks and wanna feel adventurous i'd recommend it, if you want a challenge the game is not for you
A refreshingly original game with a cool art style and music.

Great vibes for the first several hours. [spoiler] I was disappointed to have a gorgeous relaxing environment replaced by a bland hideous one several hours in [/spoiler], but I still enjoyed my time with it and felt like I got my money's worth.
Just a chill game about cycling and upgrading your bike. Can be finished in a few hours and didn't overstay it's welcome!

loved riding around in this world so much, after having so much fun on Steam, I bought it digitally for PS5, there is enough in the world to finish the game and tune your bike with found parts to complete the game, then there is the challenge of beating Tom's Time and finding ALL the parts that keeps me going back to it.   I think the simple art style allows for smooth frame rate and I didn't  notice any frame drops on  my 30080 super, it's just smooth sailing
Good gameplay, and great soundtrack. It is in the vein of Sable, so this game is made for me

Driving around is fun and the vibes of the world are good. Game is a bit short with the second half being more frustrating than the first with debris and other riders on the road for races which lead to "unfair" crashes and restarts. 100% completion is maybe another 10 hours collect-a-thon with no in-game tools to help you other than the lone riders flags so you will need to look up online the locations of the boxes etc. Overall enjoyable experience with fun gameplay and a more casual tone.
Wheel world is a joyful experience - the soundtrack is a true highlight: props to all the contributing artists!

I do love racing games and when I stumbled upon wheel world I was intrigued, a racing game on bicycle with customisation and a fun story? Sign me in and for the most part is really that, a fun and cozy racing game where you control kat and you race others to get reputation (rep in short) in a world where biking is everything and I love it, almost every aspect is done well enough with multiple biomes each with different styles: from the classic rural and country side with lot's of Flatlands and farming lots which brings me back to when I usually drove my bike in scenery similarly to those to more chaotic city landscape where you need to not only pay attention to the other people your competing with in the race but you need to pay attention to cars in the streets and other obstacles, of course every type of biomes and race need different types of bikes and the customisation is here to help, there are a lot of parts from the most serious and technical players to the silly players that want to make the most impractical bike like a hot dog bike for example, you can mix and match a lot of parts and it's always interesting see new components and confronting them to make your bike the best there is for that race specifically.
One other thing I really loved is the graphics, from the really good scenery, to the silly and goofy characters and the overall good style this game has a really charming world to explore.
But unfortunately I think there something that need to be discussed, sure it's a more cozy game than a racing game, but where the challenge? Sure there are some very late races that are somewhat challenging, but man, most of the races are ridiculously easy, even the so called "hard" races where really really easy, sure you have some challenges in each races but they are easy too, too easy, sure I love the coziness of all but some challenge would be nice too.
In the end the game is great, it has some really good world and some nice scenery, races even if easy are still fun and learning what it's you best type of bikes is fun too, a lot of customisation and lots of things to explore, if you like a more cozy racing game with bikes this is for you.
Awesome chill vibes game, fun racing and colorful world that draws you in.

Great exploring game, chock full of biking jargon you learn along the way. No death: if you make a mistake, you're put back where you were a couple seconds ago; this is only detrimental during a timed race. Fun and relaxing.
what open worlds should be, keanu would be proud
bom bagarai

At first, Wheel World felt like a dreamy, laid-back cousin to mainstream racing games - cruising through vibrant streets, customizing my bike, and losing myself in the game’s stunning art style and lovely soft synth pop soundtrack.

But then came the second world, which is round about 80% into the game. The difficulty spiked so suddenly and so harshly that it felt like hitting a brick wall. The core gameplay loop of races never felt perfectly polished, but there it really got off rails. No matter how much I tried, the game stopped being fun because some other biker would suddenly make a 90° turn and push me into a wall etc.

Eventually, I just had to call it quits. It’s a shame, because the world and vibe were so captivating—but the frustration just wasn’t worth it anymore.

The first island, round about 80% of the game, is amazing and fun. The game is worth it for this alone, but not at full price - buy it on sale. I hope they'll release a big patch and / or develop a sequel, which fixes said problems.

This is a good, but not great game. I'd recommend it for a good mostly relaxing time, since the difficulty I found to be pretty easy. The riding feels really good. The world was fun to explore, I liked the themes of the different racing groups and the race courses themselves were varied enough to not get stale. However there are some negatives as well. 

The second "world" is where the game starts to lose it's charm. The roads in this world are littered with garbage scattered along the road where you need to ride. If you hit this garbage you get sent flying in a random direction, not a fun mechanic. Also the races in this world feature narrow pathways. which wouldn’t be a problem if it weren’t so easy for CPU racers to knock you off track with little you can do to prevent it. The tight passage ways make this problem even worse. Also some trophies are just not fun to achieve, for example needing to walk your bike 1km without mounting or fast traveling, which equates to what felt like 10 mins. For a game about riding a bike, that is not fun. Overall my cons are just that there are some design choices that are more frustrating than fun.

Some quality of life/feature suggestions as well. 
- Bike Build Management: Saving multiple different bike builds a toggle between them. Often I just left my bike as it is when getting a new part because I didn't want to go through the menus to change it and then change it back, for forget how I had the build before.
- Character customization: big part of cycling culture is also the types of clothes, (lycra, backpack, hi-viz), being able to customize the look of the character and have some perks attached to that would be cool. 
- Completion map: going through and finding all the collectables and Lone Wolf riders I felt were not worth it without some sort of map or guide.
- The geared bikes were unlocked little too late. By the time I unlocked a geared drive chain I already found a good single speed build so the drawbacks of the gear shifting made it not worth it.
A very fun and relaxing game with a beautiful cel-shaded art style. I really wish we had more cycling titles as enjoyable as this one.

For the price, this is a pretty fun, relaxing little racing game. It has all the standard fare: secret jumps, collecting letters, beating specified times, and a variety of parts to choose from in order to create different builds. That's how I would describe this game in general: standard.

There are a few things that hold it back for me. Firstly, the part selection isn't too great in terms of real build variety. Once you find a part that provides a pretty good bonus in a certain desired stat, there are few other parts that will ever compete with it or provide an acceptable trade-off, as only a handful of part types tend to provide stat bonuses across various stats. Also, geared drivetrains seem to serve no purpose. In service of keeping parity between fixed and geared drivetrains, it appears that geared drivetrains tend to offer an advantage only in the acceleration up to certain speeds, with MAJOR drawbacks given the game's tendency to instantly decelerate you if you even touch another racer. A geared drivetrain should not only offer better acceleration (with the drawback of timing gear changes correctly), but it should also offer greater top end speed when climbing or descending, depending on the part itself. Since these are tied to stats instead, and the stats are relatively equal among geared and fixed drivetrains, there is practically zero reason to choose a geared drivetrain.

The grip system really saps the fun out of the game when it comes to how collisions are handled. When NPCs are instantly and constantly pulling out on you, the absolute disaster that a collision can cause you whilst the NPC gets away nearly scot-free will have you almost ignoring the stat entirely in favor of restarting at every setback. The cost of high grip builds is often too high to be worth the marginal reduction in annoyance at the collisions with other racers. What could have been a stat focused around giving you the choice between pulling off sick but costly drifts that allow even tighter turns or smooth but less dramatic turns feels like a missed opportunity at best, and a burden at worst.

The devs tried to resolve this problem by making grip even MORE relevant in the second area of the game, but ultimately this just adds to the frustration. The second area features garbage ALL OVER the tracks. This could have been an interesting addition, but with its haphazard placement it feels like more of a burden. These items could have been skillfully placed in the way of valuable shortcuts that would allow the player an edge, forcing them to use precise control or bunny-hops to take advantage of the track design. Instead, they are strewn ALL OVER the track and many are so small that your character blocks your view of them. When you find yourself turned 90 or even 180 degrees and brought to a full stop by a cardboard box, the grip mechanic loses pretty much all its entertainment value. They couldn't even keep the garbage off the two boost ring freeways in the area, which are supposed to just be fun little ways to enter and exit the city. I almost never had a smooth run on them because some small piece of debris would send me careening into the 100 or so abandoned cars on the thing and halt all my progress. 

Ultimately, it wasn't enough to make me stop playing, but it did make me feel like the most enjoyable part of the game was the first region, and the pure  enjoyment of Mt. Send was neither varied, long, or interesting enough to feel like a worthy payoff for the second region gauntlet.

I was a courier for a decade and this game brings me great joy. 
I just wish the storyline was longer (Just finished it) and the races harder.

[h1] The devs are bike-nerds [/h1]

Bought it day one with no regrets. The feeling of riding bikes is there plus the custom is really fun.

An easy recommendation for a pretty unique bicycle open world game.

Wheel World takes place in a fairly big and colorful open world made of zones, that you can choose to tackle in the order you desire (with a couple caveats I won’t spoil). Each district is designed with different terrains, and different type of road and trafic, more or less tortuous; and will require different types of bikes to be as fast or as nimble as possible, depending on the races in races.

But you only have one bicycle. Fortunately, it’s a customizable and magical one! Possessed by an ancient skull spirit (or the skull is the bike) who slept too much on their job of traversing some sort of veil to another world...and well, they need their chosen one bicyclist to get their magical parts back to save the world(s).

---

The first main motivator for playing the game is facing each district champion who stole or ended up with a magical parts, and who will give them back only if you race them and win. Except they only accept races with notorious racers...you got to work on your rep!

So the crux of the game is racing as much group of bicyclists as possible, winning and also validating as much secondary objectives in those races to gain reputation points! Each threashold gives access to a certain champ.

The handling in the game is pretty solid, being arcady and lenient enough that you can make mistake and save them, while also requiring some finess and actual use of braking and trajectory. It’s not perfect, especially collisions with other cyclists that can nightmarishly slow you down. But it’s overall fun, even in harder races by the end, where more precision is required. And the game will force you to try out and search for different parts.

The only gripe I have with the game is the gear system. Your bike can have one or several gears systems. When you choose a gearing shifter with several gears, it works manually, as in you have to shift gear with a shoulder button when the moment is right: i.e when going up, choose a small gear, when going down or in flat straight line, shift up to gain speed. 

I'm sure the mechanic is fun once learnt, but the way it is display and announced in terms of sound was not obvious enough for me to work out the muscle memory of shifting gears; in a car game, the engine roar is loud and clear enough that I can use manual shifting just using my ears. But in Wheel World, when I tried to learn, shifting gears was too distracting to do while simultaneously handling the bike normally. If you want to classify that under "skill issue", it’s fine. My real complaint is more...the game never really forced me to learn. I went my entire 10hrs run without ever really needing the extra speed of shifting manually. One gear shifters worked perfectly fine. Which feels like a waste of a cool mechanic.

---

Since races are dependant on the terrain, some requiring thin wheels and light bodies, others big all terrain wheels and more weight, or anything in between, you will have to explore to find new pieces and spend some currency on buying new gear from a couple of vendors spread in the world.

I was always super happy to find new gear, most of it having some cool and fun design, and really changing stats, at times dramatically. I was just hoping for a tad more customization, like color swapping, so that you can build your own weird bike, while still having one color scheme for esthetic cohesion.

---

Finally, the game looks good, with very fun character designs and a simple but cute toon shader and great cartoonish sfx! The dialogs are funny and the soundtrack is great, with electro songs I still listen to almost a year later.

Really fun game with a lot of soul behind it! Racing customization options are near endless, the soundtrack is a total bop, and everything feels incredibly smooth/polished. There's a little jank here and there but that's to be expected in a bike racing game where you can traverse pretty much every surface.

Game didn't feel too hard or too easy, everything was just the right amount of challenging to keep the player engaged throughout. 

Also skulls with sunglasses are cool. 😎

Really fun racing game. Really good indie music. Really pretty art style. Great vibes overall. Definitely recommend. Loved the multi-speed bikes personally. They make the game feel more grown. 

My beef: Game is so easy that is has to cheat to be challenging. Cars and other bikers constantly swerve straight into you. Your bike will just randomly run you into things. Camera will make races harder by bugging out when you get too close to objects or other racers or when too many things are on screen or when you make a turn less than 90 degrees.

Chill racing game with biking part as a unique spin for bike lovers. Cool relaxing music. 
First and third worlds are great, second world can be a bit annoying because of all the issues of biking in between the garbage and visibility troubles.
Loved the first part with the beautiful world, then got pushed back by the look of the second part and stopped there. I still enjoyed my time, it's a fun game!

I was skeptical about playing this game. I saw a couple reviews and while some enjoyed it, some complained how basic it was. I like the gameplay, just something I can play to feel relaxed without any intensity. 

I am a music producer/musician so at times I need to take breaks after intense listening. I load up a game for some fun and I have to say, the game may be simple, but it's fun. I've never played a game with this type of art style before. It's just gorgeous! 

For skeptics, if you aren't picky about gaming to relax like you might be for your work, lol It's definitely worth a try.  

Kudos, to the devs on this game. I love it! 😁

I just finished the story and every race and I really loved this game. I was playing on steam deck and had minor glitches occasionally but it was super enjoyable regardless. Collecting, racing, and exploring was fun enough that my own bike came out of storage for the first time in ages. I have been obsessed with this game for the past week.

Very good sense of humor about cycling culture. If you love bicycles, you will love this game. Beautiful art style, great world building. Physics feel a bit off, but in a way that's consistent with racing games from the 90s. Sometimes frustrating if you're trying to order a cheesecake from Home Depot.

Very chill game. But it has some advanced mechanics. Open-underwordly bike rpg. Music and graphics - AMAZING. Top tier cel-shading, next to Hellboy: Web of Wyrd, every Jet Set and Bomb Rush. Nothing breathtaking... besides the views... The feeling of speed is really good. And... is it just me or the sound effects sound similar to WindWaker? :D
8-8+/10 - for me. Nice combination of relaxing and engaging :)

A perfect racing game for a handheld like the Steam Deck. Music is charming and the art style is simplistic but helps with playability. For those that like to 100% games, there is a fair amount of collectibles and parts to customize your bike with.

I would leave a neutral review if i could but i do think this game's pros overwhelm the cons.

What the game gets right:
1 Moebius artsyle is great 
2 Music is amazing
3 Riding a bike is very fun and feels distinct (not like you are riding a motorcycle)
4 Different race tracks are all fun in their own right
5 The vibes are great in world 1 and 3, you can almost feel the wind in your face

Where the game falls short:
1 It is way too easy, most of the time you will come first and beat tom's time without really trying
2 The physics are straight up jank, you can clip something tiny and still get sent to the shadow real
3 Enemies' AI is bad, a lot of the time they will randomly turn into you (well and just be too slow)
4 Collecting letters in each race is not fun, it makes you think of how to collect the letters in a way that will still allow you to beat tom's time instead of trying the lowest time possible. And yeah technically you can first collect then try actually racing but considering how easy the races are there is no challenge in them
5 Second world feels uninspired, while in world 1 for some tom's times you had to use shortcuts, here you just race and get it without trying. It looks meh, it's all a green blob and yeah, people ruined it blah blah blah, still not great to look at. The races become even easier, if in first world you had to change your bike to suit the track style, in world 2 i changed absolutely nothing and still won most races first try (the retries came from dying on a random cardboard box)

Overall it's a great "artists game" where visuals are king and the gameplay is okay enough so that the game doesn't feel annoying. I personally enjoyed the game you might too, if you have a spare 10 bucks and wanna feel adventurous i'd recommend it, if you want a challenge the game is not for you
fanstastic game, it made me buy a bike and take an interest in cycling.

Jet Set Radio on a bike. Really enjoyed this game on the deck, ran very smooth. Visually appealing with great colors. The music while racing is amazing and relaxing, there were times where the music wouldn't play but hopefully a update will fix that. This world really reminded me of jet set radio only difference is the bike. The races weren't difficult even though they are marked hard they aren't.
A cycling game for those who like relaxing games that are played with controllers.
Awesome game. I appreciated the difficulty spike in the second area, made me have to think about the parts to use. Only criticism is too many useless parts lying around that I never used,
A refreshingly original game with a cool art style and music.

Great vibes for the first several hours. [spoiler] I was disappointed to have a gorgeous relaxing environment replaced by a bland hideous one several hours in [/spoiler], but I still enjoyed my time with it and felt like I got my money's worth.
Just a chill game about cycling and upgrading your bike. Can be finished in a few hours and didn't overstay it's welcome!

loved riding around in this world so much, after having so much fun on Steam, I bought it digitally for PS5, there is enough in the world to finish the game and tune your bike with found parts to complete the game, then there is the challenge of beating Tom's Time and finding ALL the parts that keeps me going back to it.   I think the simple art style allows for smooth frame rate and I didn't  notice any frame drops on  my 30080 super, it's just smooth sailing
Good gameplay, and great soundtrack. It is in the vein of Sable, so this game is made for me

Driving around is fun and the vibes of the world are good. Game is a bit short with the second half being more frustrating than the first with debris and other riders on the road for races which lead to "unfair" crashes and restarts. 100% completion is maybe another 10 hours collect-a-thon with no in-game tools to help you other than the lone riders flags so you will need to look up online the locations of the boxes etc. Overall enjoyable experience with fun gameplay and a more casual tone.

Fun little bike game! Love the concept and will be interested to see what these devs cook up next :)

Really loved the controls, art-style, and music especially. 

One thing I will say, I would have been waaay more into achievement hunting, going for 100%, etc if there weren't a few design issues holding things back imo... the lack of an instant rematch option after races, lack of endgame fast travel across zones, and the absence of endgame stat-tracking to see progress on sidegigs, if those features were present I would have spent at least another several hours trying to hit better race times and find collectibles.
Exceptional art style.  Smooth game play.  Great writing and characters.  Praise Cog!

So much fun and created by people who really ride and are part of the community! So many cool little details that call back to certain bicycle subcultures (nyc messengers, strava roadies, etc). It's been really enjoyable to explore this world with a story that is fun and compelling. Soundtrack is also a banger!

I can't remember the last time I played a game that was such concentrated joy that didn't have "Mario" or "Zelda" in the title.  You can play any way you like, either minimaxing your stats and chasing slightly better times in each event in a way that's not at all unlike Wipeout, or just chillaxing and touring the countryside, taking events as they come and killing as many hours as you want just jamming to the absolutely perfect soundtrack and visuals.  Yes, there are a few things to criticize: the physics can get a little wonky, the soundtrack occasionally stutters, it really needs a broader selection of difficult races, etc., but you can't really fault the overall presentation or pick-up-and-play-but-can't-put-it-down gameplay.  There's a fast travel system you can unlock but I've never once been tempted to use it because the "regular travel" is just so perfect.  I even bought the soundtrack since (a) it's all bangers and (b) I wanted to support the developers, for whom this is clearly a labor of love.  Really hoping we get some additional islands and harder events as DLC--- the way Horizon Chase ramped up the difficulty curve in the expansions is great model here.

Great game. Must be the most unique racing game that was made, with bikes and cozey, with a story too. The art style is very cool, especially in the first half of the game. After exploring there I want to start biking more, and go for more hikes in the forest. The second half though I understand is essentiel to the story they want to tell, was a bit too gloomy and contrasted too much with the first half. Gameplay wise it is fun to play, and the second part of the game adds some needing challenge. 

Without spoiling anything, the story is your typical Cozzy Game story, about environnement and the impact of technology. Nothing original or outstanding, but it gets the job done, and is a nice setting for the game. Their is lots of humour in the game, and I like the laughts and pokes at the ''pro consummer'' bike culture. 

I do reccomend this game for people who want to try racing with a twist, and who enjoy colorful relaxing art :)
Fun, free-flowing game-play. Explore the world on your bike, collect items and upgrades, meet characters and compete in races.
What a fantastic short game. It was a pleasure to complete. Lots of cycling meme humor, fun races, cool art, and a good soundtrack.  Would recommend.

Wheel World is a nice game where I can ride a bike and vibe to the music.

A nice vacation spot in the middle of now her and I all have is this bike, some music, and another group of bikers that make me love it so much.

Lets see, it has a nice pastel and cel-shaded world that makes it look like it is hand-drawn and was taken directly from the artist’s hands, and scanned into the digital world. Quite frankly, it also enhances the lsush, 

The main character is Kat, and you are given access to the spirit of the Wheel World and your goal is to save the world. Honestly, this is a nice premise in a rather peaceful world.

The story is interesting enough, as I get to deal with a group that likes to race. Each of the character has some surprising depth behind it. 

The usage of a bicycle is an interesting mechanic, something we don’t see often in video games. And I appreciate it. The races are nicely designed but repetitive, which is a drawback.

The music is pretty good. And I appreciate it. 
Are there any downsides?
Honestly, the races can be a bit repetitive,
Story, feels a bit light even though the lore can be expanded upon,n given the topic.  Like, tell me a bit more on the world, and spirits, and the general inhabitants. 

Overall good game. 

A few other random words to say about the game. 
The developer put some serious effort into the game, and I love it. I love the art direction and wish to see more. 

Also, the publisher Annapurna Interactive, and published the game after the drama in August 2024. A decent start bounceback. It even was described as the video game version of Hollywood studio A24. I look forward to more games from them, but now, I guess I can wait.

In the end, I thank you for making it this far into the review for spending time with me and hope it helped you make a decision.

Score:80%
Recommendation: If you like races, and chill games, and light story. Then this game is for you. Achievements: 5 hours
Replay value: Not too much.
Cute bicycling game. Chapter 2 is dreary and doesnt make me want to keep playing.

Beautiful art direction, fun game concept, runs okay on Steam Deck, sound track is a banger, game physics and AI behaviour could be a bit better but are not a deal breaker. Overall I had a lot of fun playing. Worth the price!

Wheel world is a really cool game that's well worth the price. The base mechanics are fun, the world (at least most of the game) is pretty and nice to be in. Definitely a game where you'll know within a couple of hours if it's your vibe.

On a deeper review, it's a solid base set of fun cycling mechanics in a game that feels painfully misled by its creative choices. The story is absolutely fine as a background narrative push for the game, but it takes up far too much space and frankly too many clicks. A lot of essentially repeat dialogue that has no agency. The random player choices in dialogue feel out of place.

Usually I wouldn't care about a game having no customizable character, but it feels like they missed a trick here. The game would definitely have an extra zing if you could pick a 3 letter name and design a character with a few simple choices since it's such an aesthetic/vibe game. Unlocking more gear as you go, letting you match/contrast/etc with your bike design. Not a problem with the game really, just something that could've added a little more player choice.

Overall, really enjoyable gameplay within a game that feels like it missed the real fun - racing. I wanted crazier tracks, more challenging races, and just a little bit more. in a complete finish in 4 hours, I was never even close to losing a single race. Way, way too easy but still a fun time for the price.

As a bike mechanic, courier, and racer this game scratches the cycling itch better than anything I've ever played. Like any bike nerd I've got my nitpicks and would LOVE to see DLC or a sequel that takes the amazing core gameplay and makes things like gearing options more distinct and better balance against the AI so you can't just streamroll every race by going off the front with ease but especially for a $20 game (or $13 during the steam sale like it got it!) it was very worth the money and one I'll come back to for sure.

tl;dr: This game is a fantastic little ride with a few small bumps in gameplay that is easy to recommend to anyone interested. I got through 100%-ing all of the races in about 8 hours and will probably go back to do a full 100%.

I was super interested in this game since launch and finally picked it up on sale and completed it in 3 days exclusively playing on my Steam Deck. Performance on the deck was acceptable but definitely varied between smooth and a bit choppy. Perfectly playable but if you're looking for a solid 60fps I wasn't able to achieve that. Dropping settings down helps a bit but I didn't find it all that distracting.

Gameplay wise I have to agree with another reviewer: for about 80% of the game it's a nice, at times relaxing at times a little challenging, bike racing and exploration game. It's immersive, the skills are easy to get a hang of but difficult to master when you introduce the parts mechanics, and while the story isn't anything to write home about the characters you meet are fun and the whole experience feels nice and complete.

It's that final 20% that kind of turns this into an almost entirely separate game!

For one, there is a bit of a difficultly spike, with terrain being much more of a factor, AI cyclists going haywire, garbage on the roads, and needing to optimize your build based on the scenario being even more critical. To be fair, while these were at times a little frustrating, all it really did was change my play style a little bit and force me to do more re-runs than the first 80% of the game did. Some might really enjoy this additional difficulty, especially since it really only affects the last bit of the game, while others might find the switch up a little more frustrating.

I found myself somewhere in between. I was definitely getting more frustrated with AI, cars, garbage, and needing to start races over from simply oversteering myself into a wall. But at the same time winning those races or completing those achievements felt even better afterwards and at no point did I get frustrated enough to actually walk away form the game.

Overall, I really loved playing Wheel World, especially as someone who enjoys bikes and cycling. Playing portably on the deck was more than acceptable and by the end I felt pretty satisfied with the entire experience.

8/10 and would definitely recommend to anyone who is even remotely interested!

You can really tell that the developers love bikes, and that makes the game for me. I also _love_ the soundtrack. I do wish that it had some more content or was priced lower. It took me about 5 hours to beat the game and that's pretty quick for the money. Overall, I think this is a great game to get on sale especially if you like bikes.

I'd recommend this game to anyone who's into cycling and sees this in a sale. The core mechanics are great; the visual style is appealing and the cycling feels great. Swapping out bike parts to get a different "feel" for your bike is really fun and noticeable.
The thing is, you don't need to bother with any of it. The game is very easy and short. Finished it in about 5 hours with 0 challenge. Even the "insane" difficulty races were won first try 90% of the time and the other 10% is just unlucky pathing from NPCs who push you against a tree.

When I started the game I felt very excited to see how it would unfold, only to find out there is little to actually unfold. There's some bikeparts with "perks", but it turns out there aren't more then a handful of perks either, and none of them are so game changing that you really want or need to go for it. I was excited at the prospect of seeing how the gameplay would change with various bike-builds, but it doesnt.

Again, the core gameplay loop feels very polished, but also very scarce. Every race has the same goals and once you have a decent enough bike that has decent top speed, there is zero need to have a certain 'type' of setup for any race. There's 2,5 biomes which also has little variation (one colourful nature based one, one drab city one and then the "epilogue"). 

The story might as well not have been there, but that's OK. Tony Hawks Pro Skater didnt need a story either, and the tongue-in-cheek tone was done consistently enough that makes it clear that you don't have to take any of it very seriously. I do wish they add some DLC or make a sequel that expands on the core systems; I expected tricks, weird bike parts that meaningfully change the way the core loop goes, and races/npcs/quests that actually require something different.

Furthermore, the ending is quite underwhelming. There isn't even a "well that was the story but feel free to go back to the open world and do all the races and quests" dialog, I just got kicked to the menu without fanfare and can go back before the final 'thing'. Real big missed opportunity. The same goes for some of the UX, it's weird to have quick and fast gameplay and then stop you in your tracks at the end of every race to slowly show you the same points after every race.

All in all, I really enjoyed my time with it and it doesn't overstay it's welcome with the short run time. The thing is, I wanted more, expected more. But game dev is hard and this is a tiny studio doing their best, so all I can say I salute that wholeheartedly and please, expand on this premise! The story, however silly it is, has plenty of hooks to do cool and weird things on a bike.
It's very chill, it's full of cycling puns, and it's making me think maybe I should buy a bike IRL.

Original Game, Great Vibes
TLDR: A cycling exploration game that nails atmosphere and bike feel. Soundtrack slaps, world is gorgeous, customization has real depth. Gear progression is backwards and some UI choices annoy, but worth it if you want cozy with challenge underneath.

The good:
- Original concept that actually works
- Bike customization with real tradeoffs (no "best build")
- Gorgeous world, each area feels distinct
- Soundtrack is fantastic: dreamy synth pop you'll want to listen to outside the game
- Easy to learn, satisfying to master
- Charming goofy characters

The rough:
- Controller vibration way too strong (even 10% in the settings)
- Button mashing sections get tedious
- Conquering roads is nice idea but feels unfulfilling: needs visual payoff on the map (roads conquered etc)
- Best gear comes early: later stuff feels like downgrades

Such a wonderful relaxing joy. I just wish it was longer. I thought there would be several more areas based on the stats of the pieces acquired whilst exploring/playing. But, overall, felt fantastic. As close as one could get perhaps to riding a bike in a video game with a controller... Thank you devs, you did great. :)

Great little bike-racing game.
- cute and engaging story
- beautiful art style
- amazing soundtrack

The exploring and customisation are super strong. The Lone Wolf races are really fun and I ended up going off track a couple times to just race around. Riding around and feeling the difference between bike stats is super fun.

Listening to dreamy synth pop while zipping around a beautifully designed world. The core gameplay loop is very satisfying and strikes the right balance of chill to challenging. Once you get into the second realm, the mechanics you've practiced will be tested - but it does a good job of "tuning" you up to the challenge. The world building is great, the dialogue is fun and the goofy characters are refreshing. 

I'd give this game a 10/10 if it weren't for some minor game design issues. Adding new parts to the bike is fun but ultimately moot, since the first level gives you the best gear by far - more item boons on later level gear would have kept the tuning more fresh. Also, while winning races is a blast - having to button mash through every individual challenge completed and rep point gained before getting back out into the realm is a slog. I'd wish there was deeper customization with Kat skins and changeable part boons. 

That being said, absolutely worth the buy if you want something fresh and fun. Probably my top indie game this year - 9/10 worth it.

Excellent racing game. Relaxing while still challenging at the end, with a great soundtrack and pretty visuals as well! Not much to complain about (minus the game having some stuttering problems but that's fixed on a restart).

Can be relaxing and challenging at times too, the music is great just wish there was more of it!

Its a simple game with a good handful of mechanics. its a simple racer, the physics tend to make intuitive sense once you learn what each stat does, the Lone wolves are a bit of a pain and the hidden shops are good a asking you to explore the map. id recommend it based on both my love of the appearance of games, but also that i played nearly all of it within a week and a half.

Stellar music and a funky-cute art style. The grounded yet arcade-y controls make it easy to play, and a lot of fun to try and master. 
I look forward to hunting the more elusive and hidden achievements.
Surprisingly fun game. Mechanics are quite simple (literally ride a bike) and the game doesn't overstay it's welcome.

Awesome little racing game. Short but really fun. 
Now the only thing missing is a sequel.
Amazing game, have concluded my crashes into walls are purely a skill issue on my part. Would reccomend. The game, not slamming into walls that is.
This game is fun but could be better, gutted theres no wheelie machanic tho

A chill bike racing game with quite a bit of customization. Great soundtrack and fun to just do a couple of races here and there. Not very long or hard for that matter, but I would still recommend this one!

Wheel World is honestly a really fun and relaxing game. The art style is beautiful, and the whole look of the world makes it easy to get lost in. The music is great too and fits the vibe perfectly. If you're into cycling, this one’s definitely worth checking out.

I’m also excited about Wheel World, which came out last week. The blurb is essentially “Annapurna published an open-world cycling game” and if that doesn’t sell you on it, I don’t know what will. I played the demo a few months ago and loved it. The art style, soundtrack and writing give it the right amount of quirk without feeling silly, and it’s easy-going and accessible enough despite the open world scale. (Update - I've played the full version and can confirm that it's great!)
drinking an overpriced batch coffee made by gay barista while playing this, feeling very original

Great world building, and the soundtrack is pretty chill.
Wheel world is a joyful experience - the soundtrack is a true highlight: props to all the contributing artists!

Very chill cycling game. Took around 9 hours for the main story (a mighty fine length). Not too difficult, hits that good flow state for me - caveat being the "difficulty" might feel inconsistent, specially in the second world. There's also no "best build" meta situation here, a great variety of builds can work depending on the race. Overall, I had a good time!

If you think any suika is good, you’re probably onto something. It’s essentially still a physics-based game, and it can lead to some fun chaos. However, I tend to believe the roguelite aspect of the game also strips down the simplicity of what makes the original "Suika" so good. But its derivatives can be fun too!

 I would rather give a cautious thumbs up than a soft thumbs down because it’s still suika. You really need to play a few games to get used to its physics. There are several arenas, and each works differently. Take Canyon as an example, you’ll need to block the gap in-between two sides and maintain it. Overcrowding the pot is still usually unwanted, though there are some objects that truly let you brute-force the game, such as disco balls (you can combine any disco ball with another, and they get smaller while handing you more points) and storages that cannot combine but upgrade into a better one once the required amount is reached. You can then combine these builds with passives to create strong synergies, for instance, each storage could reward you with one VHS tape, or storages might require one less object to upgrade, and so forth. Storages are essentially great because they don't need to TOUCH, they just need to be present. 

You may not hit the required number of points for that stage, and that’s alright. You won’t lose the game; however, you’ll be punished with a malus (usually an immovable wheel, for starters) that will occupy your pot. It’s still manageable, as you can use a passive that lets you reduce their size or get a Ferris wheel, which will eat the regular wheels.

Passives will also be stored in your pot, and you can lose them if they somehow fall out. There are quite wacky moments where multiple objects spawn and go out in arenas like Boat, Canyon, or the last one where you must use bricks to create your own boundaries. It’s frustrating but also part of the fun and learning curve.

Each arena has a boss level, and you’ll face challenges such as continuously dropping things and such. There are certain quests that will earn you a medal, and medals will unlock new objects; however, you can only unlock one medal each game, which is unnecessary in my humble opinion.

Overall, though, it’s mostly a neat game though you’ll probably stick to the same strategies once you figure them out, so each playthrough won’t be that different. I think games like Ballionaire handle that aspect better, but they end up being much more frustrating at the same time.

Steve.

If you think any suika is good, you’re probably onto something. It’s essentially still a physics-based game, and it can lead to some fun chaos. However, I tend to believe the roguelite aspect of the game also strips down the simplicity of what makes the original "Suika" so good. But its derivatives can be fun too!

 I would rather give a cautious thumbs up than a soft thumbs down because it’s still suika. You really need to play a few games to get used to its physics. There are several arenas, and each works differently. Take Canyon as an example, you’ll need to block the gap in-between two sides and maintain it. Overcrowding the pot is still usually unwanted, though there are some objects that truly let you brute-force the game, such as disco balls (you can combine any disco ball with another, and they get smaller while handing you more points) and storages that cannot combine but upgrade into a better one once the required amount is reached. You can then combine these builds with passives to create strong synergies, for instance, each storage could reward you with one VHS tape, or storages might require one less object to upgrade, and so forth. Storages are essentially great because they don't need to TOUCH, they just need to be present. 

You may not hit the required number of points for that stage, and that’s alright. You won’t lose the game; however, you’ll be punished with a malus (usually an immovable wheel, for starters) that will occupy your pot. It’s still manageable, as you can use a passive that lets you reduce their size or get a Ferris wheel, which will eat the regular wheels.

Passives will also be stored in your pot, and you can lose them if they somehow fall out. There are quite wacky moments where multiple objects spawn and go out in arenas like Boat, Canyon, or the last one where you must use bricks to create your own boundaries. It’s frustrating but also part of the fun and learning curve.

Each arena has a boss level, and you’ll face challenges such as continuously dropping things and such. There are certain quests that will earn you a medal, and medals will unlock new objects; however, you can only unlock one medal each game, which is unnecessary in my humble opinion.

Overall, though, it’s mostly a neat game though you’ll probably stick to the same strategies once you figure them out, so each playthrough won’t be that different. I think games like Ballionaire handle that aspect better, but they end up being much more frustrating at the same time.

★ ★ ★ ☆ ☆

I can't find words to describe how engaging this game is.

The Multiplayer Platform Golf concept is a cool idea that works most of the time.
The graphics are clean, controls intuitive and the aesthetics are playful. It falls a little short in terms of some annoying glitches, such as the ball moving when it collides with your feet, which can cause unintended touches when restarting the ball. It's also very common to be unable to hit the ball when it's close to a wall, resulting in losing another turn. The platforming takes too much of the spotlight away from the golf, although this depends on the game mode and course selected.
Overall, it's a decent quality game with lots of levels and a workshop. There's almost no incentive to play in single-player mode, but it's fun as well as frustrating with friends.

I recommend it on sale or trying the demo first.

FAIRY TAIL: Beach Volleyball Havoc is a deliberately offbeat spin-off that takes the familiar faces of the FAIRY TAIL universe and places them into an intentionally absurd setting built around magical beach volleyball. Rather than attempting to extend the series’ main storyline or deliver a traditional action RPG experience, the game fully commits to being a lighthearted party title, prioritizing chaotic fun and fan service over depth or realism. From the moment a match begins, it’s clear that the game’s goal is not to simulate volleyball accurately, but to create fast, silly encounters where magic, explosions, and exaggerated character abilities take center stage.

The core gameplay is built around two-on-two volleyball matches played on compact beach courts, with controls that are extremely easy to grasp. Movement, jumps, passes, and spikes are all simplified, making the game accessible even to players with little experience in sports titles. What distinguishes the gameplay from standard volleyball is the constant presence of magical elements. As rallies continue, magical crystals drop onto the court, and collecting them fills a meter that allows characters to unleash special abilities. These attacks can drastically alter the flow of a match, summoning additional balls, distorting trajectories, or overwhelming opponents with visual chaos, turning what could have been a straightforward rally into an unpredictable spectacle.

Character variety is one of the game’s biggest draws, especially for fans of the franchise. With a large roster of mages pulled from across the FAIRY TAIL world, each character brings unique magic that subtly changes how they play. While the basic mechanics remain the same regardless of who you choose, these special abilities give matches personality and help prevent the roster from feeling purely cosmetic. The game’s pixel-art presentation complements this well, offering expressive animations and colorful effects that emphasize humor and exaggeration rather than precision. The beach setting, bright colors, and playful sound design all reinforce the sense that this is a game meant to be enjoyed casually rather than competitively.

That said, the simplicity that makes Beach Volleyball Havoc approachable also limits its long-term appeal. Beyond the initial novelty of magical volleyball and character abilities, the mechanics do not evolve much over time. Player control over ball placement and strategy is fairly loose, which can make matches feel chaotic to the point of randomness. Success often depends less on careful positioning or tactical play and more on timing special attacks or benefiting from unpredictable interactions. For some players, this randomness adds to the fun, while others may find it undermines any sense of mastery or skill development.

The single-player experience offers only modest longevity. While it provides a way to unlock characters and artwork, there is little in the way of structured progression, narrative framing, or escalating challenge. As a result, solo play can begin to feel repetitive once you’ve seen most of what the game has to offer. Multiplayer is where the game shines most, particularly in local couch play. With friends, the chaotic physics and flashy magic can lead to laugh-out-loud moments, sudden turnarounds, and entertaining matches that feel more like a party game than a competitive sports title. Remote Play Together support helps extend this appeal, even if the lack of traditional online matchmaking limits broader multiplayer engagement.

Overall, FAIRY TAIL: Beach Volleyball Havoc succeeds by embracing its own silliness and staying true to its identity as a casual, fan-focused spin-off. It is not a deep or technically refined sports game, nor does it aim to offer a long, content-rich experience. Instead, it delivers short bursts of chaotic fun powered by recognizable characters, flashy magic, and an intentionally unbalanced sense of humor. For FAIRY TAIL fans looking for a playful side experience or groups of friends in search of a lighthearted party game, it can be an enjoyable diversion. For players seeking depth, precision, or long-term competitive appeal, it’s best approached as a novelty rather than a fully fleshed-out volleyball title.

If you happen to see this review, just know that this VN is a nukige. Yes, it was made for fapping, so you should at least know what to expect.

It’s a very short VN. The first half of the story is relatively compelling compared to the second half. After that, it’s pretty much straight-up corn (lol). 

That being said, I like how realistic it feels. For context, I started reading this VN after I graduated from secondary school, when I was working to earn money before going to university. As someone who had just stepped into the adult world, I realized that reality is much harsher than you expect. Being scolded by your boss or seniors, spending most of your energy at work, and ending up too exhausted to do the things you love.

It feels like you lose the joy in life, dedicating all your time to responsibilities and nothing else. So naturally, you’d want someone to comfort you, to provide emotional support. And maybe be a little selfish for a while and indulge yourself a bit. I think that’s what makes this VN feel somewhat realistic despite its intentions.

All in all, I like the VN. Good soundtrack, good voice acting, beautiful artwork. Since the VN mainly focuses on Seira and Haruto (the MC), you don’t get much of a supporting cast with sprites except for, well, Seira, lol.

Since I don’t really have issues with nukige, I’ll give it a 6.9/10

Really kind of fun to have cute anime girls wandering around your desktop if you're gonna be writing or coding or scrolling, anything but actually playing a game where they'd get in the way. Otherwise, just a fun desktop accessory. Works really well, they stay put and their heads follow your mouse cursor. You can click on them and sometimes they do things, or say a greeting. I wasn't able to figure out how to get more than a point or two from clicking, so unsure how you'd ever purchase additional girl unlocks or clothes in the shop but those feature do exist if you're interesting in clicking 900+ times! Would be cool to see even more interactivity with the characters, I'll keep my eye on this game! Definitely try it if you've got a lot of monotonous work you do on your pc!

Edit: I think the more they like you the more they will slowly begin to spin around so that they're facing away.. ahaha, I can't wait to unlock the wooden mannequin and raise its affection till it turns around and shows off that wooden booty!!! :P
Cute little sisters, what else could an Onii-chan ever want (Siscon's Unite)
sucked at this

game_name: Automobilista 2 - Circuit des 24 Heures du Mans
review_text:
Incredible track I recommend  this dlc (when it's on sale becuase I gotit for 4.49 bucks instead of 8) because if you have all of the endurance dlcs you can recreate the 24 hours of LeMans by racing with Lmdhs and GT3S. One of my favorite track in the game : )

Great DLC.

game_name: Automobilista 2 - Circuit des 24 Heures du Mans
review_text: One really long straight line, broken up by 2 chicanes but hey, they go the opposite way so you can't get bored. And it all ends in a right angle corner just at the point where you started to doze off. Then a trip through the back streets and some squiggly bits to get you back home so you can do it all again. I've never been to France but I knew a real Frenchman once. He told me the French weren't very nice. I have eaten some Croissants as well. They were very nice And I have driven this track which also seems to be very French in almost every way.

I read the reviews before posting my own, so I could try to understand and relate to the ones that were negative.  I think, for the most part, this type of game simply wasn't for them, or what they were expecting.  Regardless, here's my experience and opinion thus far:

To start, I've not encountered any bugs or jank or optimization issues in 60+ hours.
For me, the big draw was WoW Arena-style combat, but without the millions of other things in WoW you have to do to gear up.  This game is a skill-over-gear game, making gear a much less significant factor.

If you like medieval games, you'll probably like it.
If you like extraction games you'll probably like it.
If you like arena/dm combat, you'll probably like it.
If you like solo/coop survival, you'll probably like it.

PVE-only is available and commonplace.
You can play solo, with random people, or with your friends.

If you're on the fence and thinking about buying it, just get it and thank me later.
I've enjoyed it in full so far, and expect I'll be playing it a long time!
This is best described as a dad mmo.  Hop on, play a few rounds, and enjoy your loot.  No sweat required or toxicity.

I'll give it a positive review since the game has kind of come a long way from where it was, and it seems to be going in the right direction; however, at the moment, it's a very mediocre experience. It's not a bad game. But there should be some kind of gear retrieval system that works in case of a crash (game crash) death (like the one I had). Overall some areas I would like to see improvement on are:

1) Mobs hit you while ignoring the stagger (sometimes, so you can't predict if your hit will stagger or not, and I'm not talking about the stagger state; I'm talking about a kickback from a normal attack on a neutral mob that sometimes fails to work).

2) Some mobs are far too much of a bullet sponge.

3) I saw this on your roadmap, but choosing your gender and some basic customization options would be nice (I know this is kind of a big deal and a lot more work than it seems, but immersion matters in a game like this).

4) Work on your PR (a lot of people don't know about your game, and very little content is online for folks to see; I knew about it from before and barely found it).

I've been enjoying Eldegarde a lot. I was confused when they changed the name but why ♥♥♥♥♥ about that? The combat is great and I like how I can jump into PvP or PvE modes depending on what I am in the mood for. Dying and loosing everything doesn't feel too bad because it is pretty easy to gear back up through crafting or just farming the maps. I get strong vanilla WoW vibes every time I jump into one of the maps, like getting jumped when gathering mats, finding a chest with good ♥♥♥♥ in it, or teaming up with others to take down a tough boss. My biggest gripe is the city UI, I liked the old version better because it seemed more streamlined. Overall, I think it is pretty damn entertaining for $25 and I hope it continues to grow.

The rating isn't justified, maybe when it came out a year ago, but today the game is AMAZING. Think of it as an MMO-lite, with PvE zones, PvP zones, PvE dungeons, and arenas. It has a good progression system. The game is very beautiful and the gameplay is very, very smooth.

I can only recommend it at this point.

I'm not normally an extraction game player, but after they added PvE on launch, I decided to give it a try.

The classes all seem to play well. I don't think there is a single piece of loot that ever becomes useless, even just to process and sell. Money never goes out of style.

Every character has a couple weapons which heavily changes how your combat works for them. The game has a set of moves that are all always available (block, charge-attack, dodge, etc.), but all have unique skills attached determined by the class.

Losing your gear if you die kinda sucks, but I've been able to rebuild my gear fairly easily. Only died twice, and were very much my fault for overextending my character.

The areas are fun to explore and getting special loot keeps the dopamine up.
Combat is very fun. All the enemies, mini-bosses, all their move sets, and styles keep things interesting.

Overall, super fun game, just be prepared for the grind.
Extraction arpg mixed with wow pvp. Great graphics and well optimized. I hadn't heard anything about this game before today, it's seriously fun, @Devs ramp up the marketing!

I’m absolutely hooked on the game. I’ve mostly done the PvE stuff, but already sunk 60hrs into it during the short time I’ve had it. The combat is better and smoother than New World. The extraction makes getting low an adrenaline rush. The dungeon is actually kinda difficult, which makes it fun. Over all HIGHLY recommend.

Very fun game. the fighting mechanics are fluid and responsive. the lveling experience is really fun. The only issue is that random matchmaking doesnt seem to actually fill your team up like it is supposed to. More often than not i just join a game solo

Game is super fun, actually rewards the player for playing the game, getting a full green set of gear is easy as ♥♥♥♥ as long as you have a regular IQ. take any greens and disenchant. craft then upgrade. easy. Hoping the devs continue updates and make the game even better but for now i definitely recommend playing this game with friends. its 3 player parties.

Amazing game.  Awesome combat, superb artwork, and perfect mechanics.  The game is addicting and really fun.  I definitely got my moneys worth buying, and I'd recommend the game to anyone who has a love for sword and sorcery in a fantasy realm.  Definitely worth looking into, and the greatest part is more classes are yet to come.  Each battle is different, but the greatest part about this game is that you can join up with your friends and go on an adventure as your own fantasy class, Pvpve style, or strictly Pve.  Build your lodge, the choice is yours.

Game is great
Don't listen or read the hate people cry about everything and don't give credit where credit is due enough.
PVE or PVP choose what you want to do what's the big deal? 
Hopefully the game keeps rising in the player base which it has since the name swap the last few days.

Game failed as "Legacy: Steel & Sorcery" So they had to Rebrand as "eldegarde" to try to save their dying game (no joke it use to have 30-50 daily players). Its an okay game and its enjoyable if you're into extraction games. The PvE is fun and the gameplay loops is enjoyable is the only reason I gave it a thumbs up, but i think what a lot of ppl find to dislike is the minimal class balance and lack of care from the Devs.
amazing play pve. still needs work like loot rolls like wow and this and that but lots and lots of fun
I usually do not like extraction games, but Eldegarde caught my interest, and I'm pleasantly surprised that it's fun adn engaging. give this game a try.
Great game so far. I'm having a lot of fun. Pve and PvEvP. The only bad thing, all the ninja looters hahaha.

There really isn’t another multiplayer, action-combat, high-fantasy game quite like this. If you loved New World’s combat, this game is absolutely for you. Honestly, it feels like a much more refined version of it. You have iframe dodging similar to GW2 and New World, plus weapon-based blocking that uses its own block-pip resource separate from stamina. Once you understand the animation timing and get a real feel for the combat, it’s hard to go back to other games, especially tab-target ones.

Content-wise, there’s a PvE-only extraction mode, a PvP extraction mode, dedicated PvP areas (1v1, 2v2, 3v3), a social hub to practice PvP, and an endgame dungeon. The PvE alone can give you many hours of playtime, and you never have to touch PvP if you don’t want to. For PvP players, though, there’s real incentive (better loot luck) so there’s a solid risk-versus-reward loop.

The new Arena mode is fantastic: you bring in the gear you earn from PvE and PvP extraction to flex on other players. That said, skill still matters most, good players will beat geared players every time. The roadmap also looks very promising, with new classes, maps, and UI improvements planned.

Overall, this game is a hidden gem. They also run free weekends whenever Steam allows (about every six months), so if you’re even remotely interested, it’s absolutely worth trying.

This game has pulled me in like playing a brand new MMO expansion on release day. Art direction is phenomenal, combat is engaging enough to keep me raiding, and that night time difficulty is just peak. Really enjoying my time with this game. Praying that it gets support for a long time.

fun game with cool dungeons and great mechanics and people who love the D&D feel. rewarding and even relaxing.. PVP is great as well.. you have the pve side to build your character and lodge and that pvp side for when u want to really just decimate your foes.. all in all i would recommend this game to anyone who trulu loves dungeon crawlers with an extraction aspect
Game is really fun! Kind of like Dark and Darker but feels way better.

i like the leveling  both character and vendor/lodge . i like the gameplay 
i dislike the fact that you loose all your gear and stuff on death . i like to solo stuff and being ganked on pvp maps ( for better loot ) at the extraction point is really ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥

i really don't understand the negative reviews! they put me off for weeks while i contemplated trying out the game ,after a while i thought screw the reviews Im going to try it and Im so glad i did! the games polished looks great , combat is impactful and feels solid, the music and sfx are great , runs fine on my mid range rig and so far im really enjoying the progression. if your on the fence give it a try im sure you will be pleasantly surprised and if not you can always refund! i hope more people give it ago because its a gem of a game and is only going to improve!

I've put a little bit of time into it, no PVP thus far but the PVE is fun for what it is. It's a shame this isn't taking off the way I think it could, because there's a lot of potential here. I was able to loot around the PVP map without encountering anyone and I feel like more emergent gameplay in both social interaction and intensity would make this a lot more fun.

There's also just something about the animations that feels a little off, kind of stiff. But the combat doesn't necessarily suffer from this too much. I just think adding some fluidity to the movements would help. It feels too much like MMO movement, and after playing something like Arc Raiders, having more interaction with the environment and the realistic animations that game has would be welcome here. Obviously, I'm not knocking the indie extraction game for not having the fidelity of a larger budget game, but if any more efforts were to be put into the technical side of this game I would look there first.

I'd like to see this get bigger. Even if it doesn't reach the 20k+ mark, I think a good, niche community could grow around this game and keep it alive. I'll continue to play it for sure, interested to see where they take it from here.

Highly recommend.
Honestly surprising how fun it is, it feels like a survival-extraction hybrid.

3 maps each with day / night variants and always 1 PvE que and 1 PvP que going at a time so you can pick your play style as well as a boss fight que and an arena mode where you don't lose gear when you die.

6 different classes all with unique talent trees, multiple weapons per class that each have unique move sets and abilities

There are multiple gear sets for each class that each give unique perks

Crafting lets you use the things you gather in matches to make more gear / add bonuses to yourself / make class tools like spell scrolls or lockpicks or throwing weapons to bring into matches


It has deep a progression with multiple layers of customization and fully PvE options for play.

Overall a good game. The classes feel very unique and the fact that you can play the various maps on a rotating PvP vs PvE map is a really cool approach to the extraction looter genre.  Additionally not losing gear in arena fights make it enjoyable as you now have a fun place to PvP with low risk.

Rather fun. Still in the works and they have an openly viewable Dev roadmap that shows quite a bit of what they are working towards. 
Classes are fun though I struggle with melee. 
WARNING: The game has a seasonal wipe and I have no idea yet what that entails. 
Gosh I hope I get to keep my lodge upgrades.

i played it when it was legacy and steel but this game is better now. they need to find the guy who did the controller port for WoW to port this, but support the game cause it is a really fun game both pve and pvp

I haven't played many extraction games but this pretty much plays like Arc Raiders in a high fantasy setting and as an ARPG instead of FPS of course. AFAIK this offers PvE only maps unlike most if not all competitors.

The city options (vendors and lodge) can be a bit overwhelming at first but it quickly all makes sense. With upgrading your base and the quests/campaigns you always have something to work towards. That's besides leveling all the different classes.

I am having a really good time so far.
Very fun game, it's a light and fun experience. Good to chill and play with buddies
pretty good for casual, upgrading gear and your stash is quite horrible tho

This accidental find has been a breath of fresh air, I haven't been excited about a game since I've loaded into WoW ~15 years ago.

There are hundreds of "copy-paste" shooters, tens of "copy-paste" souls-like, and only a handful of games with deep, impactful skill-based combat like this one has. The combat even more enjoyable & immersive because it is true 3D combat (not a 2.5D like in WoW or most diablo-likes). This game is what I would expect WoW2 would be like ...

It could use more polish in areas like UI and fix some minor bugs, but the core gameplay feels solid and fun enough to keep enjoying it regardless, well done!

The only major pain-point with Eldegarde I would point out is that for a new player it's pretty hard to transition from PvE to PvP, the learning curve to win in PvPs is very very steep, there are players who spent hundreds of hours in the game and encountering those on a PvP-enabled map (or even in a practice Arenas) is just not fun. I think devs need to find a way to group people together by their play-time (and other telling metrics) for PvP to work well.

It's a really fun game, the combat feels great and the maps are cool to explore. Even though it is a extraction game, PVP is completely optional and its really easy to get your stuff back via crafting more equipment, finding more equipment, or buying it back from the pawnshop. 

Would 100% recommend to anyone looking for a chill game to hop into to grind for hours or even just for a few minutes.

Bought it for myself but it turns out it's my 12 year old's first real PC game. He's in love. Took me a while to figure out what was going on with the quests and vendors and Lodge but maybe it's ok to have to work things out for yourself sometimes.
Absolutely Love it! Combat feels rewarding. No pressure to PVP. Looting and quest feel rewarding. Looking forward to more content.

Highly recommend to reach out to players in game, it requires team work in PVE for a fun time and easier way to learn. Awesome to see people help each other out getting picked up by randoms when getting downed. Destiny 2 like grind, but with the risk of actually losing gear adds high steaks. 

Wish there was more to the traits having limits or expanding on it, but the game is in a good start in my opinion for a niche game. would love to see more games like this in this genre.

Game is good, love a non FPS extraction game and this one is inclusive enough and easy to get into. 

Good option to include PvE modes and PvPvE modes to cater to playstyles and moods.
Exactly what I have been looking for in an extraction game. Loving it!

Very well designed.  Combat is interesting and responsive.  Diverse classes.  Enemies provide a good challenging.  Great visuals with great readability.

No meaningful bug.  Only complain is the awkward menus.  Overall, much much smoother and better than Dark and Darker.
Really Enjoy the PVE. Game plays great with a awesome art style. PVP is spicy its cool that its there but not my favourite,

about 30 hours in at time of review, and so far i am enjoying this game a ton.  plenty of crafting recipes to learn, so much to do at the main hub town area. ive played mostly solo wiith about every class now. looking forward digging deeper. 

PS. build all lodge crafting areas ASAP as well as always check the quest board good luck and have fun!

This game is a breath of fresh air in the extraction genre. Boasting a beautiful art style and crisp combat mechanics, it combines the best aspects of WoW battlegrounds and extraction looters and fighting games into a truly unique experience. The different character classes are distinct from each other with their own strengths and weaknesses, and none of them feel especially overpowered; and the looting and itemization gives the player something to chase.

The game has added content at a good pace - new classes, maps, game modes  - with more to come, and if I had any complaints it would be to ask some minor polish and QoL improvements.

I have thoroughly enjoyed this game so far and I'm keen to see where it goes next, and I highly recommend it.

Only 36 hour played so far, but easily see this game getting weekly hours sunk in. It looks and feels how i've wanted decades old MMOs to aim towards. Definitely scratching the itch, without actually being an MMO. 10/10 would recommend picking up.

Enjoying it so far! It kind of those you in though and there is quite a lot that I just kind of had to figure out. The UI needs a little work in that regard but I assume they will continue to work on that and fine tune. 

I assume this issue is because I am on Linux but all cut scenes and embedded video is broken and give basically an old school static screen.

when this was legacy steel and sorcerery or whatever it was lame. NGL 
The game is so much better now, account doesnt reflect it but i have over a 1000 hours in and its only been in EA for about a year
Pretty fun fantasy extraction game as something different from all the modern shooter versions. Glad they added a pve mode so you can learn the game at your pace before entering pvp.

Like that other guy said, this is totally a "dad mmo" and I'm okay with that. I hope the devs see the potential for their PvE audience and adjust their PvP-centric penalties to retain their newfound audience.

Eldegarde is a breath of fresh air for the extraction genre. There are PVE only maps and PVP maps. Even in PVE maps you still have to proceed certain areas and enemies with caution as they will give you a challenge, and dying with result in a loss of all equipped gear and items! Lots of npc's to advance with for better benefits as well as crafting stations, forging, looms, etc. Am looking forward to the content they will release in the future!

If you enjoy extraction games, WoW, MOBAs, and hoarding loot with a bit of grind, you’ll probably love this game.

Some people call it a “dad MMO.” I wouldn’t go that far—it’s not really an MMO—but it definitely has a light MMO vibe.

The city/crafting system itself is very logical and well designed, but the UI around it is terrible: confusing, clunky, and badly presented.

PvP is fun and feels reasonably balanced. There’s clear room for skill expression—good players perform well even in bad gear, which is always a big plus.

PvE is chill and enjoyable. It’s perfect for grabbing a coffee, farming resources, and working on your crafting and gear upgrades without stress.

It is the first game of this type for me to play, so I can't give a comparison to recent games that have hit the top 10, but it is well worth buying and playing, it gives a good fantasy experience, the character fighting and abilities feel solid and take a little skill to use to their full extent, but not out of reach for those that do not have 40 fingers... :)  Meaning young and old crowds can get enjoyment out of it.  though you can sit for a half hour and make progress, you will find after a few hours that the game is deeper than that, and you can through many hours into it if you wish, and most of us, want quick, but also want depth, and this gives both.

Very enjoyable game and loop. My only wish is there was a way to play through more of the game, to get better gear, through more difficult PvE scaled content, rather than just PvP.

Either way, love the game.
