I really enjoy Eldegarde: the gameplay is fun, the atmosphere is great, and the PvP / PvE / co-op  loop works well. You can feel there’s real progression to build (gear, goals, exploration), and that’s exactly what makes you want to come back.

The big issue is the season model with full wipes.
When you invest dozens (or even hundreds) of hours into long-term progression, losing everything every season feels more like a punishment than a reset. And now that the game is also attracting a PvE audience, it clearly hurts motivation and long-term retention.

I’d 100% support a “soft wipe” model:
Keep the important persistent progression (vendors / research / base development, etc.)
Reset only competitive elements / ladder / seasonal content + rewards

If you feel the same, please support the official post here:
https://feedback.eldegarde.com/en/p/season-model-without-full-wipes-keep-mmo-persistence-reset-seasonalcompetitive

Great game, kind of reminds me of a mix of Arc Raiders and Dark Souls and a little bit of WoW. 
I do recommend this game for those that enjoy the genre, for the most part this game is quite solid, but I want to talk about a few things that take away from the overall experience. 

1: The inventory system is a nightmare! It is similar to Diablo, however you have no way of quick stacking anything nor is there a sorting option. If you want to keep your stashes organized it must all be done manually... I would love to see some improvement in this aspect.
2: You cannot reconnect to games. This truly blows sometimes when your game crashes or you just straight up disconnect, because you are unable to reconnect to games you are in, which means you lose everything you had with you, and you might be able to get some of it back from the goblin if you are lucky, but it really sucks. One time I zoned into a game and it spawned me inside the map, and instead of unstucking me the game instantly killed me and I lost all my gear. 
3: The loot system is not well designed for group play. When I play with my friends I have found that we have to compete for resources since we all need the same stuff, and it discourages me from playing with them when I am farming for resources (which you need a metric fckton of). I find I end up just solo grinding more than anything else, which isnt as fun as playing with friends, but feels necessary for quicker progression. 

These are all just quality of life improvements, the core game itself is excellent and enjoyable. I hope that the devs are able to address these issues in the future.

Amazing narrative. Such a refreshing and grounded take on sci-fi. In just 15 hours, you'll find yourself buried beneath many layers of plot and theme. Family, friends, systems, religion, authority, generational trauma, womanhood, Asian diaspora - oh, and it does aliens well. It's somehow a well-contained story even with all that going on.

   Vibes are moody, quiet and mostly sparse. Minimalist art style with a clear visual language. Nice ambient music/sounds - even eerie at times. My only gripe is with the navigation and movement. Navigating The Orchard (hub) can be confusing and the layout is *sigh* inducing. Pretty though! Movement is a little clunky but, at least there's limitless sprint. However, the grapple jumping mechanic is not my cup of coffee.

   Mechanical nitpicks aside, this is a brand new dev with an incredible story worth any little bump or bruise. I believe the VAs were all local stage talent or something? *smiles and nods* Nice. Worth checking out, especially if you're a thinker or a feeler! Or a watcher! Or a healer! Or a fix... ok, I'll stop.

A beautiful game. This is an experience, one must see, all must see. There's not much to say apart from, yes, buy it and play it. 

I would 100% recommend it for a more mature audience, because of some of the themes and contexts. But one, you need to play, you need to see, you need to hear, you need to read, you need to experience.

When trying to write my review for 1000xResist, so many thoughts come to my head, yet it feels impossible to accurately judge the rollercoaster that this game takes you through.

I was coming off of playing Nier: Automata, which is my favorite game of all time. I wanted another narrative-focused game to dig into, one that had similar plot twists and surprisingly emotional moments.

I had such little knowledge of this game upon picking it up. I had no idea what the narrative was like, what the gameplay loop would be, if there was combat in the game, or if it would even run well on my Steam Deck, yet I pushed onward for what I would quickly learn to be an entirely unique experience.

1000xResist is a phenomenal game that risks everything in a “live by the narrative, die by the narrative” approach. The innovative and compelling story is the sole focus of this experience, as there is no combat and no consistent gameplay loop for players to lean into for engagement. In my opinion, the creative team and developers absolutely nailed it. There were genuinely moments where I shut off my Steam Deck after a quick 30–45 minute session, and I was giddy and excited to come back and see what happens next. This all comes from a guy who, up to this point, has never played any visual novels or movie-like games (Detroit: Become Human being an example). As I wrote this review, my mind was constantly racing to find something to compare this experience to, but I’ve concluded that it simply isn’t possible. This is a narrative that can only be accomplished in a game format. This game belongs in a genre of its own. It’s not quite a visual novel, and not quite a movie-like game. The game manages to generate a WIDE range of emotions through letting you see stationary moments in time. You’re able to go interact with NPCs and hear the events and conversations taking place, but it’s always in a comic-book-adjacent way. There are only a handful of moments that are shown via cutscene, and that scarcity makes them significantly more impactful.

[spoiler] One of the best implementations of living in various snapshots of time was when you experience someone jumping from a ledge and killing themself. The game presents this series of events through various moment-to-moment timeslots. You’re able to see one moment where the person stands near the railing, the next where they are standing on the railing, and then eventually falling. This allows players to individually experience the events, hearing both the individual’s thoughts a couple moments before, and the thoughts of the surrounding squad whenever they realize what is happening. It’s a method of storytelling that has to be experienced to understand the full value of it.

To some, Iris’ father may have just been an inconsequential side character, but to me, he was really impactful in the emotion and personality within the story. His portrayal as a father trying to do his best given his circumstances—trying to make light in dark times—was so innately human to me. It felt like true thought was put into how his emotional state would be, and he felt like a complex character that we never fully got to KNOW. In a weird way, I felt like I could completely understand him without knowing his trauma or how his life story went. There are obviously more impactful characters within the second leg of the story, but seeing his excitement for creating “Moodle” and “Moodle 2.0” in the first few chapters was incredibly charming to me. It was such a powerful illustration of a father desperately trying to connect with his daughter, trying to make her feel loved, and trying to provide what he can, even though it’s not as fancy as he wished.

A lot of those earlier flashbacks really hit me hard, like Iris preparing to leave and her father having a moment with her where he tells her that it’s okay for her to say that she’ll return to visit, even if she doesn’t mean it. He explained to her that he simply wanted hope, even if it was fake. The portrayal of a more emotional father figure, paired with a borderline abusive mother, elicited a lot of sympathy from me toward the father. This is all without mentioning the final few chapters, where the father reaches his tragic end, and players experience two emotional and sympathetic moments simultaneously, with Watcher and Iris’ father both sitting in front of cameras recording videos pertaining to their own unique and complex situations.

The game allows players to select the tonality of various messages in both video recordings; however, it’s clear that in the father’s case, it is his farewell video to his daughter before taking his own life. To me, his final moments felt like a mixture of wishing he could’ve done more for his daughter, but also accepting that he did the best he could and that he lived a good life with people that he loved. With how emotional this moment was, the final time we see the father is by far one of the most wholesome yet emotional parts… I’ll leave it at that.
[/spoiler]

To me, this narrative presents both sides to every coin: love and hate, loyalty and betrayal, wise and naïve, reality and simulation, control and freedom. It beautifully executes many of the plot twists and moments of realization that keep narrative-focused games engaging. The attention-getter at the beginning is truly amazing, and it does a great job of pacing the narrative in bite-sized pieces where you can gradually start to understand the climax that you witness at the start, and eventually WHY you witnessed it twice.

At this point, the game has been out for almost two years, and those of us who have experienced this game in full truly understand the gem that it is. I think the game will continue to fly under the radar and remain underappreciated by a wider audience; however, I also understand that this type of narrative experience, despite being short (around 10–13 hours), is NOT for everyone. I can imagine a lot of people won’t be able to stay engaged through some of the flashback moments, and others may not be able to stay in the absence of combat elements; however, I think those people are missing out on one of the most beautifully told stories that any game has ever had.

Despite only taking me 13 hours and a little under two weeks to 100%, this game will live in my memory for the rest of my life. I look forward to years down the road when I can replay this gem and get a gleam of the magic within this phenomenally told story.

Really enjoyed this one! It's a strange and artsy game about generational trauma and resistance. The biggest issue is that the map layout for one area is extremely poorly laid out. Even with a map it's very painful to navigate. But the story and atmosphere are great. The devs are clearly working with a limited indie budget but they make up for it with very strong presentation. This can feels like a movie with some of the framing and cinematography.

A beautiful amalgamation of philosophy. science fiction, politics, generational trauma and social uprising against authoritarian regimens that no other game can rival. I absolutely recommend this work of art.

This game doesnt have much gameplay and focuses on talking to NPCs, solving some quick time teleportation puzzles, some dialogue choices and a lot walking simulator sections but its beautiful message makes its a 100% worth it.

This is one of the most interesting science fiction works I've experienced in a video game. It's hard to describe, but it felt like reading a well-written book, fully immersed and invested in its world—but in video game format, which, in my opinion, is a superior way to experience fiction.
The game is well-written; everything from the soundtrack to the voice acting and direction is impressive. At times, the dialogue was so good I thought it was adapted from a book. However, I couldn't find any evidence to support this. If you know of a book it's based on, let me know in the comments.
In the end, I’m not gonna get into talks about the political and social commentary present in this game, just that it was very well implemented, which I've seen only done in a handful of video games like ever.
Not gonna go on any longer, I could sing praises for great games infinitely, but I think this is enough. This game really needs to be appreciated by more people. It is a very well-done work of science fiction, and I highly recommend it, especially to science fiction readers and fans. Hair to Hair.

I don't write a lot of reviews as I'm not good with words, so I prefer to save them for games that truly left an impression on me. This is an amazing sci-fi narrative with great theming, plot, and writing, all while presented in an artfully scrambled mess of a timeline. Its varied and creative use of perspectives (both visually and narratively) made the experience incredibly enjoyable. My only criticism is that the pacing starts dragging a little around the halfway point, but otherwise, this goes on my list of top games!
This game lived up to the hype. Story was engaging and i was drawn in. Linear in progression but it doesn’t feel like it. Looks and runs beautiful on the Steam Deck. Honestly a 10/10

Incredible story, never though a VN-type game would wow me so hard. At first it seemed to be a visual novel with limited interaction  [i](gameplay stays minimal more as just a vehicle to pushing along the story but i dont mind as the story is the star here. On top of that the environments were pretty interesting to explore and the music was amazing)[/i]  became extremely engrossing as more elements were introduced.

1000xResist was successful in making me actually care about characters through such a unique original story that deeply dives into a wide range of subjects, scifi, politics, guilt, regret, love, friendship, forgiveness, and truth . I'd recommend it to anyone who wants to feel something (heartbreak, hope, sadness, rage, this really has it all) and experience a story that sits with you afterward. Truly one of those rare games that you ponder for years to come after. Definitely one of my favorite games ive played

1000xRESIST is a narrative focused sci-fi adventure game. You walk around, talk to characters and solve not too difficult dialog puzzles and point to point 3D space navigation puzzles. The less I say, the better. If you like Life is Strange you should play this. If you like the cosmic horror of Mass Effect, you should play this. If you're just a fan of sci-fi or dramatic fiction in general, this is a must play. This is the kind of thing that could have been a show on Toonami back in the day, perhaps if REBOOT and Evangelion were smashed together at CERN's LHC.

A little bit slow in the beginning, but once you get past chapter 3 it's hard to put this game down. Each chapter leaves you with questions to be answered by the next. It's like playing a really good book. Visually, I normally dislike heavy chromatic aberration in games but the way it's implemented here feels immersive. Like the player is living a blurry mystery. Not super complex or hard to run at all but if you have a decent monitor/OLED like myself it's actually a really pretty game.

Wonderful game with an excellent written story, packaged with a great soundtrack, voice cast and presentation even in spite of its smaller developmental scale. Think this one will stick with me for a long while.

Hair to hair, and all that jazz.

Cool story-driven game with very light gameplay elements like conversation choices, exploration, and platforming. The focus is the narrative and the characters, which are well done and keep you wanting more: every day, I couldn’t wait till the next time I got to play and find out more about this strange world. Best to play it blind! 

The game is more thriller than horror, which was something I was worried about when I started. There are a few areas you explore that have an unsettling atmosphere, but it’s not a scary game per se, and there aren’t any jump scares in those areas. There are a couple jump scares in the cut scenes, but nothing all that bad (and I hate jump scares).

Great story game. Asks a lot of difficult questions and touches on a lot of subjects that have a lot of connections with events that are still unfolding after it's release in 2024. Starts slow and confused me a lot but really grasped me a few chapters in once I could see all the pieces being shuffled while other pieces were added. Definitely recommend to all of my sisters resisting through difficult times.

Hekki grace.

This one defied all expectations. I'm not a fan of visual novel games, cryptic "Kojima-like" storytelling, or the art style of the trailer. I only played this game on a recommendation, and wow, I'm glad I did. The story is beautiful, gut-punching, thrilling, and (for me, at least) evocative.

I personally struggle with understanding my place in the world, with generational trauma, with learning how to cope with loss, change, and mortality. Increasingly rare it is to find media that feels like it meets me where I am, including all my struggles, without being too direct or prescriptive. Too often these days am I braced for a story that takes no risks, as it must be safe and digestible to draw as wide of an audience as possible. 1000XResists shattered my guard within hours with an elegantly told story of humanity, willing to let you get lost in its oddities and turbulence. I hope this story sticks with me for a while, but we don't always get to choose what we keep and leave behind. 

I'm not much of a "review guy". For a visual novel, I hope its clear that I think the story is pretty frickin good, and that makes this a solid two thumbs up from me. If I had to balance out the high praise, I'd say some of the voice actors felt...unenthusiastic. Also, running around the orchard was disorienting (though this might have been because I have "talk to everybody" syndrome, YMMV).

This is a great and unique science fiction story that really sets itself apart in a gaming landscape where science fiction themes usually mean you are mowing down waves of generic aliens. Be warned there isn't much actual gameplay here but the story absolutely worth the time and effort.

I got this game as a gift from a friend, and I'm glad I did.
This game is a wonderful telling of the harm that unresolved trauma and deceit can do throughout generations. It tells a story of resistance against those who are an integral part of the continued suffering of those living under their rule. 1000xResist is emotional and almost real, but in a sci-fi type of way.

Hekki Grace.

[h2]Quck and fast and nonsense review.[/h2]
I really enjoyed this, a lot of situations, a lot of story, keeps you guessing but entrances you in it's story and atmosphere. From wishint for an outcome to just experiencing and getting confused in it's exposition. Everytime you think it's over it delivers more, a twist, a change.

Core chapters have unique gameplay and story elements, it's always fresh, and takes you to weird places. A lot of different angles, a lot of ways to convey the game. Multiple stories exposed, multiple persons involved, this is not just about you it's about an entanglement of destinies.
Way longer than I was expecting but I am not complaning.

I think the multitude of genres is something amist of Nier Automata, without playing it, but I know Nier really goes to gender(game) bending mechanics.
While the soundtrack is nothing amazing and grandeur, it's soundtrack and audio manages to engross you more into the story being exactly what is needed.

[h3]NEGATIVE'S[/h3]
- The Game Hub - Really hard to navigate and figure it out, even with the map. Having to visit it and navigate it each intermission was a dread. I know, I could just speed rush to the missions, but I wanted to explore everything and talk to everyone. It get's more and more harder to figure out who you talked towards the end (bomb part), where everyone appears on the compas as interactables, even if you spoke with them.
- The Final Choice - There is nothing innerent wrong with the choices, they are ok. But I would have loved that me interacting with everyone in a certain way along the game will shape the ending. I know after going back and forth, that most of the choices in dialogue in the game are reduntant, and the core discussions and progression is already preset by the game and you don't change anything, but, at the final ending I would have wished for the "Sometimes you don't fit in the backpack" to mean more. [spoiler]Like I would have wished that you could not choose to keep the light on for everyone, and when you went to the submit choices, for the Keeper to say, sorry, but not everyone fits in the backpack.  You still get the truth of the declaration if you choose that choice as an ending, and you discover that indeed is the truth, will have been even greater that you wouldn't be allowed to do that.[/spoiler]

[h2]This game is really unique, and I hope it will remain in my head for a while. A lot of hard messages, and messages that should stick in your head are included, it's about you, who you are, what you believe, sacrifices, it's about what family means, your friends, your sisters/brothers, it's not always about you and a lot of heavy events that happened in real life, including the Hong Kong 2019-2020 Protests and the 2020-2023 Covid 19 Pandemic.[/h2]

I am not sure if I ever played this type of games before, but this was really good. Maybe, what Remains of Edith Finch?

Thank to this video https://youtu.be/zxAmzJ4N0PA?si=_0HJ2dVgHqV7gVrl on Youtube, I am able to get a better understanding of my game after my first playthrough. 

It doesn't matter to me that it is not the most visually stunning, because the game made it up with the extremely good and in-character voice acting and meticulous story-writing. This game is full of soul, humane and thought-provoking, that every scene never failed to make me think. 

Spoilers alert! (I guess)




One thing I really liked about the game was the freedom to choose our own endings, although only two of them are the real ending. Nonetheless, it served as a perfect recap and reiteration of the themes raised throughout the game, and putting us in the shoes of the characters to really feel their pain and conflict. Characters might make questionable or unforgettable decisions, but we ought to understand that all of us are shaped by our pasts, and our pasts are not easy and unable to be observed by outsiders.

We cannot and shall not erase memories, what is done will always remain, and serve as great teachings for the civilization and reminders of our identities. However, we have the choice to unburden ourselves and those around us by choosing to carry on the good and leave out the bad, and also show mercy to the broken parts of the world and within us, as these are the ways to get us out of the loop of self-sabotaging with mistakes of the pasts. 

The dialogues were very poetic and some may find it tough to follow at times, but everything will make sense if you'd watch a recap on Youtube and then discover how beautiful and painful the story actually is. Every characters have that bit of fragileness and tenderness no matter their appearance, I think that is why I find the game so endearing while travelling all across memories and unravelling secrets. The atmospheric factor was really on-point as well, with creative angles and world-building that matches the fleeting state of the communions, enhancing the story-telling aspect of the game.

Thank you devs for this game, looking forward for more. Hekki Grace!

 PLAY 1000XRESIST
As far as walking simulators ago, this is a considerable cut above the rest. This game's story will stay with me for a long, long time, and I can't think of a greater compliment than that.

1000xRESIST is a game with a completely new idea, or at least a very original one. It tells a story where we dive into the memories of a "goddess", a girl who was immune to a certain virus and eventually became the last of humanity, creating clones and assigning each of them specific roles.

The game explores themes such as existentialism and escapism, the anxiety caused by personal struggles, family bonds, relationships, and even the colors and the weight and meaning behind our words and actions. In this story there is no villain and there is no hero. They are all people and beings trying their best to escape a false illusion, trying to escape the truth and face it in an idealized way, trying to be free, while in reality they are slaves to a completely hierarchical society.

I loved this game, especially the parts involving Iris and her family. The father is the GOAT.

A tremendously beautiful narrative that deserves as much praise as I can give. I went into it entirely blind, and it managed to impress me at every turn. It doesn't feel like a single line of dialogue is wasted, and there wasn't any point throughout the whole experience where I didn't feel invested. The world and characters were so well crafted that I always had a desire to learn more. It's safe to say I don't think there are many games that have resonated with me in the way this one has. I know this is one experience that will remain with me for a long time to come. I truly can't recommend it enough.

Truly a game to persist in the mind for years after you play it. This game is beautiful, poetic, philosophical, honest, and brutal. It's a game about generational trauma, about mothers and daughters, told in a metaphoric way as a science-fiction thriller. You find a piece of yourself within this game as it talks about a home you dearly miss but cannot return to, as it talks about a change that comes too sudden and violent, as it talks about trauma passed down in generations with the wound that persists, as it talks about losing your identity and feeling detached from where you come from, as it talks about the horrors of queer relationships in an unforgiving society, and as it talks about mothers who love in their own ways but cannot help hurting. I'm sure this one hit way too close to home for people who grew up in immigrant families, but the themes it talks about are everybody's for the taking and understanding.

I can go on for hours writing essays about how brilliant this game is, but it is an experience that everyone should live by themselves. It's worth every cent you will pay for it, so buy it with ease and drink up every second of the most profound 10 (or so) hours of your life you're about the spend.

I was initially very hesitant to play this on account of the shoddy-looking production, and even during the first hour I  found myself disoriented. But after the first chapter? I was all-in. Even in retrospect I'm still stunned at how quickly this managed to engross me. I normally despise walking simulators and visual novels. 1000xResist not only makes full use of the video game medium in its visual style, it understands that major moments have to be anchored and driven by interaction.

And I cannot say enough good things about the story. Anybody with a taste for dreamlike environments (think David Lynch, Lain, and especially Evangelion/End of Evangelion) would love this. As others have noted, it'd take forever to unpack everything the game communicates about generational trauma, self-hatred, and diaspora communities. What seems like a basic setting is fleshed out to an incredible degree. The game's script surprised me on a nearly hourly basis and continued to do so until the ending. It's all peak.

If you have any interest at all, play it. We need more stories like this, capable of expressing real issues and science fiction fun in equal measure.

1000xRESIST is a dense, deeply layered narrative that continues to grow on me the longer I sit with it. I initially felt some emotional distance, but after reflecting and digging into the story more, I realized just how much it rewards close attention and patience… there are so many small details, motifs, and quiet revelations that are easy to miss on a first pass. This is a game that almost invites rereading, or replaying, not to change outcomes but to deepen understanding. Its exploration of memory, generational trauma, and inherited obligation is careful and precise, and the writing is confident enough to trust silence, repetition, and implication. What struck me most is how the emotional weight doesn’t always land immediately, but accumulates over time, settling in slowly and thoughtfully. It’s a remarkably well-written story, and one that feels more touching and resonant the further away from it I get.

There is very little "gamey" about 1000xResist. It's closer to a walking/interaction simulator along the lines of What Remains of Edith Finch, Firewatch and games of that ilk. The games focuses on 2 main protagonists of the same lineage over the course of an indeterminate amount of time with a really unique and uniquely both hopeful and despairing setting. The story is what you come for rather than the gameplay, and the story does suck you in. Much of it is down to how much you want to explore, and it is quite linear, though with one or two important choices toward the end. It is quite slow in pace, giving you time to think and that may be offputting for some, but I genuinely enjoyed it and would recommend it to anyone wanting to get lost in a different, unique narrative setting.

It is quite simply the best Formula 1 simulation experience.

It is truly sad to see that sales have not lived up to expectations to the point that the developer has decided to stop its contract with Formula 1 and abandon this license which had huge potential. Fans of this game continue to prove its worth, given the number of diverse and varied mods available on dedicated websites, which continue to be updated even today...

Even after EA's major update of F1 25 a few months ago, in order to adopt the codes of the F1 Manager license, the result does not live up to what Frontier was able to offer us over three years. F1 25 is an arcade game while F1 Manager 2024 is a simulation taken to its peak.

Make your choice: If you prefer management and technical aspects, try F1 Manager. If you're more into arcade driving that's open to everyone, F1 25 is for you.

Thanks to the developers for their work on this license. Even though there are no plans for a reboot at this time, i sincerely hope there will be one during the new era of regulations. There is still so much to do with this game...

I have been playing this game for 100 Hours on steam and for over 400 hours on xbox. In my opinion this is the best F1 game you can get and it is well worth its price. The game is very good even without mods but there are some issues regarding the progression in the vanilla game as it gets way too easy after 2025 even on hard mode, i had do develop the car worse on purpose. Anyways i had great fun. If you decide to mod the game, using the data base editor is a must as you can make the ai much more competitive. I personally had the best experience using the extra hard research boost and ultralightweight parts mod in the database editor. Other mods i reccomend are visual mods such as liveries to switch things up from time to time. And also a must is the Realistic Racing mod as it makes overtaking much harder like in real life.

Fun and immersive. Not arcade like but also not too super in depth nor a steep learning curve. It stays technical while offering endless playability. Only downsides are it's a little vague in letting you know certain strategies and some things like tire temperature I'm sure have actual effects towards gameplay but it's hard to know exactly what they are and how much of it you can ignore. Hard mode is definitely hard mode. Would absolutely recommend for hard core race fans like myself. Newer fans might not be entirely too excited about the gameplay but I wouldnt consider it a turn off. Worth a solid try at the very least. 8.8/10

[b]F1 Manager 2024[/b] is the definitive F1 management sim as of its release — deep, methodical and rewarding for players who enjoy systems design, resource allocation and high-stakes decision making. It’s best described as a robust evolutionary step rather than a radical reinvention: if you loved the 2023 entry you’ll find more depth and meaningful additions (notably Create-A-Team and expanded customisation), and if you’re new to the series it’s an accessible—albeit complex—gateway into motorsport management. Expect the occasional simulation quirk, but also expect that [i]Frontier[/i] will continue to refine the game through updates.

[b]F1 Manager 2024[/b] has been active post-launch: bug fixes, balance changes and quality-of-life patches show a willingness to iterate. The community on [i]Steam[/i] and subreddit threads has been a productive source of feedback and tactics guides; [i]Frontier[/i]’s patch notes reflect that feedback loop. Continued updates are likely to further refine edge cases in AI and race simulation.

The most complete F1 management sim to date.

If you were waiting for this game to be "fixed" before buying, 2026 is the time. After a year of updates, the "Create A Team" mode is finally as deep as we wanted it to be. You’re no longer just picking a livery; you’re managing engine suppliers, affiliate drivers, and a brand-new Mentality System that actually makes your staff feel like people rather than just stats.

The Standout Features:

Create A Team: Starting as the 11th team on the grid and fighting up from the back is the most rewarding way to play.

Season Customization: The ability to shuffle the calendar and move drivers between other teams keeps the world feeling fresh in Season 5 and beyond.

Mechanical Failures: Unlike previous years, you actually have to worry about the car's reliability, making race strategy much more dynamic.

The Cons:

AI Logic: While improved, the AI can still make some questionable pit-stop choices during rain.

Visual Damage: The 3D race engine is beautiful, but car damage still feels a bit "light" and unrealistic visually.

Verdict: If you own 2022 or 2023, the 2024 version is a significant enough leap (especially with the recent v1.11 customization update) to justify the upgrade.

It is quite simply the best Formula 1 simulation experience.

It is truly sad to see that sales have not lived up to expectations to the point that the developer has decided to stop its contract with Formula 1 and abandon this license which had huge potential. Fans of this game continue to prove its worth, given the number of diverse and varied mods available on dedicated websites, which continue to be updated even today...

Even after EA's major update of F1 25 a few months ago, in order to adopt the codes of the F1 Manager license, the result does not live up to what Frontier was able to offer us over three years. F1 25 is an arcade game while F1 Manager 2024 is a simulation taken to its peak.

Make your choice: If you prefer management and technical aspects, try F1 Manager. If you're more into arcade driving that's open to everyone, F1 25 is for you.

Given this seems to be the end of the line for this series, this is for the sure the best its ever been... and, most likely, the way it should've been from the first entry. The graphics aren't particularly impressive, no matter what settings you set, but the parts that you'll care about, particularly the cars, look amazing. The custom team feature more or less makes this game for me- you truly feel like you're in charge of your own legacy when you're not just stapling yourself into an existing team.

Its not perfect though, and it can be easy to figure out how to move forward with time and progress. I suggest playing it for a while without looking anything up, and gradually do so as you move along. I suggest moving forward with the difficulty settings on new game start to shake things up if you think its getting too easy. There are some nitpicks, like drivers losing helmets to generic ones upon leaving their 2024 teams (none in the case of the F2 drivers) which is something the EA F1 game has a better approach too, just allowing them to keep their helmet always even despite some incongruity. The lack of choice on these sorts of minor things don't matter to most games, but for these sorts of management fantasy trips, they become important.

You have to really want what this game has to offer, to want this game- a broader audience would find it annoying.

Anyways i had great fun. If you decide to mod the game, using the data base editor is a must as you can make the ai much more competitive. I personally had the best experience using the extra hard research boost and ultralightweight parts mod in the database editor. Other mods i reccomend are visual mods such as liveries to switch things up from time to time. And also a must is the Realistic Racing mod as it makes overtaking much harder like in real life.

A fantastic strategy roguelike that's like a weird cross between Shogi, One Finger Death Punch and Slay the Spire.

The gameplay is wonderful, as is the music. You move your character left and right along tiles, while enemies spawn. You have 'cards' you can play in methodical, strategic, turn-based combat which quickly becomes addictive.

I love the slow pace (you can take as long as you wish planning your moves) and watching a chain of attacks combo-kill multiple enemies is incredibly satisfying.

As a huge fan of roguelikes, I love how difficult it is and the permadeath nature. Each failed run lets you buy new tiles to use in the next run, plus there are unlockable characters which keep things interesting.

Another wonderful indie gem that I highly recommend.

[h1]Short description[/h1][hr][/hr]
A turn-based roguelike where you acquire abilities during the run and use them to perform spectacular combinations. For instance, toss enemies into each other's attack or feel like a Neo by perfectly dodging arrow so it kills the enemy behind you. This game is not just about winning the run. It's about winning it perfectly in one way or another. Or at least make yourself feel cool.

[h1]Worth highlighting / What is unique[/h1][hr][/hr]
* Overall game loop idea. It's very unique. To be honest, “Fights in Tight Spaces was definitely a direct reference,” yet it has many differences and is unique enough :D
* Many opportunities to play perfectly. Here you can make almost any build, and with careful planning, avoid taking hits during the run as an example. Moreover, with different characters and abilities provided, you have lots of ways to play the game. There aren't many abilities, but the balance is top-notch. It allows you to play how you want, not how the developer wants
* Win perfectly > just win. For every hero, you not only have difficulty levels like almost any roguelike, but also 5 stamps with 2 tiers (normal and ultimate):
	* Win in less than 20 minutes
	* Win on day 7
	* Win in less than 800 turns
	* Win with 100 combo kills
	* Win with no hits during the run
These are insanely cool goals that would be highly appreciated by some players.

[h1] Overall rating 7/10[/h1][hr][/hr]
This game is good if you like completing achievements, playing perfectly, planning every move very carefully, or feeling like Neo. Sounds cool to you? Play it!
Even if you're not sure, you can give it a go. At least it's a fresh experience.

I personally didn't enjoy it. It's just not for me :D
Here you are always very strong and can win most runs with ease if you wouldn't hurry too much. I personally feel my strength and always hurry a lot.
Planning every move carefully to earn stamp is not for me; I'm not that patient.
Nevertheless, I'm not saying it's bad. It's not for me, but it's probably a good fit for you cause this game is good.

🏯 Shogun Showdown: Ajedrez samurái con esteroides  ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ (9/10)

Una joya indie que fusiona la estrategia táctica por turnos, elementos roguelike y la construcción de mazos de forma brillante. Combina la planificación cerebral de Into the Breach con el adictivo bucle de "una partida más" de Slay the Spire, pero en un plano 2D donde cada paso y cada giro pueden significar la diferencia entre una victoria perfecta o una muerte instantánea.

🧠 La Esencia: El posicionamiento absoluto. El juego se desarrolla en una línea 2D donde no solo importa dónde estás, sino hacia dónde miras. Debes "programar" tus ataques en una cola de acciones y prever los movimientos del enemigo varios turnos antes para salir ileso.

⚔️ Mecánicas: Combate rítmico y gestión de tiempos. Tus ataques tienen tiempos de preparación y enfriamiento (cooldowns), lo que te obliga a bailar entre los enemigos, empujándolos, intercambiando posiciones o aniquilándolos antes de que ejecuten su golpe.

🎨 Aspecto Técnico: Un pixel art encantador con animaciones fluidas y efectos de "gore" pixelado muy satisfactorios. La banda sonora acompaña perfectamente la tensión táctica sin volverse repetitiva.

⏱ Duración: Unas 10-15 horas para ver el final principal y desbloquear personajes básicos. Entre 25 y 40 horas para completar todos los desafíos, logros y desbloquear todo el contenido (Día 7, skins, etc.).

Lo mejor

✔️ El sistema de combate es justo, profundo y premia la inteligencia sobre los reflejos.
✔️ Gran variedad de personajes y habilidades ("tiles") que cambian radicalmente la estrategia de cada partida.
✔️ Es perfecto para sesiones cortas (20-30 min) o maratones largos gracias a su ritmo ágil.

A mejorar

➖ El desbloqueo de contenido nuevo (meta-progresión) puede sentirse un poco lento al principio.
➖ Algunos jefes pueden volverse repetitivos una vez descubres el "truco" para vencerlos.
➖ La curva de dificultad en los niveles más altos (Días avanzados) puede ser un muro frustrante para algunos.

I slept on this when it released and only recently found it; I wasn't expecting *too* much, but honestly this game is outstanding.

A key component of what are now called roguelike games is that you should learn by doing, and 95% of the time you die, you should know what you did wrong and understand how to've prevented it. This.. is not really the case for many of them -- but it is for Shogun Showdown. I always see when I've misplayed something that will get me hit in a few turns, and each time I do just a little better, 

The nature of how the progression is structured is about standard for what you expect for a 'roguelike' of this era (I am never going to excuse the misuse of this term), which is to say you accrue meta-currency and use that to unlock more tiles, the game's term for weapons and repositioning abilities. I would say that they are all extremely close to one another in terms of power level; each tile has three to four qualities: its cooldown, its damage, and its number of expansion slots. There is also the option for an enchantment on most tiles; these four qualities are used to balance tiles in their beginning state, and you only modify them for the duration of the run while you're making the run (which is, of course, a large part of the game's strategy).

You are given a random upgrade shrine for the expansion slots CD, an enchantment, higher damage, etc) and a choice of two tiles every stage, with the ability to select which shop you'd like to go to in-between stages, each of which has two traits: the upgrade shrine that's in there for your tiles, and the type of *traits* it sells, which are modifiers to you and your playstyle for that run, which you cannot get elsewhere.

There's five or six characters, each of which has three starting loadouts you can unlock (two fixed, one that's random), and each of which has a special movement type ability.

This game is really, really good. I honestly cannot stop playing it; it's very satisfying.

Also pulling out combos is super satisfying. 

I don't usually slip into hyperbolics but this is truly a fantastic game in every aspect.

ENG Review – Shogun Showdown

Shogun Showdown is a fantastic tactical game where every move matters and one bad turn can end an entire run. The weapon card system and its upgrades are extremely satisfying — especially when luck finally gives you the perfect setup.
The music is also a pleasure to listen to — it perfectly supports the atmosphere and gives even the most stressful fights a smooth, immersive flow.
At the same time, the game heavily relies on RNG: getting a good weapon card or the right upgrade can completely change your run… or completely ruin it.
Every unlocked character forces you to rethink and replan your entire run, keeping the gameplay fresh and preventing it from becoming repetitive.
Day 6 and Day 7 modes are on a whole different level — this is not just a challenge, it’s a titanic nightmare that tests not only your strategy, but also your mental endurance. Brutal, unforgiving, yet strangely addictive.
If you enjoy tactical games with high risk and huge rewards for perfect decision-making, Shogun Showdown will keep you hooked for a long time.

PL Recenzja – Shogun Showdown

Shogun Showdown to świetna taktyczna gra, w której każdy ruch ma znaczenie, a jedna źle zaplanowana tura potrafi zakończyć cały run. System kart broni i ich ulepszeń daje mnóstwo satysfakcji — szczególnie wtedy, gdy los w końcu obdarzy nas idealnym zestawem.
Gra jest też bardzo przyjemna dla ucha — muzyka idealnie buduje klimat i sprawia, że nawet najbardziej stresujące walki mają swój rytm i flow.
Jednocześnie rozgrywka mocno opiera się na szczęściu: trafienie dobrej karty broni albo kluczowego ulepszenia potrafi całkowicie odmienić przebieg rozgrywki… albo ją pogrzebać.
Każda odblokowana postać zmusza do zupełnie nowego planowania runa, dzięki czemu gra długo pozostaje świeża i nie pozwala wpaść w jeden utarty schemat.
Tryby Dnia 6 i 7 to już zupełnie inna liga — to nie jest zwykłe wyzwanie, to tytaniczny koszmar, który testuje nie tylko strategię, ale też odporność psychiczną gracza. Brutalne, bezlitosne, ale jednocześnie dziwnie satysfakcjonujące.
Jeśli lubisz taktyczne gry z nutą ryzyka i ogromną nagrodą za perfekcyjne decyzje, Shogun Showdown potrafi wciągnąć na długie godziny.

The game is good. Charming and heart-warming. I have my nitpicks on the story and characters but it still connects pretty well, with great emotional moments. There is a decent amount of choices to make. Some are simply flavour text, but there are quite a bunch that push you to the 4 endings. I obviously won't spoil anything, but be assured: there are NO arbitrary dating sim-esque choices here. Like: "Aw man, I chose the chocolate croissant instead of the Strawberry cake gift, now I'm gonna get a **** ending now..."

Man... in fact, some choices were really difficult on the first playthrough. Maybe its the fact that the protagonist is a wet rag at times (for a reason, mind you), or maybe I'm stuck between trying to be nice or honest in a world of dinosaurs that can rip you a new one... some choices I had to really sit and think about for 10 minutes. It is cool that there isn't obvious "be evil" or "be good" choices. Most of the choices in the game have plausible or realistic reasoning for choosing (which make getting the bad endings more... real?). I recommend you just do what you would naturally do on your first playthrough, and stick to them. The endings too... hoo boy, they are some heavy hitters... it must be rough to get the best ending first, only to work your way downwards to the more...harsh ones.

The soundtrack... has some real sublime moments. There's an official Soundtrack and a "side" collection of royalty free tracks, and they fit the moments too. I tend to listen to it on my phone every so often. I'd love to learn the theme on piano someday.

I do like the characters (there aren't that many, which is a plus imo, I tend to lose track of who's who), Damian is the GOAT. Mia sucks. Everyone is designed in a pleasing, cute or tasteful way. No weird fetish stuff. In fact, despite 2-3 suggestive images, there is no "adult" content in this game. So if that disappoints you, well... it ain't no Katawa Shoujo I'm afraid (no offense of course, that game is also good). It remains a personal story of two characters, Inco and Olivia, and the story focuses more on the latter. So, no meandering filler plots or boring side-episodes; everything feels like it has a good place in the narrative, and neatly paced for the most part (again, personal nitpicks for certain moments aside). I encourage getting all the 4 endings too, as they show more detail to the characters, how they've grown (or not...). It's a pretty short game, all things considered, so it's a tighter experience that stuck itself in my brain for ages. So, I think the devs accomplished what they wanted there.

Side note:
After I finished Wani, I've played Cavemanon's other work, Snoot Game. I can confidently say its my favourite of the two. Don't get me wrong though, Wani has enough qualities to justify its own purchase--but I can understand why some fans of Snoot Game were a tad bit disappointed. I went to their website blog and it's stated they had quite the rocky development with Wani so it makes sense. Speaking of their blog...

Cavemanon seem to know a thing or three about game development: their behind-the-scenes on Snoot Game... starting as a group on 4chan, making a parody game--turning into a passion project is inspiring stuff (quite a few good games seem to come from there, hm?). Their next game in development "Exit 665" looks awesome, but it is much different and bigger game by the looks of things. Best of luck to them on tackling a genre like RPG's especially when it will inevitably be compared to Goliaths like Clair Obscur or Deltarune. I think their style is different enough to distinguish it as it's own thing but--bah, I'm no expert. I'm sure they know what they are doing.

I went into this game with no prior visual novel experiences outside of Snoot Game (Same Developers) and I definitely wasn't disappointed. This is 100% a game you should go into blind, trust me, it will hit much better with little to no knowledge of what is to come :D

The characters are pretty fleshed out and work well, you get a proper understanding of what kind of role certain characters play out and watching the interactions, relationships, and overall development of these characters play out was enough to get me to get through the game. The choices you make as Inco to try to go for a good ending (ending 4) aren't always obvious but I think that helps out the game in its own way, you don't just go through everyday life knowing how to go about everything perfectly, right? The buildup and the interactions involved in the eventual relationship between Olivia and Inco are very heartwarming, you can tell just how much they care for one another (The level of care varying upon which ending route you go on with). Alongside all the upsides, just want to mention it is not easy for a game to make me cry (or even really tear up for that matter) but the dance scene in ending 4 was enough to break through me with just how beautiful the build up to the scene is :,)

As for negatives: With every story comes the parts that make you question your retention skills, just like Snoot Game, this games build up is quite slow and takes a while for it to get good/engaging. While this one might just be personal preference, I would've loved to see some kind of bonus chapters where you get a view from the other characters following the events of Ending 4 just like Snoot Game did, but with just how much this game was in development hell, it is just something you have to fill the blanks in for yourself. There is also the controversy surrounding the developer team (Whichever one of many), so this is a game you have to apply the "separate the art from the artist" mentality to.

I remember seeing this game a while back and watched various videos discussing it as well as how it was based off of Snoot Game which was based off of Goodbye Volcano High. I took the discounted deal and gave it a shot since I never play visual novel games before. What followed was one of the most heart-wrenching and eye-opening experiences I ever had from a game. 
  
  I expected a romance visual novel but I was met with a game that felt genuine. I saw real emotions and love blossom onto my screen. The same mouth which laughed along and sung praises of a character was the same mouth which cursed and gnashed venom. I wept tears of happiness and tears of sorrow.

  Even throughout the roller-coaster of emotions I experienced, I understood their reasons and process for doing so. The characters have depth and you feel what they are going through and understand why the behave the way they do. I understand the burning desire to be independent, the useless struggle to fix what can not be fixed only to make everything worse, and the need to hide what should never be revealed from the world even at the expense of throwing others under the bus.

  This game tackles something I see many relationships struggle throughout my life and that is struggle. The individual does have struggle from the past, in the future, as well as the upcoming future. When a relationship comes then that relationship will come with its own struggles pertaining to what is carried over from the past, what is held down in the future, and what hangs above for the future. The game handles struggle beautifully as it brings forth the complexity that is dating as well as outcomes that come with it if struggle is not handled properly.

  Once I completed the whole game I am left with ambiguity in my life, why did such a game, a scaly romance visual novel, of all things make me question my life choices? This game humbled me, I took advantage of what I had, I begged Christ for more and cursed him if I did not get it. I looked upon others with jealousy with what they had not knowing I to have what others prayed for. I needed to revaluate myself, to understand my outlook on life and the decisions I made to get to where I am at. 

  I am blessed to have had experienced, even in a game, the complexity and struggle of genuine love. I pray for everyone that they may experience such love and overcome the hardships that come with it. I pray even my enemies may find this love.

Do I really wanna leave art behind in high school, or am I truly passionate enough to take it with me to college. A game has not made me think so in depth about myself in a long time. Seeing all the endings really showed me how a person can change based on various situations. Liked this game a lot and I am a little sad that I have done everything,  but would recommend.

Man... in fact, some choices were really difficult on the first playthrough. Maybe its the fact that the protagonist is a wet rag at times (for a reason, mind you), or maybe I'm stuck between trying to be nice or honest in a world of dinosaurs that can rip you a new one... some choices I had to really sit and think about for 10 minutes. It is cool that there isn't obvious "be evil" or "be good" choices. Most of the choices in the game have plausible or realistic reasoning for choosing (which make getting the bad endings more... real?). I recommend you just do what you would naturally do on your first playthrough, and stick to them. The endings too... hoo boy, they are some heavy hitters... it must be rough to get the best ending first, only to work your way downwards to the more...harsh ones.

The soundtrack... has some real sublime moments. There's an official Soundtrack and a "side" collection of royalty free tracks, and they fit the moments too. I tend to listen to it on my phone every so often. I'd love to learn the theme on piano someday.

I do like the characters (there aren't that many, which is a plus imo, I tend to lose track of who's who), Damian is the GOAT. Mia sucks. Everyone is designed in a pleasing, cute or tasteful way. No weird fetish stuff. In fact, despite 2-3 suggestive images, there is no "adult" content in this game. So if that disappoints you, well... it ain't no Katawa Shoujo I'm afraid (no offense of course, that game is also good). It remains a personal story of two characters, Inco and Olivia, and the story focuses more on the latter. So, no meandering filler plots or boring side-episodes; everything feels like it has a good place in the narrative, and neatly paced for the most part (again, personal nitpicks for certain moments aside). I encourage getting all the 4 endings too, as they show more detail to the characters, how they've grown (or not...). It's a pretty short game, all things considered, so it's a tighter experience that stuck itself in my brain for ages. So, I think the devs accomplished what they wanted there.

Side note:
After I finished Wani, I've played Cavemanon's other work, Snoot Game. I can confidently say its my favourite of the two. Don't get me wrong though, Wani has enough qualities to justify its own purchase--but I can understand why some fans of Snoot Game were a tad bit disappointed. I went to their website blog and it's stated they had quite the rocky development with Wani so it makes sense. Speaking of their blog...

Cavemanon seem to know a thing or three about game development: their behind-the-scenes on Snoot Game... starting as a group on 4chan, making a parody game--turning into a passion project is inspiring stuff (quite a few good games seem to come from there, hm?). Their next game in development "Exit 665" looks awesome, but it is much different and bigger game by the looks of things. Best of luck to them on tackling a genre like RPG's especially when it will inevitably be compared to Goliaths like Clair Obscur or Deltarune. I think their style is different enough to distinguish it as it's own thing but--bah, I'm no expert. I'm sure they know what they are doing.

Fan rambling aside, to put it simply: Gator love story good. Me wait for next game. Dino love human. Human love dino. Life happy. No. Life not happy for long. Bad time will come. So, need to be good to be strong. Must change to be good. Hard journey. Journey to good, not happy. Sometimes cry.

Earlier this month I had posted a negative review for the game that I am now rescinding. 
On my first playthrough I had gotten Ending 1 and at the time it felt like being shot in the face by the game. I did everything I would do in real life and was faced with the worst possible outcome. Seeing characters that I cared about turn into the worst versions of themselves, especially Inco, made me feel awful. I thought I had made enough correct choices to at least get a decent ending, but no. Everything falls apart because of a few bad decisions. I was filled with loneliness and guilt. I wanted to experience the life changing story that many had lauded the game for that I also missed out on in Snoot Game by getting Ending 1 as well. At the time, I didn't think I would go back to the game, but after a few days of it sticking to the back of my mind, I decided to go back, and I'm glad I did.
I Wani Hug That Gator, assuming you get Ending 4, is a beautiful story. In many ways, I relate to Olivia's story. As an artist with a disability, I have felt many of the negative thoughts she does through the game. Always feeling like compliments towards you are shallow and insincere, never feeling like you'll amount to anything because of your condition, feeling like you're a nuisance to friends and family, hating accommodations made to your life even if they do benefit you because you hate the feeling of being treated differently. All things that I have experienced before. This level of relation is probably why Ending 1 hit me so hard - it was like the game is telling me that it was impossible to overcome and all that awaits me is despair. Going back and getting Ending 4 made me fall in love with the characters again and refilled me with hope. Seeing them all finally get their happy endings filled me with a sort of elation that I don't usually get from games or other narratives. I'm glad I got to learn more about Ben, who was previously one of my least favorite characters.
I still do think the structure of the game is a bit flawed in a few places. Some choices are kind of cryptic to figure out the correct choice for without a guide (or maybe I'm just too autistic to figure out the social cues you're supposed to see). The car ride to school with Damien and Liz before getting called to Scaler's office having the same positive dialogue no matter what ending you're locked onto does feel like the game is giving you a false sense of security that you're on the right path even if you're getting Ending 1. 
Despite all that, this is still a great game that I highly recommend. I am glad I decided to go back and hug that gator!

TLDR; the game is beautiful if you get the good ending and awful if you get the bad one.

The choices you make as Inco to try to go for a good ending (ending 4) aren't always obvious but I think that helps out the game in its own way, you don't just go through everyday life knowing how to go about everything perfectly, right? The buildup and the interactions involved in the eventual relationship between Olivia and Inco are very heartwarming, you can tell just how much they care for one another (The level of care varying upon which ending route you go on with). Alongside all the upsides, just want to mention it is not easy for a game to make me cry (or even really tear up for that matter) but the dance scene in ending 4 was enough to break through me with just how beautiful the build up to the scene is :,)

As for negatives: With every story comes the parts that make you question your retention skills, just like Snoot Game, this games build up is quite slow and takes a while for it to get good/engaging. While this one might just be personal preference, I would've loved to see some kind of bonus chapters where you get a view from the other characters following the events of Ending 4 just like Snoot Game did, but with just how much this game was in development hell, it is just something you have to fill the blanks in for yourself. There is also the controversy surrounding the developer team (Whichever one of many), so this is a game you have to apply the "separate the art from the artist" mentality to.

The core concept of the game is pretty simple: explore a prison and execute an escape within 21 days. While there is a time limit on your victory, the game is pretty lenient: time only passes when you actually perform an action, so you aren't punished if you have to backtrack or need some time to decide what you want to do. There's a lot of decision-making in each day: do you want to keep your head down and earn some money with prison jobs, build up relationships with other inmates, make a toothbrush shiv and stab that loser Diego?

The 46 fellow inmate NPCs add a lot of life to this game. They all have their own stories going on, which you can learn about by eavesdropping on their conversations or bonding with them. There's a good variety between them; some of the inmates are violent whackjobs, some had compelling reasons for the crimes they committed, and others are just kinda dumb. Each one also gives you a different bonus when you max out your relationship with them, which can add a bit of variety to each playthrough since you can't make friends with everyone.

My favorite feature, though, is that there are two different playable characters you can select which massively changes how the plot unfolds. As a panther, Bob has night vision, superior athletics, and combat training that Thomas the fox completely lacks at the start of the game. However, his mission has quite a few complications that limit his escape options and he's already joined a prison gang which prevents him from getting rewards from the other 2. Thomas the fox, meanwhile, starts relatively unsuited for prison life, but is able to do jobs for any of the 3 prison gangs and has some support from his ex-girlfriend Maggie who can mail over items and money to help you out. The best part is, the character you don't play as still shows up in the prison as an inmate, and you can see parts of their story unfold in the background.

The only real criticism I have is that once you've done a playthrough as both characters, the replayability is a bit awkward. A lot of the escape paths require you to do similar things, and the NPC schedules and events are the same from playthrough to playthrough. There is a lot of variability to the game, but a lot of it happens toward the end, so experiencing other endings if often best experienced by loading a few days earlier and just escaping through a different route you found. There's a NG+ mode, but it makes you so overpowered by retaining all of your skills that it mostly becomes busywork.

Overall, this was a great experience. I enjoyed the planning, character building, and exploration. There was enough challenge that finally finding an escape route felt triumphant, but it wasn't so strict that playing the game felt stressful.

Back to the Dawn surprised the hell out of me.
On paper it sounds weird: a prison sim mixed with detective work and an escape story, all starring animal characters. But it actually works. It’s inventive, it feels like it’s treading on new territory, and the overall concept is way more interesting than I expected going in.

The story is the best part of the game, hands down.
It’s genuinely great. Every character feels like they have a purpose, and the game does a good job making you care about them instead of just treating them like background NPCs.

Gameplay-wise though… it’s a mixed bag.
Combat is simple but easy to master, and the UI is clean and easy to understand. Performance was also flawless for me — no bugs, no crashes, nothing.

The problem is the pacing and the tedious busywork.
There’s a lot of repetitive back-and-forth that makes basic tasks feel like a slog, and the game takes way too long to get to its point. Some challenges also feel needlessly difficult for no clear reason.

And the biggest frustration: it’s way too easy to lock yourself out of things.
There are so many little steps you have to do just to move forward, and it can feel almost impossible to stay “ahead” without save scumming.

By around 30 hours, it honestly started to overstay its welcome for me. I had fun for a good chunk of it, but I hit a point where I was forcing myself to keep going — and I have zero interest in doing another playthrough.

Verdict: Only recommended on sale.
It’s creative, well-written, and worth experiencing… but it drags too long and gets too tedious to justify full price unless you’re absolutely sure it’s your kind of game. 6.7/10

---------------------------------------------------------Review------------------------------------------------------
[h1] I think this game deserves a solid 8/10 and No major spoiler ahead so don't worry about it [/h1]
I will rate the game on [b] my overall experience [/b]. I have only completed Thomas's story, will update this review after the Bob's route and perhaps the DLC character's route? 

[h3] Plot [/h3] 

The plot is well-written and the methods used to help present the plot are also good. I am talking about whether the escaping route feels right or not. By right I mean the steps needed to escape do not feel too forcefully put together. Obviously this game is not realistic [spoiler] and there are magical powers and come on you can play as a fox that walks on two legs [/spoiler], but i rarely notice a step that is way too wild for the plot. 
 
To better explain this since English isn't my main language, the game follows this simple rule, "If I need to get through a wall, I will need a pickaxe and so I will try to get myself a pickaxe." 
Although some routes are more tedious than the others. However, at least I didn't see any "If I need to get through a wall, I will need to build a rocket launcher or shrink myself by whatever the hell the devs are coming up with and the crafting recipes for those things are forced into other places of the game and the plot just feels FORCEFULLY PUT TOGETHER FOR THE SAKE OF ONE OR TWO STEPS." 
I hope this can explain it? 

[h3] Gameplay [/h3] 

The gameplay revolves around dice-roll that functions like skill check. Not all of them are a check (at least that's what I think, idk the correct terms here, so I apologize in advance) some of them only require you to roll TO a certain amount until you finish a quest, but those also require your skill to be higher so that you take less tries which essentially translate to less time and stamina used. 

Since it uses dice-roll, that means you can use your "luck" to beat some seemingly impossible checks, I passed a 3% check to open something that requires a bit of resources and strength because I have just enough stamina to try it. However, if you are a pessimistic person, then that whole sentence could be understood as "you can potentially fail a 97% check because life is just this unfair (or fair in some sense), even in video games." 

The fighting mechanics are decent, it is turn-based, you use your combat skills learnt through different ways to beat people up, those skills get better when your stats are higher, simple as that. 
Just to remind any people who wish to avoid combats, you will probably get into a combat or two depending on what kind of person you are trying to be in your playthrough. So try and learn some combat skills too. 

There are QTEs and mini-games, they don't require any dice-roll and they are generally fun as well. 

[h3] Summary? [/h3]

I love the gameplay and the plot, but it could be quite tedious and just don't expect to escape the prison on day 1 or 2, it takes time to escape so be patient, the main quest or some side quests aren't as straight forward so it COULD get annoying if you are trying to rush the game, but if you take the time to chat with the other prisoners, work inside the prison, flirt with the nurse, try to get back with your girlfriend, order some pizza, buy lottery, call the club and etc then you will probably enjoy the game a bit more.

Oh, did I also mention that you can gamble in this game? Stay away from gambling... [spoiler] unless you know how to cheat :) [/spoiler]

作为国产独立游戏中极少数的 CRPG 游戏，而且是以监狱为背景，《动物迷城》凭借扎实的文本功底、精致的美术呈现和非常有趣味性的系统设计，收获了不少玩家的青睐。深入体验后不难发现，这款游戏虽存在一些可优化的短板，但整体亮点突出，其展现出的制作水准和创新尝试，已然成为国产独立 CRPG 领域的一份亮眼答卷。

《动物迷城》的优点集中在文本、美术和基础玩法框架上。游戏的美术采用精致的像素风格，搭配现代光影效果，将监狱的压抑氛围与细节感拉满，无论是场景布局还是角色演出，都能感受到制作组的用心，让玩家获得较强的沉浸感。文本方面，游戏塑造了近50位各有特色的狱友角色，每个角色都有专属的故事线和任务，单看个体角色塑造十分鲜活，再加上主线围绕21天限时越狱展开，搭配多样的支线内容，故事本身具备足够的吸引力，基础的四维属性养成、制作系统和箱庭探索设计，也让玩法维度显得十分丰富，具备了高自由度 CRPG 的所有要素。

最令人遗憾的是，游戏的限时机制，较为严苛的结局达成方法与 CRPG 核心的自由度形成了不小的相悖。达成不同结局的难度不低，21天的限时要求玩家必须对每天的工作进行精准规划，这在不看攻略的第一周目体验中尤为困难，而当玩家进入二周目、三周目，想要慢慢探索支线、深入了解每个角色的故事时，又会被主线的时间压力紧紧牵着走，陷入两难境地。

明明游戏的支线任务设计精良，角色也足够有趣，但为了高效完成主线、成功越狱，最合理的玩法竟然是放弃这些支线探索，专注于刷取主线所需的道具和好感，这种玩法最优解与内容体验的矛盾，违背了 CRPG “自由探索、自主选择”的核心，让玩家陷入“想探索却不敢，想通关又觉得可惜”的别扭处境。

[b]- 8 / 10[/b]

Do I recommend this game?: The short answer is yes, the longer take is yes *BUT* I have a few gripes with it that I personally feel would really push the game from being pretty good, to really great and one of a kind.

My main issue is that there’s a MASSIVE and I mean HUGE waste of pressure, the time limit is there sure, but the game never *feels* like it pressures you enough to do anything. Why have the option to hide contraband is guards don’t check your cell?

Why when you go out and have to be back at certain hours do they magically teleport you back? There’s no sensation of ‘I really need to hurry back!’ It all feels a bit pointless in a way? 

Some bits in the story feel like they go absolutely nowhere. It would be really cool to get a sort of ‘what happened to them after’ type of really brief cutscene of all the prisoners or the ones you’ve max friended? 

Honestly I keep looking at games like the Escapists and comparing them. Even if they aren’t alike in a way I feel like having something similar. Being able to miss headcount and having the guards call a lockdown, having more events, *real* deadlines would really hammer that need for time in.

It feels bland in spots, there’s not much incentive (personally) to join one gang over the other, the rewards don’t feel good enough to prioritize with the negatives. 

Having more items, decor, events, stories and even real push would make the game even more of a gem in my opinion.

The core concept of the game is pretty simple: explore a prison and execute an escape within 21 days. While there is a time limit on your victory, the game is pretty lenient: time only passes when you actually perform an action, so you aren't punished if you have to backtrack or need some time to decide what you want to do. There's a lot of decision-making in each day: do you want to keep your head down and earn some money with prison jobs, build up relationships with other inmates, make a toothbrush shiv and stab that loser Diego?

The 46 fellow inmate NPCs add a lot of life to this game. They all have their own stories going on, which you can learn about by eavesdropping on their conversations or bonding with them. There's a good variety between them; some of the inmates are violent whackjobs, some had compelling reasons for the crimes they committed, and others are just kinda dumb. Each one also gives you a different bonus when you max out your relationship with them, which can add a bit of variety to each playthrough since you can't make friends with everyone.

My favorite feature, though, is that there are two different playable characters you can select which massively changes how the plot unfolds. As a panther, Bob has night vision, superior athletics, and combat training that Thomas the fox completely lacks at the start of the game. However, his mission has quite a few complications that limit his escape options and he's already joined a prison gang which prevents him from getting rewards from the other 2. Thomas the fox, meanwhile, starts relatively unsuited for prison life, but is able to do jobs for any of the 3 prison gangs and has some support from his ex-girlfriend Maggie who can mail over items and money to help you out. The best part is, the character you don't play as still shows up in the prison as an inmate, and you can see parts of their story unfold in the background.

The only real criticism I have is that once you've done a playthrough as both characters, the replayability is a bit awkward. A lot of the escape paths require you to do similar things, and the NPC schedules and events are the same from playthrough to playthrough. There is a lot of variability to the game, but a lot of it happens toward the end, so experiencing other endings if often best experienced by loading a few days earlier and just escaping through a different route you found. There's a NG+ mode, but it makes you so overpowered by retaining all of your skills that it mostly becomes busywork.

Overall, this was a great experience. I enjoyed the planning, character building, and exploration. There was enough challenge that finally finding an escape route felt triumphant, but it wasn't so strict that playing the game felt stressful.

Back to the Dawn surprised the hell out of me.
On paper it sounds weird: a prison sim mixed with detective work and an escape story, all starring animal characters. But it actually works. It’s inventive, it feels like it’s treading on new territory, and the overall concept is way more interesting than I expected going in.

The story is the best part of the game, hands down.
It’s genuinely great. Every character feels like they have a purpose, and the game does a good job making you care about them instead of just treating them like background NPCs.

Gameplay-wise though… it’s a mixed bag.
Combat is simple but easy to master, and the UI is clean and easy to understand. Performance was also flawless for me — no bugs, no crashes, nothing.

The problem is the pacing and the tedious busywork.
There’s a lot of repetitive back-and-forth that makes basic tasks feel like a slog, and the game takes way too long to get to its point. Some challenges also feel needlessly difficult for no clear reason.

And the biggest frustration: it’s way too easy to lock yourself out of things.
There are so many little steps you have to do just to move forward, and it can feel almost impossible to stay “ahead” without save scumming.

By around 30 hours, it honestly started to overstay its welcome for me. I had fun for a good chunk of it, but I hit a point where I was forcing myself to keep going — and I have zero interest in doing another playthrough.

Verdict: Only recommended on sale.
It’s creative, well-written, and worth experiencing… but it drags too long and gets too tedious to justify full price unless you’re absolutely sure it’s your kind of game. 6.7/10

Back to Dawn blends its CRPG mechanics and story in a really natural way. Whether you play as the framed reporter or the undercover cop, everything you do in the prison—surviving, talking to people, sneaking around, investigating—feeds directly into the narrative. It makes the world feel alive, and every action feels like it matters.

What impressed me most is that the story isn’t about simple good vs. evil. As the plot moves forward, you’re constantly faced with choices that don’t have a clear right answer. There’s rarely a purely “just” or “wicked” path—only that uneasy grey area in between. It’s in those moments of doubt and compromise that the game really shines, making the ideas of justice and morality feel genuinely complicated.

Overall, Back to Dawn is more than just a prison-escape RPG. It’s a game that uses its systems to tell a thoughtful story, challenges your sense of right and wrong, and makes every decision feel meaningful.
