---------------------------------------------------------Review------------------------------------------------------
[h1] I think this game deserves a solid 8/10 and No major spoiler ahead so don't worry about it [/h1]
I will rate the game on [b] my overall experience [/b]. I have only completed Thomas's story, will update this review after the Bob's route and perhaps the DLC character's route? 

[h3] Plot [/h3] 

The plot is well-written and the methods used to help present the plot are also good. I am talking about whether the escaping route feels right or not. By right I mean the steps needed to escape do not feel too forcefully put together. Obviously this game is not realistic [spoiler] and there are magical powers and come on you can play as a fox that walks on two legs [/spoiler], but i rarely notice a step that is way too wild for the plot. 
 
To better explain this since English isn't my main language, the game follows this simple rule, "If I need to get through a wall, I will need a pickaxe and so I will try to get myself a pickaxe." 
Although some routes are more tedious than the others. However, at least I didn't see any "If I need to get through a wall, I will need to build a rocket launcher or shrink myself by whatever the hell the devs are coming up with and the crafting recipes for those things are forced into other places of the game and the plot just feels FORCEFULLY PUT TOGETHER FOR THE SAKE OF ONE OR TWO STEPS." 
I hope this can explain it? 

[h3] Gameplay [/h3] 

The gameplay revolves around dice-roll that functions like skill check. Not all of them are a check (at least that's what I think, idk the correct terms here, so I apologize in advance) some of them only require you to roll TO a certain amount until you finish a quest, but those also require your skill to be higher so that you take less tries which essentially translate to less time and stamina used. 

Since it uses dice-roll, that means you can use your "luck" to beat some seemingly impossible checks, I passed a 3% check to open something that requires a bit of resources and strength because I have just enough stamina to try it. However, if you are a pessimistic person, then that whole sentence could be understood as "you can potentially fail a 97% check because life is just this unfair (or fair in some sense), even in video games." 

The fighting mechanics are decent, it is turn-based, you use your combat skills learnt through different ways to beat people up, those skills get better when your stats are higher, simple as that. 
Just to remind any people who wish to avoid combats, you will probably get into a combat or two depending on what kind of person you are trying to be in your playthrough. So try and learn some combat skills too. 

There are QTEs and mini-games, they don't require any dice-roll and they are generally fun as well. 

[h3] Summary? [/h3]

I love the gameplay and the plot, but it could be quite tedious and just don't expect to escape the prison on day 1 or 2, it takes time to escape so be patient, the main quest or some side quests aren't as straight forward so it COULD get annoying if you are trying to rush the game, but if you take the time to chat with the other prisoners, work inside the prison, flirt with the nurse, try to get back with your girlfriend, order some pizza, buy lottery, call the club and etc then you will probably enjoy the game a bit more.

Oh, did I also mention that you can gamble in this game? Stay away from gambling... [spoiler] unless you know how to cheat :) [/spoiler]

I’ve only played through one of the campaigns so far, yet I’ve already invested 53 hours into this game. This game is [i]detailed[/i]. I was worried the time limit would be a major stressor which would make or break the game for me, but thankfully, it wasn’t as brutal as I feared. I was able to befriend most prisoners and discover most escape routes on my first playthrough despite dilly-dallying for the first few days. And even if you fail to finish the game within the allotted 21 days, you’ll still be able to maintain your progress with New Game+, so you’ll have another shot at beating the game without losing the skills you’ve worked for.
The visuals are [i]gorgeous[/i]. I’ll be honest, when Square Enix revealed their HD-2D style, I wasn’t a fan. The sprites felt at odds with the world they inhabited, so when I saw this game also featured 2D sprites with 3D environments and lighting, I was pretty sure this game wouldn’t impress me either. ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥, was I wrong. The lighting is immaculate and the 2D characters feel right at home in the game’s 3D environments. Not to mention just how animated each character is. Seriously, their sprite sheets are [i]huge[/i]. This game has some of the most expressive and animated spritework you’ll see. When I saw Thomas shoot up in his chair with a furrowed expression of frustration at [spoiler]Angelo’s surprise visit[/spoiler], a subtle emotional cue which was animated specifically for this scene and not used [i]anywhere[/i] else, it really hit me just how much effort was put into making these characters move as if they’re alive.
And why stop there? Not only do these characters [i]look[/i] alive, but they truly [b]feel[/b] alive. This game has a [i]massive[/i] amount of dialogue. For each ingame day, inmates will be chatting with one another, all having unique conversations you can eavesdrop on. None of it is repeated (unless you start a new game, of course), and these conversations give you a glimpse into the going-ons of the characters and the prison they inhabit. You’re also able to befriend each prisoner and learn about their backstory, each of which is written with a great deal of detail. These aren’t just NPCs, they’re people living their own lives. And they’ve all grown on me, even the ones I didn’t click with at first.
Overall, this game is absolutely a hidden gem of a title. If you’ve read this far into my review, you’re probably considering giving this game a go. Believe me, it is worth it. Take the plunge, go in blind. Don’t be shy to explore everything the game has to offer. And the save/load system is your friend, as you can discover multiple escape routes in a single playthrough.
Gameplay is fun, story is interesting. Enjoyed every moment of this game.

This game consumed my life for around 2 weeks. I did 100% completion all achievements, did everything I wanted to do because it captivated me so much. The gameplay is kind of like Disco Elysium except if it was furry and actually good. The game's setting is a prison, and it's supposed to be miserable here. But the prison feels welcoming and closer to a home than a miserable prison. It's extremely lively and people have full lives here. If you dig in, you'll find out that some of the characters actually choose to stay in prison because it's comfortable. In some ways, I'm not even shocked at that. There's a ton to do and explore and you have an incredible amount of freedom to do it, which seems weird for prison. While you get a 21 day time limit, if you don't waste your time or get punished (segregation, injury, etc.), you should not have any trouble getting a normal ending within that time limit even on your first attempt. Part of the fun of the game is strategizing, using your time and resources efficiently, discovering plot points, and progressing the story. The other underrated aspects of the game to enjoy is the atmosphere - a bustling and social prison life or meditative and peaceful night time. Bonding with the other prisoners is pretty fun - I enjoyed most of the characters.

For the negatives, there are some bugs, especially in the combat section which seems to be the least tested. The combat mechanics in general are very weak. Because you can get away with not interacting with the combat throughout the majority of the game, you are never quite aware of how strong you are. You're forced to invest in combat without seeing exactly how strong you got because if you don't invest in it, then you'll be useless when the combat does matter. The puzzles are also poorly calibrated, usually in the way too easy range and other times poorly explained and simply requiring guesswork. The game is also way too repetitive if you're going for the 100% though that is completely optional, so I'll give them some slack there.

Despite the negatives I mentioned, they're nothing in comparison to the joy I had when playing this game. It's very fun and addicting. Even if you don't end up being obsessed with the game like I was, I'm sure you'll at least enjoy it for one full playthrough. It's very impressive how much content is packed into the game and there's a lot about it to enjoy.

Do I recommend this game?: The short answer is yes, the longer take is yes *BUT* I have a few gripes with it that I personally feel would really push the game from being pretty good, to really great and one of a kind.

My main issue is that there’s a MASSIVE and I mean HUGE waste of pressure, the time limit is there sure, but the game never *feels* like it pressures you enough to do anything. Why have the option to hide contraband is guards don’t check your cell?

Why when you go out and have to be back at certain hours do they magically teleport you back? There’s no sensation of ‘I really need to hurry back!’ It all feels a bit pointless in a way? 

Some bits in the story feel like they go absolutely nowhere. It would be really cool to get a sort of ‘what happened to them after’ type of really brief cutscene of all the prisoners or the ones you’ve max friended? 

Honestly I keep looking at games like the Escapists and comparing them. Even if they aren’t alike in a way I feel like having something similar. Being able to miss headcount and having the guards call a lockdown, having more events, *real* deadlines would really hammer that need for time in.

It feels bland in spots, there’s not much incentive (personally) to join one gang over the other, the rewards don’t feel good enough to prioritize with the negatives. 

Having more items, decor, events, stories and even real push would make the game even more of a gem in my opinion.

Back to the Dawn has lots of elements that I love in a game. There is loot, crafting, strategy, dice rolls and a "don't starve" style mechanic for hunger and sanity. I really really like this game! I played it for 35.1 hours in 2 weeks time JUST doing Bob's campaign.

I want you to think of this game as Baldur's Gate 3 skill-checks system mixed with Persona calendar/activity system (where time only goes forward when you do an activity.)

Each day you have to make decisions on whether to boost your character stats, make money from jobs, build rapport with prisoners for perks, or investigate ways to escape. It's highly replayable, and fleshed-out. So many routes to follow, lots of lore, lots of fun.

The game has a 21 in-game days time limit with a central plot for each character to follow. Some people see this as a negative, but I think that's a big part of the fun for me. Besides, the game isn't super strict about it, it's surprisingly flexible.

With that said there's SO many things to do in the game, and the game actually rewards you for thinking of the many ways you can "solve" a quest. For example, one quest has you look for ways to investigate something in the infirmary. You can either take a laxative to go to the infirmary and ask the doctor yourself, or ask another inmate who works there.

The game also has NG+ where your stats carry over for you to try things differently. All in all, I highly recommend this game. I am surprised that I don't see enough people talking about it. Please consider playing!

[h1]Introduction[/h1]
Going into Don’t Scream, I expected a short horror gimmick built around a novelty mechanic. The idea of a game that forces you to stay silent sounds clever, but also risky—if the concept doesn’t hold up, the experience could easily feel shallow or frustrating.

After playing it, Don’t Scream proves that its core idea is strong enough to carry the experience. While it’s not a long or complex game, it delivers a focused, tense, and genuinely nerve-wracking horror experience that succeeds because of its simplicity.

[h1]Bias[/h1]
I enjoy psychological horror more than action-heavy or jump-scare-driven games. Games that rely on tension, atmosphere, and player restraint tend to resonate with me more than those that overwhelm you with constant threats.

Because of that, Don’t Scream immediately appealed to me. However, players who dislike trial-and-error gameplay or who find microphone-based mechanics uncomfortable may have a harder time enjoying what the game offers.

[h1]Gameplay[/h1]
The gameplay of Don’t Scream revolves around one simple rule: you must survive for a set amount of time without making a sound. Using your microphone, the game listens for any noise—screams, sudden reactions, or even accidental sounds can reset your progress.

Exploration is slow and deliberate. You’re encouraged to move carefully through a dark forest environment while unsettling events unfold around you. The lack of combat or traditional objectives shifts the focus entirely onto player composure and awareness.

What makes the game effective is how it turns the player’s own reactions into the main challenge. Fear isn’t just something you experience—it’s something you must actively suppress.

[h1]Balance, issues, and things to know before playing[/h1]
Don’t Scream can be frustrating at times, especially when progress is lost due to minor or unavoidable noises. This is part of the design, but it won’t appeal to everyone.

The game is also relatively short, with limited replayability once you’ve learned most of the scares. Its impact relies heavily on first-time tension rather than long-term depth.

Before playing, it’s important to be in a quiet environment and to understand that patience and self-control matter more than skill. This is a game about endurance, not mastery.

[h1]Conclusion[/h1]
Don’t Scream is a clever and effective psychological horror experience that uses a unique mechanic to create genuine tension. It doesn’t overstay its welcome and succeeds by fully committing to its core idea.

If you enjoy experimental horror games and don’t mind short, intense experiences, Don’t Scream is worth trying—especially if you’re confident you can keep your cool under pressure. 😶‍🌫️

First impression: The game is graphically beautiful. The world breathes like an actual forest. And when the sun went down I was genuinely terrified of what's to come. There was a handful of good jump-scares and unintentional yelps. with each location feeling like a room in a haunted horror-house.

Second impression (30ish minutes in): It felt like a walking simulator. You'll occasionally get killed by an unavoidable bear, or fall into a shallow pit and have to restart. Having to walk to each place again and experience the same jump-scares felt super repetitive. It's missing sustenance; perhaps a compass that only showed unlocked locations, or a map showing uncharted territory so I could explore newer locations.

The game feels like a 30 minute haunted house attraction. each room offering a uniquely suspenseful or thrilling moment. Once it's over it doesn't need to be revisited, at least until next Halloween.

I would recommend this game to people who enjoy walking around and being scared.

When it comes down to it, I am very hard to scare in any kind of horror game.  So, no, I didn't die to screaming.  However, that doesn't mean this is a bad game.  This game still managed to give me chills at some points, and even got me itching for a bit there.  It's been quite fun.

That said, there's some things you need to understand.  First, no, just surviving the night is not the same as beating the game.  There are 46 points of interest to find, plus a bunch of number of hidden items.  You can't visit them all at once, so it's going to take multiple playthroughs.

In addition to all the jumpscares, there are hunters.  This can be anything from bears, to creepy marionettes, to a shadowy literal hunter.  Each has distinct sounds you'll learn, and once they get close you can hear heartbeats to signify the danger.  So, you'll need to learn how to tell the difference between illusion, and real threats.

A lot of changes were made based on community feedback.  More points of interest, lore, the hunters, etc. were all based on community feedback.  That's why you'll notice a major difference between recent reviews and the overall reviews.  The devs here listened, and made this into an amazing experience.

That said, as with all things in life, you will need to do your part.  In other words, if you just play a small part of a game, don't be surprised when you don't get much out of it.  Personally, I got 100% completion before writing this review, and loved every minute of it.

DON’T SCREAM is an experimental horror game that thrives on a brilliantly simple but psychologically intense idea: you must wander through a dark, haunted forest until 8:00 a.m., but if you scream, gasp, yelp, or make any sound loud enough to trigger your microphone, the game immediately ends. Developed and published by Digital Cybercherries, the game turns silence into the player’s most fragile lifeline and fear into an enemy that’s entirely internal. Rather than relying on complex systems or elaborate mechanics, it strips horror down to a raw confrontation between the player and their own involuntary reactions. The resulting experience is an unusual blend of dread, restraint, and personal vulnerability, making every footstep and every faint noise feel meaningful and dangerous.

The structure of the game revolves around a found-footage aesthetic, presenting the entire experience through the grainy, unstable lens of a handheld camcorder. This visual choice gives the forest an eerie, documentary-like quality, as though you’ve stumbled into a cursed VHS tape or an old paranormal investigation gone wrong. Time only progresses when you move, which creates a deliberate, suffocating tension—standing still feels safe but impotent, while walking becomes a gamble where every shadow, rustle, or distant noise might trigger a scare that forces you to start all over. The environment uses minimal storytelling cues but excels in mood, surrounding the player with darkness, abandoned objects, spectral silhouettes, and subtle audio cues that chip away at your composure long before any large scare arrives.

The game’s core mechanic—the microphone-based scream detection—is both its defining feature and the source of much of its chaotic charm. By linking survival to the player’s real-world reactions, DON’T SCREAM creates a deeply personal form of horror where losing control of your own voice becomes the central threat. Jump scares are frequent and occasionally unpredictable, and because they target physiological startle responses, even a small jolt of fear can undo an entire run. Early players have shared stories ranging from being undone by a sudden scare to accidentally triggering failure through a sneeze, cough, or burst of nervous laughter. These moments, while frustrating, create a unique tension that few horror games can replicate—the fear that your own body might betray you.

However, the game’s design also makes it inherently limited in scope. Once the novelty of the scream mechanic wears off, the lack of deeper narrative elements, puzzles, or evolving gameplay loops becomes apparent. The forest, while atmospheric, is not large enough to sustain long-term exploration, and repeated runs may lead to familiarity that dilutes the impact of the scares. The experience is intentionally streamlined: you are not meant to uncover an elaborate story or master a complex system, but simply to endure. For some players, especially those who prefer horror that unfolds slowly or offers narrative richness, this minimalism may feel shallow. The game’s reliance on jump scares over psychological buildup also means that tension rises and falls in sharp spikes, which not every player will find satisfying over multiple sessions.

Despite its brevity and focus on shock, DON’T SCREAM succeeds at delivering an intense, high-adrenaline form of horror that many players find refreshingly direct. Its strength lies in the authenticity of your own reactions—the game’s power comes not from scripted monsters or cinematic sequences, but from the knowledge that the smallest loss of composure has real, immediate consequences. There is no safety in shouting, no relief in venting fear, no catharsis in vocal release; the horror builds inside you, quietly, until it explodes. This dynamic gives even the simplest environmental cue a weight that more elaborate horror games often struggle to achieve.

In the end, DON’T SCREAM stands out not because it offers a deep or complex horror experience, but because it commits fully to a single, memorable concept. It is an experimental title best approached with the understanding that its purpose is to provoke, startle, and toy with your senses rather than immerse you in a long narrative. For those who enjoy intense, self-contained horror challenges—or for anyone curious about a game that turns their own voice into the ultimate enemy—it offers a striking and surprisingly effective experience. While it may not have the longevity or depth of larger horror titles, its commitment to atmospheric dread and self-imposed silence creates moments that linger long after the screen fades to black.

[h1]Introduction[/h1]
Going into Don’t Scream, I expected a short horror gimmick built around a novelty mechanic. The idea of a game that forces you to stay silent sounds clever, but also risky—if the concept doesn’t hold up, the experience could easily feel shallow or frustrating.

After playing it, Don’t Scream proves that its core idea is strong enough to carry the experience. While it’s not a long or complex game, it delivers a focused, tense, and genuinely nerve-wracking horror experience that succeeds because of its simplicity.

[h1]Bias[/h1]
I enjoy psychological horror more than action-heavy or jump-scare-driven games. Games that rely on tension, atmosphere, and player restraint tend to resonate with me more than those that overwhelm you with constant threats.

Because of that, Don’t Scream immediately appealed to me. However, players who dislike trial-and-error gameplay or who find microphone-based mechanics uncomfortable may have a harder time enjoying what the game offers.

[h1]Gameplay[/h1]
The gameplay of Don’t Scream revolves around one simple rule: you must survive for a set amount of time without making a sound. Using your microphone, the game listens for any noise—screams, sudden reactions, or even accidental sounds can reset your progress.

Exploration is slow and deliberate. You’re encouraged to move carefully through a dark forest environment while unsettling events unfold around you. The lack of combat or traditional objectives shifts the focus entirely onto player composure and awareness.

What makes the game effective is how it turns the player’s own reactions into the main challenge. Fear isn’t just something you experience—it’s something you must actively suppress.

[h1]Balance, issues, and things to know before playing[/h1]
Don’t Scream can be frustrating at times, especially when progress is lost due to minor or unavoidable noises. This is part of the design, but it won’t appeal to everyone.

The game is also relatively short, with limited replayability once you’ve learned most of the scares. Its impact relies heavily on first-time tension rather than long-term depth.

Before playing, it’s important to be in a quiet environment and to understand that patience and self-control matter more than skill. This is a game about endurance, not mastery.

[h1]Conclusion[/h1]
Don’t Scream is a clever and effective psychological horror experience that uses a unique mechanic to create genuine tension. It doesn’t overstay its welcome and succeeds by fully committing to its core idea.

If you enjoy experimental horror games and don’t mind short, intense experiences, Don’t Scream is worth trying—especially if you’re confident you can keep your cool under pressure. 😶‍🌫️

First impression: The game is graphically beautiful. The world breathes like an actual forest. And when the sun went down I was genuinely terrified of what's to come. There was a handful of good jump-scares and unintentional yelps. with each location feeling like a room in a haunted horror-house.

Second impression (30ish minutes in): It felt like a walking simulator. You'll occasionally get killed by an unavoidable bear, or fall into a shallow pit and have to restart. Having to walk to each place again and experience the same jump-scares felt super repetitive. It's missing sustenance; perhaps a compass that only showed unlocked locations, or a map showing uncharted territory so I could explore newer locations.

The game feels like a 30 minute haunted house attraction. each room offering a uniquely suspenseful or thrilling moment. Once it's over it doesn't need to be revisited, at least until next Halloween.

I would recommend this game to people who enjoy walking around and being scared.

When it comes down to it, I am very hard to scare in any kind of horror game.  So, no, I didn't die to screaming.  However, that doesn't mean this is a bad game.  This game still managed to give me chills at some points, and even got me itching for a bit there.  It's been quite fun.

That said, there's some things you need to understand.  First, no, just surviving the night is not the same as beating the game.  There are 46 points of interest to find, plus a bunch of number of hidden items.  You can't visit them all at once, so it's going to take multiple playthroughs.

In addition to all the jumpscares, there are hunters.  This can be anything from bears, to creepy marionettes, to a shadowy literal hunter.  Each has distinct sounds you'll learn, and once they get close you can hear heartbeats to signify the danger.  So, you'll need to learn how to tell the difference between illusion, and real threats.

A lot of changes were made based on community feedback.  More points of interest, lore, the hunters, etc. were all based on community feedback.  That's why you'll notice a major difference between recent reviews and the overall reviews.  The devs here listened, and made this into an amazing experience.

That said, as with all things in life, you will need to do your part.  In other words, if you just play a small part of a game, don't be surprised when you don't get much out of it.  Personally, I got 100% completion before writing this review, and loved every minute of it.

DON’T SCREAM is an experimental horror game that thrives on a brilliantly simple but psychologically intense idea: you must wander through a dark, haunted forest until 8:00 a.m., but if you scream, gasp, yelp, or make any sound loud enough to trigger your microphone, the game immediately ends. Developed and published by Digital Cybercherries, the game turns silence into the player’s most fragile lifeline and fear into an enemy that’s entirely internal. Rather than relying on complex systems or elaborate mechanics, it strips horror down to a raw confrontation between the player and their own involuntary reactions. The resulting experience is an unusual blend of dread, restraint, and personal vulnerability, making every footstep and every faint noise feel meaningful and dangerous.

The structure of the game revolves around a found-footage aesthetic, presenting the entire experience through the grainy, unstable lens of a handheld camcorder. This visual choice gives the forest an eerie, documentary-like quality, as though you’ve stumbled into a cursed VHS tape or an old paranormal investigation gone wrong. Time only progresses when you move, which creates a deliberate, suffocating tension—standing still feels safe but impotent, while walking becomes a gamble where every shadow, rustle, or distant noise might trigger a scare that forces you to start all over. The environment uses minimal storytelling cues but excels in mood, surrounding the player with darkness, abandoned objects, spectral silhouettes, and subtle audio cues that chip away at your composure long before any large scare arrives.

The game’s core mechanic—the microphone-based scream detection—is both its defining feature and the source of much of its chaotic charm. By linking survival to the player’s real-world reactions, DON’T SCREAM creates a deeply personal form of horror where losing control of your own voice becomes the central threat. Jump scares are frequent and occasionally unpredictable, and because they target physiological startle responses, even a small jolt of fear can undo an entire run. Early players have shared stories ranging from being undone by a sudden scare to accidentally triggering failure through a sneeze, cough, or burst of nervous laughter. These moments, while frustrating, create a unique tension that few horror games can replicate—the fear that your own body might betray you.

However, the game’s design also makes it inherently limited in scope. Once the novelty of the scream mechanic wears off, the lack of deeper narrative elements, puzzles, or evolving gameplay loops becomes apparent. The forest, while atmospheric, is not large enough to sustain long-term exploration, and repeated runs may lead to familiarity that dilutes the impact of the scares. The experience is intentionally streamlined: you are not meant to uncover an elaborate story or master a complex system, but simply to endure. For some players, especially those who prefer horror that unfolds slowly or offers narrative richness, this minimalism may feel shallow. The game’s reliance on jump scares over psychological buildup also means that tension rises and falls in sharp spikes, which not every player will find satisfying over multiple sessions.

Despite its brevity and focus on shock, DON’T SCREAM succeeds at delivering an intense, high-adrenaline form of horror that many players find refreshingly direct. Its strength lies in the authenticity of your own reactions—the game’s power comes not from scripted monsters or cinematic sequences, but from the knowledge that the smallest loss of composure has real, immediate consequences. There is no safety in shouting, no relief in venting fear, no catharsis in vocal release; the horror builds inside you, quietly, until it explodes. This dynamic gives even the simplest environmental cue a weight that more elaborate horror games often struggle to achieve.

In the end, DON’T SCREAM stands out not because it offers a deep or complex horror experience, but because it commits fully to a single, memorable concept. It is an experimental title best approached with the understanding that its purpose is to provoke, startle, and toy with your senses rather than immerse you in a long narrative. For those who enjoy intense, self-contained horror challenges—or for anyone curious about a game that turns their own voice into the ultimate enemy—it offers a striking and surprisingly effective experience. While it may not have the longevity or depth of larger horror titles, its commitment to atmospheric dread and self-imposed silence creates moments that linger long after the screen fades to black.

game_name: Revenge of the Boxer: Moscow Criminality
review_text:
Revenge of the Boxer: Moscow Criminality, developed by Alligator Friends Software and published by Strategy First, is a gritty, retro-inspired beat-’em-up that transports players into the harsh underworld of Moscow’s criminal scene. Originally released in the mid-2000s and later reintroduced on Steam, the game embraces an old-school action formula built around straightforward combat, urban environments, and a revenge-driven storyline. While it does not attempt to reinvent the genre, it offers a nostalgic, arcade-style brawling experience that leans heavily into atmosphere and raw momentum.

The story centers on a professional boxer whose life is shattered when he is falsely accused of murder. Stripped of his career and freedom, he is thrown into prison for a crime he did not commit. Refusing to accept his fate, he escapes custody and embarks on a violent quest through Moscow’s criminal underworld to clear his name. This simple but effective premise fuels the action from beginning to end. Rather than presenting a deeply layered narrative, the game uses its revenge plot as a catalyst for continuous confrontations with gang members, corrupt figures, and a colorful cast of street-level adversaries.

Gameplay unfolds in a side-view perspective reminiscent of classic arcade brawlers. Players guide the protagonist through various urban environments, engaging in hand-to-hand combat against waves of enemies. The mechanics are intentionally accessible: punches, blocks, dodges, and basic combinations form the core of the combat system. Unlike modern fighting games that emphasize intricate combo trees or layered mechanics, Revenge of the Boxer focuses on timing and positioning. Success often depends on reading enemy movements, choosing when to strike, and maintaining control of crowd situations. The pacing keeps fights energetic, with a steady rhythm that encourages aggressive play while still rewarding cautious defense.

The protagonist’s boxing background influences the feel of combat. Strikes carry weight, and the emphasis on close-range fighting reinforces the character’s identity as a trained athlete thrust into street warfare. While enemy AI may not be particularly advanced, encounters gain tension through numbers and varied enemy types. Some opponents rush aggressively, while others hang back, forcing the player to manage spacing and movement carefully. Though the AI can feel simplistic at times, the sheer volume of confrontations maintains engagement.

Visually, the game reflects its era with stylized 3D character models and environments presented from a side-on perspective. The art direction captures a gritty urban atmosphere, featuring dim alleyways, industrial zones, and rundown neighborhoods that reinforce the crime-driven narrative. Character designs lean toward exaggerated archetypes, adding personality to the adversaries encountered throughout the campaign. While the graphics do not meet modern standards of detail or fluidity, they carry a distinct charm rooted in early 2000s action titles.

The game offers multiple modes to extend replay value. Career Mode drives the narrative forward through successive stages, while standalone battle modes allow players to jump directly into combat scenarios. A local multiplayer option adds further appeal, enabling two players to engage in head-to-head fights. Though the absence of online multiplayer limits its competitive longevity, the inclusion of varied modes ensures there is more than just a linear campaign to experience.

One of the game’s defining strengths lies in its accessibility. Controls are straightforward, and players can quickly understand the mechanics without extensive tutorials. This simplicity allows the focus to remain on the visceral satisfaction of landing punches and clearing groups of enemies. At the same time, extended play sessions may reveal repetition in enemy behavior and stage progression. The game does not introduce significant mechanical evolution over time, so enjoyment largely depends on appreciation for its straightforward design.

Despite its limitations, Revenge of the Boxer: Moscow Criminality manages to carve out a unique identity. Its setting distinguishes it from more common Western crime narratives, offering a Moscow backdrop that adds cultural texture. The combination of revenge story, street-level combat, and exaggerated characters gives the game a slightly pulpy tone that borders on camp at times. For players who enjoy retro beat-’em-ups and straightforward brawling action, this tone can be part of its appeal.

Ultimately, Revenge of the Boxer: Moscow Criminality is a product of its time, delivering a focused and uncomplicated action experience. It may lack the polish and complexity of modern fighting titles, but it compensates with energetic pacing and a clear sense of purpose. For those seeking a nostalgic return to arcade-style combat infused with a gritty urban revenge narrative, it provides a surprisingly engaging ride through Moscow’s criminal underbelly.

Rating: 8/10

Innovative but flawed game that’s ahead of its time 4/5 

I played the beta in 2010 and hated it because back then I was still used to playing traditional RTS games like WarCraft III and C&C Generals. I’ve been a huge fan of RTS since the 1990s. I have played games like Red Alert, Rise of Nations, Age of Empires, StarCraft, Sudden Strike, Company of Heroes, Homeworld, Supreme Commander, and many others. 

Given the passage of time, it is clear that the developers were ahead of their time in recognizing the death of RTS. Since the release of C&C 4, RTS has been mostly dead other than a few rare gems like StarCraft II and Age of Empires IV. 

Why? Players have left RTS and went to games that focused on sub-elements of strategy games that they enjoy the most. For example, if you love base building it’s more fun to play something like Anno. If you love microing individual units it’s more fun to play MOBA. You also have games like Total War that focus on combat involving thousands of soldiers. 

It’s just hard to get people engaged with RTS where you spend 10 minutes building workers and making sure you don’t lose your whole base to a zerg rush. 
Commendably, C&C 4 solves that by: 
1.	Getting rid of building workers, aging up, researching tech, managing resource collectors 
2.	Have respawning “bases” that allows a player to restart without having to quit their current game 
3.	Focus on combat which is what most players want

Look at some of the most popular arcade maps on StarCraft. Doesn’t C&C 4 look similar to Nexus Wars or Desert Strike? 

When StarCraft II came out, almost all of my gamer friends bought the game, but very few of them ended up playing the multiplayer ladder. It was simply not fun to spend 10 minutes optimizing workers only to die to some unexpected rush. 

The fact that so few RTS games have succeeded in the 15 years since C&C 4’s release proves that the formula simply doesn’t work anymore. C&C 4 brings a ton of innovation and should be commended as a good attempt to bring more players into RTS.
1t 1s still a great game

The worst mainline C&C game. The conclusion to the Tiberium saga is unsatisfying, it just boils down to some screwing around, before Kane leaves earth using the last remaining Scrin tower, and earth is saved. None of the foreshadowing and plot elements from the previous games survive to this game. Cutscenes and writing are the lowest quality in the whole series.

The gameplay sucks. They completely removed all base building and economy, you create units with points from a mobile build/base unit and that's it. It's basically a MOBA with multiple units. Skirmish games end up just you pushing back and forth with Identical point sized armies. Because of the simplistic "rock, paper, scissors" counter design, the back and forth could take forever. 

Worst of all some genius decided that you need to level up your profile to unlock higher tier units before you can use them in game... even in the campaign!

As of December 2025 the game still works, despite the mass negative reviews saying otherwise. What you need to do is very simple. Change your EA account password to be exactly 8 characters long with no special symbols, only letters and numbers. This is the minimum strength password allowed by the EA account. The C&C4 client doesn't allow any special characters and it has a max password length of 8. If your password is longer it won't login, and give you an unhelpful error message.

Edit: (Added Clarification)

Despite having nothing but criticisms, I still enjoyed CNC4 enough to play a bunch of skirmish and completed both campaigns.

There are plenty of games that I have little to criticise, but I never picked up again for a second session. If that's not an endorsment then I don't know what is.

Skirmish games end up just you pushing back and forth with Identical point sized armies. Because of the simplistic "rock, paper, scissors" counter design, the back and forth could take forever. 

Worst of all some genius decided that you need to level up your profile to unlock higher tier units before you can use them in game... even in the campaign!

As of December 2025 the game still works, despite the mass negative reviews saying otherwise. What you need to do is very simple. Change your EA account password to be exactly 8 characters long with no special symbols, only letters and numbers. This is the minimum strength password allowed by the EA account. The C&C4 client doesn't allow any special characters and it has a max password length of 8. If your password is longer it won't login, and give you an unhelpful error message.

Edit: (Added Clarification)

Despite having nothing but criticisms, I still enjoyed CNC4 enough to play a bunch of skirmish and completed both campaigns.

There are plenty of games that I have little to criticise, but I never picked up again for a second session. If that's not an endorsment then I don't know what is.

Innovative but flawed game that’s ahead of its time 4/5 

I played the beta in 2010 and hated it because back then I was still used to playing traditional RTS games like WarCraft III and C&C Generals. I’ve been a huge fan of RTS since the 1990s. I have played games like Red Alert, Rise of Nations, Age of Empires, StarCraft, Sudden Strike, Company of Heroes, Homeworld, Supreme Commander, and many others. 

Given the passage of time, it is clear that the developers were ahead of their time in recognizing the death of RTS. Since the release of C&C 4, RTS has been mostly dead other than a few rare gems like StarCraft II and Age of Empires IV. 

Why? Players have left RTS and went to games that focused on sub-elements of strategy games that they enjoy the most. For example, if you love base building it’s more fun to play something like Anno. If you love microing individual units it’s more fun to play MOBA. You also have games like Total War that focus on combat involving thousands of soldiers. 

It’s just hard to get people engaged with RTS where you spend 10 minutes building workers and making sure you don’t lose your whole base to a zerg rush. 
Commendably, C&C 4 solves that by: 
1.	Getting rid of building workers, aging up, researching tech, managing resource collectors 
2.	Have respawning “bases” that allows a player to restart without having to quit their current game 
3.	Focus on combat which is what most players want

Look at some of the most popular arcade maps on StarCraft. Doesn’t C&C 4 look similar to Nexus Wars or Desert Strike? 

When StarCraft II came out, almost all of my gamer friends bought the game, but very few of them ended up playing the multiplayer ladder. It was simply not fun to spend 10 minutes optimizing workers only to die to some unexpected rush. 

The fact that so few RTS games have succeeded in the 15 years since C&C 4’s release proves that the formula simply doesn’t work anymore. C&C 4 brings a ton of innovation and should be commended as a good attempt to bring more players into RTS.
